topics.title: Synthetic Fertilizers, Classification of Fertilizers, Role of Essential Elements in Plant Growth, Macro Elements and Micro Elements, Application of Fertilizers Considering Nutrient Balance and Types of Crop, Development of Fertilizer Industry
Fertilizer Production and Consumption in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fertilizer Production and Consumption in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fertilizer (FAO Definition, Adopted in Fertilizer Control Order - FCO 1985, India):**
A fertilizer is any organic or inorganic material of natural or synthetic origin (other than liming materials) that is added to a soil to supply one or more **primary nutrients** (Nitrogen-N, Phosphorus-P, Potassium-K), **secondary nutrients** (Calcium, Magnesium, Sulphur), or **micronutrients** (Zn, Fe, Mn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl) essential for plant growth and soil fertility maintenance.

**Fertilizer Production (Industry Definition):**
Fertilizer production in India refers to the indigenous manufacture of straight nitrogenous, phosphatic, potassic, and complex fertilizers through chemical process industries (CPI), measured in metric tonnes (MT) of finished product or in terms of plant nutrient content (N, P₂O₅, K₂O).

**Fertilizer Consumption (Agricultural Definition):**
The total quantity of plant nutrients (in **'000 nutrient tonnes** or **kg per hectare**) actually applied to agricultural soils during a cropping season, aggregated nationally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically requires understanding the **gap between production and demand**, the **dependency on imports** for raw materials (rock phosphate, sulphur, MOP), and the **policy frameworks** that govern Indian fertilizer sector.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Fertilizers as Vitamins for Plants"

Think of Indian agriculture as a massive, **fitness-driven population of 1.4 billion** whose food security depends on continuous, balanced nutrition. Just like a human body requires carbohydrates (energy), proteins (growth), and minerals (immunity), plants require **Nitrogen (leafy growth)**, **Phosphorus (root & flower development)**, and **Potassium (overall plant vigour & disease resistance)**.

**The Indian Fertilizer Industry = The National Pharmaceutical Supply Chain:**
- **Pharmaceutical Companies** = Fertilizer Manufacturers (IFFCO, NFL, RCF)
- **Generic Medicines** = Straight/Common Fertilizers (Urea, DAP, MOP)
- **Multivitamins** = Complex/Mixed Fertilizers
- **Government Price Control** = Fertilizer Subsidy Policy
- **Imports of Active Ingredients** = Raw Material Imports (Rock Phosphate from Morocco, MOP from Belarus/Russia/Canada)

The Indian government, like a responsible "health insurer," subsidizes fertilizers to ensure **affordability for the farmer (the consumer)**, while balancing the **industry's commercial viability**.

---

### 1.3 Key Statistical Indicators at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Current Position of India in Global Fertilizer Economy (≈ 2024 data):**
> - **2nd largest consumer** of fertilizers globally (after China)
> - **3rd largest producer** globally (after China & Russia)
> - **Per-hectare consumption:** **≈ 137 kg/ha** (nutrient basis) — significantly higher than world average of **≈ 135 kg/ha**
> - **Subsidy burden:** **₹1.5 – 2.0 Lakh Crore per annum**
> - **Self-sufficiency:** Urea ≈ 85% indigenous; DAP ≈ 50% indigenous; **MOP = 100% imported**

![India Fertilizer Industry Overview Banner - Conceptual Placeholder]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Indian Fertilizer Industry

The Indian fertilizer industry can be classified along **four axes**, which are frequently asked in KTU examinations:

#### A. By Nutrient Type
1. **Nitrogenous Fertilizers:** Urea, Ammonium Sulphate (AS), Ammonium Nitrate (AN), Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)
2. **Phosphatic Fertilizers:** Single Super Phosphate (SSP), Triple Super Phosphate (TSP), Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP)
3. **Potassic Fertilizers:** Muriate of Potash (MOP), Sulphate of Potash (SOP)
4. **Complex Fertilizers:** NPK grades (10:26:26, 12:32:16, 14:35:14, 15:15:15, 17:17:17, 19:19:19)
5. **Micronutrient Mixtures & Fortified Fertilizers:** Zincated Urea, Boronated SSP, DAP fortified with Zinc

#### B. By Sector
| Sector | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Public Sector** | NFL (National Fertilizers Ltd.), RCF (Rashtriya Chemicals & Fertilizers), FACT (Fertilizers & Chemicals Travancore), GNFC, GSFC |
| **Cooperative Sector** | IFFCO, KRIBHCO, Krishak Bharati Cooperative |
| **Private Sector** | Tata Chemicals, Chambal Fertilizers, Zuari Industries, Coromandel International, Deepak Fertilizers |

#### C. By Feedstock Route
- **Gas-based** plants (preferred, ~85% of urea capacity) — natural gas from KG basin, Hazira, Dahej
- **Naptha-based** plants (legacy, being phased out)
- **Coal gasification-based** (Talcher, Ramagundam — under revival)
- **Furnace oil-based** plants (closed/revived)

#### D. By Production Status
- **Operating Units** (registered with DOF)
- **Closed Units** (awaiting revival under New Investment Policy)
- **Sick Units** (BSFC, HFCL, etc.)

---

### 2.2 Historical Evolution — Timeline (Frequently Asked in 2-Mark Questions)

```
1926 — First fertilizer unit (Sindri, Bihar) — conceptualization
   |
1942 — Travancore Chemicals started SSP production (Kerala)
   |
1951 — Sindri Fertilizer Plant (first public sector plant, Jharkhand)
   |
1961 — Fertilizer Control Order (FCO) promulgated
   |
1966 — IFFCO founded as cooperative
   |
1973 — Oil crisis → shift to gas-based plants
   |
1977 — Retention Pricing Scheme (RPS) introduced — subsidy era begins
   |
1991 — Decontrol of P&K fertilizers (partial liberalization)
   |
2003 — New Pricing Scheme (NPS) for urea
   |
2010 — Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) for P&K fertilizers
   |
2015 — 100% Neem Coated Urea mandate
   |
2018 — New Urea Policy (NUP-2018)
   |
2022 — PM PRANAM, Urea Gold launched; One Nation One Fertilizer (Bharat Brand)
```

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Standard Units / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Nutrient Content from Fertilizer | $\text{Nutrient mass} = \text{Fertilizer mass} \times \frac{\% \text{ nutrient}}{100}$ | tonnes (t) |
| NPK Ratio | $\text{Ratio} = N : P_2O_5 : K_2O$ | dimensionless |
| **Ideal NPK Ratio (India)** | $4 : 2 : 1$ | Agronomically recommended |
| **Actual NPK Ratio (India)** | $\approx 6.5 : 2.7 : 1$ | Imbalanced — excess N |
| Per-hectare Consumption | $\text{kg/ha} = \frac{\text{Total nutrient consumed (kg)}}{\text{Total cropped area (ha)}}$ | **India ≈ 137 kg/ha**; World ≈ 135 kg/ha |
| Import Dependency | $\% \text{ Import} = \frac{\text{Imported quantity}}{\text{Indigenous} + \text{Imported}} \times 100$ | MOP = 100%, Urea ≈ 15%, DAP ≈ 50% |
| Capacity Utilization (CUF) | $\text{CUF} = \frac{\text{Actual production}}{\text{Installed capacity}} \times 100$ | %; typically 90–110% |
| Subsidy per Tonne | $\text{Subsidy/t} = \text{MRP} - \text{Cost of production (with normative norms)}$ | ₹/t |
| Energy Consumption (Urea) | $\text{Gcal/t of urea} = \frac{\text{Total energy input}}{\text{Urea produced}}$ | **Best: 4.5 Gcal/t; Average: 6.5 Gcal/t** |

---

### 2.4 Policy Framework — The "Big Three" Subsidy Schemes

#### A. Urea Subsidy — Under New Urea Policy (NUP-2018)
- **Pricing Method:** Cost-plus approach with group-based norms
- **MRP:** **₹266.50 per 45 kg bag** (since 2018, recently raised)
- **Subsidy:** ≈ ₹1,000–2,000 per bag (Government bears ~₹2.5 lakh crore annually)
- **Special Urea:** **Neem-coated urea (100%)**, **Urea Gold** (with humic acid & sulphur)

#### B. Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) — for P&K fertilizers (from 2010)
- **Subsidy is fixed per kg of nutrient** (N, P, K, S) — NOT per product
- **PPS system** (Per Tonne Subsidy) — adjusted annually based on global P&K prices
- **Market-determined MRP** for P&K fertilizers
- Fortified fertilizers get **additional 5–15% subsidy**

#### C. PM PRANAM (Promotion of Alternate Nutrients for Agriculture Management) — 2023
- Aims to **reduce chemical fertilizer use by promoting organic alternatives**
- States incentivized to reduce consumption

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Chemical Engineers** — Design of ammonia synthesis loops (Haber-Bosch), urea reactors, granulation plants.
2. **Mechanical Engineers** — High-pressure equipment (synthesis converters, CO₂ compressors), heat exchangers, prilling towers.
3. **Instrumentation Engineers** — Distributed Control Systems (DCS) for fertilizer plants.
4. **Industrial Economics** — Subsidy modeling, payback period calculations for fertilizer plants.
5. **Environmental Engineering** — Greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂, N₂O from urea), eutrophication management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Calculation 1 — Nutrient Content from a Given Fertilizer Mass

**Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark):**
A farmer applies **200 kg of urea (46% N)** and **100 kg of DAP (18% N, 46% P₂O₅)**. Calculate the total nitrogen supplied.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Nitrogen from Urea:**
$$N_{\text{urea}} = 200 \text{ kg} \times \frac{46}{100} = 92 \text{ kg of N}$$

**Step 2 — Nitrogen from DAP:**
$$N_{\text{DAP}} = 100 \text{ kg} \times \frac{18}{100} = 18 \text{ kg of N}$$

**Step 3 — Total Nitrogen supplied:**
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{urea}} + N_{\text{DAP}} \\
&= 92 + 18 \\
&= 110 \text{ kg of N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total P₂O₅ supplied (from DAP only):**
$$P_2O_5 = 100 \text{ kg} \times \frac{46}{100} = 46 \text{ kg of } P_2O_5$$

---

### 3.2 Calculation 2 — Per-Hectare Fertilizer Consumption

**Problem:**
India consumed **18.5 million tonnes of N, 7.8 MT of P₂O₅, and 3.2 MT of K₂O** in a recent year. Total cropped area = **215 million hectares**. Calculate (a) Total nutrient consumption, (b) Per-hectare consumption, (c) NPK ratio.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Nutrient Consumption (N + P₂O₅ + K₂O):**
$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 18.5 + 7.8 + 3.2 \\
&= 29.5 \text{ MT (million tonnes)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Per-Hectare Consumption:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{kg/ha} &= \frac{29.5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}}{215 \times 10^6 \text{ ha}} \\
&= \frac{29{,}500{,}000{,}000}{215{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 137.2 \text{ kg/ha}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — NPK Ratio (divide each by smallest, K₂O):**
$$\begin{aligned}
N : P_2O_5 : K_2O &= \frac{18.5}{3.2} : \frac{7.8}{3.2} : \frac{3.2}{3.2} \\
&= 5.78 : 2.44 : 1.00 \\
&\approx 5.8 : 2.4 : 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:** Actual ratio (5.8:2.4:1) vs Ideal ratio (4:2:1) → **Excess Nitrogen, deficient Potash** → **Imbalanced fertilization**, leading to soil degradation.

---

### 3.3 Calculation 3 — Import Dependency Analysis

**Problem:**
India's DAP requirement is **12 MT/year**. Indigenous production = **5.5 MT/year**. Calculate (a) Import quantity, (b) % Import dependency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Import Quantity:**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{import}} &= \text{Requirement} - \text{Indigenous Production} \\
&= 12 - 5.5 \\
&= 6.5 \text{ MT}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — % Import Dependency:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Import} &= \frac{D_{\text{import}}}{\text{Total Requirement}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6.5}{12} \times 100 \\
&= 54.17 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Note:** The remaining 45.83% is indigenous. India is the **world's largest DAP importer** because domestic production depends on imported **rock phosphate** (Morocco, Jordan, Egypt) and **phosphoric acid**.

---

### 3.4 Calculation 4 — Capacity Utilization of a Urea Plant

**Problem:**
A urea plant has installed capacity = **1,500 tonnes/day**. It operated for **330 days** in a year and produced **4,50,000 tonnes**. Calculate (a) Annual installed capacity, (b) CUF%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual Installed Capacity (theoretical max):**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{installed}} &= 1{,}500 \text{ t/day} \times 330 \text{ days} \\
&= 4{,}95{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Utilization Factor:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CUF} &= \frac{\text{Actual Production}}{C_{\text{installed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{4{,}95{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 90.91 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**KTU Interpretation:** CUF > 90% is considered **excellent** for a urea plant. Best plants in India (IFFCO Kalol, NFL Vijaipur) achieve **>100% CUF** (operating beyond nameplate capacity after debottlenecking).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — India's Top Fertilizer Producers (Product-Wise, Recent Estimates)

---

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each — Short Answer Type)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]`**
**What are the different categories of fertilizers based on the number of primary nutrients they contain? Give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**
Fertilizers are categorized based on the number of primary nutrients (N, P, K) they supply:

1. **Straight Fertilizers (Single Nutrient):** Supply only one primary nutrient.
   - *Examples:* Urea (46% N), Muriate of Potash - MOP (60% K₂O), SSP (16% P₂O₅)
2. **Complex Fertilizers (Multi-Nutrient):** Supply two or more primary nutrients in a single chemical compound/granule.
   - *Examples:* DAP (18-46-0), 10:26:26 NPK, 12:32:16 NPK, 15:15:15 NPK
3. **Mixed/Bulk Blended Fertilizers:** Physical mixtures of straight fertilizers.
   - *Examples:* Customized field blends
4. **Fortified/Value-Added Fertilizers:** Coated or enriched with secondary/micronutrients.
   - *Examples:* Zincated Urea, Boronated SSP, DAP with Zn, Urea Gold

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]`**
**Why is India 100% dependent on imports for Muriate of Potash (MOP)? Explain the strategic implications.**

**Model Answer:**
**Reasons for 100% Import Dependency on MOP:**

1. **Geological Scarcity:** India does not have commercially viable potash mineral deposits (Sylvite, Carnalite) in the country. The major reserves are in Canada (Saskatchewan), Russia, Belarus, Germany, and Israel/Jordan (Dead Sea brines).
2. **No Domestic Manufacturing:** India has zero installed capacity for MOP production.
3. **Economic Non-Viability:** Even with marginal reserves, extraction cost is uncompetitive.

**Strategic Implications:**

- **Forex Drain:** India spends approximately **₹15,000–20,000 crore annually** on MOP imports.
- **Price Volatility:** Exposed to international price fluctuations and cartel behavior (e.g., Belarus/Russia pricing).
- **Subsidy Burden:** Heavy Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) on K, since K-supply is critical for balanced fertilization.
- **Government Initiatives:** Promoting **alternative K sources** — SOP (Sulphate of Potash), K from seawater bitterns, banana pseudostem ash, and PM PRANAM to reduce K use through micronutrient alternatives.
- **Long-term Diplomatic Strategy:** India is exploring **potash partnerships** with Canada, Russia, Jordan, and Israel for long-term supply contracts and equity stakes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**`[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Internal Choice A]`**

**(a) Discuss in detail the production and consumption trends of fertilizers in India over the last decade. Highlight the role of urea, DAP, and complex fertilizers in Indian agriculture.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction — India's Fertilizer Landscape:**
India's fertilizer industry has undergone a paradigm shift, transitioning from **chronic shortages (1960s–80s)** to a position of **near self-sufficiency in nitrogenous fertilizers (urea)** while remaining **heavily import-dependent for phosphatic and potassic raw materials**.

**2. Production Trends (Last Decade):**

| Fertilizer | 2014-15 Production (MT) | 2023-24 Production (MT) | Growth Trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| Urea | ~22.5 | ~25.0 | Moderate growth (~11%) |
| DAP | ~3.6 | ~5.5 | Strong growth (~53%) |
| Complex | ~7.5 | ~9.0 | Steady growth (~20%) |
| SSP | ~4.0 | ~5.0 | Moderate growth |

**Key drivers of production growth:**
- Revamp of IFFCO, NFL, and Chambal plants
- Capacity additions in private sector (Chambal-III, Matix)
- Coastal plants (RCF-Thal, IFFCO Paradeep) with imported ammonia flexibility

**3. Consumption Trends:**

| Nutrient | 2014-15 Consumption (MT) | 2023-24 Consumption (MT) | Growth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | ~16.95 | ~18.50 | +9% |
| **P₂O₅** | ~6.10 | ~7.80 | +28% |
| **K₂O** | ~2.55 | ~3.20 | +25% |
| **Total** | ~25.60 | ~29.50 | +15% |

**Per-hectare consumption** rose from ~125 kg/ha to **~137 kg/ha** — now matching/exceeding the world average.

**4. Role of Key Fertilizers:**

- **Urea (46% N):** Workhorse of Indian agriculture. **85% of N consumption** comes from urea. Governed by **urea subsidy** (~₹2 lakh crore/year). Recently **100% neem-coated**.
- **DAP (18-46-0):** Preferred phosphatic fertilizer for basal application in **wheat, paddy, sugarcane**. Subject to **NBS subsidy**.
- **Complex fertilizers (10:26:26, 12:32:16):** Growing acceptance due to balanced nutrition in single application. Avoids multi-step mixing.

**5. Challenges:**
- Imbalanced NPK ratio (actual ≈ 5.8:2.4:1 vs ideal 4:2:1)
- Rising subsidy bill
- Raw material import dependence

**6. Conclusion:**
India must focus on **balanced fertilization**, **micronutrient fortification**, and **organic/integrated nutrient management** to sustain productivity.

---

**(b) Explain the Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme. Compare it with the urea subsidy regime. Why is the NPK ratio in India skewed toward nitrogen?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) Scheme — Introduced in 2010:**

**Concept:** Under NBS, subsidy is paid **per kg of nutrient** (N, P, K, S) regardless of the fertilizer product. Manufacturers fix the **MRP** based on market dynamics; Government pays a fixed per-kg subsidy amount called **Per Tonne Subsidy (PTS)**.

**Coverage:** All **Phosphatic (P) & Potassic (K) fertilizers** — DAP, MOP, SSP, TSP, 17 grades of NPK complex fertilizers, and ammonium sulphate (P-bearing).

**Subsidy Rates (Typical, ₹/kg of nutrient, illustrative):**
- N ≈ ₹47/kg
- P ≈ ₹55/kg
- K ≈ ₹25/kg
- S ≈ ₹2–3/kg

**2. Urea Subsidy Regime (Different Model):**

- Urea continues under the **cost-plus pricing model** with **New Urea Policy (NUP-2018)**.
- **MRP is fixed by Government** (₹266.50 per 45 kg bag, recently raised).
- Subsidy = Difference between normative cost of production and MRP.
- Highly distortionary: Urea is **artificially cheap** → farmers over-apply → imbalanced NPK ratio.

**3. Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | **Urea Subsidy** | **NBS (P&K)** |
|---|---|---|
| Year Introduced (Current Form) | 1977 (RPS), 2018 (NUP) | 2010 |
| Subsidy Basis | Per tonne of urea | Per kg of nutrient |
| MRP | Fixed by GoI | Market-determined |
| Pricing Method | Cost-plus | Subsidy-based, MRP floating |
| Subsidy Outflow | Very High (~₹1.5 lakh Cr) | Moderate (~₹40,000 Cr) |

**4. Why NPK Ratio is Skewed Toward Nitrogen:**

**(a) Price Distortion:** Urea is **2–3 times cheaper per kg of nutrient** than DAP/MOP.
$$\text{Cost per kg N from Urea} \approx ₹ 30-40 \text{ vs Cost per kg N from DAP} \approx ₹ 80-100$$

**(b) Easy Availability:** Urea is over-supplied through PDS and cooperatives, while MOP imports are constrained.

**(c) Farmer Perception:** Farmers erroneously equate **more urea = more yield**, ignoring the law of minimum (Liebig's Law).

**(d) Subsidy on Urea:** Higher implicit subsidy per unit of nitrogen.

**(e) Government Failure to Reform Urea:** Successive committees (Alagh, Tiwari) recommended urea decontrol, but political constraints (farmer votes) prevent reform.

**5. Conclusion:** Gradual rationalization through **Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)** in fertilizers, **PM PRANAM**, and **Fortified Fertilizers** aims to correct the imbalance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**`[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Internal Choice B]`**

**(a) Describe the major fertilizer producing companies in India under public, cooperative, and private sectors. Mention their plants and product specializations.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Public Sector Companies:**

- **National Fertilizers Ltd. (NFL)** — Plants at Nangal, Panipat, Bhatinda, Vijaipur (MP). **Products:** Urea primarily. Also manufactures **Biofertilizers**. Headquartered in Noida.
- **Rashtriya Chemicals & Fertilizers (RCF)** — Plants at Trombay (Mumbai) and Thal (Raigad). **Products:** Urea, complex fertilizers, **industrial chemicals** (methanol, dimethyl ether). Pioneer in India.
- **Fertilizers and Chemicals Travancore (FACT)** — Plants at Udyogamandal & Kochi (Kerala). **Products:** Urea, SSP, complex fertilizers. **Oldest fertilizer PSU** — recently revived.
- **Gujarat Narmada Valley Fertilizers (GNFC)** — Bharuch, Gujarat. **Products:** Urea, **nitric acid, ammonium nitrate**. Leverages gas from Hazira basin.
- **Gujarat State Fertilizers (GSFC)** — Vadodara, Sikka. **Products:** Urea, DAP, ammonium sulphate. **Diversified** into chemicals and petrochemicals.

**2. Cooperative Sector:**

- **Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative (IFFCO)** — **Largest fertilizer producer in India** by volume. Plants at Kalol, Kandla, Aonla, Phulpur, Paradeep. Products: Urea, DAP, NPK complex, **Nano Urea** (innovative). Pioneered **neem-coated urea** at scale.
- **Krishak Bharati Cooperative (KRIBHCO)** — Hazira plant (Gujarat). Gas-based urea plant. Recently forayed into DAP and complex fertilizers.
- **Indian Potash Limited (IPL)** — Facilitates **MOP imports** and distribution, not a manufacturer.

**3. Private Sector:**

- **Chambal Fertilizers** — Gadepan (Rajasthan), 3 plants. **Largest private-sector urea manufacturer.** Recently commissioned **Nano Urea** plant.
- **Coromandel International** — Visakhapatnam, Ennore, Kakinada. **Largest DAP manufacturer in India.** Also produces SSP, complex, **specialty fertilizers**.
- **Zuari Agro Chemicals** — Goa (Zuari Nagar). DAP, urea, complex, **water-soluble fertilizers**.
- **Tata Chemicals** — Babrala (UP), Haldia. **Soda ash + Urea** integrated plant.
- **Deepak Fertilizers** — Taloja (Maharashtra). Specializes in **nitric acid & ammonium nitrate**.

**4. Summary of Indian Industry Structure:**
- 3 sectors — **Public (≈ 35%), Cooperative (≈ 35%), Private (≈ 30%)** of urea production.
- **Heterogeneous feedstock** — natural gas dominant; naptha, coal, and imported ammonia-based units coexist.
- **Cooperative model unique to India** — globally distinctive approach.

---

**(b) Write notes on: (i) 100% Neem Coated Urea, (ii) Nano Urea, (iii) PM PRANAM Scheme.** **[7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) 100% Neem Coated Urea (NCU):**

- **Year of Mandatory Implementation:** 2015 (100%) by Department of Fertilizers.
- **Process:** Urea granules are coated with **neem oil/extract (~0.5–1% by weight)** during manufacturing.
- **Mechanism of Action:** Neem oil contains **nimbidin and azadirachtin** compounds which **inhibit urease enzyme activity** in soil → slows urea hydrolysis.
- **Benefits:**
  - Reduces nitrogen loss through volatilization (~10–15%).
  - Acts as slow-release fertilizer.
  - **Reduces urea consumption by ~10%**.
  - Improves crop yield by 5–15%.
  - Deterrent for **industrial/non-agricultural diversion** of urea.
- **Recent Advancement:** **Urea Gold** (2023) — fortified with humic acid, zinc, and sulphur for better nutrient delivery.

**(ii) Nano Urea:**

- **Developer:** IFFCO (Indian Farmers Fertilizer Cooperative).
- **Definition:** Liquid fertilizer containing **nanoscale urea particles (~30–50 nm size)** suspended in water.
- **Concentration:** Each bottle (500 ml) replaces one 45-kg bag of conventional urea → equivalent to 4% nitrogen.
- **Advantages:**
  - **Higher nitrogen use efficiency** (~85% vs 30–50% in conventional urea).
  - Reduces logistics burden (1 bottle vs 50 kg bag).
  - Environment-friendly — reduces soil and water pollution.
  - Targeted delivery via **foliar spray** during critical growth stages.
- **Commercial Production:** IFFCO Kalol plant (commissioned 2022). **World's first nano urea.**
- **Adoption Status:** Gradually expanding; over 5 crore bottles sold.

**(iii) PM PRANAM Scheme (Promotion of Alternate Nutrients for Agriculture Management):**

- **Launched:** 2023.
- **Objective:** To **reduce chemical fertilizer consumption** by promoting alternate nutrient sources (organic, biofertilizers, city compost, nano fertilizers).
- **Mechanism:**
  - States that reduce chemical fertilizer consumption by a target percentage are incentivized.
  - **50% of subsidy savings** from reduced consumption → returned to the state as grant.
  - **30%** → used by state for farmer education, awareness, and adoption of alternatives.
  - **20%** → given to farmers/fertilizer retailers as incentive.
- **Alignment:** Supports **organic farming, natural farming, balanced NPK use**, and reduction of **urea/DAP overuse**.
- **Significance:** First scheme to **financially incentivize states for reducing chemical fertilizer use** — addresses the long-standing imbalanced NPK problem.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Topic — Lose Up to 3-4 Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Urea Subsidy with NBS:** Writing that "DAP is subsidized by GoI" without specifying it is under the **NBS scheme**, while urea is under **NUP/Urea Subsidy** — different pricing models.
> 2. **Writing "India is self-sufficient in fertilizers":** Wrong — India is self-sufficient only in **nitrogen (urea)**, but **100% import-dependent for MOP** and **~50% for DAP** (rock phosphate + sulphur).
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Calculations:** Per-hectare consumption must be in **kg/ha** (NOT tonnes/ha). Always specify.
> 4. **Conflating "Production" vs "Capacity":** Always use **Installed Capacity** vs **Actual Production** distinction. **CUF = Actual/Installed × 100**.
> 5. **Skipping Sector Classification:** If asked about "major producers," mentioning only IFFCO without specifying **Public/Cooperative/Private** sector loses marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning Kerala's FACT:** Kerala students often forget to mention **FACT Udyogamandal & Kochi** — examiners love region-specific examples.
> 7. **Date/Precision Errors:** FCO was **1985**, not 1980. NBS was **2010**, not 2005. IFFCO founded **1966**. New Urea Policy = **2018**.
> 8. **Missing "Imbalanced NPK Ratio" insight:** If asked about challenges, ALWAYS mention **5.8:2.4:1 vs 4:2:1 ideal** — this is the **most frequently tested insight**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔥 High-Frequency Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before Exam:**

- ✅ **India = 2nd largest consumer, 3rd largest producer globally**
- ✅ **Per-hectare consumption ≈ 137 kg/ha; World average ≈ 135 kg/ha**
- ✅ **Actual NPK ratio = 5.8 : 2.4 : 1 vs Ideal = 4 : 2 : 1** (Imbalanced)
- ✅ **Subsidy: Urea ≈ ₹1.5 lakh Cr; NBS (P&K) ≈ ₹40,000 Cr; Total ≈ ₹2 lakh Cr annually**
- ✅ **MOP = 100% imported; DAP ≈ 50% imported; Urea ≈ 15% imported**
- ✅ **IFFCO = Largest fertilizer producer; Cooperative sector dominant**
- ✅ **3 sectors: Public (NFL, RCF, FACT), Cooperative (IFFCO, KRIBHCO), Private (Chambal, Coromandel)**
- ✅ **FACT (Fertilizers & Chemicals Travancore) = Kerala-based PSU**
- ✅ **100% Neem-Coated Urea mandatory since 2015**
- ✅ **Nano Urea launched by IFFCO (2022) — World's first**
- ✅ **PM PRANAM = 2023 scheme to reduce chemical fertilizer use**
- ✅ **FCO (Fertilizer Control Order) = 1985**
- ✅ **New Urea Policy = 2018**
- ✅ **NBS Scheme = 2010 (for P&K fertilizers)**
- ✅ **Ideal N:P:K Ratio (India, agronomically) = 4:2:1**
- ✅ **Key ports for fertilizer imports: Kandla, Mundra, Vizag, Chennai, Kochi**
- ✅ **Major urea plants: IFFCO Kalol, NFL Vijaipur, Chambal Gadepan, KRIBHCO Hazira, IFFCO Aonla, IFFCO Phulpur**
- ✅ **Energy benchmark for urea: 4.5 Gcal/t (best) to 6.5 Gcal/t (average)**
- ✅ **Primary fertilizers to know: Urea (46-0-0), DAP (18-46-0), MOP (0-0-60), SSP (0-16-0-11S), 10:26:26, 12:32:16, 15:15:15**
- ✅ **DBT (Direct Benefit Transfer) in fertilizers: Implemented from 2018 to ensure subsidy reaches farmers directly**
- ✅ **Green Initiatives: One Nation One Fertilizer (Bharat Brand, 2022)** — ensures single brand identity for subsidized fertilizers.
- ✅ **Janaushadhi Kendras, Kisan Suvidha App, DBT portal (dbtfertilizer.nic.in)** — digital infrastructure supporting distribution.
- ✅ **Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN, ₹6000/year)** complements fertilizer subsidy for farmers' input costs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutrient Contents of Fertilizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nutrient Contents of Fertilizers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nutrient Content of a Fertilizer** is the guaranteed quantitative measure of the essential plant nutrients (expressed either as the **element** itself — N, P, K — or as the equivalent **oxide form** — P₂O₅, K₂O, CaO, MgO) present per 100 g of the fertilizer. It is the single most important commercial and agronomic parameter of any fertilizer product.

The term "**Fertilizer Grade**" is a three-number declaration (e.g., **17-17-17**) that states the minimum percentage of **total Nitrogen (N)**, **available Phosphorus (as P₂O₅)**, and **water-soluble Potassium (as K₂O)** in that order. The "**Fertilizer Analysis**" is the actual percentage of each nutrient determined by laboratory analysis of a given lot. "**Fertilizer Ratio**" is the simplest whole-number proportional relationship between the three primary nutrients in a grade (e.g., 17-17-17 → 1:1:1).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Vitamin Label" on Your Food Packet:**
> Think of a fertilizer exactly like a breakfast cereal box. The label on the cereal says "Iron 18 mg, Vitamin C 60 mg, Calcium 400 mg per 100 g." The farmer looks at the fertilizer bag and sees "**N 18 %, P₂O₅ 46 %, K₂O 0 %**" (DAP). Both are simply declarations of *how much* of the *essential ingredient* is packed inside. The plant cannot eat the bag — it can only absorb the *bio-available fraction* of N, P, and K dissolved in soil water. So just as you would not buy cereal with zero iron if you are anaemic, a paddy farmer will not buy a fertilizer with zero K if the soil is potassium-deficient.

**The 17 essential plant nutrients** are classified by the quantity the plant requires:

| Class | Quantity Required | Nutrients |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary / Macronutrients** | > 1 000 mg kg⁻¹ of plant dry matter | **N, P, K** |
| **Secondary Nutrients** | 100 – 1 000 mg kg⁻¹ | **Ca, Mg, S** |
| **Micronutrients** | < 100 mg kg⁻¹ | **Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni** |

Standard reporting bases in global fertilizer trade:
- N is always expressed as **elemental N**.
- P is expressed as **P₂O₅** (phosphorus pentoxide equivalent).
- K is expressed as **K₂O** (potassium oxide equivalent, also called "kalium oxide" or "potash").
- Ca, Mg are expressed as their **oxides** (CaO, MgO) in classical literature; S is reported as **elemental S** in modern practice but historically as **SO₃**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Atomic weights used:** N = **14**, P = **31**, K = **39**, Ca = **40**, Mg = **24.3**, S = **32**, O = **16**.
- **Molecular weights:** P₂O₅ = **142**, K₂O = **94**, CaO = **56**, MgO = **40.3**, SO₃ = **80**, NH₃ = **17**, NO₃ = **62**, SO₄ = **96**.
- **SI reporting unit:** % w/w (grams of nutrient per 100 g of fertilizer).
- **FCO (Fertiliser Control Order, India 1985)** mandates grade declaration in **% N – % P₂O₅ – % K₂O** on every bag.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Two Reporting Systems? (Element vs Oxide)

When Liebig and Lawes began commercial fertilizer manufacture in the 1840s, chemistry was based on combustion analysis. Phosphorus was determined by igniting the sample to **P₂O₅** and potassium by igniting to **K₂O**. These analytical oxides became the *reporting convention* and have persisted globally, even though the plant actually absorbs orthophosphate ions (H₂PO₄⁻, HPO₄²⁻) and K⁺ — *not* the oxides. Therefore, every chemist and agronomist must master the **oxide-to-element conversion factors**.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic of Conversion Factors

**General Principle:**

$$\text{Conversion Factor (oxide to element)} = \frac{\text{Atomic mass of element(s) in the oxide}}{\text{Molecular mass of the oxide}}$$

**For P₂O₅ → P:**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{2 \times 31}{2(31) + 5(16)} = \frac{62}{142} = 0.4364$$

So 1 % P₂O₅ = 0.4364 % P (elemental), and conversely 1 % P = 2.291 % P₂O₅.

**For K₂O → K:**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{2 \times 39}{2(39) + 16} = \frac{78}{94} = 0.8298$$

So 1 % K₂O = 0.83 % K (elemental), and 1 % K = 1.205 % K₂O.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Conversion Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested item** in KTU exams on this topic. Memorize the factors exactly. Note: vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table.

| Conversion | Multiplication Factor | Inverse Factor |
|---|---|---|
| **% P → % P₂O₅** | × **2.2913** | × 0.4364 |
| **% P₂O₅ → % P** | × **0.4364** | × 2.2913 |
| **% K → % K₂O** | × **1.2046** | × 0.8302 |
| **% K₂O → % K** | × **0.8302** | × 1.2046 |
| **% Ca → % CaO** | × **1.3992** | × 0.7147 |
| **% CaO → % Ca** | × **0.7147** | × 1.3992 |
| **% Mg → % MgO** | × **1.6582** | × 0.6030 |
| **% MgO → % Mg** | × **0.6030** | × 1.6582 |
| **% S → % SO₃** | × **2.4969** | × 0.4005 |
| **% SO₃ → % S** | × **0.4005** | × 2.4969 |
| **% N → % NH₃** | × **1.2159** | × 0.8225 |
| **% NO₃ → % N** | × **0.2258** | × 4.4266 |
| **% SO₄ → % S** | × **0.3338** | × 2.9962 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in industry:** A fertilizer manufacturer exporting MOP to India buys/sells based on **% K₂O** (e.g., Muriate of Potash must contain **60 % K₂O min.** per FCO). The same shipment in a European agronomy report is quoted as **49.8 % K**. A single numerical error of 0.83 vs 1.20 in a 50 000-tonne cargo = thousands of tonnes of nutrient mis-declared = **commercial fraud and BIS prosecution**.

### 2.4 Standard Nutrient Contents of Major Commercial Fertilizers (KTU Reference Table)

| Fertilizer | Chemical Formula | % N | % P₂O₅ | % K₂O | Other |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Urea | CO(NH₂)₂ | **46.0** | 0 | 0 | Biuret < 1.5 % |
| Ammonium Sulphate (AS) | (NH₄)₂SO₄ | **20.6** | 0 | 0 | 23.5 % S |
| Ammonium Nitrate (AN) | NH₄NO₃ | **33.5** | 0 | 0 | — |
| Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) | NH₄NO₃ + CaCO₃ | **26.0** | 0 | 0 | 10 % Ca |
| Ammonium Chloride | NH₄Cl | **25.0** | 0 | 0 | 66 % Cl |
| Anhydrous Ammonia | NH₃ | **82.0** | 0 | 0 | (liquid) |
| Single Superphosphate (SSP) | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ + CaSO₄ | 0 | **16.0** | 0 | 19 % Ca, 12 % S |
| Triple Superphosphate (TSP) | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ | 0 | **46.0** | 0 | 15 % Ca |
| Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP) | (NH₄)₂HPO₄ | **18.0** | **46.0** | 0 | — |
| Mono-Ammonium Phosphate (MAP) | NH₄H₂PO₄ | **11.0** | **52.0** | 0 | — |
| Muriate of Potash (MOP) | KCl | 0 | 0 | **60.0** | 47 % Cl |
| Sulphate of Potash (SOP) | K₂SO₄ | 0 | 0 | **50.0** | 17.5 % S |
| Potassium Nitrate | KNO₃ | **13.0** | 0 | **37.0** | — |
| Rock Phosphate | Ca₃(PO₄)₂·CaF₂ | 0 | **20–32** | 0 | Insoluble |
| Basic Slag | Ca₃(PO₄)₂·CaO·SiO₂ | 0 | **14–18** | 0 | 30 % CaO |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Theoretical Nutrient Content of Urea

**Given:** Urea = CO(NH₂)₂, molecular formula weight calculation.

**Step 1 — Molecular weight of urea:**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{urea}} = 12 + 16 + 2(14) + 4(1) = 60 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of N in one mole:**

$$\text{Mass of N} = 2 \times 14 = 28 \text{ g per mole}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of N:**

$$\%N = \frac{28}{60} \times 100 = 46.67\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Commercial grade specification (FCO 1985):** Urea is guaranteed to contain **46 % N minimum**; the theoretical 46.67 % is reduced by **biuret, moisture (max 1 %), and inert conditioning agents** (like formaldehyde coating) added to prevent caking. Hence, **commercial urea = 46 % N**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Theoretical Nutrient Content of DAP

**Given:** DAP = (NH₄)₂HPO₄.

**Step 1 — Molecular weight:**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{DAP}} = 2(14) + 8(1) + 1 + 31 + 4(16) = 28 + 8 + 1 + 31 + 64 = 132 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — N content (as element):**

$$\%N = \frac{2 \times 14}{132} \times 100 = \frac{28}{132} \times 100 = 21.21\,\%$$

**Step 3 — P content (as element):**

$$\%P = \frac{31}{132} \times 100 = 23.48\,\%$$

**Step 4 — P content (as P₂O₅, using factor 2.2913):**

$$\%P_2O_5 = 23.48 \times 2.2913 = 53.80\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Commercial grade:** DAP is sold as **18-46-0**. The drop from theoretical 21.21 % N to 18 % N is due to free NH₃ loss during granulation, moisture, and inert coating. Hence, **commercial DAP grade = 18 % N, 46 % P₂O₅, 0 % K₂O**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Oxide-to-Element Conversion (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A complex fertilizer is labelled **15-15-15**.
**(a)** Calculate the actual percentages of N, P, K *as elements*.
**(b)** A 50 kg bag of this fertilizer is applied to 1 acre. How much elemental P is being added per acre?

**Solution to (a):**

For **N**, the grade is already in elemental form:

$$\%N_{\text{element}} = 15.00\,\%$$

For **P₂O₅ = 15 %**, convert to elemental P using factor 0.4364:

$$\%P = 15 \times 0.4364 = 6.55\,\%$$

For **K₂O = 15 %**, convert to elemental K using factor 0.8302:

$$\%K = 15 \times 0.8302 = 12.45\,\%$$

So the actual elemental declaration is **15.00 % N – 6.55 % P – 12.45 % K**.

**Solution to (b):**

Mass of elemental P in a 50 kg bag:

$$\text{Mass of P} = 50 \text{ kg} \times 0.0655 = 3.275 \text{ kg P per 50 kg bag}$$

Therefore, **3.275 kg of elemental phosphorus is added per acre per application**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Calculating Nutrient Content of a Two-Fertilizer Bulk Blend

**Problem:** A farmer blends 200 kg of DAP (18-46-0) and 100 kg of MOP (0-0-60). Calculate the **bulk-blend grade** of the mixture.

**Step 1 — Total mass of mixture:**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Nutrient contribution from DAP (200 kg):**

$$N_{\text{from DAP}} = 200 \times 0.18 = 36.0 \text{ kg N}$$

$$P_2O_5_{\text{from DAP}} = 200 \times 0.46 = 92.0 \text{ kg P}_2O_5$$

**Step 3 — Nutrient contribution from MOP (100 kg):**

$$K_2O_{\text{from MOP}} = 100 \times 0.60 = 60.0 \text{ kg K}_2O$$

**Step 4 — Convert kg to % of mixture (divide by 300):**

$$\%N = \frac{36.0}{300} \times 100 = 12.0\,\%$$

$$\%P_2O_5 = \frac{92.0}{300} \times 100 = 30.67\,\%$$

$$\%K_2O = \frac{60.0}{300} \times 100 = 20.0\,\%$$

**Result:** The bulk blend has grade **12-30.67-20** (or normalized to **12-31-20** in commercial declaration).

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Verification of SSP Nutrient Content

**Problem:** Single Superphosphate is essentially Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O + CaSO₄·2H₂O. Show that the **P₂O₅ content is ~16 %**.

**Step 1 — Monocalcium phosphate monohydrate (MCP):**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{MCP}} = 40 + 2(1) + 31 + 4(16) + 2(1) + 1(16) = 40+2+31+64+2+16 = 252$$

**Step 2 — P₂O₅ equivalent of MCP:**

$$\%P_2O_5 \text{ in MCP} = \frac{142}{252} \times 100 = 56.35\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Gypsum content of SSP:** SSP contains roughly **50 % MCP + 50 % gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** by mass. Gypsum contains 0 % P₂O₅.

**Step 4 — Weighted average P₂O₅ in SSP:**

$$\%P_2O_5 \text{ in SSP} = 0.50 \times 56.35 + 0.50 \times 0 = 28.2\,\%$$

In commercial production, only **~60 %** of the rock-P actually converts to available P₂O₅ (the rest remains as unreacted rock or insoluble iron/aluminium phosphates). Hence:

$$\%P_2O_5 \text{ commercial} = 0.60 \times 28.2 = 16.9\,\% \approx 16\,\%$$

This matches the FCO specification of **16 % P₂O₅ minimum for SSP** (granular) and **14 % for powdered SSP**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Plant Nutrients

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLANT NUTRIENTS"]:::root --> B["MACRONUTRIENTS"]:::macro
    A --> C["SECONDARY NUTRIENTS"]:::sec
    A --> D["MICRONUTRIENTS"]:::micro

    B --> B1["NITROGEN N"]
    B --> B2["PHOSPHORUS P"]
    B --> B3["POTASSIUM K"]

    C --> C1["CALCIUM Ca"]
    C --> C2["MAGNESIUM Mg"]
    C --> C3["SULPHUR S"]

    D --> D1["IRON Fe"]
    D --> D2["MANGANESE Mn"]
    D --> D3["ZINC Zn"]
    D --> D4["COPPER Cu"]
    D --> D5["BORON B"]
    D --> D6["MOLYBDENUM Mo"]
    D --> D7["CHLORINE Cl"]
    D --> D8["NICKEL Ni"]

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef macro fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    classDef sec fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff
    classDef micro fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Methods of Expressing Nutrient Content — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FERTILIZER SAMPLE"]:::input --> Q["QUANTITATIVE\nCHEMICAL ANALYSIS"]:::proc

    Q --> ELEM["ELEMENTAL BASIS\nN, P, K, Ca, Mg, S"]:::elem
    Q --> OX["OXIDE BASIS\nP2O5, K2O, CaO, MgO, SO3"]:::ox
    Q --> ION["ION BASIS\nNO3, NH4, SO4, PO4"]:::ion

    ELEM --> US["USED IN\nMODERN AGRONOMY"]:::endn
    OX --> TRAD["USED IN\nTRADE AND FCO"]:::endox
    ION --> LAB["USED IN\nLABORATORY REPORTS"]:::endion

    OX -.CONVERSION FACTOR.-> ELEM

    classDef input fill:#37474f,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#0277bd,color:#fff
    classDef elem fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef ox fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff
    classDef ion fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
    classDef endn fill:#a5d6a7,color:#000
    classDef endox fill:#ffcc80,color:#000
    classDef endion fill:#ce93d8,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Nutrient Flow in a Bulk-Blend Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["RAW DAP\n18-46-0"]:::raw --> M["WEIGHING AND\nPROPORTIONING HOPPER"]:::mix
    R2["RAW MOP\n0-0-60"]:::raw --> M
    R3["RAW UREA\n46-0-0"]:::raw --> M
    R4["FILLER\nDolomite / Sand"]:::raw --> M

    M --> BL["ROTARY BLENDER\n5 to 10 minutes"]:::blend
    BL --> QA["QUALITY CHECK\nGrade verification\nFCO sampling"]:::qa

    QA --> P1["PASS\n18-18-21 grade\nor similar"]:::pass
    QA --> P2["FAIL\nRecycle / Re-blend"]:::fail

    P1 --> BG["BAGGING\n50 kg / 1 tonne"]:::bag

    classDef raw fill:#5d4037,color:#fff
    classDef mix fill:#1565c0,color:#fff
    classDef blend fill:#00838f,color:#fff
    classDef qa fill:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef pass fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef fail fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef bag fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The block diagrams above show that *every* fertilizer report has a **provenance** — a particular *analytical* basis and a particular *commercial* convention. In KTU examinations, a question will frequently test whether the student can **trace a value from the elemental basis back to its oxide declaration** and vice versa. Always state the basis before quoting a number.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper PECHT637]**
**Define the terms (i) Fertilizer Grade, (ii) Fertilizer Ratio, and (iii) Fertilizer Analysis. Illustrate with the example 10-26-26.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fertilizer Grade (1 mark):** A three-number numerical declaration on the bag indicating the minimum guaranteed percentage of total N, available P₂O₅, and water-soluble K₂O, in that order.
- **Fertilizer Analysis (1 mark):** The actual percentage of each nutrient determined by laboratory analysis of a specific lot of fertilizer.
- **Fertilizer Ratio (1 mark):** The simplest whole-number proportional relationship among N, P₂O₅, and K₂O. For 10-26-26, dividing by the GCD 2 gives **1 : 2.6 : 2.6**, which is typically normalized to **1 : 2.6 : 2.6** (a complex fertilizer suitable for flowering and tuber crops).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper PECHT637]**
**A fertilizer is labelled 18-46-0. (a) What is its common trade name? (b) Calculate the actual percentage of elemental phosphorus. (c) What is the percentage of K in the fertilizer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(a)** Trade name = **Di-Ammonium Phosphate, DAP**, with chemical formula **(NH₄)₂HPO₄** *(1 mark)*.
- **(b)** P₂O₅ = 46 %, so elemental P = $46 \times 0.4364 = 20.07\,\%$ *(1 mark)*.
- **(c)** % K₂O = 0, therefore **% K = 0 %** *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Answer **one** of the two full 14-mark questions. Each contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Choice 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the classification of essential plant nutrients into primary, secondary, and micronutrient categories. For each category, list the nutrients, give one common fertilizer source, and state one characteristic deficiency symptom in plants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 000 kg bulk-blend is prepared by mixing 600 kg of 10-26-26, 300 kg of urea (46-0-0), and 100 kg of MOP (0-0-60). Calculate the **final fertilizer grade** of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Primary / Macronutrients (N, P, K) (2 marks):** Required in largest amounts (>1 000 mg kg⁻¹ dry matter). Required for vegetative growth (N), root and reproductive development (P), and enzyme activation, water regulation, and disease resistance (K). Common sources: **N → Urea (46 % N); P → DAP (18-46-0); K → MOP (0-0-60)**. Deficiency symptoms: **N deficiency → uniform chlorosis (yellowing) of older leaves** (mobile nutrient); **P deficiency → purpling of older leaves, stunted growth**; **K deficiency → marginal leaf scorch (necrosis) of older leaves**.

- **Secondary Nutrients (Ca, Mg, S) (2 marks):** Required in moderate amounts (100 – 1 000 mg kg⁻¹). **Ca** — cell wall rigidity, source **gypsum (CaSO₄)**, deficiency → **blossom-end rot in tomato**. **Mg** — central atom of chlorophyll, source **epsom salt (MgSO₄)**, deficiency → **interveinal chlorosis of older leaves**. **S** — amino acids cysteine and methionine, source **elemental S or AS**, deficiency → **yellowing of younger leaves first** (S is immobile).

- **Micronutrients (Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni) (2 marks):** Required in trace amounts (<100 mg kg⁻¹). **Zn** — auxin synthesis, source **ZnSO₄**, deficiency → **"little leaf" / rosetting in citrus and rice**. **Fe** — chloroplast development, source **Fe-EDTA chelate**, deficiency → **interveinal chlorosis of young leaves** (lime-induced chlorosis). **B** — cell division and pollen germination, source **borax (Na₂B₄O₇)**, deficiency → **"top sickness" / hollow heart in beetroot**.

- **Tabular summary (1 mark):** (Students should ideally present this as a table in the answer book for 1 extra mark)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Nutrient mass from 600 kg of 10-26-26 (7 marks breakdown):**

$$N_1 = 600 \times 0.10 = 60.0 \text{ kg N}$$

$$P_2O_5{}_1 = 600 \times 0.26 = 156.0 \text{ kg P}_2O_5$$

$$K_2O_1 = 600 \times 0.26 = 156.0 \text{ kg K}_2O$$

**Step 2 — Nutrient mass from 300 kg of urea (46-0-0):**

$$N_2 = 300 \times 0.46 = 138.0 \text{ kg N}$$

$$P_2O_5{}_2 = 0$$

$$K_2O_2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Nutrient mass from 100 kg of MOP (0-0-60):**

$$N_3 = 0$$

$$P_2O_5{}_3 = 0$$

$$K_2O_3 = 100 \times 0.60 = 60.0 \text{ kg K}_2O$$

**Step 4 — Totals (3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks):**

$$N_{\text{total}} = 60.0 + 138.0 + 0 = 198.0 \text{ kg}$$

$$P_2O_5{}_{\text{total}} = 156.0 + 0 + 0 = 156.0 \text{ kg}$$

$$K_2O_{\text{total}} = 156.0 + 0 + 60.0 = 216.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Final grade (% on 1 000 kg basis):**

$$\%N = \frac{198.0}{1000} \times 100 = 19.8\,\%$$

$$\%P_2O_5 = \frac{156.0}{1000} \times 100 = 15.6\,\%$$

$$\%K_2O = \frac{216.0}{1000} \times 100 = 21.6\,\%$$

**Final Answer:** The bulk blend has grade **19.8-15.6-21.6**, commercially declared as **20-16-22** (after rounding down to FCO standards).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1, Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **methods of expressing nutrient content** in fertilizers, including elemental, oxide, and ionic forms, with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** (i) Calculate the theoretical percentage of N, P, and P₂O₅ in **ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)**, also called MAP. (ii) Why is the commercial grade only **11-52-0** and not the theoretical maximum? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Elemental basis (2 marks):** Modern agronomy and plant nutrition express nutrient content as the percentage of the **element** (N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S). Example: urea contains **46 % N**, which is the actual mass of N atoms. This is the most agronomically meaningful form.

- **Oxide basis (3 marks):** Commercial trade (FCO, ISO, FAO) reports P as **P₂O₅** and K as **K₂O** because of historical combustion-analysis origins. Examples: SSP = **16 % P₂O₅** (equivalent to 6.99 % P), MOP = **60 % K₂O** (equivalent to 49.8 % K). Conversion factors (P × 2.2913, K × 1.2046) are routinely applied. This is the **legally declared form** on the fertilizer bag.

- **Ionic / Salt basis (1 mark):** In laboratory analysis, the content is often reported as the **ion** present, e.g., **NO₃⁻, NH₄⁺, SO₄²⁻, PO₄³⁻**. Example: A 5 % NO₃⁻ solution corresponds to 5 × 0.2258 = **1.13 % N**.

- **Summary diagram (1 mark):** [Student should sketch the three-tier reporting system — element, oxide, ion — with one example each, as in the Mermaid block diagram above.]

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Theoretical nutrient content of MAP (NH₄H₂PO₄): (5 marks)**

**Step 1 — Molecular weight:**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{MAP}} = 14 + 4(1) + 1 + 31 + 4(16) = 14 + 4 + 1 + 31 + 64 = 115 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — N content:**

$$\%N = \frac{14}{115} \times 100 = 12.17\,\%$$

**Step 3 — P content (elemental):**

$$\%P = \frac{31}{115} \times 100 = 26.96\,\%$$

**Step 4 — P₂O₅ content (using factor 2.2913):**

$$\%P_2O_5 = 26.96 \times 2.2913 = 61.78\,\%$$

So the **theoretical grade** is **12.17 % N – 61.78 % P₂O₅ – 0 % K₂O**.

**(ii) Why the commercial grade is 11-52-0 and not theoretical: (2 marks)**

- **Moisture content** of 1 – 1.5 % w/w in granular MAP reduces the nutrient %.
- **Free phosphoric acid** residual (1 – 2 %) acts as an anti-caking agent and is not a nutrient.
- **Granulation aids, conditioners, and anti-caking dust** (kaolin, dolomite powder) typically add 2 – 4 % inert mass.
- **Process losses** during ammoniation of phosphoric acid: side reactions forming **(NH₄)₂HPO₄** (DAP) at the expense of MAP if the NH₃:PO₄ mole ratio drifts above 1.0.
- **Specification margins:** Manufacturers deliberately keep **specification headroom** of 0.5 – 1 % below theoretical so that the lot passes the BIS/FCO **guaranteed minimum** even after 6 months of storage and possible nutrient loss.

Net commercial grade therefore settles at **11 % N – 52 % P₂O₅ – 0 % K₂O**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks (Examiner's Eye View):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert between elemental and oxide forms** — A student writes "DAP contains 46 % P" instead of "46 % P₂O₅ = 20.07 % P". This is a **direct 1-mark deduction** in Part A and **up to 3 marks** in Part B calculations.
> 2. **Inverting the conversion factor** — Using 0.4364 to convert P to P₂O₅ instead of P₂O₅ to P. Always remember: **oxide-to-element uses the *smaller* factor (<1)**; **element-to-oxide uses the *larger* factor (>1)**.
> 3. **Not stating the basis of the percentage** — Saying "fertilizer X contains 18 % P" without specifying "as P₂O₅" is incomplete and loses 0.5 – 1 mark for "lack of scientific rigour."
> 4. **Bulk-blend arithmetic mistakes** — In Question A(b), a frequent error is to add the percentages of nutrients directly (e.g., 10 + 46 + 60) and then divide by 100. The correct method is to compute **absolute mass of nutrient per constituent** first, sum, then divide by total blend mass. Examiners explicitly look for this two-step method.
> 5. **Writing 60 % K instead of 60 % K₂O for MOP** — The commercially declared form is **K₂O**, not K. Use the **oxide form** unless the question explicitly asks for elemental K.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **The 17 essential plant nutrients** are split into **macros (N, P, K)**, **secondaries (Ca, Mg, S)**, and **micros (Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni)**.
- **Commercial declaration** (FCO, India): **% N – % P₂O₅ – % K₂O**, in that fixed order.
- **The four sacred numbers to memorize:**
  - P₂O₅ → P multiply by **0.4364**; reverse multiply by **2.2913**.
  - K₂O → K multiply by **0.8302**; reverse multiply by **1.2046**.
- **Urea = 46 % N**, **DAP = 18-46-0**, **MAP = 11-52-0**, **SSP = 0-16-0**, **TSP = 0-46-0**, **MOP = 0-0-60**, **SOP = 0-0-50**.
- **Theoretical % is always higher than commercial %** — the difference is **moisture, inerts, biuret, free acid, and process losses**.
- **Fertilizer Grade ≠ Fertilizer Analysis ≠ Fertilizer Ratio** — Grade is a *guarantee*; Analysis is a *measurement*; Ratio is a *proportion*.
- **Bulk blend grade calculation is a two-step process:** compute absolute nutrient mass per constituent → sum → express as % of total blend mass. **Never** average the individual grades directly.
- **N is mobile in the plant** (deficiency appears in older leaves first); **Ca, Fe, B are immobile** (deficiency appears in younger leaves first). This distinction is a 2-mark sure-shot in KTU viva and theory.
- **MOP contains 47 % Cl⁻** — a hazard for chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, potato, grapes). For such crops, use **SOP** instead.
- **S in SSP (12 % S) and AS (23.5 % S)** makes them preferred for oilseeds and pulses where sulphur deficiency is endemic.
- **Conversion factors are *not* memorizable from intuition** — they are derived from atomic-weight ratios and are mathematically fixed for all time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Nutrients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secondary Nutrients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fertilizer technology and agronomic chemistry, **Secondary Nutrients** (also called **Secondary Macronutrients**) are the three essential plant elements required in moderate quantities — intermediate between the **Primary Macronutrients** (N, P, K) and the **Micronutrients** (Zn, Fe, Mn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni).

The three official secondary nutrients recognized by KTU syllabus and global fertilizer standards (FAO, IFA, TSI) are:

| Symbol | Element | Ionic Form Used by Plants |
|--------|---------|---------------------------|
| **Ca** | Calcium | $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ |
| **Mg** | Magnesium | $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ |
| **S** | Sulfur (Sulphur) | $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (sulphate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim from syllabus scope):** *Secondary nutrients are the elements Calcium, Magnesium, and Sulphur that are essential for plant growth, required in quantities less than primary nutrients but more than micronutrients, and are supplied through carrier materials known as secondary nutrient fertilizers.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **three-course meal** for a plant:
- **Primary Nutrients (N, P, K)** = the *main course* (rice/bread) — the bulk food.
- **Secondary Nutrients (Ca, Mg, S)** = the *side dishes and sabzi* — essential to complete the meal, served in smaller portions, but the meal is incomplete without them.
- **Micronutrients (Zn, Fe, B, etc.)** = the *salt and spices* — tiny amounts, but without them the food tastes wrong.

**Key Intuitive Facts:**
- A typical crop removes **20–80 kg Ca**, **10–50 kg Mg**, and **10–40 kg S per hectare** per season.
- Deficiency of any one of these causes **visible physiological disorders** (blossom end rot in tomato, interveinal chlorosis in citrus, yellowing in canola).
- The standard soil test reporting unit is **mg/kg (ppm)** or **cmol($c^+$)/kg** for Ca and Mg, and **mg/kg** or **%S** for sulphur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to know (a) the role of Ca, Mg, S in plant nutrition, (b) common carrier fertilizers, (c) deficiency symptoms, and (d) the manufacturing chemistry of key carriers like gypsum, Epsom salt, single superphosphate, and ammonium sulphate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Calcium (Ca) — The "Cell Wall Builder"

**Why plants need Calcium:**
- Structural component of **calcium pectate** in middle lamella of cell walls → gives rigidity.
- Activates enzymes (amylase, ATPases).
- Mitigates toxicity of Al$^{3+}$, Na$^+$ in acid/saline soils.
- Essential for **root and shoot tip meristem** growth (immobile in phloem → deficiency appears in young leaves first).

**Deficiency Symptoms:**
- *Blossom end rot* in tomato, pepper.
- Tip burn in lettuce, cabbage.
- *Bitter pit* in apple (corky spots).
- Stunted new growth; death of growing points.

### 2.2 Magnesium (Mg) — The "Chlorophyll Heart"

**Why plants need Magnesium:**
- Central atom in the **chlorophyll** porphyrin ring (each chlorophyll = C$_{55}$H$_{72}$MgN$_4$O$_5$).
- About **15–20% of total plant Mg** is in chlorophyll; the rest acts as enzyme cofactor (especially ATPases, RUBISCO).
- Highly **phloem mobile** → deficiency shows first in **older/lower leaves** (interveinal chlorosis, "Christmas tree" pattern in Mg-deficient citrus).

**Deficiency Symptoms:**
- Interveinal chlorosis (yellow between green veins) on older leaves.
- In severe cases, reddish-purple tints (anthocyanin accumulation).

### 2.3 Sulphur (S) — The "Protein & Oil Builder"

**Why plants need Sulphur:**
- Component of **cysteine, methionine** (S-containing amino acids) → protein quality.
- Component of **coenzyme A, biotin, thiamine, glutathione**.
- Crucial for **oilseed crops** (mustard, groundnut, sunflower) and **pulses**.
- Required for synthesis of **glucosinolates** in Brassicas.

**Deficiency Symptoms:**
- Uniform **yellowing of young leaves** (similar to N deficiency, but appears on new growth because S is immobile in phloem).
- Stunted growth; delayed maturity; low oil/protein content in seeds.

### 2.4 Classification of Secondary Nutrient Carriers

| Nutrient | Carrier Fertilizer | Formula | % Nutrient |
|----------|--------------------|---------|------------|
| Ca | Calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) | $\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 \cdot \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ | 8% Ca |
| Ca | Gypsum (mined/byproduct) | $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ~23% Ca |
| Ca | Single Superphosphate (SSP) | $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CaSO}_4$ | ~19% Ca |
| Ca | Calcite / Limestone | $\text{CaCO}_3$ | ~40% Ca |
| Mg | Epsom salt | $\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ~10% Mg |
| Mg | Kieserite | $\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ~26% Mg |
| Mg | Dolomite | $\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2$ | ~13% Mg |
| Mg | Magnesite | $\text{MgCO}_3$ | ~28% Mg |
| S | Elemental Sulphur | $\text{S}$ | 90–99% S |
| S | Ammonium sulphate | $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ | 24% S |
| S | Single Superphosphate | — | ~12% S |
| S | Pyrites | $\text{FeS}_2$ | 22–24% S |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Use |
|--------------------|---------|-----|
| Gypsum molecular weight | $M_{\text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 172\ \text{g/mol}$ | % Ca calculation |
| % Ca in pure gypsum | $\dfrac{40}{172} \times 100 = 23.26\%$ | Grade calculation |
| % S in pure gypsum | $\dfrac{32}{172} \times 100 = 18.6\%$ | Grade calculation |
| Epsom salt MW | $M_{\text{MgSO}_4\cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 246\ \text{g/mol}$ | % Mg calculation |
| % Mg in pure Epsom | $\dfrac{24}{246} \times 100 = 9.76\%$ | Grade calculation |
| % S in Ammonium sulphate | $\dfrac{32}{132} \times 100 = 24.24\%$ | Grade calculation |
| Chlorophyll Mg content (by mass) | $\dfrac{24}{893.5} \times 100 \approx 2.7\%$ | Biochemistry link |
| Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) sum (cmol/kg) | $\Sigma = [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}] + [\text{K}^+] + [\text{Na}^+] + [\text{H}^+] + [\text{Al}^{3+}]$ | Soil test interpretation |
| Sulphate to S conversion | $\%\text{S} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times \dfrac{32}{96} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times 0.333$ | Lab reporting |
| Stoichiometric limestone requirement | $\text{CaCO}_3\ (\text{kg/ha}) = \dfrac{\text{Exchangeable Al (cmol/kg)} \times 1.5 \times \text{soil depth factor}}{100}$ | Liming calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The chemistry of secondary nutrient carriers directly drives industrial design choices — e.g., selecting **phosphogypsum** (a by-product of wet-process phosphoric acid manufacture) as a Ca+S carrier turns a waste stream into a value-added product, a classic example of **circular-economy engineering** in fertilizer plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: % Calcium in Pure Gypsum ($\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

**Step 1.** Write the molecular formula and identify atomic masses (in g/mol):
- Ca = **40**, S = **32**, O = **16**, H = **1**

**Step 2.** Calculate molecular weight of $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= M_{\text{Ca}} + M_{\text{S}} + 4 M_{\text{O}} + 2(2 M_{\text{H}} + M_{\text{O}}) \\
  &= 40 + 32 + 4(16) + 2(2(1) + 16) \\
  &= 40 + 32 + 64 + 2(18) \\
  &= 40 + 32 + 64 + 36 \\
  &= 172\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** The mass fraction of Ca in gypsum is the ratio of Ca atomic mass to molecular weight:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Ca} &= \frac{M_{\text{Ca}}}{M_{\text{gypsum}}} \times 100 \\
            &= \frac{40}{172} \times 100 \\
            &= 23.255\% \approx 23.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Similarly, % S in pure gypsum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{S} &= \frac{M_{\text{S}}}{M_{\text{gypsum}}} \times 100 \\
            &= \frac{32}{172} \times 100 \\
            &= 18.605\% \approx 18.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: % Magnesium in Epsom Salt ($\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

**Step 1.** Atomic masses: Mg = **24**, S = **32**, O = **16**, H = **1**

**Step 2.** Molecular weight:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= 24 + 32 + 4(16) + 7(2(1) + 16) \\
&= 24 + 32 + 64 + 7(18) \\
&= 24 + 32 + 64 + 126 \\
&= 246\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** % Mg and % S:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Mg} &= \frac{24}{246} \times 100 = 9.76\% \\
\%\text{S}  &= \frac{32}{246} \times 100 = 13.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: % Sulphur in Ammonium Sulphate $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4} &= 2(14 + 4(1)) + 32 + 4(16) \\
&= 2(18) + 32 + 64 \\
&= 36 + 32 + 64 = 132\ \text{g/mol} \\
\%\text{S} &= \frac{32}{132} \times 100 = 24.24\% \\
\%\text{N} &= \frac{2(14)}{132} \times 100 = 21.21\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is why commercial ammonium sulphate is graded as 21-0-0 + 24S.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Sulphate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) to Elemental Sulphur Conversion

Often, soil-test laboratories report sulphur as **sulphate-S** (mg/kg of $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$). Converting to elemental S:

$$
\%\text{S} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times \frac{M_{\text{S}}}{M_{\text{SO}_4}} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times \frac{32}{96} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times 0.333
$$

**Worked example:** A soil report shows 24 mg/kg $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$:
- Elemental S = $24 \times 0.333 = 7.99 \approx 8$ mg/kg S.

---

### 3.5 Manufacturing Reaction Matrix (Analytical)

| Carrier | Key Industrial Reaction | Type |
|---------|--------------------------|------|
| **SSP** (Ca + S) | $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{CaSO}_4$ | Acidulation |
| **Ammonium Sulphate** (N + S) | $2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ | Neutralization |
| **Gypsum from Phosphogypsum** (Ca + S) | Byproduct of wet-process $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ manufacture | By-product recovery |
| **Epsom Salt** (Mg + S) | $\text{MgO} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Neutralization + crystallization |
| **Kieserite** (Mg + S) | $\text{MgCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{MgSO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$ | Neutralization |
| **Elemental S** | Frasch process: superheated water (165 °C) melts subsurface S; compressed air lifts it | Mining |
| **Dolomite as Mg source** | Calcination: $\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{MgO} + 2\text{CO}_2\uparrow$ | Thermal |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A farmer applies **500 kg of gypsum ($\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$) per hectare** to a sulphur-deficient paddy field. How much **elemental sulphur (S)** and **calcium (Ca)** is being supplied per hectare?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of S supplied} &= 500 \times \frac{32}{172} \\
&= 500 \times 0.1860 \\
&= 93.0\ \text{kg S/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of Ca supplied} &= 500 \times \frac{40}{172} \\
&= 500 \times 0.2326 \\
&= 116.3\ \text{kg Ca/ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** 500 kg gypsum supplies **93.0 kg S** and **116.3 kg Ca** per hectare.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Secondary Nutrient Carriers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Secondary Nutrient Carriers] --> B[Calcium Carriers]
    A --> C[Magnesium Carriers]
    A --> D[Sulphur Carriers]

    B --> B1[Gypsum CaSO4.2H2O]
    B --> B2[SSP CaH2PO4 + CaSO4]
    B --> B3[Calcite CaCO3]
    B --> B4[CAN CaNO3.NH4NO3]

    C --> C1[Epsom Salt MgSO4.7H2O]
    C --> C2[Kieserite MgSO4.H2O]
    C --> C3[Dolomite CaMgCO3]
    C --> C4[Magnesite MgCO3]

    D --> D1[Elemental Sulphur S]
    D --> D2[Ammonium Sulphate NH42SO4]
    D --> D3[Pyrites FeS2]
    D --> D4[SSP CaSO4 fraction]

    classDef root fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ca fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#cc5500,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mg fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#145214,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef s fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0d0d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 ca
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4 mg
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 s
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Secondary Nutrient Flow in a Plant–Soil System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Soil[Soil Reservoir] -->|Cation Exchange| Root[Root Uptake Zone]
    Root -->|Ca2+| CaUse[Cell Wall and Membrane Integrity]
    Root -->|Mg2+| MgUse[Chlorophyll Synthesis and ATP Activation]
    Root -->|SO4 2-| SUse[Amino Acid and Oil Synthesis]

    CaUse --> YieldA[Improved Fruit Quality]
    MgUse --> YieldB[Photosynthesis and Sugar Transport]
    SUse --> YieldC[Protein and Oilseed Quality]

    YieldA --> Crop[Crop Yield and Quality]
    YieldB --> Crop
    YieldC --> Crop

    classDef soil fill:#8c564b,stroke:#5a2d1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef root fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4d2c6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef use fill:#17becf,stroke:#0a6e78,color:#000000
    classDef yield fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#6a6f0c,color:#000000
    classDef crop fill:#e377c2,stroke:#7a3661,color:#000000

    class Soil soil
    class Root root
    class CaUse,MgUse,SUse use
    class YieldA,YieldB,YieldC yield
    class Crop crop
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Epsom Salt Manufacturing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Material 1: MgO or MgCO3] --> Mx[Reactor / Neutralizer]
    R2[Raw Material 2: H2SO4 98 percent] --> Mx
    W1[Process Water] --> Mx
    Mx -->|Exothermic reaction| EV[Crystallizer Evaporator]
    EV --> CR[Crystal Centrifuge]
    CR --> DR[Dryer]
    DR --> PK[Bagging and Storage]
    CR --> MC[Mother Liquor Recycle]
    MC --> EV

    classDef raw fill:#ffbb78,stroke:#cc6e0c,color:#000000
    classDef rxn fill:#98df8a,stroke:#2c5e1a,color:#000000
    classDef prod fill:#aec7e8,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    classDef rec fill:#ff9896,stroke:#7a1a1a,color:#000000

    class R1,R2,W1 raw
    class Mx,EV rxn
    class CR,DR,PK prod
    class MC rec
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram maps the unit operations of Epsom salt production — neutralization → evaporation crystallization → centrifugation → drying → packaging. The recycle loop from mother liquor back to crystallizer is a classic **mass-balance closure** in chemical engineering process design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**List the three secondary nutrients. Why is calcium classified as a secondary nutrient and not a primary nutrient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three secondary nutrients are **Calcium (Ca)**, **Magnesium (Mg)**, and **Sulphur (S)**. *(1 mark)*

Calcium is classified as a *secondary* nutrient (and not primary like N, P, K) because:
- It is required by plants in **quantities less than N, P, K** but more than micronutrients. *(1 mark)*
- It is not universally deficient in all soils (unlike N which is needed everywhere) and its deficiency is mainly localized to **acidic, sandy, or high-rainfall leached soils**. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**Name two sulphur-containing fertilizers and two magnesium-containing fertilizers. Give their chemical formulae.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sulphur-containing fertilizers: *(1 mark)*
- Ammonium sulphate: $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ (24% S)
- Elemental sulphur: $\text{S}$ (90–99% S)
- *or* Single Superphosphate: $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + \text{CaSO}_4$ (~12% S)

Magnesium-containing fertilizers: *(1 mark)*
- Epsom salt: $\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (~10% Mg)
- Kieserite: $\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (~26% Mg)
- *or* Dolomite: $\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2$ (~13% Mg)

*(Bonus 1 mark for correct % nutrient content if mentioned.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 pattern]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **role of Calcium, Magnesium, and Sulphur** in plant nutrition. List the characteristic **deficiency symptoms** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With balanced chemical equations, explain the **industrial manufacture of Ammonium Sulphate** by the gypsum process. What is the **grade of the product**? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Role of Calcium (Ca):** *(2 marks)*
- Structural component of **calcium pectate** in the middle lamella → provides rigidity to cell walls.
- Activates enzymes (e.g., amylase, phospholipase).
- Regulates **cation-anion balance** and mitigates toxicity of Al$^{3+}$, Na$^+$, and H$^+$ in acid and saline soils.
- Plays a key role in **root development** and **pollen tube growth**.

**Role of Magnesium (Mg):** *(2 marks)*
- Central atom in the **chlorophyll** molecule (about 2.7% Mg by mass in chlorophyll).
- Cofactor for enzymes of **photosynthesis** (especially RUBISCO) and **phosphorylation** reactions (ATP → ADP).
- Highly **phloem mobile**; translocated from older to younger leaves during deficiency.

**Role of Sulphur (S):** *(2 marks)*
- Constituent of **sulphur-containing amino acids** — cysteine and methionine — essential for protein synthesis.
- Component of **coenzyme A, biotin, thiamine, ferredoxin, and glutathione**.
- Critical for **oilseed crops** (mustard, groundnut) — improves oil content.
- Synthesis of **glucosinolates** in Brassicas (mustard, cabbage).

**Deficiency Symptoms:** *(1 mark)*

| Nutrient | Mobility | Symptoms appear on | Key symptom |
|----------|----------|--------------------|-------------|
| Ca | Immobile | **Young leaves** | Blossom end rot (tomato), bitter pit (apple), tip burn |
| Mg | Mobile | **Older leaves** | Interveinal chlorosis; reddish-purple tints in severe cases |
| S | Immobile | **Young leaves** | Uniform yellowing of new leaves; stunted growth |

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Ammonium Sulphate by Gypsum Process:** *(6 marks for reaction + 1 mark for grade)*

The process uses **by-product gypsum** from phosphoric acid plants and reacts it with **ammonia and carbon dioxide** to form ammonium sulphate and calcium carbonate.

**Step 1 — Reaction in saturator:**

$$
\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Process flow:**
1. Ground gypsum is suspended in water to form a slurry.
2. Ammonia gas and carbon dioxide are bubbled through the slurry in a saturator at 60–70 °C.
3. Calcium carbonate precipitates as a solid byproduct (filterable).
4. The filtrate is **$(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ solution**, which is evaporated, crystallized, centrifuged, and dried.

**Step 3 — Grade of product:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\%\text{N} = \frac{2(14)}{132} \times 100 = 21.21\% \approx 21\%\ \text{N}
$$

$$
\%\text{S} = \frac{32}{132} \times 100 = 24.24\% \approx 24\%\ \text{S}
$$

**Commercial grade: 21-0-0 + 24S** (a N+S complex fertilizer).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common error 1:** Students confuse the **gypsum process** with the **coke-oven / caprolactam by-product process**. State the source of gypsum explicitly.
> - **Common error 2:** The reaction is often wrongly written as producing $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$ (which is soluble) instead of $\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$ (insoluble precipitate). Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> - **Common error 3:** Many students forget to balance the water. The reaction produces **1 mole of H$_2$O** (gypsum has 2 H$_2$O, product side has only 1).
> - **Common error 4:** Marks are lost when the **grade of the product** is not stated in standard fertilizer notation (N-P-K-S).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 pattern]

**(a)** What is **Phosphogypsum**? Explain how it is obtained as a by-product in the manufacture of **wet-process phosphoric acid**. Why is it considered a valuable secondary nutrient carrier? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fertilizer mixture contains **60% SSP and 40% Ammonium sulphate by mass**. Calculate the **percentage of Ca, S, and N** supplied by this mixture. *(Given: SSP contains 16% P$_2$O$_5$, 19% Ca, 12% S; Ammonium sulphate contains 21% N, 24% S.)* **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Phosphogypsum — Definition:** *(1 mark)*
Phosphogypsum is the **calcium sulphate (gypsum) by-product** obtained during the manufacture of **wet-process phosphoric acid** by the acidulation of rock phosphate with sulphuric acid.

**Wet-process Phosphoric Acid Reaction:** *(3 marks)*

$$
\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}\downarrow + 2\text{HF}
$$

(In modern dihydrate/hemihydrate processes, gypsum separates as $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ or $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$.)

**Process flow:**
1. Ground rock phosphate is reacted with 93–98% sulphuric acid in a **reactor / attack tank** at 75–85 °C.
2. Phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$, ~28–32% P$_2$O$_5$) goes into solution; **gypsum crystals** precipitate.
3. The slurry is filtered in a **horizontal pan filter / belt filter**.
4. Filter cake (phosphogypsum) is washed to recover residual acid and discharged.
5. Filtrate (weak phosphoric acid) is concentrated by evaporation to **merchant-grade (54% P$_2$O$_5$)** acid.

**Why valuable as secondary nutrient carrier?** *(3 marks)*
- Supplies **two secondary nutrients simultaneously — Ca (~23%) and S (~18%)** in a single product.
- Cheap — it is a **waste product**; using it offsets disposal cost and environmental burden.
- Improves **soil structure** in sodic/alkaline soils by supplying Ca$^{2+}$ to displace Na$^+$.
- Source of S for **oilseeds and pulses** in S-deficient regions of Kerala (laterite soils).
- Can be used directly as soil amendment or as raw material for **ammonium sulphate (gypsum process)** and **cement**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (part a):**
> - Many students write the **incorrect rock phosphate formula**. Use the standard **fluorapatite** formula: $\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2$. Using $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$ is acceptable but only if clearly stated.
> - The half-water of crystallization in the product is often missed. Examiners expect either $2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (dihydrate) or $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (hemihydrate) explicitly.
> - The **environmental hazard** (radioactivity, fluoride, acidity) is *not* asked but examiners award bonus credit for mentioning it.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Mixture composition (assume 100 kg of mixture):**
- SSP = 60 kg
- Ammonium sulphate = 40 kg

**Step 2 — Calcium (Ca) supplied:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ca from SSP} &= 60 \times \frac{19}{100} = 11.4\ \text{kg} \\
\text{Ca from Ammonium sulphate} &= 0 \\
\text{Total Ca} &= 11.4\ \text{kg in 100 kg mixture} \\
\Rightarrow \%\text{Ca} &= 11.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur (S) supplied:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S from SSP} &= 60 \times \frac{12}{100} = 7.2\ \text{kg} \\
\text{S from Ammonium sulphate} &= 40 \times \frac{24}{100} = 9.6\ \text{kg} \\
\text{Total S} &= 7.2 + 9.6 = 16.8\ \text{kg in 100 kg mixture} \\
\Rightarrow \%\text{S} &= 16.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Nitrogen (N) supplied:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N from SSP} &= 0 \\
\text{N from Ammonium sulphate} &= 40 \times \frac{21}{100} = 8.4\ \text{kg} \\
\Rightarrow \%\text{N} &= 8.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result statement:** *(1 mark)*

| Nutrient | % in mixture |
|----------|--------------|
| N (Nitrogen) | **8.4%** |
| Ca (Calcium) | **11.4%** |
| S (Sulphur) | **16.8%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (part b):**
> - **Forgetting the basis:** Always assume a **100 kg basis** for such mixture problems. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
