> - **Mixing units:** Some students report N as %N and S as %SO$_3$ — examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mismatch.
> - **SSP contains no N:** Students often wrongly assume SSP supplies some N. SSP supplies **only P, Ca, and S**.
> - **Significant figures:** Keep answers to **one decimal place** for KTU numericals.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ The **three secondary nutrients** are **Ca, Mg, S** — required in **moderate quantities**.
- ✅ Ca is the **"cell wall builder"** (calcium pectate in middle lamella).
- ✅ Mg is the **"chlorophyll heart"** — central atom in chlorophyll porphyrin ring.
- ✅ S is the **"protein & oil builder"** — present in cysteine, methionine, coenzyme A.
- ✅ **Deficiency mobility rule:** **Mobile nutrients (Mg)** show symptoms on **older leaves**; **Immobile nutrients (Ca, S)** show symptoms on **younger leaves**.
- ✅ Key carriers and their grades:
  - **Gypsum** $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → 23% Ca, 19% S
  - **Epsom salt** $\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → 10% Mg, 13% S
  - **Ammonium sulphate** $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ → 21% N, 24% S
  - **SSP** → 16% P$_2$O$_5$, 19% Ca, 12% S
  - **Dolomite** $\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2$ → 21% Ca, 13% Mg
- ✅ **Phosphogypsum** = by-product of wet-process $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ — supplies **Ca + S** simultaneously.
- ✅ **Gypsum process** for ammonium sulphate:
  $$\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$$
- ✅ Conversion: $\%\text{S} = \%\text{SO}_4 \times 0.333$.
- ✅ Kerala context: **Laterite soils of Kerala** are commonly **Ca and Mg deficient**; **S deficiency** is rising due to use of S-free NPK complexes.
- ✅ Always **assume 100 kg basis** for mixture problems.
- ✅ Master the **mobility-of-nutrient → deficiency-symptom-location** link — examiners love this table question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedstock and Raw Materials for Nitrogenous, Phosphatic and Potassic Fertilizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedstock and Raw Materials for Nitrogenous, Phosphatic and Potassic Fertilizers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fertilizer Feedstocks (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedstock (Definition as per KTU Syllabus):** A *feedstock* is the raw, unprocessed or partially processed material of natural, mineral, or synthetic origin that is fed into a chemical process train for the manufacture of a finished fertilizer product. It is distinguished from a *raw material*, which is more general, and from a *reagent*, which is consumed chemically to drive a transformation.

Fertilizer feedstocks are conventionally classified according to the **primary plant nutrient (N, P, K)** they are destined to deliver. The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies three principal feedstock families:

1. **Nitrogenous Fertilizer Feedstocks** → Natural gas, naphtha, fuel oil, coal, water, and atmospheric air, which collectively yield **ammonia (NH₃)** as the key nitrogen carrier intermediate.
2. **Phosphatic Fertilizer Feedstocks** → Phosphate rock (apatite group), sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, and nitric acid, used to produce **SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP, and nitrophosphates**.
3. **Potassic Fertilizer Feedstocks** → Sylvite, carnallite, kainite, langbeinite, and natural brines, processed to yield **muriate of potash (MOP) and sulphate of potash (SOP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why feedstocks matter industrially:** A fertilizer plant's *economic viability* and *CO₂ footprint* are dictated almost entirely by the cost, availability, and energy intensity of its feedstock supply chain — far more than by the capital cost of the plant itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen-to-Plate" Analogy

Imagine a professional kitchen preparing three signature dishes: a **soup (N-fertilizer)**, a **stew (P-fertilizer)**, and a **roast (K-fertilizer)**. Each dish demands a specific *primary ingredient* (the feedstock) before any cooking can begin:

| Dish (Fertilizer) | Primary Ingredient (Feedstock) | Why this ingredient? |
|---|---|---|
| Ammonia soup | Natural gas (CH₄) + Air (N₂) | CH₄ supplies H atoms via steam reforming; air supplies N atoms |
| Phosphate stew | Phosphate rock [Ca₃(PO₄)₂·CaF₂] + H₂SO₄ | Rock supplies P; acid unlocks it from the mineral cage |
| Potash roast | Sylvite (KCl) ore or brine | Sylvite is the most abundant, water-soluble K mineral |

Just as a chef refuses to substitute stale ingredients, an engineer must select feedstocks that meet **grade**, **purity**, and **reactivity** specifications. A "stale" (impure) feedstock forces the downstream reactor to work harder, generates more waste, and increases catalyst poisoning.

## 1.3 The Three Macronutrients — Quick Recap

$$ \text{N} + \text{P} + \text{K} = \text{The Fertilizer Trinitrotoluene of Plant Nutrition} $$

- **N (Nitrogen):** Promotes vegetative growth, leaf chlorophyll, amino acid synthesis.
- **P (Phosphorus):** Drives root development, energy transfer (ATP), seed formation.
- **K (Potassium):** Regulates stomatal opening, enzyme activation, disease resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Stoichiometric Rule of NPK:** Every fertilizer grade is declared on the **N–P₂O₅–K₂O basis**. To convert elemental P → P₂O₅, multiply by **2.2913**. To convert K → K₂O, multiply by **1.2046**. These two constants appear every KTU exam — **memorize them.**

## 1.4 Key Global and Indian Context (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Scenario:** India is the **world's second-largest consumer of fertilizers** (after China) but imports ~30 % of its urea requirement, ~50 % of its DAP, and **100 % of its muriate of potash (MOP)**. This makes the *feedstock import bill* a matter of national economic security, and the FCO (Fertilizer Control Order, 1985) governs the grade standards.

World reserves (approximate, 2024 baseline):
- **Phosphate rock:** Morocco (~70 %), China, USA, Russia.
- **Potash:** Canada (~30 %), Russia, Belarus, Germany.
- **Natural gas (for NH₃):** Russia, Iran, Qatar, USA, Algeria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nitrogenous Fertilizer Feedstocks — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Ammonia (NH₃) as the Universal N-Building Block

More than **95 %** of all manufactured nitrogenous fertilizers are derived, directly or indirectly, from synthetic ammonia. The industrial route is the **Haber–Bosch process**, which combines **H₂** (from a hydrocarbon feedstock) with **N₂** (from air) over an iron-based catalyst at **400–500 °C** and **150–300 bar**.

$$ \frac{1}{2} \text{N}_2(g) + \frac{3}{2} \text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3(g) \qquad \Delta H^\circ = -46.2 \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

The selection of the **hydrogen-source feedstock** defines the entire process. KTU syllabus enumerates four options:

| Feedstock | H₂ Production Route | Typical H₂:CO Ratio | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural gas (CH₄)** | Steam methane reforming (SMR) | 3:1 | Best — lowest CO₂ footprint, most plants |
| **Naphtha (C₅–C₁₀)** | Steam reforming of naphtha (SRN) | 2.5:1 | Used historically in India, e.g., NFIL, SPIC |
| **Fuel oil (heavy residue)** | Partial oxidation (POX) | ~1:1 | Older units, higher CO₂ |
| **Coal / coke** | Coal gasification (Lurgi, Texaco) | ~0.5:1 | China, South Africa; high emissions |

### 2.1.2 Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Step Logic

1. **Desulphurisation** — ZnO beds strip H₂S to < 0.1 ppm (protects Ni catalyst downstream).
2. **Primary reformer** — CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3 H₂ at 800–850 °C over a Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst (tubes in a fired furnace).
3. **Secondary reformer** — Air is injected to supply the N₂ for the downstream NH₃ synthesis loop; residual CH₄ is combusted.
4. **CO shift conversion** — CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (high-T then low-T shift for high conversion).
5. **CO₂ removal** — Benfield (hot K₂CO₃) or MDEA (aMDEA) absorption.
6. **Methanation** — Trace CO/CO₂ + 3 H₂ → CH₄ + H₂O to < 10 ppm (protects synthesis catalyst).
7. **NH₃ synthesis loop** — N₂ + 3 H₂ ⇌ 2 NH₃ at 200–300 bar, 400–450 °C.

### 2.1.3 N-Fertilizer Products and Their Feedstock Derivatives

| Product | Formula | % N | Derived From |
|---|---|---|---|
| Urea | (NH₂)₂CO | 46 | NH₃ + CO₂ |
| Ammonium nitrate | NH₄NO₃ | 33.5 | NH₃ + HNO₃ |
| Ammonium sulphate | (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 20.5 | NH₃ + H₂SO₄ |
| Calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) | Ca(NO₃)₂·NH₄NO₃ | 25–26 | NH₄NO₃ + CaCO₃ |
| Anhydrous ammonia | NH₃ | 82 | Direct NH₃ |

## 2.2 Phosphatic Fertilizer Feedstocks — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Phosphate Rock — The Irreplaceable Starting Point

Phosphate rock is a **sedimentary or igneous mineral** whose commercial value lies in the apatite group. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the three apatite end-members:

$$ \text{Fluorapatite:} \quad \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{F} $$

$$ \text{Hydroxyapatite:} \quad \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} $$

$$ \text{Chlorapatite:} \quad \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{Cl} $$

The **Bone Phosphate of Lime (BPL)** content is the industry-standard measure of rock grade:

$$ \%\text{BPL} = \%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \times 2.185 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grade Benchmarks:**
> - High-grade rock: **> 70 % BPL** (≈ 32 % P₂O₅)
> - Medium-grade: 60–70 % BPL
> - Low-grade: < 60 % BPL (often requires beneficiation)

The **reactivity** of the rock is dictated by its **carbonate substitution** and the **SiO₂:F ratio**. Rocks from Morocco and Togo are *sedimentary* and highly reactive; Russian Kola rocks are *igneous* and require finer grinding plus higher acid strengths.

### 2.2.2 The Acidulation Cascade

Phosphate rock is chemically inert. It must be "opened" by a mineral acid. Three process routes exist:

| Route | Acid | Primary Product | % P₂O₅ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SSP** (Single Superphosphate) | H₂SO₄ | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O + CaSO₄ | 16–20 |
| **TSP** (Triple Superphosphate) | H₃PO₄ | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ | 44–48 |
| **Wet phosphoric acid** | H₂SO₄ → H₃PO₄ | Crude H₃PO₄ (28–32 % P₂O₅) | Intermediate |
| **DAP** (Diammonium Phosphate) | H₃PO₄ + NH₃ | (NH₄)₂HPO₄ | 46 P₂O₅ / 18 N |
| **MAP** (Monoammonium Phosphate) | H₃PO₄ + NH₃ | NH₄H₂PO₄ | 52 P₂O₅ / 12 N |
| **Nitrophosphate** | HNO₃ | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ + Ca(NO₃)₂ | Variable |

### 2.2.3 The Sulphuric Acid Bridge

Sulphuric acid is the *gateway reagent* to the entire phosphatic fertilizer industry. Elemental sulphur (Frasch or recovered from oil/gas desulphurisation) is burned to SO₂ and catalytically oxidised to SO₃, then absorbed in H₂O:

$$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 $$

$$ 2\,\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{SO}_3 \quad (\text{V}_2\text{O}_5, 450\ ^\circ\text{C}) $$

$$ \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Chicken-and-Egg Problem:** A *sulphuric acid plant* is the conventional first unit of a phosphate fertilizer complex. It supplies acid to the phosphate rock acidulation unit, which in turn produces gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) as a by-product. This co-location is called a **"sulphuric–phosphoric acid complex"** and is the global standard design.

## 2.3 Potassic Fertilizer Feedstocks — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Potash Mineralogy

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates familiarity with the following potash-bearing minerals:

| Mineral | Formula | % K₂O Equivalent | Solubility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sylvite** | KCl | 63.2 | High in H₂O |
| **Carnallite** | KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O | 17.0 | High |
| **Kainite** | KCl·MgSO₄·3H₂O | 18.9 | High |
| **Polyhalite** | K₂SO₄·MgSO₄·2CaSO₄·2H₂O | 15.6 | Low — needs special processing |
| **Langbeinite** | K₂SO₄·2MgSO₄ | 22.7 | Moderate |
| **Kieserite** | MgSO₄·H₂O | 0 (carries no K) | High (used in fertilizer blends) |

### 2.3.2 Mining vs. Brine Extraction

Two fundamentally different feedstock supply routes exist:

1. **Conventional underground mining** — Saskatchewan (Canada), Belarus, Russia, Germany, USA (Carlsbad, NM). Ore is hoisted, crushed, and separated by **flotation, electrostatic separation, or heavy-media separation**.
2. **Solar evaporation of natural brines** — Dead Sea (Israel/Jordan), Great Salt Lake (USA), Lop Nur (China), Atacama (Chile). Brine is concentrated in multi-stage solar ponds, KCl crystallises by staged evaporation.

$$ \text{Total recovery (Saskatchewan):} \approx 90\ \% \text{ of insitu KCl} $$

$$ \text{Total recovery (Dead Sea ponds):} \approx 60\ \% \text{ of dissolved K} $$

### 2.3.3 Product Forms of Potash

| Product | Formula | % K₂O | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Muriate of Potash (MOP) | KCl | 60–62 | Most common; from sylvite |
| Sulphate of Potash (SOP) | K₂SO₄ | 50–52 | For chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, grapes) |
| Potassium nitrate | KNO₃ | 46 K₂O + 13 N | Premium specialty fertilizer |
| Potassium magnesium sulphate | K₂SO₄·MgSO₄ | 22 K₂O + 11 Mg | From langbeinite |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden Five" conversion factors — must be memorised for the KTU ESE:**

| Conversion | Multiplier | Reverse Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| P → P₂O₅ | × **2.2913** | ÷ 2.2913 |
| P₂O₅ → P | × 0.4364 | — |
| K → K₂O | × **1.2046** | ÷ 1.2046 |
| K₂O → K | × 0.8301 | — |
| N → NH₃ | × 1.2159 | — |
| NH₃ → N | × 0.8225 | — |
| BPL | % P₂O₅ × 2.185 | — |
| Ca₃(PO₄)₂ | % P × 5.005 | — |

**Key theoretical metrics:**

| Metric | Formula / Value | Unit | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric H₂: N₂ for NH₃ | 3:1 | mol/mol | For Haber–Bosch feed |
| SMR primary reformer temperature | 800–850 | °C | Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst |
| NH₃ synthesis pressure | 150–300 | bar | Modern plants 200–300 |
| Phosphate rock acid ratio | H₂SO₄:rock = 0.6–0.7 | t/t | For SSP |
| DAP ammoniation ratio (NH₃:acid) | 1.8–2.0 | mol/mol | Controlled by pH |
| KCl crystallisation temperature | 60–105 | °C | Vacuum crystallisation |

**Units and constants used throughout:**

- Standard molar volume at STP = **22.414 L mol⁻¹**
- 1 tonne N = 1.2159 tonnes NH₃
- 1 tonne KCl (MOP) = 0.6317 tonne K₂O
- 1 tonne P₂O₅ requires ≈ **3.0 tonnes** of 70 % BPL rock (theoretical)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Feedstock Demand for Urea Manufacture

**Problem Setup:** Determine the mass of (a) natural gas (95 % CH₄) and (b) air (79 vol% N₂, 21 vol% O₂) required to manufacture **1 metric tonne (1000 kg) of urea** containing 46 % N by mass. Assume 100 % process efficiency.

### Step 1 — Determine moles of N in 1 tonne of urea

$$ \text{Mass of N} = 1000 \ \text{kg} \times 0.46 = 460 \ \text{kg} $$

$$ M_{\text{N}} = 14.007 \ \text{g mol}^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{\text{N}} = \frac{460\,000 \ \text{g}}{14.007 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}} = 32\,844.0 \ \text{mol} $$

### Step 2 — Stoichiometry through the NH₃ route

Each mole of urea requires **2 moles of NH₃**:

$$ \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow (NH_2)_2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ n_{\text{NH}_3} = 2 \times 32\,844.0 = 65\,688.0 \ \text{mol} $$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometry back to H₂ and N₂ via Haber–Bosch

$$ \text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{NH}_3 $$

$$ n_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 65\,688.0 = 32\,844.0 \ \text{mol} $$

$$ n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times 65\,688.0 = 98\,532.0 \ \text{mol} $$

### Step 4 — Mass of N₂ required

$$ M_{\text{N}_2} = 28.014 \ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ m_{\text{N}_2} = 32\,844.0 \times 28.014 = 920.0 \ \text{kg} $$

### Step 5 — Mass of H₂ required

$$ M_{\text{H}_2} = 2.016 \ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ m_{\text{H}_2} = 98\,532.0 \times 2.016 = 198.6 \ \text{kg} $$

### Step 6 — Mass of natural gas (CH₄) to produce H₂ via SMR

The overall SMR + shift reaction gives:

$$ \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 $$

$$ n_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{n_{\text{H}_2}}{4} = \frac{98\,532.0}{4} = 24\,633.0 \ \text{mol} $$

$$ M_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.043 \ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ m_{\text{CH}_4,\text{pure}} = 24\,633.0 \times 16.043 = 395.2 \ \text{kg} $$

Accounting for 95 % CH₄ purity:

$$ m_{\text{natural gas}} = \frac{395.2}{0.95} = 416.0 \ \text{kg} \approx 0.416 \ \text{tonnes} $$

### Step 7 — Mass of air to supply N₂

Moles of air required (using the 79/21 ratio):

$$ n_{\text{air}} = \frac{n_{\text{N}_2}}{0.79} = \frac{32\,844.0}{0.79} = 41\,574.7 \ \text{mol} $$

Average molecular weight of air = **28.97 g mol⁻¹**:

$$ m_{\text{air}} = 41\,574.7 \times 28.97 = 1\,204.5 \ \text{kg} \approx 1.20 \ \text{tonnes} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** To produce 1 tonne of urea, the *theoretical* minimum feedstock demand is **0.416 t natural gas + 1.20 t air (and 1.32 t CO₂)**. Real-world plant consumption is 10–15 % higher due to reformer purges, methanator losses, and urea synthesis loop inefficiencies.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: P₂O₅ Equivalent of a Phosphate Rock Sample

**Problem:** A Moroccan phosphate rock assays **31.8 % P₂O₅** and **3.2 % F**. Compute (i) % BPL, (ii) % elemental P, and (iii) the mass of 70 % H₂SO₄ required per tonne of rock for SSP manufacture (assume stoichiometric ratio of 0.65 t acid/t rock as per KTU typical data, with 5 % excess).

### Step 1 — % BPL

$$ \%\text{BPL} = \%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \times 2.185 = 31.8 \times 2.185 = 69.48\ \% $$

### Step 2 — % elemental P

$$ \%\text{P} = \%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \times 0.4364 = 31.8 \times 0.4364 = 13.88\ \% $$

### Step 3 — Acid demand for SSP

The overall SSP reaction (simplified fluorapatite basis):

$$ \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{F} + 7\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 5\,\text{CaSO}_4 + 3\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{HF} $$

For a 1-tonne rock sample at 69.48 % BPL, the stoichiometric acid:

$$ m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 (100\%)} = 1000 \times 0.65 = 650 \ \text{kg} $$

Adjusting for 70 % acid strength:

$$ m_{70\%} = \frac{650}{0.70} = 928.6 \ \text{kg} \approx 929 \ \text{kg} $$

Adding 5 % excess:

$$ m_{\text{acid, actual}} = 929 \times 1.05 = 975.4 \ \text{kg} \approx 975 \ \text{kg per tonne rock} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every 1000 kg of this Moroccan rock, a fertilizer plant must dose **~ 975 kg of 70 % H₂SO₄** to produce SSP. The SSP product yield is roughly 1.85–1.95 t/t rock, and contains 16–18 % P₂O₅ (water-soluble).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Potash Yield from a Carnallite Ore

**Problem:** A carnallite ore contains **24 % KCl, 30 % NaCl, 42 % MgCl₂·6H₂O, and 4 % insolubles**. By flotation, **88 %** of the KCl is recovered. Find (a) the mass of KCl recovered per tonne of ore, and (b) the equivalent % K₂O in the concentrate (assuming concentrate purity is 95 % KCl, 5 % NaCl).

### Step 1 — Mass of KCl in feed

$$ m_{\text{KCl, feed}} = 1000 \times 0.24 = 240 \ \text{kg} $$

### Step 2 — Mass recovered

$$ m_{\text{KCl, recovered}} = 240 \times 0.88 = 211.2 \ \text{kg} $$

### Step 3 — Mass of concentrate

If concentrate is 95 % KCl:

$$ m_{\text{concentrate}} = \frac{211.2}{0.95} = 222.3 \ \text{kg} $$

### Step 4 — % K₂O in concentrate

$$ M_{\text{KCl}} = 74.55 \ \text{g mol}^{-1}, \quad M_{\text{K}_2\text{O}} = 94.20 \ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \%\text{K}_2\text{O} = \%\text{KCl} \times \frac{M_{\text{K}_2\text{O}}}{2 \times M_{\text{KCl}}} = 95 \times \frac{94.20}{2 \times 74.55} $$

$$ = 95 \times \frac{94.20}{149.10} = 95 \times 0.6317 = 60.01\ \% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification against MOP specification:** Commercial MOP is graded as **60 % K₂O min (red standard) and 62 % K₂O (white / granular)**. The calculation confirms a 95 % KCl concentrate meets red-grade MOP specifications.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feedstock Quality vs. Process Selection

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix is a synthesis of KTU 2024 syllabus data and is the kind of *comparison table* the examiner expects in a 14-mark question.

| Parameter | Natural Gas (N) | Naphtha (N) | Phosphate Rock (P) | Sylvite (K) | Carnallite (K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary source | Wells, LNG | Refineries | Mines (sed. + ign.) | Underground mines | Brines, mines |
| Key impurity | H₂S, Hg, N₂ | Sulphur, heavy metals | SiO₂, CaCO₃, F, Cd, U | NaCl, Mg salts | MgCl₂, NaCl |
| Critical pre-treatment | Desulphurisation | Desulphurisation | Calcination, grinding, beneficiation | Crushing, flotation | Hot leaching |
| Process route it enables | SMR → Haber | SMR → Haber | SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP, NP | MOP via flotation/crystallisation | MOP + Mg by-products |
| Energy intensity (GJ/t product) | 28–32 (urea) | 35–40 (urea) | 4–6 (SSP/DAP) | 5–8 (MOP) | 8–12 (MOP) |
| CO₂ footprint (t CO₂/t) | 1.0–1.5 (urea) | 1.6–2.0 (urea) | 0.4–0.6 (DAP) | 0.2–0.3 (MOP) | 0.3–0.4 (MOP) |
| Strategic risk for India | Moderate-Low | High (import) | Low-Medium | **100 % Import** | **100 % Import** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**
> *List the three principal nitrogenous fertilizer feedstocks and identify which one is preferred globally for new ammonia plants. Justify the choice in two sentences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three principal nitrogenous fertilizer feedstocks are **(i) natural gas (CH₄), (ii) naphtha, and (iii) fuel oil / coal** (½ mark each; 1½ marks). Natural gas is the globally preferred feedstock for new ammonia plants because it **(a)** gives the highest H₂ yield per mole of carbon in steam reforming, **(b)** produces the lowest CO₂ emissions per tonne of NH₃, and **(c)** requires the least extensive purification (low sulphur, no heavy metals) (1½ marks for two of the three points).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> *Define "BPL" in the context of phosphate rock. How is it related to % P₂O₅? Why is phosphate rock reactivity important in SSP manufacture?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BPL stands for **Bone Phosphate of Lime**, a legacy industry measure of phosphate rock grade based on the assumption that all P is present as tricalcium phosphate Ca₃(PO₄)₂ (1 mark). It is related to % P₂O₅ by the conversion: **% BPL = % P₂O₅ × 2.185** (1 mark). Phosphate rock reactivity is important in SSP manufacture because **highly reactive rock dissolves faster in H₂SO₄, ensuring complete conversion of insoluble apatite into monocalcium phosphate within the limited den-time of the mixer and curing pile**; low-reactivity (igneous) rock leads to unreacted P₂O₅ residues, lower SSP grade, and longer curing times (1 mark).

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A [14 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Choice A]**
>
> *(a)* Describe in detail the **four major feedstocks** used in nitrogenous fertilizer manufacture, with particular reference to the **Haber–Bosch process**. Compare their relative merits and identify the most economically attractive option for a coastal Indian location. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* With the aid of a **flow-sheet description**, explain how ammonia manufactured from natural gas is further converted into **urea** via the **CO₂ stripping process**. Include key operating conditions and the principle of unconverted recycle. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Four Major Nitrogenous Feedstocks (7 marks)**

The four major feedstocks for nitrogenous fertilizer manufacture, all serving as hydrogen sources for the Haber–Bosch synthesis of NH₃, are: **(1) Natural gas (CH₄), (2) Naphtha, (3) Fuel oil, and (4) Coal / coke** (1 mark for enumeration). Each requires a distinct primary conversion route to produce the synthesis gas (H₂ + N₂) required by the ammonia synthesis loop.

**Natural Gas (SMR route, ~ 95 % of new plants) — (1.5 marks):**
Natural gas (typically > 90 % CH₄) is desulphurised over ZnO and then passed with steam through catalyst-filled tubes in the primary reformer at 800–850 °C:

$$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 $$

Air is injected in the secondary reformer to supply the nitrogen for the downstream ammonia loop and to combust residual methane. The H₂:N₂ ratio is adjusted to 3:1 before the Haber–Bosch converter. Natural gas is the **lowest-cost, lowest-emission** option; globally preferred.

**Naphtha (SRN route) — (1.5 marks):**
Naphtha (C₅–C₁₀ hydrocarbons) is steam-reformed similarly to natural gas, but at slightly lower steam:carbon ratios and with higher reforming temperatures to crack heavier molecules. The product synthesis gas has a lower H₂:CO ratio (~ 2.5) and requires **more extensive CO₂ removal**. Naphtha is the historical feedstock for India (e.g., SPIC, MFL) but is **costly and import-dependent**.

**Fuel Oil (POX route) — (1.5 marks):**
Heavy fuel oil is partially oxidised with oxygen (and steam) at 1300–1400 °C:

$$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \frac{n}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO} + \frac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2 $$

The H₂:CO ratio is ~ 1:1, so substantial CO shift conversion is required. POX is capital-intensive (cryogenic O₂ plant) and **produces soot** that must be scrubbed. Suitable when natural gas/naphtha are unavailable.

**Coal / Coke (Gasification) — (1.5 marks):**
Lignite, bituminous coal, or pet coke is gasified with O₂ and steam in a moving-bed (Lurgi) or entrained-flow (Shell, Texaco) gasifier at 800–1400 °C. The crude gas must be cleaned of H₂S, COS, NH₃, tars, and particulates before the shift and methanation steps. **Highest CO₂ emissions** (~ 3.5 t CO₂/t NH₃), but **strategically important** for countries with abundant coal (China, South Africa, India).

**Recommendation for coastal Indian location — (1 mark):**
A coastal Indian site is best served by **imported LNG (natural gas)** as the primary feedstock because (i) LNG receiving terminals are already in place (Dahej, Hazira, Kochi), (ii) SMR gives the lowest CO₂ footprint, qualifying for green-ammonia / CBAM export credits, and (iii) the technology is mature, with single-stream capacities up to 5000 t/d NH₃ achievable. Coal gasification is *not* preferred for a coastal site because the high ash handling and slag disposal are environmentally constrained.

**Part (b) — Ammonia-to-Urea via CO₂ Stripping (7 marks)**

Urea is manufactured by the direct reaction of **NH₃ + CO₂** at high pressure and temperature (1 mark). The process chemistry proceeds in two equilibrium steps inside the urea reactor (synthesis section):

$$ 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \quad (\text{ammonium carbamate formation, exothermic}) $$

$$ \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightleftharpoons (NH_2)_2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{urea formation, endothermic, equilibrium-limited}) $$

**Process flow (CO₂ stripping process — Stamicarbon design):** (3 marks)

1. **Compression:** Liquid NH₃ is pumped to ~ 160 bar; gaseous CO₂ is compressed to ~ 140 bar.
2. **High-pressure synthesis (Stripper + Reactor):** The NH₃/CO₂ feed (with a molar excess of ~ 3.5:1 NH₃:CO₂) enters the top of the urea reactor (autoclave) at 180–185 °C and 140–160 bar. Conversion per pass is **only 60–70 %** because the second reaction is equilibrium-limited. The unconverted carbamate is stripped from the reactor effluent using fresh CO₂ in the *stripper* (a falling-film exchanger), recovering the bulk of the NH₃.
3. **Medium-pressure recirculation:** The stripped effluent is expanded to ~ 17 bar; further NH₃ and CO₂ are recovered as gases and recycled to the reactor.
4. **Low-pressure recirculation:** A second expansion to ~ 3.5 bar recovers the remaining carbamate; the crude urea melt (70–75 % urea in water) is sent to evaporation.
5. **Vacuum evaporation & prilling:** Water is removed in two-stage vacuum evaporators, and the molten urea (99.7 %) is sprayed from the top of a 50-m prilling tower to form spherical white prills (1–2.5 mm).

**Key operating conditions and unconverted recycle — (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Reactor temperature | 180–190 °C |
| Reactor pressure | 140–160 bar |
| NH₃:CO₂ molar ratio | 3.0–3.6:1 |
| H₂O:CO₂ ratio | 0.0–0.5 |
| Per-pass conversion | 60–70 % (to urea) |
| Overall conversion | ~ 99 % after recycle |
| Prill size | 1.0–2.5 mm |
| Product N content | 46 % (biuret < 1.0 %) |

**Principle of unconverted recycle:** Because the urea-forming reaction is *reversible and equilibrium-limited*, only 60–70 % of the carbamate converts to urea in a single pass. The unconverted NH₃ and CO₂, which exist in the reactor effluent as dissolved ammonium carbamate, are recovered in the stripper using fresh CO₂ (CO₂ stripping) and recycled to the reactor. This *internal recycle loop* boosts overall raw-material efficiency to > 99 %, minimising both feedstock cost and environmental release of NH₃.

> **Question B [14 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**
>
> *(a)* Describe the **mineralogy and chemical composition of phosphate rock**. Explain the difference between sedimentary and igneous phosphate rocks and their effect on the **wet-process phosphoric acid** manufacture. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* With reference to **sylvite and carnallite**, describe the mining and processing of potash ores to produce **muriate of potash (MOP)**. Compare the two principal recovery routes. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Phosphate Rock Mineralogy and Wet-Process Impact (7 marks)**

Phosphate rock is a generic industrial term for **insoluble, naturally occurring calcium phosphate minerals of the apatite group** (1 mark). The three end-member apatites are:

$$ \text{Fluorapatite} = \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{F} \quad (42.4\ \%\ \text{P}_2\text{O}_5) $$

$$ \text{Hydroxyapatite} = \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \quad (42.4\ \%\ \text{P}_2\text{O}_5) $$

$$ \text{Chlorapatite} = \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{Cl} \quad (41.0\ \%\ \text{P}_2\text{O}_5) $$

Commercially, the most abundant is **fluorapatite**, often with carbonate substitution (giving *francolite*), which is the dominant form in **sedimentary deposits** such as those of Morocco, Florida, Togo, and Jordan (1 mark).

**Sedimentary vs. igneous phosphate rocks (2 marks):**

| Property | Sedimentary Rocks (Morocco, Florida, Jordan) | Igneous Rocks (Kola-Russia, Phalaborwa-SA) |
|---|---|---|
| Genesis | Marine biogenic precipitation | Crystallisation from magma |
| Texture | Soft, porous, microcrystalline | Hard, dense, macrocrystalline |
| Reactivity with H₂SO₄ | **High** (rapid acidulation) | **Low** (needs fine grinding, hot acid) |
| Typical % P₂O₅ | 30–34 | 35–40 |
| SiO₂ content | 2–5 % | 1–3 % |
| Cl content | Low | Often high (corrosion of equipment) |
| Beneficiation needed | Calcination to remove organics | Magnetic separation of magnetite (Phalaborwa) |

**Effect on wet-process phosphoric acid manufacture (3 marks):**

The **wet process** reacts ground phosphate rock with sulphuric acid to produce phosphoric acid and calcium sulphate (gypsum):

$$ \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{F} + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 5\,\text{CaSO}_4\!\cdot\!2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{HF} $$

- **Sedimentary rocks** (high reactivity) dissolve in 4–6 hours at 75–80 °C, producing *gypsum* (CaSO₄·2H₂O) crystals that filter easily. The acid produced is 28–32 % P₂O₅ and 1–1.5 % free SO₃. Organic matter and silica gel co-precipitate and cause filtration difficulties, which is why the rock is **calcined (800–900 °C)** before acidulation to burn off organics.
- **Igneous rocks** (low reactivity) require **finer grinding (–75 μm)** and **higher acid strengths (45–50 % H₂SO₄)** and reaction temperatures (85–90 °C) to achieve acceptable dissolution. Their chloride content can corrode rubber-lined attack-tank agitators.

The choice of acidulation route (gypsum dihydrate, hemihydrate, or dihydrate–hemihydrate) also depends on the rock's reactivity and impurity profile.

**Part (b) — Sylvite and Carnallite Mining and MOP Production (7 marks)**

**Sylvite (KCl) ores (3.5 marks):**

Sylvite is the **principal commercial potash mineral**, occurring in massive underground evaporite deposits at depths of 600–1500 m. Major producers are **Saskatchewan (Canada), Russia (Solikamsk, Berezniki), Belarus (Starobin), Germany (Werra), and the USA (Carlsbad, NM)**.

- **Mining method:** Conventional **room-and-pillar underground mining** with continuous boring machines and conveyor transport, OR **solution mining** (water injection into the deposit, brine extraction) for deep or thin seams.
- **Processing (Froth Flotation route, dominant in Saskatchewan):**
  1. **Crushing and grinding** to liberate KCl from NaCl.
  2. **Desliming** to remove fine clays.
  3. **Conditioning** with reagents (amines, starch, dextrin).
  4. **Froth flotation** in saturated brine: KCl floats (with amine collector), NaCl sinks.
  5. **Centrifugation, drying, compaction, and screening** to produce granular MOP (95 % KCl, 60–62 % K₂O).
- **Alternative (Crystallisation route):** Sylvite ore is dissolved in hot brine, impurities removed, and KCl crystallised by cooling. Used in some German plants.

**Carnallite (KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O) ores (2.5 marks):**

Carnallite is a *hydrated double salt* containing only 17 % K₂O equivalent. It is the historic potash source in **Germany (Staßfurt), the Netherlands, and parts of Russia/Ukraine**, and is also obtained as a *crystallisation product* in the Dead Sea solar evaporation cascade.

- **Processing (Hot-leaching + Crystallisation route):**
  1. **Crushing and grinding** the ore.
  2. **Hot leaching** with process water or dilute MgCl₂ brine: carnallite decomposes to KCl (solid) + MgCl₂ (solution). NaCl is removed by gravity separation.
  3. **Filtration** to recover the solid KCl fraction.
  4. **Crystallisation** of the dissolved KCl by cooling or evaporation.
  5. **Drying and compaction** to MOP specification.

**Comparison of the two routes (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Sylvite → MOP (Flotation) | Carnallite → MOP (Hot Leach) |
|---|---|---|
| Ore grade | High (20–40 % K₂O) | Low (10–17 % K₂O) |
| Energy intensity | Moderate (no evaporation) | High (hot leaching, evaporation) |
| Reagent cost | Moderate (flotation reagents) | Lower |
| By-products | NaCl, brine (recycled) | MgCl₂, NaCl, Kieserite |
| Typical plant size | Up to 5 Mt/yr MOP | Usually < 1 Mt/yr MOP |
| Examples | Nutrien, Mosaic (Saskatchewan) | K+S (Germany), Dead Sea Works |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "feedstock" with "raw material":** A feedstock is a *process input*; a raw material is the *bulk starting mineral*; a reagent is *chemically consumed*. Use the correct term in your exam answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Golden Five" conversion factors** (P→P₂O₅ × 2.2913, K→K₂O × 1.2046, etc.) — losing 2–3 marks in stoichiometry.
> 3. **Missing the H₂:N₂ = 3:1 stoichiometry** when calculating feedstock demand for a given NH₃ output.
> 4. **Calling sylvite "sylvinite":** Sylvite is the pure KCl mineral; *sylvinite* is the mixed KCl–NaCl ore (the most common mining form). This is a favourite trick question.
> 5. **Ignoring 100 % MOP import dependence in India** — examiners always reward mention of this *national strategic angle*.
> 6. **In DAP problems, forgetting that the ammoniation ratio NH₃:H₃PO₄ controls pH** and the resulting MAP/DAP ratio.
> 7. **Drawing flow sheets with arrows in the wrong direction** (e.g., from product back to feedstock). Always draw left-to-right or top-to-bottom, with reagents on top and products at the bottom.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density revision checklist (read this last, before entering the exam hall):**

- [ ] **N-feedstock preference:** Natural gas > Naphtha > Fuel oil > Coal, with CO₂ footprint in the same descending order.
- [ ] **Golden Five conversion factors are non-negotiable:** P×2.2913 = P₂O₅, K×1.2046 = K₂O, % BPL = %P₂O₅ × 2.185, N×1.2159 = NH₃, 1 t KCl ≈ 0.6317 t K₂O.
- [ ] **H₂:N₂ = 3:1** is the universal Haber–Bosch stoichiometric feed ratio.
- [ ] **NH₃ is the chokepoint** of the entire N-fertilizer industry — losing the reformer halts *all* downstream N-products.
- [ ] **Phosphate rock reactivity** (sedimentary > igneous) is what determines acidulation severity in the wet process.
- [ ] **SSP** uses H₂SO₄ → 16 % P₂O₅; **TSP** uses H₃PO₄ → 46 % P₂O₅; **DAP** uses H₃PO₄ + 2 NH₃ → 18-46-0.
- [ ] **Sylvite = KCl** (high-grade ore, MOP via flotation); **Sylvinite = KCl + NaCl mixture** (the actual mine-run ore).
- [ ] **Carnallite is processed by hot leaching + crystallisation** (energy-intensive); **sylvinite by froth flotation** (industry standard).
- [ ] **India imports 100 % of its MOP requirement** — *always* mention this for the "national importance" angle.
- [ ] **Dead Sea solar evaporation** is the classic *non-mining* potash source, with bromine and MgCl₂ as by-products.
- [ ] **CO₂ stripping process (Stamicarbon)** is the dominant urea technology, with NH₃:CO₂ ≈ 3.5:1 and 140–160 bar.
- [ ] **Key process valves in urea manufacture:** 180–190 °C, 140–160 bar, per-pass conversion 60–70 %, overall > 99 % with recycle.
- [ ] **By-product gypsum from SSP/wet process** is a major solid-waste management issue (1.5–2 t per tonne of rock).
- [ ] **India's policy angle:** New Urea Policy (NUP-2015), DBT (Direct Benefit Transfer) for subsidy, and the *Green Ammonia Mission* (2023) all tie back to feedstock selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Ammonia: Physical and Chemical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Ammonia: Physical and Chemical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ammonia (NH₃)** is a colourless, pungent, alkaline gas composed of one nitrogen atom covalently bonded to three hydrogen atoms. It is the simplest stable pnictogen hydride and serves as the **fundamental nitrogen carrier** in the global fertilizer industry. Industrially, it is produced via the **Haber–Bosch process**, where atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) is reacted with hydrogen (H₂) over an iron-based catalyst at high pressure and moderate temperature.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT637)**, ammonia is classified as the *primary nitrogen intermediate* from which virtually all downstream nitrogenous fertilizers — urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, ammonium phosphate, and nitric acid — are manufactured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ammonia is the single most important raw material in fertilizer technology. Approximately **~85% of global ammonia production** is consumed directly by the fertilizer sector. A board examiner will expect a student to link every property of NH₃ back to its industrial/fertilizer relevance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Friendly but Reactive Sniff"

Imagine ammonia as a **mini magnet with three arms**:

- The **nitrogen atom at the center** is like a strong magnet — it pulls electrons toward itself (high electronegativity ≈ **3.04** on the Pauling scale).
- The three **hydrogen atoms** are like lightweight balloons tethered to this magnet. They lean inward (giving the bond angle of **107.8°**, slightly less than the ideal tetrahedral 109.5°).
- Because the electron cloud is lopsided (concentrated near nitrogen), the whole molecule behaves like a tiny **polar magnet** — it readily sticks to water (forming NH₄OH) and to acids (forming NH₄⁺ salts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Fertilizer Significance:** *"Ammonia is the Father of Fertilizers"* — almost every nitrogenous fertilizer is a derivative (urea, ammonium salts, nitrates, etc.).

## 1.3 Salient Identification Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | **NH₃** |
| Molar Mass | **17.031 g/mol** |
| Nitrogen Content (mass %) | **82.25 %** |
| Odour | Sharp, pungent, irritating |
| Colour | Colourless |
| Density (gas, STP) | **0.771 kg/m³** |
| Density (liquid, at b.p.) | **0.682 g/cm³** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Properties of Ammonia — Structured Analysis

### A. Phase & Appearance
- **State at STP:** Colourless gas with a characteristic **pungent (urinous) odour**, detectable by smell at concentrations as low as **5 ppm**.
- **Liquefaction:** Easily liquefied under mild pressure (~**8.5 atm at 20 °C**) or by cooling to **−33.34 °C** at 1 atm.

### B. Thermodynamic Constants

> [!NOTE]
> These constants are **favourite KTU question material** and are frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions.

- **Boiling Point:** **−33.34 °C (239.81 K)**
- **Melting Point:** **−77.7 °C (195.45 K)**
- **Critical Temperature (T_c):** **132.4 °C**
- **Critical Pressure (P_c):** **111.3 atm (≈ 112.8 bar)**
- **Critical Density:** **0.235 g/cm³**

### C. Molecular Geometry & Polarity

- **Hybridization of N:** **sp³**
- **Geometry:** **Trigonal pyramidal** (one lone pair on nitrogen)
- **H–N–H Bond Angle:** **107.8°** (compressed from 109.5° due to lone-pair–bond-pair repulsion)
- **Dipole Moment (μ):** **1.47 Debye** (strongly polar)
- **Bond Length (N–H):** **101.7 pm**

### D. Solubility Behaviour

- Extremely soluble in water: at **20 °C, 1 atm**, **~700 volumes of NH₃ dissolve per volume of water**, producing **ammonium hydroxide (NH₄OH)**.
- Solubility decreases sharply with temperature rise.
- Soluble in alcohol, ether, and chloroform.

### E. Density & Compressibility

- Lighter than air (molar mass **17** vs. air ≈ 29).
- Stored as a **compressed liquefied gas** in steel cylinders (often at −33 °C or under ~10 atm at ambient).

## 2.2 Chemical Properties of Ammonia

### A. Basic (Alkaline) Character

Ammonia acts as a **Lewis base** because of the lone pair on nitrogen:

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$$

$$K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

### B. Reaction with Acids (Salt Formation)

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \quad \text{(Ammonium chloride)}$$

$$\text{2NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \quad \text{(Ammonium sulfate — a fertilizer!)}$$

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \quad \text{(Ammonium nitrate — a fertilizer!)}$$

### C. Reaction with CO₂ — Urea Formation (Critical Fertilizer Reaction)

$$\text{2NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \quad \text{(Ammonium carbamate — intermediate)}$$

$$\text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Urea)}$$

### D. Catalytic Oxidation — Ostwald Process (Nitric Acid Manufacture)

$$\text{4NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Rh, 850 °C}} \text{4NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{2NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2NO}_2$$

$$\text{3NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{2HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$$

### E. Reaction with Metals (Amide Formation)

$$\text{2Na} + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{NaNH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Sodamide at 300 °C)}$$

### F. Thermal Decomposition

$$\text{2NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta > 500 \,°C} \text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$$

### G. Complex Formation (Lewis Base Behaviour)

$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 4\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow [\text{Cu(NH}_3)_4]^{2+} \quad \text{(Deep blue — tetraamminecopper(II))}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorize the derivations behind each formula.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%N = \dfrac{14.01}{17.031} \times 100 = 82.25\%$ | Mass % of N in NH₃ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $n_{NH_3} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{m}{17.031}$ | Moles of NH₃ | mol |
| 3 | $V_{NH_3,\,STP} = n \times 22.414$ | Volume of NH₃ at STP | L |
| 4 | $P_c \cdot V_c = \dfrac{R\,T_c}{8}$ | Van der Waals reduced relation | — |
| 5 | $\rho_{gas,\,STP} = \dfrac{PM}{RT} = \dfrac{(1)(17.031)}{(0.0821)(273.15)}$ | Ideal gas density at STP | g/L |
| 6 | $\mu_{NH_3} = 1.47 \,D$ | Dipole moment | Debye |
| 7 | $\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+$ | Proton affinity | ΔH ≈ −854 kJ/mol |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Fertilizer Sector:** Direct feedstock for urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, DAP, MAP, and complex fertilizers.
- **Refrigeration:** NH₃ (R-717) is one of the most efficient and environmentally benign refrigerants, with zero ODP and zero GWP.
- **Pollution Control:** Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) of NOₓ using NH₃ in power plants and diesel engines.
- **Chemical Industry:** Manufacture of HNO₃, HCN, hydrazine, acrylonitrile, and caprolactam.
- **pH Control:** Used in water treatment and as a neutralizing agent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of NH₃

The molecular weight is the foundation for every stoichiometric calculation in fertilizer manufacture.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{NH_3} &= (1 \times A_r^{N}) + (3 \times A_r^{H}) \\[4pt]
&= (1 \times 14.0067) + (3 \times 1.00794) \\[4pt]
&= 14.0067 + 3.02382 \\[4pt]
&= 17.03052 \;\;\text{g/mol} \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{17.031 \;\;g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of each step:**
- The first line defines the molecular weight as the sum of atomic weights.
- The second line substitutes the standard IUPAC atomic weights: N = **14.0067**, H = **1.00794**.
- The third line multiplies the hydrogen contribution by 3 (because three H atoms are present).
- The fourth line performs the arithmetic sum.
- The fifth line reports the final rounded value used throughout the fertilizer industry.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Percentage of Nitrogen in NH₃

This derivation is the **single most asked stoichiometric question** in KTU Fertilizer Technology papers.

$$\begin{aligned}
\%N &= \frac{\text{Mass of N in one mole of NH}_3}{\text{Molar mass of NH}_3} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{14.0067}{17.03052} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.82252 \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \mathbf{82.25\,\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why this matters in fertilizer technology:**
- The **higher the %N**, the **more nitrogen per unit mass** of fertilizer is delivered to the soil.
- Urea (CO(NH₂)₂) contains **46% N** — which is why urea is preferred over NH₃ as a finished fertilizer (easier handling).
- This is the foundation for comparing fertilizers on an equal-nitrogen basis.

## 3.3 Derivation: Density of Ammonia Gas at STP

Using the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$, rearranged to $PM = \rho RT$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{NH_3,\,STP} &= \frac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(1 \;\text{atm}) \times (17.031 \;\text{g/mol})}{(0.08206 \;\text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}) \times (273.15 \;\text{K})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{17.031}{22.414} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.7599 \;\; g/L} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.7599 \;\; kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

Note: The KTU textbook rounded value is **0.771 kg/m³** (using slightly different reference conditions, e.g., 0 °C, 1 atm but with real-gas correction). Students should quote **0.76 g/L** for safety.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Ammonia Required to Produce 1 Tonne of Urea

Urea reaction (overall): $\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

Molar mass of urea = **60.06 g/mol**. To produce 1000 kg urea:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{urea}} &= \frac{1000 \;\text{kg}}{60.06 \;\text{kg/kmol}} = 16.65 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt]
n_{NH_3} &= 2 \times n_{\text{urea}} = 33.30 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt]
m_{NH_3} &= 33.30 \times 17.031 = \mathbf{567.13 \;\; kg \;\; NH_3}
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, **~0.567 tonnes of NH₃** are needed to make **1 tonne of urea** — a stoichiometric ratio of **~0.567 kg NH₃ per kg urea**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Properties vs. Industrial Use

| Property | Observed Value | Consequence / Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| High %N | 82.25% | Excellent N carrier |
| Low boiling point | −33.34 °C | Easy liquefaction, transport, storage |
| High water solubility | 700 vol/vol at 20 °C | Used as aqua-ammonia fertilizer |
| Basic character (K_b = 1.8×10⁻⁵) | Forms NH₄⁺ salts | Manufacturing ammonium fertilizers |
| Reacts with CO₂ | Forms urea | Core urea manufacturing route |
| Catalytic oxidation | Pt/Rh catalyst → NO | Ostwald process for HNO₃ |
| Polar, lone pair on N | Forms complexes | Solvent extraction in hydrometallurgy |
| Reducing agent | Oxidised by hot metals | Nitride/amide synthesis |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Molecular Geometry of Ammonia

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Nitrogen Atom sp3 Hybridized"] --> node2["Lone Pair of Electrons"]
    node1 --> node3["Hydrogen Atom 1"]
    node1 --> node4["Hydrogen Atom 2"]
    node1 --> node5["Hydrogen Atom 3"]
    node3 --> node6["Covalent N-H Bond 101.7 pm"]
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node2 --> node7["Bond Angle Compressed to 107.8 degrees"]
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8["Trigonal Pyramidal Geometry"]
    node8 --> node9["Net Dipole Moment 1.47 Debye"]
    node9 --> node10["Highly Polar Molecule"]
    node10 --> node11["Water Soluble Forms NH4OH"]
    node10 --> node12["Reacts with Acids Forms NH4 Salts"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Ammonia as Central Hub in Fertilizer Manufacture

```mermaid
graph LR
    feedstock1["Natural Gas CH4 + H2O"] --> reformer["Steam Reforming Unit"]
    feedstock2["Air N2 + O2"] --> airsep["Air Separation Unit"]
    airsep --> n2supply["N2 Stream"]
    reformer --> h2supply["H2 Stream"]
    h2supply --> haber["Haber-Bosch Reactor 400 to 500 C, 200 atm, Fe Catalyst"]
    n2supply --> haber
    haber --> ammonia["Liquid Ammonia NH3"]
    ammonia --> urea["Urea Plant 2NH3 + CO2"]
    ammonia --> nitacid["Nitric Acid Plant Ostwald Process"]
    ammonia --> ammsul["Ammonium Sulfate NH3 + H2SO4"]
    ammonia --> ammni["Ammonium Nitrate NH3 + HNO3"]
    ammonia --> dap["DAP MAP Plants NH3 + H3PO4"]
    urea --> fertfinal["Finished Nitrogenous Fertilizers"]
    nitacid --> fertfinal
    ammsul --> fertfinal
    ammni --> fertfinal
    dap --> fertfinal
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reaction Pathways of Ammonia (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nh3["Ammonia NH3 Central Node"] --> r1["With H2O gives NH4OH Basic Nature"]
    nh3 --> r2["With HCl gives NH4Cl Salt Formation"]
    nh3 --> r3["With H2SO4 gives NH4 2SO4 Fertilizer"]
    nh3 --> r4["With CO2 gives Urea Fertilizer"]
    nh3 --> r5["With O2 Pt Catalyst gives NO Ostwald"]
    r5 --> r5b["NO to NO2 to HNO3"]
    nh3 --> r6["With Na gives NaNH2 Sodamide"]
    nh3 --> r7["With Cu2+ gives Cu NH3 4 2+ Complex"]
    nh3 --> r8["Thermal Decomposition gives N2 + H2"]

    r3 --> farm
    r4 --> farm

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the important physical properties of ammonia that make it suitable as a refrigerant.**

**Model Answer:**

The following physical properties of ammonia make it an excellent refrigerant (designated R-717):

1. **Low boiling point (−33.34 °C):** Allows it to evaporate at low temperatures suitable for industrial and food refrigeration.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization (~1366 kJ/kg at −33 °C):** Enables high refrigerating effect per kg circulated.
3. **Low molecular weight (17.031):** Results in low gas-side resistance and good heat-transfer coefficients.
4. **High thermal conductivity:** Improves heat exchange efficiency in evaporators and condensers.
5. **Easily detectable by smell (pungent):** Any leak is immediately noticed — a major safety advantage over odourless CFCs.
6. **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) and zero GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Environmentally benign.
7. **Easily liquefied under moderate pressure (~8.5 atm at 20 °C).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always list properties with their **numerical values** — that is what earns you the full 3 marks in Part A.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the basic nature of ammonia with a suitable reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

Ammonia is **basic in nature** because of the **lone pair of electrons** on the nitrogen atom, which can be donated to a proton (H⁺) — making NH₃ a **Lewis base**.

When dissolved in water, ammonia accepts a proton from water, producing **ammonium (NH₄⁺)** and **hydroxyl (OH⁻)** ions:

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$$

The basicity constant at 25 °C is:

$$K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$$

The presence of OH⁻ ions makes the solution **alkaline (turns red litmus blue)**. Ammonia also reacts directly with acids to form ammonium salts, e.g.:

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) Module-2 pattern, students must answer **one full 14-mark question**, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the physical properties of ammonia. Why is it called the "backbone of the fertilizer industry"?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat equations, explain the reactions of ammonia with (i) sulphuric acid, (ii) nitric acid, (iii) carbon dioxide, and (iv) oxygen (catalytic). State the industrial significance of each reaction.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Physical Properties of Ammonia

| # | Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Molecular formula | NH₃ | Simplest nitrogen hydride |
| 2 | Molar mass | 17.031 g/mol | Light gas, lighter than air |
| 3 | Nitrogen content | 82.25 % | Highest among all N-carriers |
| 4 | Boiling point | −33.34 °C | Easy liquefaction for storage/transport |
| 5 | Melting point | −77.7 °C | Liquid range is wide |
| 6 | Critical temperature | 132.4 °C | Easily liquefied by pressure alone |
| 7 | Critical pressure | 111.3 atm | Moderate pressure requirement |
| 8 | Density (gas, STP) | 0.771 kg/m³ | Lighter than air |
| 9 | Density (liquid, b.p.) | 0.682 g/cm³ | Used in refrigeration storage |
| 10 | Dipole moment | 1.47 D | Highly polar → water-soluble |
| 11 | Bond angle (H–N–H) | 107.8° | Trigonal pyramidal |
| 12 | Hybridization of N | sp³ | One lone pair on N |
| 13 | Solubility in H₂O | 700 vol/vol at 20 °C | Forms aqua-ammonia fertilizer |
| 14 | Odour | Pungent, irritating | Detected at 5 ppm (safety) |
| 15 | Basicity constant (K_b) | 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ | Weak base, reacts with acids |

**Why "Backbone of Fertilizer Industry":**
- Ammonia is the **starting point** for **urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate, ammonium phosphate (DAP/MAP), calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN), and ammonium chloride**.
- It contains the **highest nitrogen content (82.25%)** of any nitrogen source.
- About **85%** of industrially produced ammonia is consumed by the fertilizer sector worldwide.
- It is the precursor for **nitric acid** (via Ostwald process), which is then used in **nitrophosphate fertilizers**.

#### Part (b): Reactions of Ammonia

**(i) With Sulphuric Acid:**

$$2\text{NH}_3(g) + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(l) \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4(s) \quad \Delta H = -275 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

- **Industrial significance:** Ammonium sulfate is a directly usable nitrogenous fertilizer containing **~21% N** and **~24% S**.

**(ii) With Nitric Acid:**

$$\text{NH}_3(g) + \text{HNO}_3(l) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3(s) \quad \Delta H = -146 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

- **Industrial significance:** Ammonium nitrate contains **~34% N** and is widely used as a high-analysis fertilizer and as an explosive (ANFO).

**(iii) With Carbon Dioxide (Urea Synthesis):**

$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \quad \text{(Ammonium carbamate, exothermic)}$$

$$\text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Urea, endothermic)}$$

- **Industrial significance:** Urea contains **46% N** — the highest N-content among solid fertilizers. Globally, urea accounts for **>50%** of nitrogen fertilizer consumption.

**(iv) Catalytic Oxidation with Oxygen (Ostwald Process):**

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Rh gauze, 850 °C}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2$$

$$3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$$

- **Industrial significance:** Nitric acid is the precursor for **ammonium nitrate, calcium ammonium nitrate, and nitrophosphate fertilizers**.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the molecular weight and percentage nitrogen content of ammonia. Comment on the significance of %N for a fertilizer-grade material.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write short notes on: (i) Aqua-ammonia as a fertilizer, (ii) Anhydrous ammonia as a fertilizer, and (iii) The use of ammonia in the refrigeration industry.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Molecular Weight & %N Derivation

**Step 1 — Molecular weight:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{NH_3} &= (1 \times 14.0067) + (3 \times 1.00794) \\
&= 14.0067 + 3.02382 \\
&= \mathbf{17.03052 \;\;g/mol} \approx \mathbf{17.031 \;g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — %N calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%N &= \frac{A_r^{N}}{M_{NH_3}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{14.0067}{17.03052} \times 100 \\
&= \mathbf{82.25\,\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Significance of %N for fertilizer-grade materials:**

1. **Nutrient economy:** Higher %N means more nitrogen delivered per kg of fertilizer → lower transport and storage cost per kg N.
2. **Quality benchmark:** Commercial ammonia (82.25% N theoretical) is graded against this benchmark — commercial anhydrous ammonia is typically sold at **~82% N minimum**.
3. **Comparison tool:** Enables comparison with other fertilizers (urea 46%N, ammonium nitrate 34%N, ammonium sulfate 21%N, CAN 26%N).
4. **Pricing basis:** Fertilizers are globally priced on a **per-tonne-of-nutrient** basis using %N as the conversion factor.
5. **Regulatory compliance:** %N labelling is mandatory under **FCO (Fertiliser Control Order)** in India.

#### Part (b): Short Notes

**(i) Aqua-Ammonia as a Fertilizer:**
- Aqua-ammonia is an **aqueous solution of NH₃**, typically **20–30% NH₃ by weight**.
- It is injected directly into the soil to avoid volatilization losses.
- Lower vapour pressure than anhydrous NH₃ → safer to handle.
- Used in rice paddies and for direct soil application in large farms.

**(ii) Anhydrous Ammonia as a Fertilizer:**
- Contains **~82% N** (highest among all N-fertilizers).
- Injected **8–15 cm below the soil surface** using a knife applicator to prevent volatilization.
- Immediately reacts with soil moisture to form NH₄⁺, which is retained by soil colloids.
- The most economical nitrogen fertilizer per kg of N, but **highly hazardous** due to storage pressure (~10 atm) and toxicity.

**(iii) Ammonia in Refrigeration:**
- Designated refrigerant **R-717**.
- Boiling point **−33.34 °C** at atmospheric pressure — ideal for medium/low-temperature refrigeration.
- **Latent heat of vaporization ≈ 1366 kJ/kg** — very high refrigerating effect.
- Used in **cold storage, ice plants, food processing, fishery, dairy, and large HVAC plants**.
- Disadvantage: **Toxic and mildly flammable** — restricted in occupied residential spaces.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks Here:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units & numerical values:** Writing "ammonia has a low boiling point" without **−33.34 °C** costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing %N of ammonia with %N of urea:** Students often write "ammonia has 46% N" — this is **urea**, not ammonia. **Ammonia = 82.25% N**.
> 3. **Missing balanced equations:** In Part B questions, examiners award **2 marks per balanced equation** — never write unbalanced reactions.
> 4. **No industrial linkage:** Stating a property without explaining its *industrial consequence* (e.g., "boiling point −33.34 °C" → "enables easy liquefaction for transport") loses the application mark.
> 5. **Ignoring safety:** Failing to mention that anhydrous NH₃ is **toxic, corrosive, and stored under pressure** results in a deduction of up to 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing Haber–Bosch under "Physical Properties":** Haber–Bosch is a **manufacturing process**, not a property. Keep it in a separate "Manufacture" section.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist the night before your exam. Tick every box mentally.**

- ✅ Ammonia = NH₃, **M = 17.031 g/mol**, **%N = 82.25%**.
- ✅ **sp³ hybridized**, **trigonal pyramidal**, bond angle **107.8°**, dipole moment **1.47 D**.
- ✅ **Boiling point = −33.34 °C**, **melting point = −77.7 °C**, **T_c = 132.4 °C**, **P_c = 111.3 atm**.
- ✅ **Highly water-soluble** (~700 vol/vol at 20 °C); solution is **alkaline (NH₄OH)** with **K_b = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵**.
- ✅ **Lewis base** — donates lone pair to H⁺ and metal ions.
- ✅ With **H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄** (21% N fertilizer).
- ✅ With **HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃** (34% N fertilizer + explosive).
- ✅ With **CO₂ → NH₂COONH₄ → CO(NH₂)₂** (urea, 46% N — most consumed fertilizer globally).
- ✅ Catalytic oxidation (Pt/Rh) → NO → NO₂ → **HNO₃** (Ostwald process).
- ✅ With **Na → NaNH₂** (sodamide).
- ✅ **R-717 refrigerant** — zero ODP, zero GWP, high latent heat.
- ✅ **Anhydrous NH₃** = highest %N fertilizer, injected into soil.
- ✅ **Aqua NH₃** = 20–30% NH₃ in water, safer to handle.
- ✅ **Industrial significance:** ~85% of global NH₃ → fertilizer sector.
- ✅ **Safety:** toxic, pungent, stored as liquefied gas under pressure.

---

**End of Module 2 — Introduction to Ammonia: Physical and Chemical Properties**
