topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Fertilizer Technology (PECHT637), **Applications** refers to the systematic methods, techniques, and agronomic practices employed for the delivery of macro- and micronutrient fertilizers to crops in order to maximize nutrient use efficiency (NUE), minimize environmental losses, and sustain soil fertility. Under the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic encompasses the classification of fertilizer application methods, principles governing nutrient placement, timing, and the industrial as well as agricultural end-uses of nitrogenous, phosphatic, and complex fertilizers covered in Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT637 – Module 2):** Applications cover the *practical deployment* of fertilizers manufactured via the Haber-Bosch, Ostwald, and contact/nitrophosphate processes. The focus is on **Method of Application, Time of Application, Rate of Application, and Placement of Fertilizers**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of fertilizer application like **medicine delivery to a patient**. A doctor does not simply dump medicine into the patient's mouth; instead, the medicine type, dosage, timing, and route (oral, IV, topical) are carefully chosen based on the patient's condition. Similarly, an agronomist selects:

- **What** nutrient to deliver (N, P, K, or micronutrients)
- **How much** to deliver (rate, normally in **kg/ha**)
- **When** to deliver (basal, top-dressing, split application)
- **How** to deliver (broadcast, band, foliar, fertigation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:** **Nutrient Use Efficiency (NUE)** = (Nutrient recovered by the crop ÷ Nutrient applied) × 100. **Bold Standard Metric:** For cereals, typical NUE of nitrogen is **30–50%**, meaning more than half of the applied N is lost to leaching, volatilization, or denitrification. The *entire purpose* of fertilizer application science is to push this number higher.

## 1.3 Categories of Fertilizer Applications

The industrial and agricultural applications of fertilizers, as classified under Module 2, fall into three broad categories:

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Soil Application** | Fertilizer placed in/on the soil near the root zone | Urea broadcasting, DAP placement |
| **Foliar Application** | Nutrient solution sprayed on leaves | 2% Urea spray on wheat |
| **Fertigation** | Fertilizer dissolved in irrigation water | Drip-fed KNO₃ in polyhouse cultivation |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Application Insight:** Beyond agriculture, certain fertilizers (e.g., ammonium nitrate, urea) serve dual purposes — ammonium nitrate is used in **mining explosives (ANFO)**, and urea is a feedstock for **urea-formaldehyde resins, melamine, and AdBlue (DEF)** in diesel exhaust treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles Governing Fertilizer Application

The science of fertilizer application rests on four governing principles. Each principle links a *physical/chemical property of the fertilizer* to a *biological need of the crop*.

### 2.1.1 Principle 1 – Nutrient Placement
The fertilizer must be placed where **roots can intercept** it before it reacts with soil or is lost. Mobile nutrients (NO₃⁻) move with water and can be placed anywhere, while **immobile nutrients (P, K)** must be placed *in the root zone* via banding or localized placement.

### 2.1.2 Principle 2 – Timing Synchronization
Application must coincide with **crop nutrient demand curves**. Nitrogen demand in cereals peaks at the **tillering-to-panicle-initiation stage (25–45 DAS)**; applying all N at sowing causes 40–60% loss.

### 2.1.3 Principle 3 – Rate Determination (Law of the Minimum – Liebig's Law)
Crop yield is governed by the nutrient present in **minimum proportion**. Therefore, application rate is set by the *most deficient nutrient* relative to the critical level in soil.

### 2.1.4 Principle 4 – Method Compatibility
The application method must match the **fertilizer's physical form** (granular, prilled, liquid, foliar-soluble) and the **crop's morphology**.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Application Methods

### A. Solid Fertilizer Application Methods
1. **Broadcasting** — uniform scattering over the entire field; followed by incorporation.
2. **Band Placement** — fertilizer placed in continuous bands 5 cm beside and 5 cm below the seed row.
3. **Pellet Placement / Starter Dose** — concentrated pellets placed in planting holes (sugarcane, coconut).
4. **Side-Dressing / Top-Dressing** — fertilizer applied between rows during active growth.
5. **Popup Application** — small quantity placed directly with the seed at sowing.

### B. Liquid Fertilizer Application Methods
1. **Foliar Spray** — nutrients absorbed through stomata and cuticle.
2. **Fertigation** — delivered through drip/micro-sprinkler systems.
3. **Injection into Soil** — anhydrous ammonia injected via knife coulters.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Examination.** Every equation below has appeared or is likely to appear in KTU ESE papers for PECHT637.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NUE} = \dfrac{\text{Nutrient Uptake by Crop}}{\text{Nutrient Applied}} \times 100$ | Nutrient Use Efficiency | % |
| 2 | $\text{Recovery Fraction (RF)} = \dfrac{N_{\text{harvest}} - N_{\text{soil}}}{N_{\text{fertilizer}}}$ | Apparent Recovery Fraction | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $\text{Agronomic Efficiency (AE)} = \dfrac{Y_{\text{fert}} - Y_{\text{control}}}{N_{\text{applied}}}$ | kg grain per kg nutrient | kg/kg |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Nutrient \% in fertilizer} \times \text{Dose of fertilizer}}{\text{Nutrient \% required by crop}}$ | Fertilizer dose calculation | kg/ha |
| 5 | $\text{Fertilizer Requirement} = \dfrac{\text{STCR-based dose}}{\text{Fertilizer grade}}$ | Soil Test Crop Response | kg/ha |
| 6 | $V_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{final}} \times V_{\text{final}}}{C_{\text{stock}}}$ | Foliar spray dilution formula | L |
| 7 | $\text{pH Buffer Capacity} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{pH}}{\Delta V_{\text{acid/base}}}$ | Urea hydrolysis alkalinity | — |
| 8 | $\text{Split Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{\text{basal}}}{N_{\text{top-dress}}}$ | N application timing | Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:** For most cereals, agronomic efficiency of N = **10–25 kg grain/kg N applied**. Below **10**, the fertilizer is wasted; above **25**, response is approaching plateau.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern precision agriculture, the principles above are implemented through:
- **GPS-guided variable-rate applicators** that change N dose every 2–5 m based on NDVI satellite maps.
- **Slow-release coated urea (SCU, PCU)** that synchronizes N release with crop demand — extending NUE from ~40% to **60–70%**.
- **Drip-fertigation systems** that achieve NUE of **80–90%** for N and K in horticultural crops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fertilizer Dose from Soil Test Value

The standard equation used by State Fertilizer Recommendation Committees (e.g., KAU, IISS) is the **STCR (Soil Test Crop Response) equation**:

$$
\text{Fertilizer dose (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{\text{Nutrient requirement of crop} - \text{Available nutrient in soil}}{\text{Content of nutrient in fertilizer} \times \text{Recovery efficiency}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation
Let:
- $Y$ = Targeted yield (kg/ha)
- $NR$ = Nutrient required per kg of grain (kg nutrient/kg grain)
- $SN$ = Soil test value for available nutrient (kg/ha)
- $CS$ = Contribution of soil nutrient (typically **0.5 × SN** for P, **0.3 × SN** for K)
- $RN$ = Recovery efficiency of applied nutrient (fraction)
- $N_f$ = Nutrient % in fertilizer (e.g., Urea = 46% N)

**Step 1:** Compute total nutrient requirement.

$$
\text{Total Nutrient Needed} = Y \times NR
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract soil contribution.

$$
\text{Nutrient to be supplied} = Y \times NR - CS
$$

**Step 3:** Adjust for recovery (since only a fraction RN is taken up).

$$
\text{Nutrient to apply} = \dfrac{Y \times NR - CS}{RN}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to fertilizer weight.

$$
\text{Fertilizer dose} = \dfrac{Y \times NR - CS}{RN \times N_f} \times 100
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A farmer in Palakkad targets a paddy yield of **6000 kg/ha**. Paddy requires **20 kg N per tonne of grain**. Soil test shows available N = **280 kg/ha**. Contribution of soil = **0.5**. Recovery efficiency of urea N = **0.5**. Find the urea dose.

**Solution (Step-wise):**

**Step 1 — Total N needed:**

$$
Y \times NR = 6000 \times \dfrac{20}{1000} = 6000 \times 0.020 = 120 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Soil contribution:**

$$
CS = 0.5 \times 280 = 140 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Net N to apply:**

$$
\text{N needed} = 120 - 140 = -20 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative result:** Soil already supplies more than crop needs. Hence, **no urea is required**; soil N is sufficient. This demonstrates Liebig's Law of Minimum in action — we follow the limiting nutrient.

**Step 4 — Urea dose (final form using the simplified equation):**

$$
\text{Urea (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{\text{N to apply (kg/ha)}}{0.46} = \dfrac{-20}{0.46} \approx -43.5 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

Since the answer is **negative**, only a maintenance dose of **30 kg N/ha** (≈ 65 kg urea/ha) is recommended as a top-dressing.

## 3.2 Derivation of Foliar Spray Dilution

When a foliar spray of urea is recommended at **2% concentration** and the farmer has **50% urea solution (biuret < 0.5%)**, dilution to 2% requires:

**Step 1:** Apply dilution law $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$.

$$
V_1 = \dfrac{C_2 \times V_2}{C_1} = \dfrac{2\% \times V_2}{50\%} = \dfrac{V_2}{25}
$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, to prepare **100 L of 2% spray**:

$$
V_1 = \dfrac{100}{25} = 4 \text{ L of stock solution}
$$

**Step 3:** Add water: $V_{\text{water}} = 100 - 4 = 96 \text{ L}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix – Selecting Application Method

| Crop / Condition | Best Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Wetland rice (puddled soil) | Broadcasting + Incorporation | Anaerobic soil reduces N loss via nitrification |
| Sugarcane planting | Pellet placement in furrows | Slow release matches long crop duration |
| Wheat in sandy loam | Band placement 5×5 cm | Reduces P-fixation in calcareous soil |
| Tomato in polyhouse | Fertigation via drip | Precision delivery, 90% NUE |
| Coconut (perennial) | Ring trench placement | Targets active root zone |
| Paddy foliar at tillering | 2% urea spray | Quick N boost during peak demand |
| Cotton in rainfed Vertisol | Side-dressing | Avoids leaching in heavy soil |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fertilizer Application System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Test Report] --> B[STCR Calculation Engine]
    C[Crop Demand Curve] --> B
    D[Climate Forecast] --> B
    B --> E[Recommended Dose kg per ha]
    E --> F1[Broadcasting Unit]
    E --> F2[Band Placement Drill]
    E --> F3[Drip Fertigation Tank]
    E --> F4[Foliar Spray Rig]
    F1 --> G[Field Application]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Crop Uptake NUE Monitor]
    H --> I{Yield Achieved?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Log to Recommendation Database]
    I -- No --> K[Feedback to STCR Engine]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing Application Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Fertilizer Selection] --> Q1{Crop Type?}
    Q1 -- Cereal --> Q2{Soil Moisture Status?}
    Q1 -- Horticulture --> Q3{Greenhouse or Open?}
    Q1 -- Plantation --> Q4{Root Zone Depth?}
    Q2 -- Wetland --> M1[Broadcasting Urea Basal + Topdress]
    Q2 -- Dryland --> M2[Band Placement NPK]
    Q3 -- Greenhouse --> M3[Fertigation Drip System]
    Q3 -- Open --> M4[Foliar Spray plus Soil Basal]
    Q4 -- Deep --> M5[Ring Trench Pellet]
    Q4 -- Shallow --> M6[Surface Broadcasting plus Mulch]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Foliar Nutrient Uptake Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spray Droplet 100 um] --> B[Cuticle Penetration]
    B --> C[Stomatal Entry]
    C --> D[Mesophyll Cell]
    D --> E[Phloem Loading]
    E --> F[Systemic Distribution]
    F --> G[Active Sink Leaves and Fruits]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Band Placement Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Seed Row] -->|5 cm side| B[Fertilizer Band 5 cm deep]
    B -->|5 cm below| S
    note[Nutrient diffuses upward and laterally into root zone]
    B --- note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The 5 × 5 cm placement rule (5 cm to the side, 5 cm below the seed) prevents salt injury to germinating seeds while ensuring young roots encounter nutrients within 3–7 days of emergence.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Nutrient Use Efficiency (NUE) and state the typical NUE values for nitrogen in cereals.**

**Model Answer:**
NUE is defined as the fraction of applied nutrient that is recovered by the crop, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{NUE (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Nutrient recovered by crop}}{\text{Nutrient applied}} \times 100
$$

For nitrogen in cereals (e.g., paddy, wheat, maize), the typical NUE under flooded/irrigated conditions ranges between **30–50%**, while for **P and K** it is higher (**15–25%** and **40–60%** respectively) because of lower mobility-related losses.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any four methods of fertilizer application with one example crop for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Example Crop |
|---|---|
| Broadcasting | Wetland paddy (urea basal) |
| Band placement | Wheat, maize |
| Foliar spray | Tomato (2% urea at flowering) |
| Fertigation | Polyhouse capsicum |
| Pellet placement | Sugarcane, coconut |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU ESE – Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of fertilizer application methods** with suitable diagrams. Mention the principles governing the choice of method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer targets a paddy yield of **7000 kg/ha**. Paddy N requirement = **22 kg N per tonne**. Soil test value = **320 kg available N/ha**. Soil contribution coefficient = **0.4**. Recovery efficiency of urea = **0.5**. Calculate the **urea dose (kg/ha)** and recommend the **split application schedule**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Fertilizer application methods are classified into **solid-application methods** and **liquid-application methods**.

**Solid Methods:**
- **Broadcasting** — Uniform scattering across the field, followed by ploughing-in or puddling-in. Suitable for paddy, wheat, and pasture crops. Limitation: high nutrient loss via fixation, volatilization, and run-off.
- **Band Placement** — Fertilizer placed in continuous bands 5 cm beside and 5 cm below the seed row. Excellent for P and K in calcareous soils.
- **Pellet/Point Placement** — Concentrated pellets (2–5 g) placed in planting holes (sugarcane, coconut, fruit trees).
- **Side-Dressing** — Applied between rows during vegetative growth; ideal for top-dressing N in cotton, maize.

**Liquid Methods:**
- **Foliar Spray** — 1–3% nutrient solutions sprayed on leaves; fast-acting.
- **Fertigation** — Through drip/micro-sprinkler systems; 80–90% NUE.
- **Soil Injection** — Anhydrous ammonia injected under pressure (10–15 cm depth).

**Principles Governing Choice:**
1. Nutrient mobility (mobile → broadcast; immobile → band).
2. Crop root architecture.
3. Soil type and pH.
4. Cropping system and labour availability.
5. Cost–benefit ratio.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Total N required:**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 7000 \times \dfrac{22}{1000} = 154 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Soil contribution:**

$$
CS = 0.4 \times 320 = 128 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Net N to apply:**

$$
N_{\text{apply}} = 154 - 128 = 26 \text{ kg N/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Urea dose:**

$$
\text{Urea (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{26}{0.46} = 56.5 \approx 57 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

**Recommended Split Schedule (Kerala Package of Practices):**

| Stage | Time | Dose |
|---|---|---|
| Basal | At transplanting | 14 kg N/ha (≈ 30 kg urea/ha) |
| Active tillering | 15–20 DAT | 8 kg N/ha (≈ 17 kg urea/ha) |
| Panicle initiation | 40–45 DAT | 4 kg N/ha (≈ 10 kg urea/ha) |

**Total = 26 kg N/ha in 3 splits** — improving NUE from ~40% to ~55%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **STCR-based fertilizer recommendation** method with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **broadcasting, band placement, and fertigation** with respect to NUE, suitability, equipment, and cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**STCR (Soil Test Crop Response)** is a fertilizer recommendation approach that integrates:
1. Soil test value (initial available nutrient status).
2. Crop nutrient requirement for the targeted yield.
3. Native soil nutrient contribution.
4. Recovery efficiency of applied fertilizer.

**General Equation:**

$$
F = \dfrac{T \times NR - CS}{RN \times N_f} \times 100
$$

where $F$ = fertilizer dose (kg/ha), $T$ = target yield, $NR$ = nutrient per unit yield, $CS$ = soil contribution, $RN$ = recovery efficiency, $N_f$ = % nutrient in fertilizer.

**Numerical Example:**
For wheat target **Y = 4000 kg/ha**, P requirement = **9 kg P per tonne grain**, soil test P = **25 kg/ha**, soil contribution coefficient = **0.6**, recovery efficiency of P = **0.25**, and P₂O₅ content in **SSP = 16%**.

**Step 1 — Total P needed:**

$$
4000 \times \dfrac{9}{1000} = 36 \text{ kg P/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Soil contribution:**

$$
0.6 \times 25 = 15 \text{ kg P/ha}
$$

**Step 3 — Net P to apply:**

$$
36 - 15 = 21 \text{ kg P/ha}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to P₂O₅ equivalent (P × 2.29):**

$$
21 \times 2.29 = 48.1 \text{ kg P}_2\text{O}_5/\text{ha}
$$

**Step 5 — SSP dose:**

$$
\text{SSP} = \dfrac{48.1}{0.16} \approx 300 \text{ kg SSP/ha}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | Broadcasting | Band Placement | Fertigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| NUE (N) | 30–40% | 45–55% | 80–90% |
| Best suited to | Wetland paddy, pasture | Row crops in arid soils | High-value horticulture |
| Equipment cost | Low (centrifuge/spinner) | Medium (drill-mounted) | High (drip system ₹80k–₹1.5L/ha) |
| Labour | Low | Medium | Low (automated) |
| Risk of loss | High (volatilization, run-off) | Moderate | Very low |
| Nutrient types | N, K | N, P, K | All water-soluble |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing NUE without the **%** sign or using kg/ha instead of kg/kg for agronomic efficiency. KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** per unit error.
> 2. **Wrong Conversion Factor:** P₂O₅ to P uses **÷2.29**, not ×2.29. Always cross-check: 1 kg P = 2.29 kg P₂O₅.
> 3. **Skipping the Soil Contribution Step:** Omitting the CS term yields an overestimated fertilizer dose — a classic answer that loses **3 of 7 marks**.
> 4. **No Recommendation:** A pure calculation answer without agronomic recommendation (split schedule, method) is marked **incomplete** under CO5.
> 5. **Diagram Missing:** For 7-mark descriptive answers, a labelled diagram is **mandatory**. KTU examiners expect at least one sketch per 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fertilizer Applications (Module 2):**

- **Definition** — Application = *placement + timing + rate* of fertilizer to crops.
- **Four governing principles** — Placement, Timing, Rate, Compatibility.
- **NUE** = (Crop uptake ÷ Applied) × 100; cereals N = **30–50%**, P = **15–25%**, K = **40–60%**.
- **Liebig's Law of Minimum** governs rate calculation.
- **STCR Equation** must be memorised in full for 14-mark questions.
- **Split application** of N in paddy: **3 splits** at basal, tillering, panicle initiation.
- **Band placement geometry:** **5 cm side × 5 cm below** the seed row.
- **Foliar urea concentration** for cereals: **2%** (biuret < 0.5% to avoid leaf scorch).
- **Conversion factors to remember:**
  - P × 2.29 = P₂O₅
  - K × 1.20 = K₂O
  - N × 1.0 = N (no conversion)
- **Best method by crop:**
  - Paddy → Broadcasting (wetland)
  - Wheat/Maize → Band placement (dryland)
  - Sugarcane → Pellet placement
  - Tomato/Pepper polyhouse → Fertigation
  - Coconut → Ring trench
- **Industrial non-agri uses** — Urea → resins, melamine, AdBlue; Ammonium nitrate → ANFO explosives.
- **Key Coatings:** Sulphur-coated urea (SCU), Polymer-coated urea (PCU) — increase NUE to **60–70%**.
- **Fertigation rule:** Use only **100% water-soluble** fertilizers (e.g., urea, MAP, KNO₃, MKP); avoid SSP and rock phosphate in drip systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis Gas by Steam Hydrocarbon Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synthesis Gas by Steam Hydrocarbon Reforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synthesis Gas (Syngas)** is a combustible gaseous mixture primarily composed of **hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)**, with varying proportions of carbon dioxide (CO₂), steam, and nitrogen (N₂) depending on the downstream application. It serves as the fundamental chemical building block for manufacturing ammonia (NH₃), methanol (CH₃OH), Fischer-Tropsch hydrocarbons, and hydrogen.

**Steam Hydrocarbon Reforming (SHR)** — also known as **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** when natural gas (methane) is the feedstock — is the dominant **heterogeneous, catalytic, endothermic industrial process** for large-scale syngas production. In this process, saturated hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, or naphtha fractions) react with **high-temperature superheated steam** over a nickel-based catalyst inside externally-fired tubular reactors (primary reformer), followed by partial oxidation with air in an adiabatic reactor (secondary reformer) when the syngas is destined for ammonia synthesis (to introduce the stoichiometric N₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT637 / Module 2):**
> The student must master — (i) primary reforming reaction stoichiometry, (ii) secondary reforming for N₂ incorporation, (iii) operating window (T = **700–1000 °C**, P = **15–40 atm**, S/C = **3–5**), and (iv) the coupled water-gas shift equilibrium. Carbon formation thermodynamics and catalyst poisoning mechanisms (sulfur, chlorine) are **frequently tested** at 14-mark depth.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are **roasting coffee beans over a fire inside a long metal pipe while blowing steam through them**. The fire provides the intense heat needed; the steam chemically "cracks open" the methane molecule (CH₄), stealing its hydrogen atoms and leaving behind a useful carbon-monoxide "skeleton". Downstream, you blow the resulting hot gas mixture through a second chamber filled with air (partial oxidation) to give it a final energy boost and — if you are making ammonia — to inject the nitrogen needed for NH₃. Finally, you pass it over a copper catalyst that "rearranges" the CO and H₂O into extra H₂ (shift reaction). The output is a clean, hydrogen-rich syngas ready for ammonia synthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The primary reformer is the **largest single energy consumer** in an ammonia plant (~60–70% of total fired duty). Therefore, heat integration, excess steam ratio (S/C), and reformer tube metallurgy (HK-40, micro-alloyed steels) are the dominant economic levers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Standard temperature | T° | **298.15** | K |
| Standard pressure | P° | **1.01325 × 10⁵** | Pa (≡ 1 atm) |
| Steam-to-carbon molar ratio | S/C | **3.0 – 5.0** | mol·mol⁻¹ |
| Primary reformer outlet T | T₁ | **700 – 850** | °C |
| Secondary reformer outlet T | T₂ | **900 – 1000** | °C |
| Reforming pressure | P | **15 – 40** | atm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Reaction Architecture

**Step A — Primary Steam Reforming (Strongly Endothermic, Tubular Reactor):**

The core heterogeneous reaction occurring on a nickel-on-α-alumina (Ni/α-Al₂O₃) catalyst inside catalyst-filled tubes is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2, \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206.2 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

- **Why endothermic?** Breaking one C–H bond and one O–H bond requires more energy than forming one C=O and three H–H bonds releases.
- **Mole change:** 2 mol gas → 4 mol gas. **High pressure penalizes equilibrium conversion** (Le Chatelier).
- **Catalyst:** Ni (~10–25 wt%) promoted with **K₂O** (carbon suppression) and **CaO / MgO** (support stability).

**Step B — Secondary Reforming (Partial Oxidation, Adiabatic):**

The partially reformed gas plus injected **process air (≈ stoichiometric)** enters a refractory-lined vessel containing a nickel catalyst bed:

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(exothermic, provides heat)}$$

$$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(exothermic)}$$

The exothermic combustion raises the gas temperature to **900–1000 °C**, driving the residual CH₄ conversion to **< 0.5 mol% (dry basis)** and simultaneously introducing the **N₂** required downstream for ammonia synthesis (H₂ : N₂ ≈ 3 : 1 molar).

**Step C — High-Temperature Shift (HTS) Conversion:**

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2, \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41.2 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

- Catalyst: **Fe₂O₃ – Cr₂O₃ – CuO** ("magnetite chrome catalyst"); T = **350–450 °C**.
- Favoured by **low temperature** but kinetically needs ≥ 320 °C.

**Step D — Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) Conversion:**

- Catalyst: **CuO – ZnO – Al₂O₃**; T = **200–250 °C**.
- Achieves residual CO **< 0.3 vol%**.
- Combined HTS + LTS gives overall CO conversion **> 99%**.

**Step E — Acid-Gas Removal & Final Purification:**

CO₂ is scrubbed using **Benfield (K₂CO₃)**, **MDEA (aMDEA)**, or **Rectisol/Selexol** solvents. Trace CO/CO₂ (< 10 ppm) is removed by **methanation**:

$$\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for KTU ESE numerical / analytical questions on syngas manufacture.

| # | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steam Reforming: $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206.2$ kJ·mol⁻¹ | $\Delta n = +2$; P ↑ → X ↓ |
| 2 | Combined Reforming: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H_{298}^\circ = +165.0$ kJ·mol⁻¹ | Net molar change = +2 |
| 3 | Water-Gas Shift: $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41.2$ kJ·mol⁻¹ | Exothermic; favoured by low T |
| 4 | Equilibrium constant (Shift): $K_p(T) = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}_2}\ P_{\text{H}_2}}{P_{\text{CO}}\ P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ | Dimensionless (P in atm) | $K_p \approx \exp\!\left(4577.8/T - 4.33\right)$ approx. |
| 5 | Carbon-formation threshold: $K_{p,c}(T) = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}}\ P_{\text{H}_2}^2}{P_{\text{CH}_4}\ P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ | — | If **actual $K_p > K_{p,c}$**, carbon forms on Ni |
| 6 | Steam-to-Carbon ratio: $\dfrac{S}{C} = \dfrac{\text{mol H}_2\text{O fed}}{\text{mol C in feed}}$ | mol·mol⁻¹ | Operating window: **3.0 – 5.0** |
| 7 | Van 't Hoff (Le Chatelier temperature dependence): $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | T in K, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Predicts $K_p$ shift with T |
| 8 | Heat duty (primary reformer): $Q = \sum_i n_i\,\Delta H_{f,i}^\circ\big\vert_{T_\text{out}} - \sum_i n_i\,\Delta H_{f,i}^\circ\big\vert_{T_\text{in}}$ | kJ·h⁻¹ per kmol feed | Dominant plant energy sink |
| 9 | Stoichiometric Air Ratio (NH₃ syngas): $\text{H}_2 : \text{N}_2 = 3 : 1$ | — | Air feed adjusted to satisfy this in secondary reformer |
| 10 | Catalyst Carbon Limit: $\text{S/C}_{\min} = f(T,P)$ | — | Read from carbon-deposition equilibrium chart |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Steam hydrocarbon reforming is the **backbone of the global hydrogen economy**, supplying **> 95% of all industrial H₂** (syngas-derived). It powers:
- **Ammonia plants** (95% of NH₃ uses this route) — used in fertilizers (urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate).
- **Methanol plants** — via Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ synthesis from CO + 2H₂.
- **Refinery hydrogen** for hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and hydrocracking.
- **Synthetic fuels** via Fischer-Tropsch.
- **Carbon capture-ready blue hydrogen** plants (PSA + CO₂ sequestration).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Composition of Steam Reformer Using Stoichiometry + S/C Specification

**Given:** Pure methane feed, steam-to-carbon ratio **S/C = 4.0**, reformer operating at T = **1100 K**, P = **15 atm**. Methane conversion at the reformer exit = **X** (assumed equilibrium; we will solve a simplified problem for stoichiometric mole balance).

**Step 1 — Set up the reaction table (per 1 mol CH₄ fed):**

Let the extent of reaction be **ξ** (in mol) for $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$.

| Species | Initial (mol) | Change | Equilibrium (mol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 1 | −ξ | 1 − ξ |
| H₂O | S/C = 4 | −ξ | 4 − ξ |
| CO | 0 | +ξ | ξ |
| H₂ | 0 | +3ξ | 3ξ |

**Step 2 — Total moles at equilibrium:**

$$n_\text{tot} = (1 - \xi) + (4 - \xi) + \xi + 3\xi = 5 + 2\xi$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions $y_i$:**

$$y_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{1 - \xi}{5 + 2\xi},\quad y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{4 - \xi}{5 + 2\xi},\quad y_{\text{CO}} = \frac{\xi}{5 + 2\xi},\quad y_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{3\xi}{5 + 2\xi}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium constant expression:**

At T = 1100 K, $K_p \approx 27.0$ (from thermodynamic tables). For the reforming reaction:

$$K_p = \frac{y_{\text{CO}}\,(y_{\text{H}_2})^3}{(y_{\text{CH}_4})\,(y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}})}\,\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)^{(3+1) - (1+1)} = \frac{y_{\text{CO}}\,(y_{\text{H}_2})^3}{y_{\text{CH}_4}\,y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\,\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)^{+2}$$

**Substituting mole fractions and simplifying the numerator/denominator:**

$$K_p = \frac{\xi \cdot (3\xi)^3}{(1 - \xi)(4 - \xi)\,(5 + 2\xi)^2}\,\left(\frac{15}{1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Insert numerical $K_p$:**

$$27.0 = \frac{27\,\xi^{4}}{(1 - \xi)(4 - \xi)\,(5 + 2\xi)^2}\,\cdot 225$$

$$\frac{(1 - \xi)(4 - \xi)(5 + 2\xi)^2}{\xi^4} = \frac{27 \times 225}{27.0} = 225$$

**Step 6 — Solve iteratively.** Try $\xi = 0.95$:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{(0.05)(3.05)(6.90)^2}{(0.95)^4} = \frac{(0.05)(3.05)(47.61)}{0.8145} \approx \frac{7.26}{0.8145} \approx 8.91$$

Try $\xi = 0.99$:

$$\text{LHS} = \frac{(0.01)(3.01)(6.98)^2}{(0.99)^4} = \frac{(0.01)(3.01)(48.72)}{0.961} \approx \frac{1.466}{0.961} \approx 1.53$$

The LHS decreases rapidly as ξ → 1. By linear interpolation between ξ = 0.99 and ξ = 1.00, **ξ ≈ 0.996**.

**Step 7 — Final equilibrium composition (mol):**

| Component | Moles | Mole % (dry) |
|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 0.004 | 0.13% |
| H₂O | 3.004 | — |
| CO | 0.996 | 24.9% |
| H₂ | 2.988 | 74.97% |

This confirms **high CH₄ conversion (~99.6%)** at industrial operating conditions.

### 3.2 Derivation: H₂ : N₂ Stoichiometry for Ammonia Synthesis Syngas

**Goal:** Compute the air-to-natural-gas feed ratio so that after primary reforming, secondary reforming (with air), and shift, the resulting syngas has $\text{H}_2 : \text{N}_2 = 3 : 1$ (molar).

**Step 1 — Per 1 mol CH₄ feed, primary reformer gives:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$$

Assume 100% conversion: **1 mol CO + 3 mol H₂** + 1 mol CO₂ via complete shift pathway (ignoring residual CH₄ for stoichiometric air calc).

**Step 2 — Air contains 21% O₂, 79% N₂ (molar). Per mol of air, O₂ carries 3.76 mol N₂.**

For 1 mol CH₄ to be completely oxidized to CO₂ by primary reformer + shift:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$$

So we produce **4 mol H₂** per mol CH₄ (fully shifted pathway).

**Step 3 — Nitrogen requirement for ammonia synthesis:**

We need $\text{N}_2 = \dfrac{\text{H}_2}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3} = 1.333$ mol.

**Step 4 — Air required to deliver 1.333 mol N₂:**

Air is typically introduced in the **secondary reformer** where it partially combusts residual CH₄. Per mol air, N₂ supplied = 0.79 mol; so:

$$\text{Air mol} = \frac{1.333}{0.79} = 1.688 \text{ mol air per mol CH}_4$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric Air-to-Carbon Ratio (A/C):**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{C} = 1.688 \quad (\text{i.e., }\sim 1.7\ \text{mol air per mol CH}_4)}$$

**Step 6 — Mass balance validation:**

O₂ in air = $1.688 \times 0.21 = 0.354$ mol. This O₂ oxidizes a portion of feed/reformed gas:

- Secondary reformer reaction: $\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$ → consumes **0.708 mol CH₄** (with 0.354 O₂).
- After 100% conversion and full shift: residual syngas H₂ = $4 - (2 \times 0.708) + (\text{O}_2 \text{to H}_2\text{O then shifted})$, where the resulting H₂O is shifted → gives ≈ **2.584 mol H₂** at exit.

For exact modern air-blown reformers with 30–35% CH₄ slip into the secondary, the stoichiometric **A/C ≈ 1.55 – 1.70**, which is the **KTU answer range** expected.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Variable Impact

| Variable ↑ | CH₄ Conversion X | H₂ Yield | Risk of Carbon Deposition | Catalyst Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | ↑↑ (favours endothermic) | ↑ | ↓ (high T suppresses coking) | ↓ (sintering) |
| Pressure | ↓ (Δn = +2) | ↓ | ↑ (suppresses) | ↑ |
| Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↓↓ (steam gasifies C) | ≈ stable |
| Space Velocity | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Sulfur in feed | — | — | — | ↓↓↓ (poison) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Steam Reforming Syngas Plant

> [!NOTE]
> This **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the unit-operation interactions of an industrial SMR-based syngas plant. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels use clean text only (Mermaid safe).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FEEDSTOCK_NATGAS_OR_NAPHTHA] --> A2[DESULFURIZATION_ZNO_GUARD_BED_HYDRODESULF]
    A2 --> A3[PREHEATER_MIXED_FEED_AND_STEAM_500C]
    A3 --> B1[PRIMARY_REFORMER_TUBULAR_NI_CATALYST_700_850C]
    B1 --> B2[PROCESS_GAS_TRANSFER_LINE]
    B2 --> C1[SECONDARY_REFORMER_AIR_INJECTION_PARTIAL_OX_900_1000C]
    C1 --> D1[WASTE_HEAT_BOILER_RECOVERY_800C_TO_300C]
    D1 --> E1[HTS_CONVERTER_FE2O3_CR2O3_350_450C]
    E1 --> E2[LTS_CONVERTER_CUO_ZNO_AL2O3_200_250C]
    E2 --> F1[CO2_REMOVAL_BENFIELD_OR_MDEA_SCRUBBING]
    F1 --> G1[METHANATOR_FINAL_CO_CO2_REMOVAL]
    G1 --> H1[SYNGAS_COMPRESSION_TO_AMMONIA_SYNTHESIS_LOOP]
    H1 --> H2[AMMONIA_SYNTHESIS_H2_PLUS_N2_TO_NH3]

    A1 -. Fired Fuel .-> B1
    B1 -. Process Air Feed .-> C1
    D1 -. Steam Generation .-> A3
    E1 -. Heat Recovery .-> A3

    style B1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#FFB380,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E2 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#C8E6A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

| Block | Function | Key Equipment / Catalyst | Operating Window |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Hydrocarbon feedstock (CH₄-rich NG, LPG, naphtha) | Pipeline NG / vaporized naphtha | T = ambient |
| A2 | Sulfur removal (< 0.1 ppm S); ZnO + Co-Mo hydrodesulfurization | ZnO adsorbent bed | 350–400 °C |
| A3 | Mix feed with superheated steam; preheat | Convective section of reformer | 500–600 °C |
| B1 | **Primary Reforming**: endothermic steam-CH₄ reaction | Ni / α-Al₂O₃ in HK-40 tubes | 700–850 °C, 15–40 atm |
| C1 | **Secondary Reforming**: air-fired partial oxidation | Ni / α-Al₂O₃ refractory vessel | 900–1000 °C |
| D1 | Heat recovery; high-pressure steam generation | Waste Heat Boiler (WHB) | 800 → 300 °C |
| E1 | **High-Temp Shift**: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ | Fe₂O₃ – Cr₂O₃ | 350–450 °C |
| E2 | **Low-Temp Shift**: residual CO polishing | CuO – ZnO – Al₂O₃ | 200–250 °C |
| F1 | Acid-gas removal; CO₂ capture | Benfield / aMDEA / Rectisol | 70–120 °C, P |
| G1 | Trace CO/CO₂ hydrogenation to CH₄ | Ni catalyst | 280–350 °C |
| H1, H2 | Syngas compression + NH₃ synthesis loop | Centrifugal compressor, Haber converter | 200–300 atm |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper, PECHT637]**

**Q1. Define synthesis gas. State the typical composition (molar) of syngas used for ammonia manufacture after secondary reforming and complete CO₂ removal.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Synthesis gas (syngas) is a mixture of hydrogen (H₂) and carbon monoxide (CO) — together with CO₂, N₂, and residual CH₄ — produced by steam reforming or partial oxidation of hydrocarbons. It is the precursor for ammonia, methanol, and Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.
>
> **Composition for NH₃ manufacture after CO₂ removal (2 marks):**
> - H₂ : N₂ (molar) = **3 : 1** (stoichiometric for Haber process).
> - Inert CH₄ + Ar ≈ **< 1 mol%** (dry basis).
> - Residual CO + CO₂ **< 10 ppm** (after methanation).

---

**Q2. What is the function of the secondary reformer? Why is air injected rather than pure oxygen?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Function (2 marks):**
> 1. Converts residual CH₄ (typically 8–12 mol% from primary reformer) to **< 0.5 mol%** via partial oxidation at **900–1000 °C**, increasing overall methane conversion.
> 2. Introduces **N₂** (via process air) into the syngas stream to establish the required **H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1** ratio for downstream ammonia synthesis.
>
> **Why air, not pure O₂ (1 mark):** Pure oxygen requires an expensive **Air Separation Unit (ASU)** (cryogenic distillation), adding **~30–40% capital cost**. Air is cheap; the accompanying N₂ is the desired diluent for ammonia synthesis anyway — a fortuitous synergy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECHT637 Module 2, 14 Marks Section]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the chemistry and engineering of primary steam reforming of methane with suitable reactions, operating conditions, and catalyst specifications.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the water-gas shift reaction — its stoichiometry, equilibrium, catalysts used in HTS and LTS, and the combined effect of a two-stage shift on residual CO.** *(7 marks)*

> **Model Solution (a):**

**Reaction (2 marks):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2, \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = +206.2 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

The reaction is **endothermic** (heat must be supplied) and proceeds with a **net increase in moles** (Δn = +2). High temperature and low pressure favour higher equilibrium conversion; excess steam also shifts equilibrium to the right.

**Operating Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Temperature:** 700–850 °C outlet (tube-skin temperature ≤ 950 °C, limited by HK-40 / micro-alloyed metallurgy).
- **Pressure:** 15–40 atm (modern ammonia plants operate at ~30–40 atm to reduce downstream compression cost, accepting lower equilibrium conversion).
- **Steam-to-carbon ratio (S/C):** **3.5–5.0** to suppress carbon deposition and enhance CH₄ conversion.
- **Space velocity:** 1500–2500 h⁻¹ (GHSV).

**Catalyst (2 marks):**
- **Active phase:** Nickel (10–25 wt%) on α-Al₂O₃ support.
- **Promoters:** K₂O (carbon gasification / suppression of whisker carbon), CaO (thermal stability), MgO (sintering resistance).
- **Catalyst shape:** Raschig rings or 7-hole pellets to minimize pressure drop and maximize external surface area.
- **Poisoning:** Sulfur (as little as 0.5 ppm), chlorine, and heavy metals permanently deactivate Ni. Hence **mandatory desulfurization** upstream.

**Reactor (1 mark):** Top-fired or side-fired **furnace** with vertical catalyst-filled tubes; radiant + convective heat transfer zones.

> **Model Solution (b):**

**Reaction & Thermodynamics (2 marks):**

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2, \quad \Delta H_{298}^\circ = -41.2 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

- **Mildly exothermic**; **favoured by low temperature** (high $K_p$ at low T).
- **Mole-conserving** (Δn = 0) → **pressure has no thermodynamic effect**; reaction rate limited by kinetics.
- Equilibrium constant: $K_p(T) = \exp(4577.8/T - 4.33)$ (approximate, valid in 700–1100 K).

**High-Temperature Shift (HTS) — 2 marks:**
- Catalyst: **Fe₂O₃ – Cr₂O₃** (magnetite-chrome), sometimes with CuO promoter.
- Operating T: **350–450 °C** (limited below by kinetics, above by thermodynamics).
- CO outlet: **~3 mol%** dry basis.

**Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) — 2 marks:**
- Catalyst: **CuO – ZnO – Al₂O₃** (also known as "low-temperature shift" or LTS catalyst).
- Operating T: **200–250 °C**.
- **Highly sensitive to sulfur and chlorine** — feed must be ultra-clean.
- CO outlet: **< 0.3 mol%** dry.

**Combined HTS + LTS Effect (1 mark):**
- HTS provides fast conversion at higher temperature (kinetically driven), drops CO from ~15% to ~3%.
- LTS exploits favourable low-T equilibrium to drop CO further to **< 0.3%**.
- **Combined conversion > 99%**, reducing CO that would otherwise poison the ammonia synthesis iron catalyst.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) With the aid of a flow diagram, describe the industrial production of syngas by steam hydrocarbon reforming for an ammonia plant. Include all major unit operations from feedstock to methanation.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Explain the role of the steam-to-carbon ratio and the carbon-formation thermodynamics. How is coking prevented in practice?** *(7 marks)*

> **Model Solution (a):**

**Process Flow (5 marks, with 2 marks for the diagram reference):**

The complete sequence of unit operations is:

1. **Feedstock preparation:** Natural gas (or naphtha) is filtered, preheated, and mixed with hydrogen recycle. **Desulfurization** is carried out in two stages: (i) **Co-Mo hydrodesulfurizer** at 350–400 °C converts organic sulfur to H₂S; (ii) **ZnO guard bed** reduces H₂S to < 0.1 ppm.
2. **Process steam mixing & preheating:** Steam at **S/C = 3.5–4.0** is mixed with the desulfurized feed and preheated in the reformer convection section to ~500–600 °C.
3. **Primary reformer (tubular):** The mixture passes through catalyst-filled tubes in a gas-fired furnace. Outlet conditions: **T = 700–850 °C**, **P = 30–40 atm**, residual CH₄ ≈ 8–12 mol% (dry). Heat is supplied by burning natural gas / purge gas.
4. **Secondary reformer (autothermal):** Process air is injected. Residual CH₄ is combusted with O₂ in the upper zone; the equilibrium reaction completes in the lower catalyst zone. Outlet T ≈ **900–1000 °C**, residual CH₄ < 0.5 mol%. The N₂ introduced by air is set to satisfy the H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1 requirement.
5. **Waste heat boiler:** Process gas is cooled to ~350–400 °C, generating **high-pressure steam** for the reformer and turbine drivers.
6. **HTS converter:** CO is shifted to H₂ at 350–450 °C over Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃ catalyst.
7. **LTS converter:** CO reduced to < 0.3 mol% at 200–250 °C over CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst.
8. **CO₂ removal:** Absorbed in Benfield (hot K₂CO₃), aMDEA, or Rectisol solvent; CO₂ vented or sequestered (for blue H₂).
9. **Methanation:** Trace CO and CO₂ (< 10 ppm each) are converted back to CH₄ over Ni catalyst at 280–350 °C, preventing downstream catalyst poisoning.
10. **Syngas compression:** Centrifugal compressor raises pressure to **200–300 atm** for ammonia synthesis loop.

*(Refer to SECTION 4 mermaid flow diagram for the block architecture.)*

**Process highlights (2 marks):**
- Heat integration: hot process gas preheats feed, generates steam, and superheats boiler feedwater — overall thermal efficiency ~ 92–95%.
- Modern SMR consumes **~ 33–36 GJ per tonne NH₃**, lower than older partial-oxidation / autothermal routes.

> **Model Solution (b):**

**Carbon-formation reactions — 3 marks:**
On nickel catalyst, three carbon-forming equilibria can occur:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\text{CO} &\rightleftharpoons \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 & \Delta H_{298}^\circ &= -172.5 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1} \\
\text{CH}_4 &\rightleftharpoons \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 & \Delta H_{298}^\circ &= +74.8 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1} \\
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} & \Delta H_{298}^\circ &= -131.3 \ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Carbon-formation thermodynamic condition:**
If the process-gas composition gives a ratio:

$$\frac{P_{\text{CO}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2}^2}{P_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} > K_{p,c}(T)$$

then **carbon (coke) deposits** on the nickel catalyst, blocking pores and causing tube hot-spots and rupture.

**Role of S/C ratio — 2 marks:**
- Higher steam partial pressure shifts the **methanation reverse reaction** and Boudouard equilibria **away from carbon**.
- The minimum S/C required is read from a **carbon-deposition limit chart** as a function of T and P. At 850 °C, 30 atm, with methane feed, **S/C_min ≈ 2.5**.
- Industrial plants operate at **S/C = 3.5–5.0** to give a safety margin of ≥ 1.0–1.5.

**Coking prevention practices — 2 marks:**
1. **Alkaline promoter (K₂O)** on Ni catalyst enhances carbon gasification.
2. **Sulfur passivation** (controlled addition of H₂S ~ 0.1 ppm) reversibly poisons active sites, suppressing carbon nucleation while still allowing steam reforming — used in naphtha reformers (e.g., **Topsoe SPARG** process).
3. **Pre-reforming (adiabatic pre-reformer)** in a Ni-rich catalyst bed at 400–500 °C to convert higher hydrocarbons completely before entering the main reformer.
4. Use of **high-activity Ni catalysts with small crystallite size** that resist whisker carbon.
5. Maintaining tube-skin temperature uniformity by **balanced firing** to avoid local hot spots.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "primary" and "secondary" reformer roles.** The primary reformer is *fired externally* and is the main endothermic CH₄ + H₂O converter; the secondary reformer is *autothermal* (air-fired) and primarily introduces N₂ + finishes CH₄ conversion. Marks are deducted when these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the Δn sign** in the steam reforming reaction (Δn = +2). This is critical for justifying *why high pressure penalizes conversion*.
> 3. **Omitting the desulfurization step** (ZnO guard bed). Even if not the "main" reactor, it is *the* prerequisite for catalyst life — KTU examiners expect it in any 7+ mark flow-diagram answer.
> 4. **Writing "CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂"** without identifying it as the **water-gas shift reaction** by name. Always pair the equation with the *name* and the catalyst type.
> 5. **Not specifying catalyst compositions.** "Nickel catalyst" alone (without α-Al₂O₃ support, K₂O promoter) loses credit.
> 6. **Missing the S/C operating range.** If you give S/C = 1.5 (too low → coking) or 8 (too high → wasteful), it signals incomplete preparation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Syngas = H₂ + CO (+ CO₂, N₂, CH₄, Ar)** — feedstock for ammonia, methanol, FT fuels, hydrogen.
- **Primary reformer:** externally fired, Ni/α-Al₂O₃, 700–850 °C, endothermic $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ ($\Delta H^\circ = +206.2$ kJ/mol).
- **Secondary reformer:** adiabatic, partial oxidation with air, 900–1000 °C, introduces N₂ for **H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1** ratio.
- **Water-gas shift:** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ ($\Delta H^\circ = -41.2$ kJ/mol), done in **two stages** — HTS (Fe₂O₃-Cr₂O₃, 350–450 °C) then LTS (CuO-ZnO-Al₂O₃, 200–250 °C).
- **Operating window:** T = 700–1000 °C, P = 15–40 atm, S/C = **3.5–5.0**, A/C ≈ **1.55–1.70**.
- **Catalyst poisons:** Sulfur (irreversible above ~0.5 ppm), chlorine, heavy metals; **must** desulfurize feed first (Co-Mo + ZnO guard bed).
- **Coking prevention:** alkaline (K₂O) promoter, controlled sulfur passivation, pre-reformer, high S/C ratio, balanced tube firing.
- **Energy economy:** Primary reformer is **~60–70% of total plant fired duty**; modern heat integration recovers > 90% thermal efficiency.
- **Pressure effect:** Steam reforming has Δn = +2; **higher pressure reduces equilibrium conversion**, but is used in modern plants to save downstream compression cost.
- **Methanation step:** final polishing of CO/CO₂ to < 10 ppm before syngas enters the ammonia synthesis loop; otherwise Haber iron catalyst is poisoned.
- **Mole-balance shortcut for NH₃ syngas:** 1 mol CH₄ + 4 mol H₂O (S/C=4) + 1.7 mol air → ~2.6 mol H₂ + 1.3 mol N₂ + CO₂ (removed), giving the required 3 : 1 ratio.
- **Combined reformer equation:** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$ (ΔH = +165.0 kJ/mol) — useful shortcut for full-shift pathway calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ammonia Converters: Types and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ammonia Converters: Types and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ammonia Converter** (also called the *Ammonia Synthesis Converter* or *Ammonia Reactor*) is the heart of any Haber–Bosch nitrogen-fixation plant. It is a high-pressure catalytic vessel in which a stoichiometric mixture of purified nitrogen and hydrogen (synthesis gas, *syngas*) is converted into ammonia over a promoted iron catalyst according to the reversible, exothermic reaction:

$$N_{2(g)} + 3H_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2NH_{3(g)} \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -46.22 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The converter must simultaneously provide:
- The **high pressure** required to shift equilibrium toward ammonia (Le Chatelier's principle).
- An **optimal temperature window** (typically **673 K – 773 K**, i.e., 400 °C – 500 °C) that is high enough to obtain acceptable reaction kinetics on the iron catalyst but low enough to retain favourable equilibrium.
- **Effective heat removal** because the reaction is strongly exothermic, and temperature rise suppresses conversion.
- **Catalyst bed architecture** that maximises gas–solid contact time while keeping pressure drop within design limits (**ΔP ≤ 5–10 bar**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A single-pass equilibrium conversion of syngas to ammonia is only about **15 – 20 %** under typical converter conditions. Therefore, industrial converters are designed as **multi-bed reactors with inter-stage heat removal or cold-shot cooling** to push overall conversion above **90 – 95 %** after gas recycle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

Imagine you are trying to fill a bucket with water from a tap, but the tap drips very slowly. To get the bucket full quickly, you don't just open one tap — you install **several taps in a row**, and after every tap you *cool the partially-filled bucket slightly* (because the water gets warm and slows down the flow). The ammonia converter works on exactly this principle:

- The "tap" is the **iron catalyst bed** that lets the slow N≡N triple bond break and react.
- The "water" is the **synthesis gas (N₂ + 3H₂)**.
- The "cooling between taps" is the **inter-stage heat exchanger** (or cold gas injection).
- The "partially filled bucket" is the **NH₃-rich gas leaving each catalyst stage**.

Different converter designs are simply **different plumbing strategies** for arranging the catalyst beds and the cooling steps.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Pressure | **150 – 300 bar** | Higher P shifts equilibrium → more NH₃ |
| Inlet Temperature | **673 K – 723 K** | Initiates fast kinetics on Fe catalyst |
| Peak Bed Temperature | **773 K – 823 K** | Must stay below catalyst sintering limit |
| Single-Pass Conversion | **15 – 22 %** | Why multi-bed design is mandatory |
| Overall Conversion (with recycle) | **> 95 %** | Process target |
| Catalyst | **Fe with K₂O, Al₂O₃, CaO promoters** | Magnetite reduced in situ to α-Fe |
| ΔH (per 2 mol NH₃) | **−46.22 kJ/mol** | Exothermic, controls bed temperature |
| Catalyst Life | **5 – 15 years** | Sensitive to sulphur/oxygen poisons |

> [!NOTE]
> The modern ammonia converter is a **self-contained heat-and-reaction management system** — its design philosophy is dictated by the simultaneous need to (a) drive the reaction forward, (b) remove the released heat, and (c) keep the catalyst active. This triple requirement is what produces the wide variety of converter types discussed below.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Backbone

### 2.1.1 Reaction Energetics
The synthesis reaction is **exothermic** and proceeds with a **decrease in the number of moles** (4 moles → 2 moles). Hence:
- **Low temperature** favours equilibrium (gives higher NH₃ yield).
- **High pressure** favours equilibrium (reduces total gas moles).
- **Catalyst** (Fe) is required to make the rate acceptable at the lower temperatures that favour equilibrium.

This is a classic **Le Chatelier compromise** — the operating point is chosen where kinetics and thermodynamics overlap favourably, typically **400 – 500 °C** and **200 – 300 atm**.

### 2.1.2 Equilibrium Constant Behaviour
The pressure-based equilibrium constant, $K_P$, is a strong function of temperature:

$$K_P(T) = \frac{P_{NH_3}}{\left(P_{N_2}\right)^{0.5} \left(P_{H_2}\right)^{1.5}}$$

Empirically (from the Gillespie–Beattie correlation, valid 600 – 800 K):

$$\log_{10} K_P = \frac{2074.8}{T} - 2.4943 \log_{10} T - 1.256 \times 10^{-4} T + 1.8534 \times 10^{-7} T^2 - 5.5209$$

> [!TIP]
> As temperature rises, $K_P$ **falls dramatically** — at 700 K, $K_P \approx 0.0091$; at 800 K, $K_P \approx 0.0064$. This is why uncontrolled temperature rise inside the catalyst bed is the *primary enemy* of ammonia yield.

### 2.1.3 The Van 't Hoff Relationship
Differentiating the equilibrium constant with temperature gives the *Van 't Hoff equation*, which rigorously shows why exothermic reactions have $K_P$ that *decreases* with $T$:

$$\frac{d \ln K_P}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$$

Because $\Delta H^\circ$ is negative, $\dfrac{d \ln K_P}{dT} < 0$ — the hallmark of an exothermic reaction.

## 2.2 Why Multi-Bed Converters?

In a single adiabatic bed, the released reaction heat would raise the gas temperature so much that the equilibrium constant collapses and the conversion levels off prematurely (often below **12 – 15 %**). The industrial solution is to **split the catalyst into several beds and cool the gas between them**. Two distinct cooling strategies exist:

1. **Direct (Quench) Cooling** — Cold synthesis gas is injected between beds to lower the temperature. The "extra" gas shifts the *local* composition and absorbs heat by sensible cooling.
2. **Indirect (Heat-Exchanger) Cooling** — The gas leaves the bed, passes through a tube-in-shell heat exchanger (often a boiler feed-water heater or process-gas preheater), and re-enters the next bed.

The choice of strategy defines the **classification of ammonia converters**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute-value and set-membership bars are written as `\vert` so that the markdown table does not break. Use this table as a last-minute revision reference before exams.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stoichiometric synthesis reaction | $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$ | — | Foundation stoichiometry |
| 2 | Standard reaction enthalpy | $\Delta H^\circ_{298} = -46.22$ kJ/mol NH₃ basis (or -92.44 for 2 mol) | kJ/mol | Quantifies heat to be removed |
| 3 | Equilibrium constant (P-based) | $K_P = \dfrac{P_{NH_3}}{(P_{N_2})^{0.5}(P_{H_2})^{1.5}}$ | bar$^{-1}$ | Predicts max possible conversion |
| 4 | Van 't Hoff equation | $\dfrac{d \ln K_P}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Predicts how $K_P$ shifts with T |
| 5 | Le Chatelier (qualitative) | High P, Low T, low inerts $\rightarrow$ higher NH₃ | — | Design heuristics |
| 6 | Space velocity (GHSV) | $GHSV = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{feed}}{V_{cat}}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Catalyst utilisation metric |
| 7 | Pressure drop (Ergun) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon^3} u_s + \dfrac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon) \rho u_s^2}{d_p \varepsilon^3}$ | Pa/m | Bed sizing & blower duty |
| 8 | Heat of reaction (T-dependent) | $\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | kJ/mol | Bed-temperature profile design |
| 9 | Inlet NH₃ mole fraction (typical) | $y_{NH_3, in} = 0.02$ to $0.04$ | mol/mol | Feed condition |
| 10 | Outlet NH₃ mole fraction (1st bed) | $y_{NH_3, out} \approx 0.12$ to $0.22$ | mol/mol | Single-pass yield |
| 11 | Catalyst life factor | Promoters K₂O, Al₂O₃, CaO delay sintering | — | Catalyst engineering |
| 12 | Recycle ratio | $R = \dfrac{\text{recycle flow}}{\text{net syngas flow}} \approx 3$ to $5$ | — | Loop energetics |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Intensity:** A modern ammonia plant consumes about **28 – 33 GJ per tonne of NH₃**, of which a large fraction is heat-management duty inside the converter. Better converter design directly lowers *specific energy consumption*.
- **Catalyst Protection:** A poorly designed temperature profile can sinter the iron catalyst, permanently reducing its active surface area from ~15 m²/g to <2 m²/g.
- **Plant Throughput:** Increasing per-pass conversion from 18 % to 22 % reduces recycle compressor duty by ~20 %, saving megawatt-hours per day.
- **Environmental Impact:** Lower per-tonne CO₂ emissions in the upstream reformer — because less hydrogen needs to be generated and recycled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Dependence of the Heat of Reaction (Kirchhoff's Law Applied to Ammonia Synthesis)

The reaction enthalpy is not constant — it varies with temperature because the heat capacities of N₂, H₂, and NH₃ differ. We will derive an explicit polynomial form of $\Delta H_T$ suitable for converter temperature-profile calculations.

### Step 1 — Kirchhoff's Equation (general form)

$$\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

### Step 2 — Heat-Capacity Data (standard polynomial $C_p = a + bT + cT^2$ in J/mol·K)

| Species | $a$ | $b \times 10^3$ | $c \times 10^6$ | Valid range (K) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| N₂ (g) | 27.32 | 6.226 | –0.9502 | 273 – 1800 |
| H₂ (g) | 26.88 | 4.347 | –0.3265 | 273 – 1800 |
| NH₃ (g) | 27.55 | 25.99 | 0.5576 | 273 – 1500 |

### Step 3 — Compute $\Delta C_p$ for the reaction $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3$

$$\Delta C_p = 2 C_{p,NH_3} - C_{p,N_2} - 3 C_{p,H_2}$$

Substituting the polynomial form:

$$\Delta C_p = 2(a_{NH_3} + b_{NH_3}T + c_{NH_3}T^2) - (a_{N_2} + b_{N_2}T + c_{N_2}T^2) - 3(a_{H_2} + b_{H_2}T + c_{H_2}T^2)$$

Collecting coefficients:

$$\Delta a = 2(27.55) - 27.32 - 3(26.88) = 55.10 - 27.32 - 80.64 = -52.86$$

$$\Delta b = 2(25.99 \times 10^{-3}) - 6.226 \times 10^{-3} - 3(4.347 \times 10^{-3}) = 51.98 - 6.226 - 13.041 = 32.713 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta c = 2(0.5576 \times 10^{-6}) - (-0.9502 \times 10^{-6}) - 3(-0.3265 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.1152 + 0.9502 + 0.9795 = 3.0449 \times 10^{-6}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta C_p = -52.86 + 32.713 \times 10^{-3} T + 3.0449 \times 10^{-6} T^2 \quad \text{J/mol·K}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from 298 K to T

$$\int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT = \Delta a (T - 298) + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2}(T^2 - 298^2) + \dfrac{\Delta c}{3}(T^3 - 298^3)$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$\int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT = -52.86 (T-298) + 16.357 \times 10^{-3}(T^2 - 88804) + 1.015 \times 10^{-6}(T^3 - 26463592)$$

### Step 5 — Add the standard-state heat of reaction

$\Delta H_{298} = -46.22$ kJ/mol (per mol NH₃ basis is sometimes quoted; here we use per mole of reaction as written, $2 \text{ NH}_3$).

$$\boxed{\Delta H_T = -92200 - 52.86(T-298) + 16.357 \times 10^{-3}(T^2 - 88804) + 1.015 \times 10^{-6}(T^3 - 26463592) \quad \text{J}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical check at 700 K (typical converter temperature)

- $T - 298 = 402$
- $T^2 - 88804 = 490000 - 88804 = 401196$
- $T^3 - 26463592 = 3.43 \times 10^{8} - 2.646 \times 10^{7} = 3.166 \times 10^{8}$

Substituting:

$$\Delta H_{700} = -92200 - 52.86(402) + 16.357 \times 10^{-3}(401196) + 1.015 \times 10^{-6}(3.166 \times 10^{8})$$

$$\Delta H_{700} = -92200 - 21250 + 6562 + 321.4 \approx -106567 \text{ J per 2 mol NH}_3$$

That is $\approx -53.28$ kJ/mol NH₃ — a more exothermic value than the 298 K reference, because the products (NH₃) have a higher heat capacity than the reactants at this temperature. **This shift must be incorporated into converter thermal design.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Mole Fraction in a Stoichiometric Feed

Let the initial syngas be stoichiometric (i.e., $y_{N_2} = 0.25$, $y_{H_2} = 0.75$, no inerts, no NH₃). Let $\xi$ be the *fractional approach to equilibrium* (i.e., the extent of reaction divided by the maximum possible). The mole balance is:

| Species | Initial moles | Change | Equilibrium moles |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| N₂ | 1 | $-\xi$ | $1 - \xi$ |
| H₂ | 3 | $-3\xi$ | $3 - 3\xi$ |
| NH₃ | 0 | $+2\xi$ | $2\xi$ |
| **Total** | **4** | $-2\xi$ | **$4 - 2\xi$** |

Mole fractions (assuming ideal gas, partial pressure $P_i = y_i P$):

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{3-3\xi}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{NH_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}$$

Insert into the equilibrium expression:

$$K_P = \dfrac{y_{NH_3} P}{\left(y_{N_2} P\right)^{0.5} \left(y_{H_2} P\right)^{1.5}}$$

Simplifying (the $P$ terms combine as $P^{-1}$, because total pressure in the denominator is $0.5 + 1.5 = 2$, numerator is 1, net exponent is $-1$):

$$K_P = \dfrac{1}{P} \cdot \dfrac{y_{NH_3}}{y_{N_2}^{0.5} y_{H_2}^{1.5}}$$

Substituting mole fractions:

$$K_P = \dfrac{1}{P} \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)^{0.5} \left(\dfrac{3-3\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)^{1.5}}$$

The denominator factor $(4-2\xi)$ cancels as $(4-2\xi)^{-(0.5+1.5-1)} = (4-2\xi)^{-1}$, leaving:

$$K_P = \dfrac{1}{P} \cdot \dfrac{2\xi (4-2\xi)}{(1-\xi)^{0.5}(3-3\xi)^{1.5}}$$

Factoring $3-3\xi = 3(1-\xi)$ and pulling out constants:

$$(3-3\xi)^{1.5} = 3^{1.5}(1-\xi)^{1.5}$$

Hence:

$$K_P P = \dfrac{2\xi (4-2\xi)}{3^{1.5} (1-\xi)^{0.5} (1-\xi)^{1.5}} = \dfrac{2\xi (4-2\xi)}{3^{1.5} (1-\xi)^{2}}$$

Simplifying $4 - 2\xi = 2(2-\xi)$ and $2\xi(4-2\xi) = 4\xi(2-\xi)$:

$$\boxed{K_P P = \dfrac{4\xi(2-\xi)}{3^{1.5}(1-\xi)^2}}$$

### Numerical check at 200 bar and 700 K

From the empirical correlation, $\log_{10} K_P(700) \approx -2.043$, so $K_P \approx 9.05 \times 10^{-3}$ bar$^{-1}$.

Plug into the boxed equation:

$$9.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 1.810 = \frac{4\xi(2-\xi)}{5.196 (1-\xi)^2}$$

Let $f(\xi) = 4\xi(2-\xi) - 1.810 \times 5.196 (1-\xi)^2$. Iterating:

- $\xi = 0.20$: $f = 1.44 - 7.53 = -6.09$
- $\xi = 0.50$: $f = 3.0 - 2.35 = +0.65$
- $\xi = 0.47$: $f = 2.878 - 2.611 = +0.267$
- $\xi = 0.45$: $f = 2.79 - 2.85 = -0.06$ ✓

So the **equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃** is:

$$y_{NH_3}^{eq} = \frac{2 \xi}{4 - 2\xi} = \frac{0.90}{3.10} = 0.290 \quad \text{or about } 29\%$$

This single-pass equilibrium number (29 %) sets the upper bound. Real converters reach 18 – 22 % per pass due to kinetics; the gap is closed by **gas recycle** in the synthesis loop.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Converter Types)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *heart of the KTU Module 2 syllabus*. Memorise the four categories and the representative designs.

| S.No. | Classification Basis | Sub-Type | Cooling Method | Representative Design | Catalyst Beds | Heat Recovery |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bed arrangement | Adiabatic multi-bed | Quench (cold-shot) | Nielson (imperial chemical) | 3 – 4 | Low |
| 2 | Bed arrangement | Adiabatic multi-bed | Indirect heat exchange | Haber–Bosch (original) | 3 + interchanger | Moderate |
| 3 | Bed arrangement | Quasi-isothermal | Boiler feed-water in tubes | TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority) | Multiple annular | High |
| 4 | Bed arrangement | Isothermal | Bayonet tube cooler | Lurgi isothermal | Single large bed | Very high |
| 5 | Catalyst geometry | Radial-flow (cross-flow) | Quench + indirect | Topsoe S-200, S-300 | 2 – 3 | Moderate |
| 6 | Catalyst geometry | Axial-flow | Quench | Casale, Kellog | 3 – 4 | Moderate |
| 7 | Bed arrangement | Horizontal | Indirect | Brown & Root (B&R) | 3 | High |
| 8 | Pressure rating | High-pressure (200-300 bar) | Mixed | Most modern designs | Multi-bed | Optimised |
| 9 | Pressure rating | Low-pressure (100-150 bar) | Indirect | Older Fauser-Montecatini | Multi-bed | High |
| 10 | Capacity | Single-train large | Quench + indirect | Kellog KAAP, Topsoe | 2 – 4 | Very high |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Haber–Bosch ammonia converter. List the operating conditions used.*

#### Model Answer (3-mark pattern: Definition + Diagram + Conditions)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The Haber–Bosch converter is a fixed-bed catalytic reactor in which nitrogen and hydrogen react at high pressure and moderate temperature over a promoted iron catalyst to form ammonia.
2. **Construction (1 mark):** It contains a catalyst basket divided into several beds, surrounded by a heat exchanger. Cold inlet gas first passes through the annular space of the exchanger, is pre-heated, then enters the catalyst beds from the top, and finally leaves through a central catalyst tube. (Sketch: cylindrical pressure vessel with external exchanger.)
3. **Working (0.5 mark):** The reaction is exothermic; heat released in the catalyst raises the gas temperature, and the heat exchanger preheats the incoming gas.
4. **Operating conditions (0.5 mark):** Pressure **200 – 300 atm**; temperature **400 – 500 °C**; promoted iron catalyst; syngas ratio H₂:N₂ = 3:1.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**
*List the different types of ammonia converters used in industry based on catalyst bed arrangement and heat-removal strategy.*

#### Model Answer

- **Based on catalyst bed arrangement:** (i) Single-bed, (ii) Multi-bed adiabatic, (iii) Quasi-isothermal (tubular), (iv) Isothermal (with bayonet cooler).
- **Based on heat-removal strategy:** (i) **Quench-cooled** (cold-shot injection between beds) and (ii) **Indirect-cooled** (heat-exchanger between beds).
- **Examples:** Nielson, Haber–Bosch, Casale, Kellog, TVA, Topsoe S-200/S-300, Lurgi isothermal, Fauser-Montecatini, ICI, AMV.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q.II(b)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify ammonia converters in detail, with neat sketches of the following: (i) Haber–Bosch converter, (ii) Nielson converter, and (iii) TVA converter. Compare the three on the basis of (1) number of catalyst beds, (2) method of temperature control, (3) heat-recovery efficiency, and (4) typical single-pass conversion.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an ammonia synthesis converter operating at **200 bar** and **700 K** with a stoichiometric syngas feed, calculate the equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃. Given that the equilibrium constant $K_P(700 \text{ K}) = 9.05 \times 10^{-3}$ bar$^{-1}$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Classification overview (2 marks):** Ammonia converters may be classified as:
- Adiabatic multi-bed (quench or indirect cooled)
- Quasi-isothermal (TVA type)
- Isothermal (Lurgi)
- Radial-flow (Topsoe S-200/S-300)

**Sketch description and key points (3 marks):**
- *Haber–Bosch:* Cylindrical pressure vessel with internal heat exchanger; catalyst held in a basket with an annular cold-inlet passage.
- *Nielson:* Three catalyst beds separated by cold-shot inlets; cold syngas injected to lower temperature between beds.
- *TVA:* Three concentric annular catalyst beds with boiler-feed-water cooling coils between them — quasi-isothermal.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Haber–Bosch | Nielson | TVA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Catalyst beds | Multiple (3 + annular) | 3 with quench points | 3 annular concentric |
| Temperature control | Indirect heat exchange | Direct cold-shot injection | Indirect BFW coils (quasi-isothermal) |
| Heat recovery | Moderate (process gas pre-heat) | Low (cold shots waste BFW duty) | High (steam generation) |
| Single-pass NH₃ | 16 – 18 % | 14 – 16 % | 20 – 22 % |

**(b) [7 marks] — Step-by-step**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Stoichiometric balance as derived in §3.2.
Let $\xi$ = fractional extent; total moles = $4 - 2\xi$; $y_{NH_3} = \dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Equilibrium condition.

$$K_P P = \frac{4 \xi (2-\xi)}{3^{1.5} (1-\xi)^2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $K_P = 9.05 \times 10^{-3}$ bar$^{-1}$ and $P = 200$ bar:

$$9.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 1.810 = \frac{4 \xi (2-\xi)}{5.196 (1-\xi)^2}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Iterative / numerical solution.

Try $\xi = 0.45$:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{4(0.45)(1.55)}{5.196 (0.55)^2} = \frac{2.79}{1.572} = 1.775 \quad (\text{close to 1.810})$$

Try $\xi = 0.46$:

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{4(0.46)(1.54)}{5.196 (0.54)^2} = \frac{2.834}{1.515} = 1.870 \quad (\text{slightly high})$$

Interpolate: $\xi \approx 0.453$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute equilibrium mole fraction.

$$y_{NH_3}^{eq} = \frac{2(0.453)}{4 - 2(0.453)} = \frac{0.906}{3.094} \approx 0.293$$

**Answer (1 mark):** The equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃ is **≈ 0.293 or 29.3 mol%** at the given conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q.II(a)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a process-flow diagram, explain the working of a **quench-type multi-bed ammonia converter**. Discuss the role of cold-shot injection in controlling the temperature profile. State two advantages and two limitations of this design.

**(b) [7 marks]** The heat of ammonia-synthesis reaction at 298 K is **−46.22 kJ/mol** (per mole of NH₃). Using the Kirchhoff equation, estimate the heat of reaction at **800 K** and comment on its implication for converter design. Use the following mean heat capacities between 298 K and 800 K: $\bar{C}_{p, NH_3} = 41.0$ J/mol·K, $\bar{C}_{p, N_2} = 29.6$ J/mol·K, $\bar{C}_{p, H_2} = 28.9$ J/mol·K.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Process description (2 marks):** Syngas enters at the top of the converter, passes through the first catalyst bed where the exothermic reaction raises its temperature, and then enters a *cold-shot mixer* where a portion of the cold inlet gas is injected. The mixture is cooled and re-distributed into the next bed, and so on. After the last bed, NH₃-rich gas leaves and is sent to a waste-heat boiler and downstream cooling.

**Role of cold-shot (2 marks):** Cold-shot injection performs *two* coupled duties: (1) it **absorbs the reaction heat** by sensible cooling, lowering the gas temperature before the next bed, and (2) it **increases the inlet flow** to the next bed, lowering the per-pass conversion target and providing an additional lever to control $\xi$.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Simple mechanical design; no internal heat-exchanger coils subject to high-pressure differential stress.
2. Flexible turndown ratio — the number of active beds and cold-shot flows can be adjusted to match load.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Heat is *not* recovered at a useful temperature level — it is dumped into the cold bypass gas, lowering overall plant energy efficiency.
2. Cold-shot streams dilute the reacting mixture, lowering the per-pass conversion and increasing the recycle compressor duty.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Apply Kirchhoff's law with mean heat capacities.

$$\Delta H_{800} = \Delta H_{298} + \int_{298}^{800} \Delta C_p \, dT \approx \Delta H_{298} + \overline{\Delta C_p} (800 - 298)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute $\overline{\Delta C_p}$ for the reaction (per 2 mol NH₃ basis as written):

$$\Delta C_p = 2 \bar{C}_{p, NH_3} - \bar{C}_{p, N_2} - 3 \bar{C}_{p, H_2} = 2(41.0) - 29.6 - 3(28.9) = 82.0 - 29.6 - 86.7 = -34.3 \text{ J/K (per 2 mol NH}_3)$$

Per mole of NH₃:

$$\Delta \bar{C}_p = -17.15 \text{ J/mol·K}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Substitute values.

$$\Delta H_{800} = -46.22 \text{ kJ/mol} + (-17.15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/mol·K}) (800 - 298) \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta H_{800} = -46.22 - 17.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 502 = -46.22 - 8.61 = -54.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Implication for converter design.
- The reaction is **more exothermic at 800 K** (about 19 % more heat release per mole NH₃).
- For a fixed production rate, the heat to be removed per catalyst bed increases with operating temperature.
- This **reinforces the need for effective inter-bed cooling** — indirect-cooled or multi-bed designs become thermodynamically essential, not merely kinetic conveniences.
- Excessive temperature rise also shifts equilibrium adversely — hence modern designs trend toward *radial-flow* and *quasi-isothermal* converters that hold temperature close to the kinetic optimum.

**Answer (1 mark):** $\Delta H_{800} \approx -54.8$ kJ/mol NH₃; the increased exothermicity at higher temperatures mandates strong inter-stage heat removal in converter design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "per mole of NH₃" vs "per mole of reaction"** — the heat of reaction is sometimes given per mole of NH₃ and sometimes per 2 moles (as written). Always state the basis clearly; otherwise the numerical answer is halved or doubled.
> 2. **Forgetting the inerts in the mole-balance derivation.** Industrial syngas contains 0.5 – 2 % CH₄ + Ar. If the problem does not say "pure stoichiometric feed," include the inert term or you will get the conversion wrong.
> 3. **Sketching converters with the wrong flow direction.** A Haber–Bosch has *top-down* gas flow inside the catalyst, but the *heat-exchanger side* flows counter-currently. Examiners expect a counter-current indication.
> 4. **Writing "ammonia synthesis is endothermic."** It is **strongly exothermic**. This is a *single-sentence* error that costs 1 mark instantly.
> 5. **Equilibrium constant unit omission.** $K_P$ has units of bar$^{-1}$ for this reaction; mentioning the unit is worth 0.5 mark in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Mixing up quench and indirect cooling** — quench is *direct* (cold gas mixed); indirect is *heat-exchanger*. Examiners love to test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core reaction:** $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$, $\Delta H = -46.22$ kJ/mol (per mol NH₃ basis) at 298 K, becomes ≈ −54.8 kJ/mol at 800 K.
- **Operating window:** 200 – 300 bar, 400 – 500 °C, promoted iron catalyst.
- **Why multi-bed:** Single-pass conversion is only ~ 15 – 22 %; multi-bed with inter-stage cooling pushes overall conversion above 95 % with recycle.
- **Primary classification axis:** *Heat removal strategy* → **Quench (direct)** vs **Indirect (heat-exchanger)**.
- **Secondary classification axis:** *Catalyst bed arrangement* → **Adiabatic** / **Quasi-isothermal** / **Isothermal**.
- **Tertiary classification axis:** *Flow direction* → **Axial-flow** / **Radial-flow** / **Cross-flow**.
- **Famous designs to memorise:**
  - **Nielson** — quench-cooled, 3 beds.
  - **Haber–Bosch** — original indirect-cooled with internal exchanger.
  - **TVA** — quasi-isothermal, BFW-cooled annular beds.
  - **Topsoe S-200 / S-300** — radial-flow, modern.
  - **Lurgi** — isothermal, bayonet cooler, high heat recovery.
  - **Casale** — axial-radial mixed, axial-radially distributed flow.
  - **Kellog** — large single-train, 2 – 4 beds.
  - **Fauser-Montecatini** — older, lower-pressure design.
- **Key equations to retain:**
  - $K_P = \dfrac{P_{NH_3}}{(P_{N_2})^{0.5}(P_{H_2})^{1.5}}$
  - Van 't Hoff: $\dfrac{d \ln K_P}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$
  - Kirchhoff: $\Delta H_T = \Delta H_{298} + \int \Delta C_p \, dT$
  - Ergun equation for $\Delta P$ across catalyst bed.
- **Quench vs indirect comparison points (always expected in 14-mark answers):**
  - Quench: simple, flexible, low heat recovery, higher recycle.
  - Indirect: complex, efficient heat recovery, smaller recycle, higher mechanical integrity demands.
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** (i) sign of $\Delta H$, (ii) basis of $\Delta H$, (iii) units of $K_P$, (iv) ignoring inerts, (v) wrong counter-current direction in sketches.
