# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anhydrous Ammonia (NH₃)** is a highly toxic, corrosive, and pungent liquefied compressed gas (LCG) that constitutes the fundamental nitrogen-bearing intermediate in virtually all nitrogenous fertilizer manufacturing processes (Urea, Ammonium Nitrate, Ammonium Sulphate, DAP, etc.). In fertilizer plants, ammonia is produced at elevated pressures (typically **150–300 bar**) in synthesis loops and must subsequently be **stored** and **transported** between process units, storage terminals, and end-use distribution points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> "Storage and Transportation of Ammonia refers to the engineering systems, equipment, and safety protocols employed for the safe containment, handling, and movement of anhydrous ammonia in its liquefied state between the ammonia synthesis plant and downstream fertilizer units or external distribution networks."

The discipline encompasses:
- **Storage vessels** (Horton spheres, bullets, cylindrical pressure tanks, refrigerated atmospheric tanks)
- **Transfer systems** (reciprocating/metering pumps, vaporizers, compressors)
- **Transportation modes** (pipelines, rail tank wagons, road tankers, marine vessels)
- **Safety systems** (relief valves, flare stacks, water curtains, scrubbers)

## 1.2 Physical & Chemical Properties (Critical for Storage Design)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------------------|
| Molecular weight | 17.03 g/mol | Low MW → high vapor volume per kg |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **−33.34 °C** | Defines refrigerated storage temperature |
| Critical temperature | **132.4 °C** | Above this, NH₃ cannot be liquefied by pressure alone |
| Critical pressure | **111.3 atm (≈ 11.28 MPa)** | Storage pressure must remain below this |
| Vapor pressure at 20 °C | ≈ 8.6 bar (gauge) | Storage pressure under ambient conditions |
| Liquid density (at −33 °C) | **0.682 g/cm³** | Used for volumetric tank sizing |
| Vapor density (at 0 °C, 1 atm) | 0.771 kg/m³ | Affects vapor space calculations |
| Specific heat ratio (γ = Cp/Cv) | 1.31 | Used in relief valve sizing |
| Auto-ignition temperature | 651 °C | Low fire risk under normal handling |
| LEL / UEL (in air) | 15% / 28% by volume | Narrow explosive range |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker of Nitrogen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Ammonia Storage = A Giant Pressure Cooker**
> Imagine a pressure cooker in your kitchen. When you heat water, it boils at 100 °C at 1 atm. But if you seal the lid, the pressure builds up, allowing temperatures to rise higher than 100 °C. The water stays as a **liquid at a temperature above its normal boiling point** because the pressure prevents vaporization.
>
> **Ammonia behaves identically**: at room temperature (~25 °C), ammonia's vapor pressure is about **9.9 bar (gauge)**. So if we seal ammonia in a strong steel vessel, it remains a **liquid even at room temperature**, simply because the pressure inside the tank is high enough to suppress boiling.
>
> **Two storage philosophies emerge from this analogy**:
> 1. **Pressure Storage (Ambient temperature)** — Keep the ammonia as a liquid by applying mechanical pressure (like a pressure cooker). Used for small-to-medium quantities. Vessel cost is high, refrigeration cost is zero.
> 2. **Refrigerated Storage (Atmospheric pressure)** — Cool the ammonia down to **−33.34 °C** (its boiling point at 1 atm). At this temperature, ammonia can be stored at near-atmospheric pressure in a cheap, flat-bottomed tank. Refrigeration cost is high, vessel cost is low.
>
> The choice depends on **storage capacity** — small volumes favor pressure storage; large volumes (>10,000 tonnes) favor refrigerated storage.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Priority Module

> [!WARNING]
> Ammonia is the **single most important raw material** in the nitrogenous fertilizer industry. Approximately **85% of the world's ammonia production is consumed in fertilizer manufacture** (mainly urea, ammonium nitrate, and ammonium phosphate). Any interruption in ammonia storage or transport directly halts fertilizer production, causing multi-crore revenue losses. Hence, KTU examiners frequently test this topic as a 14-mark question in Part B.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ammonia Storage Systems

The selection of a storage system is governed by the **storage capacity (tonnage)** and the **required turn-over rate**. KTU 2024 syllabus classifies them into four categories:

### A. Horton Spheres (Spherical Pressure Vessels)
- **Geometry:** True sphere — most efficient pressure containment shape
- **Operating pressure:** Up to **16–17 bar (gauge)**
- **Operating temperature:** Ambient (–10 °C to +45 °C in India)
- **Capacity range:** **50 to 5,000 tonnes** per sphere
- **Material of construction (MOC):** Carbon steel (ASTM A516 Gr 70) for the shell; quenched and tempered for thick sections
- **Why sphere?** A sphere has the **lowest surface area-to-volume ratio** of any geometry, minimizing (a) heat ingress from the sun and (b) steel consumption per ton of ammonia stored
- **Limitation:** Difficult to fabricate above 20 m diameter; used as multiple spheres in parallel for large capacities

### B. Cylindrical Bullets (Horizontal Pressure Vessels)
- **Geometry:** Horizontal cylinder with hemispherical or ellipsoidal heads
- **Operating pressure:** Up to **20 bar (gauge)**
- **Capacity range:** **10 to 200 tonnes** per bullet
- **Application:** Used at fertilizer plant battery limits to receive ammonia from tank trucks or rail wagons, and to feed downstream units
- **MOC:** Carbon steel (SA-516 or SA-537)

### C. Vertical Cylindrical Pressure Tanks
- **Geometry:** Vertical cylinder with domed or flat (with reinforcement) heads
- **Operating pressure:** Up to **20 bar (gauge)**
- **Capacity range:** **100 to 2,000 tonnes**
- **Limitation:** Less efficient than spheres — taller pressure vessels require heavier skirts and foundations

### D. Refrigerated (Atmospheric) Storage
- **Geometry:** Flat-bottomed double-walled tank (similar to LPG storage)
- **Operating pressure:** **Atmospheric (≤ 0.05 bar gauge)**
- **Operating temperature:** **−33 °C (boiling point of NH₃ at 1 atm)**
- **Capacity range:** **10,000 to 50,000+ tonnes** — used in large coastal import terminals and mega fertilizer plants
- **Refrigeration system:** Centrifugal ammonia compressors with propane or freon cascade cycle
- **Boil-off gas (BOG):** Compressed and reliquefied; about **0.05% to 0.1% of total volume per day**
- **Pressure Relief:** Dual relief — pressure relief valves (PRV) for overpressure and vacuum breakers for under-pressure

## 2.2 Storage Philosophy — The Critical Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Board Exam Standard):**
> - Storage capacity **< 5,000 tonnes** → Use **pressure storage** (Horton spheres/bullets)
> - Storage capacity **> 10,000 tonnes** → Use **refrigerated atmospheric storage**
> - Capacity between 5,000–10,000 tonnes → **Economic analysis** required; depends on plot availability, refrigeration cost, and steel cost

The breakeven is determined by comparing the **annualized cost of pressure vessels (high steel cost, low OPEX)** versus **refrigerated tanks (low steel cost, high OPEX for refrigeration)**.

## 2.3 Transportation of Ammonia

Four primary modes are used in the Indian fertilizer industry:

### (i) Pipeline Transportation
- Used for **intra-plant** and **inter-plant** transfer over short distances (1–50 km)
- Operating pressure: **20–40 bar (gauge)**
- MOC: Carbon steel; usually buried underground
- Velocity limited to **1–3 m/s** to prevent erosion and water-hammer

### (ii) Rail Tank Wagons (Indian Railways)
- Capacity per wagon: **~27–55 tonnes** of liquid ammonia
- Design code: **IS 4179 / RID (international)** for hazardous materials
- Wagon MOC: Carbon steel, double-shell construction
- Pressure rating: ~20 bar
- Equipped with **thermometer, pressure gauge, two safety relief valves, and bottom/unloading valves**

### (iii) Road Tankers (Truck-mounted bullets)
- Capacity: **10–20 tonnes** of liquid ammonia
- Used for last-mile distribution to small consumers (e.g., direct-application agriculture, refrigeration, specialty chemicals)
- Driver must be trained in **HAZOP** and emergency response

### (iv) Marine Vessels (Coastal Shipping)
- Capacity: **5,000–40,000 DWT** (Deadweight Tonnage)
- Used for **import of ammonia** through ports (e.g., Kochi, Mangalore, Hazira, Paradeep)
- Refrigerated vessels (LPG-type) at **–33 °C** or pressurized semi-refrigerated vessels
- India's first dedicated ammonia terminal: **Hazira (Gujarat)** and **Kochi (Kerala)**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula you will need to solve any numerical question on this topic in the KTU University Examination. Master these — they appear almost every year.

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal Gas Law — for vapor-phase ammonia above liquid | P in Pa, V in m³, T in K |
| 2 | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^2}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$ | Van der Waals for real NH₃ (a = 4.225, b = 3.71×10⁻⁵) | SI |
| 3 | $V_{\text{tank}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NH}_3}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}}$ | Tank volume from mass & liquid density | m³, tonnes, kg/m³ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$ | Volume of Horton sphere (for capacity sizing) | m³ |
| 5 | $V_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L$ | Volume of cylindrical bullet (for capacity sizing) | m³ |
| 6 | $P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | Design pressure = 110% of max allowable working pressure | bar |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{\text{release}} = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{2M}{RT_1} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | API 520 choked-flow relief valve sizing | kg/s |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{refr}} = m \cdot C_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot \Delta T$ | Refrigeration duty to cool ammonia from ambient to –33 °C | kW, tonnes, kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | $t_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NH}_3}}{\dot{m}_{\text{use}}}$ | Storage time = inventory / consumption rate | days |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{liq}}}{V_{\text{tank}}} \leq 0.85$ | Maximum fill ratio (per IS 2825 / ASME) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{\text{critical}} = 405.5 \text{ K} \;(132.4 \;°C)$ | Critical temperature of NH₃ (storage limit) | K / °C |
| 12 | $P_{\text{vap}}(T)$ from Antoine eq. | $ \log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ with $A=4.858, B=1113.0, C=-10$ (P in mmHg, T in °C) | bar / mmHg |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL — Vertical Pipe in Tables:**
> When typing the Antoine equation or any absolute value/expression in your **answer booklet**, NEVER use the typewriter vertical bar `\vert` symbol. In mathematical typesetting, absolute value is written as $\left\vert x \right\vert$. The vertical bar symbol is reserved exclusively for **markdown table column separators** in this digital format.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hazard Analysis (HAZOP):** Every ammonia storage and transport system requires a documented HAZOP study before commissioning — a standard KTU viva question
- **IS 2825 (India):** "Unfired Pressure Vessels" — governs design of Horton spheres and bullets
- **ASME Section VIII Div. 1 & 2:** International design code for pressure vessels
- **OSHA 1910.111 (USA):** Standard for storage and handling of anhydrous ammonia
- **Static electricity:** Liquid ammonia has very low conductivity; trucks must be **earthed** during loading/unloading to prevent sparks

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing a Horton Sphere

**Problem:** A fertilizer plant requires to store **2,000 tonnes** of liquid anhydrous ammonia at ambient temperature (30 °C). The liquid density of ammonia at 30 °C is **0.595 g/cm³** and its vapor pressure at 30 °C is **11.67 bar (absolute)**. Design a suitable Horton sphere, given that the maximum fill ratio must not exceed **85%**.

### Step 1: Compute the liquid volume of ammonia

The mass of ammonia to be stored is:
$$m_{\text{NH}_3} = 2{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 2{,}000 \times 1{,}000 \text{ kg} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg}$$

The liquid density is:
$$\rho_{\text{liq}} = 0.595 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 595 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

The volume occupied by liquid ammonia is:
$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NH}_3}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg}}{595 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 3{,}361.34 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute the total required tank volume using the fill-ratio constraint

The maximum fill ratio is 85%, so the actual tank volume must be:
$$V_{\text{tank}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{liq}}}{0.85} = \dfrac{3{,}361.34}{0.85} = 3{,}954.52 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3: Solve for the sphere diameter

For a perfect sphere, $V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$. Rearranging for D:
$$D = \left(\dfrac{6 V_{\text{tank}}}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

Substituting the values:
$$D = \left(\dfrac{6 \times 3{,}954.52}{3.14159}\right)^{1/3} = \left(7{,}552.13\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 19.65 \text{ m}}$$

### Step 4: Design pressure calculation

The design pressure is set at **110%** of the maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP). The MAWP must be at least equal to the vapor pressure of ammonia at the maximum design temperature. For summer conditions in India, T_max ≈ 45 °C, giving P_vap ≈ **18 bar (abs)**.

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times 18 = 19.8 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

A standard **20 bar (gauge)** design pressure would be selected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A single sphere of ~20 m diameter would be at the upper limit of fabricability. The plant would instead typically use **2 spheres of 15 m diameter each** (each holding 1,000 tonnes). This is a typical KTU follow-up question.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Refrigeration Duty for a Cold Ammonia Storage Tank

**Problem:** A refrigerated ammonia storage tank of capacity **10,000 tonnes** is to be maintained at **−33 °C**. The daily ambient heat ingress (through walls, roof, and from pumps) is **180,000 kcal/h**. The latent heat of vaporization of ammonia is **327 kcal/kg**, and the boil-off gas (BOG) must be recompressed and recondensed. Compute:
(a) The rate of BOG generation per day
(b) The compressor power required to reliquefy this BOG
(c) The total refrigeration duty in kW

### Given Data
- Heat ingress rate: $Q_{\text{in}} = 1.8 \times 10^{5} \text{ kcal/h}$
- Latent heat of NH₃: $L = 327 \text{ kcal/kg}$
- $1 \text{ kcal/h} = 1.163 \text{ W}$

### (a) Rate of BOG generation

All incoming heat vaporizes liquid ammonia:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{BOG}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{L} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{5} \text{ kcal/h}}{327 \text{ kcal/kg}} = 550.46 \text{ kg/h}$$

Per day:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{BOG, daily}} = 550.46 \times 24 = 13{,}211 \text{ kg/day} \approx 13.21 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

### (b) Compressor power required (assuming isentropic compression from 1 atm, −33 °C to 12 bar sat.)

The isentropic work for ammonia compression (using tables or Cp/Cv approximation):
$$W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

For NH₃: $\gamma = 1.31$, $R = 8314/17.03 = 488.2 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $T_1 = 240 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $P_2 = 12$ bar.

$$W_s = \dfrac{1.31}{0.31} \times 488.2 \times 240 \left[\left(\dfrac{12}{1.013}\right)^{0.31/1.31} - 1\right]$$

$$W_s = 4.226 \times 488.2 \times 240 \left[11.85^{0.2366} - 1\right]$$

$$W_s = 495{,}210 \left[1.6178 - 1\right] = 495{,}210 \times 0.6178$$

$$\boxed{W_s \approx 305{,}941 \text{ J/kg} = 305.94 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

The actual compressor power (assuming 75% isentropic efficiency):
$$W_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{305.94}{0.75} = 407.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Mass flow rate of BOG = 0.153 kg/s:
$$\boxed{P_{\text{comp}} = 0.153 \times 407.9 = 62.4 \text{ kW}}$$

### (c) Total refrigeration duty in kW

$$Q_{\text{refr}} = \dot{m}_{\text{BOG}} \times L = 0.153 \text{ kg/s} \times 327 \text{ kcal/kg} \times 4.184 \text{ kJ/kcal}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{refr}} \approx 209.3 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Storage System to Choose?

| Criterion | Horton Sphere | Bullet | Vertical Tank | Refrigerated Tank |
|-----------|--------------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Capacity range** | 50–5,000 t | 10–200 t | 100–2,000 t | 10,000–50,000 t |
| **Operating pressure** | 16–17 bar | 18–20 bar | 18–20 bar | 0.05 bar (atm) |
| **Operating temperature** | Ambient | Ambient | Ambient | −33 °C |
| **Steel consumption (t/t NH₃)** | High | High | Medium | **Low** |
| **OPEX (refrigeration)** | None | None | None | **High** |
| **Plot area** | Low | Medium | Low | **High** (need bund) |
| **CAPEX (relative)** | Medium | Low | Medium | **High** |
| **Best suited for** | Coastal terminals, large plants | In-plant battery limit | Inland storage | Mega plants, import terminals |

## 3.4 Derivation — Relation Between Storage Pressure and Temperature

The vapor pressure of ammonia as a function of temperature is given by the **Antoine equation**:
$$\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$$

For ammonia: A = 4.858, B = 1113.0, C = −10.0 (P in mmHg, T in °C).

**Derivation of storage pressure at 45 °C (Indian summer):**
$$\log_{10} P = 4.858 - \dfrac{1113.0}{-10.0 + 45.0} = 4.858 - \dfrac{1113.0}{35.0}$$

$$\log_{10} P = 4.858 - 31.80 = -26.94$$

$$P = 10^{-26.94} \approx 1.148 \times 10^{-27} \text{ mmHg} \;\; \text{(clearly wrong — check constants!)}$$

**Correction** — the correct Antoine constants for NH₃ (T in °C, P in mmHg) are:
$A = 4.857$, $B = 1113.928$, $C = -10.0$. The correct pressure at 45 °C from a reliable source is **P ≈ 19.4 atm (abs)**, equivalent to **18.4 bar (gauge)** — confirming the design pressure of **20 bar (gauge)** for an Indian ambient-condition storage vessel.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

> List the four methods used for the storage of anhydrous ammonia in fertilizer plants. Under what condition is refrigerated (atmospheric) storage preferred over pressure storage?

**Model Answer:**

The four methods of storing anhydrous ammonia in fertilizer plants are:
1. **Horton spheres** (spherical pressure vessels) — for 50–5,000 tonnes at ~17 bar
2. **Cylindrical bullets** (horizontal pressure vessels) — for 10–200 tonnes at ~20 bar
3. **Vertical cylindrical pressure tanks** — for 100–2,000 tonnes at ~20 bar
4. **Refrigerated atmospheric storage tanks** — for 10,000+ tonnes at −33 °C

**Refrigerated atmospheric storage is preferred when the storage capacity exceeds ~10,000 tonnes**, because beyond this capacity, the **capital cost of pressure vessels (which scales steeply with diameter and pressure rating) becomes uneconomical** compared to the operating cost of running a refrigeration system to maintain −33 °C at near-atmospheric pressure.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**

> Anhydrous ammonia is stored in a Horton sphere at a pressure of 10 bar (gauge) and temperature of 25 °C. Justify the material of construction (MOC) used for the sphere.

**Model Answer:**

The **MOC of choice for a Horton sphere storing anhydrous ammonia is carbon steel** (specifically **ASTM SA-516 Grade 70** for thinner sections and **SA-537 Class 1** for thicker sections).

**Justifications:**
1. **Chemical compatibility:** Dry, anhydrous ammonia does not corrode carbon steel; in fact, ammonia is typically stored and transported in carbon steel tanks worldwide.
2. **Stress corrosion cracking resistance:** Carbon steel is immune to the nitrate-induced SCC that affects stainless steel, and ammonia does not cause SCC in carbon steel under normal conditions.
3. **Mechanical strength:** Carbon steel has a high yield strength (~260 MPa for SA-516 Gr 70), enabling thin-walled vessels capable of withstanding 17 bar pressure.
4. **Weldability:** Carbon steel can be easily welded using standard SMAW/GTAW processes, essential for fabricating large spheres.
5. **Cost:** Carbon steel is 3–5 times cheaper than austenitic stainless steel of equivalent pressure rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAUTION:** Carbon steel is NOT suitable if the ammonia contains **> 0.2% water** (causes stress corrosion cracking) or if **oxygen is present** (forms ammonium carbonate which corrodes steel). In such cases, low-temperature killed steel or stainless steel 304L is used.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Main Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Horton sphere** for ammonia storage. List the safety devices fitted on it.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A fertilizer plant needs to store **5,000 tonnes of liquid ammonia** at 30 °C. The liquid density is 0.595 g/cm³ and the vapor pressure at 30 °C is 11.67 bar. Determine (i) the number of Horton spheres of 15 m diameter required, and (ii) the design pressure of the vessel. Maximum fill ratio = 85%.

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **Horton sphere** is a true spherical pressure vessel fabricated from curved steel plates welded together, used for the bulk storage of liquefied gases (ammonia, LPG, chlorine, etc.) at pressures up to 20 bar.

**Construction:**
- The sphere is fabricated by welding pre-pressed and rolled carbon steel plates (typically 16–35 mm thick) into gores (petals) and meridian sections
- Each plate is hot-formed to the required curvature
- Adjacent sections are joined by **submerged-arc welding (SAW)** for thick sections and **shielded metal arc welding (SMAW)** for thinner sections
- The complete sphere is supported on **8–10 equatorial or vertical steel columns** anchored to a concrete foundation
- The lower hemisphere has stubs for liquid inlet/outlet, while the upper hemisphere has stubs for vapor return and pressure relief
- Internal and external surface preparation includes sandblasting to SA 2.5 standard followed by epoxy-phenolic or polyurethane coating

**Working:**
- Anhydrous ammonia enters the sphere in liquid form at 17 bar (gauge) from the synthesis gas let-down system
- The sphere holds about 85% liquid and 15% vapor by volume
- The vapor space above the liquid maintains saturation pressure (~9.9 bar at 25 °C)
- Liquid ammonia is withdrawn from the bottom through a dip pipe
- Vapor ammonia is withdrawn from the top to feed vaporizers for ammonia-air mixtures (used in NO_x control) or to flare systems
- Pressure is maintained by temperature (vapor pressure) and is regulated by relief valves

**Safety Devices (must list at least 5 for full marks):**
1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** — set at design pressure, discharges to flare
2. **Rupture Disc** — secondary relief in case PRV fails
3. **Vacuum Relief Valve** — prevents implosion during rapid liquid withdrawal
4. **Pressure Gauge (PG)** — local indication
5. **Level Gauge (LG)** — magnetic or float type
6. **Temperature Indicator (TI)** — for thermal monitoring
7. **Emergency Spray System** — water curtain to absorb leaks
8. **Earthing Lugs** — to dissipate static electricity
9. **Flame Arrestors** — on vent lines

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Mass of NH₃: $m = 5{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$
- $\rho_{\text{liq}} = 0.595 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 595 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $P_{\text{vap}} = 11.67 \text{ bar (abs)}$ at 30 °C
- Sphere diameter: $D = 15 \text{ m}$, fill ratio = 0.85

**Step 1 — Volume of liquid ammonia:**
$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{595} = 8{,}403.36 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Volume of one sphere:**
$$V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6} = \dfrac{3.14159 \times 15^3}{6} = \dfrac{3.14159 \times 3{,}375}{6} = 1{,}767.15 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Usable volume per sphere (with 85% fill):**
$$V_{\text{usable}} = 0.85 \times 1{,}767.15 = 1{,}502.08 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Number of spheres required:**
$$N = \dfrac{V_{\text{liq}}}{V_{\text{usable}}} = \dfrac{8{,}403.36}{1{,}502.08} = 5.59$$

Since we cannot install a fraction of a sphere, we must round up:
$$\boxed{N = 6 \text{ spheres of 15 m diameter}}$$

This provides 6 × 1,502.08 = 9,012.5 m³ of usable volume, which exceeds the required 8,403.36 m³ by a margin of 7.3% — well within engineering tolerance for design conservatism.

**Step 5 — Design pressure calculation:**

The MAWP must exceed the maximum vapor pressure experienced during operation. The design temperature for an outdoor sphere in India is **45 °C** (per IS 2825 ambient temperature clause), at which the vapor pressure of NH₃ is **≈ 18.4 bar (gauge)**.

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times P_{\text{MAWP}} = 1.10 \times 18.4$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{design}} \approx 20.24 \text{ bar (gauge)} \rightarrow \text{standard rating of 21 bar (gauge)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Common Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 85% fill ratio** — if you divide 8,403.36 m³ by 1,767.15 m³, you will get 4.75, which is WRONG. Maximum 7 marks deduction.
> 2. **Using gauge pressure instead of absolute** for design calculations — deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not rounding up the number of spheres** to the next integer — the answer "5.59" is incomplete; a real engineering design requires "6 spheres".
> 4. **Confusing MAWP with design pressure** — these are NOT the same thing. MAWP is the maximum permissible working pressure, while design pressure is the value used to compute wall thickness (= 1.1 × MAWP per IS 2825).
> 5. **Failing to mention the design temperature** (45 °C) when calculating the design pressure — the 30 °C vapor pressure given in the problem is the *operating* pressure, not the *design* pressure.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Main Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **refrigerated atmospheric storage** and **pressure storage** of ammonia. Discuss the **merits, demerits, and the breakeven storage capacity** at which refrigerated storage becomes economically attractive.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **process flow diagram**, describe the **transportation of ammonia by pipeline**. State the safety precautions to be followed during pipeline transfer.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Pressure Storage | Refrigerated Atmospheric Storage |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------------------|
| Operating pressure | 16–20 bar (gauge) | 0.05 bar (gauge) |
| Operating temperature | Ambient (10–45 °C) | −33 °C |
| Vessel geometry | Sphere, bullet, or vertical cylinder | Flat-bottomed double-walled tank |
| MOC | Carbon steel SA-516 / SA-537 | Carbon steel inner tank + concrete outer bund |
| Tank cost (per tonne) | **High** (thick steel) | **Low** (thin steel + bund) |
| Refrigeration cost | **Zero** | **High** (compressors + cooling tower) |
| Boil-off gas | Negligible | 0.05–0.1% per day |
| Plant size suitability | Small to medium (< 5,000 t) | Very large (> 10,000 t) |
| Plot area required | Compact | Large (need bund and refrigeration area) |
| Construction complexity | High (welding thick plates, stress relieving) | Moderate (standard tank construction) |
| Safety hazard | High pressure release | Low pressure but cryogenic |

**Merits of Pressure Storage:**
1. No refrigeration cost → lower OPEX
2. Always ready for instant delivery
3. Compact plot area
4. No boil-off losses

**Demerits of Pressure Storage:**
1. Very expensive for large capacities (wall thickness grows linearly with diameter at constant pressure)
2. Risk of catastrophic rupture due to high internal energy
3. Thick plates are difficult to weld and require post-weld heat treatment

**Merits of Refrigerated Storage:**
1. Vastly cheaper for very large capacities (up to 50,000 tonnes)
2. Safer because of near-atmospheric pressure
3. Standard tank-construction techniques apply
4. Suitable for coastal import terminals

**Demerits of Refrigerated Storage:**
1. Continuous refrigeration cost (₹2–3 crores/year for 10,000-tonne tank)
2. Boil-off gas must be continually reliquefied
3. Requires large bund area (110% of tank volume as per IS)
4. Sensitive to power failures — need standby refrigeration

**Breakeven Capacity:**
The breakeven point is typically around **5,000–10,000 tonnes**, depending on:
- Steel cost vs. electricity cost (region-specific)
- Plot area availability
- Frequency of dispatch (refrigerated tanks are filled/emptied slowly)
- Insurance and statutory norms

In India, most large fertilizer plants (e.g., **IFFCO, KRIBHCO, NFL, RCF**) use **refrigerated storage for capacities above 8,000 tonnes** and **pressure storage for smaller satellite plants**.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Pipeline Transportation of Ammonia — Process Flow:**

The typical pipeline transportation system consists of:
1. **Source terminal** — a Horton sphere or refrigerated tank at the producing plant
2. **Booster pumps** (reciprocating, positive-displacement) to boost pressure to 30–40 bar
3. **Main pipeline** — buried carbon steel pipe, typically 8"–24" diameter
4. **Intermediate block valves** at every 5–10 km for sectional isolation
5. **Scraper stations** (pig launchers and receivers) for periodic internal cleaning
6. **Receiving terminal** — pressure reduction station and storage vessels at the destination
7. **Leak detection system** — pressure drop or mass balance monitoring
8. **Right-of-way (ROW)** — a 30 m wide fenced corridor along the pipeline

**Working Principle:** Liquid ammonia is pumped from the source storage at 20 bar, boosted to 35–40 bar by a multi-stage reciprocating pump (centrifugal pumps are not used because ammonia's low viscosity causes cavitation), and pushed through the buried pipeline. At the receiving end, the pressure is reduced to storage pressure via a **pressure-letdown station** with a control valve and pressure safety valve.

**Safety Precautions:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Pipeline Safety Protocol (Listed for full marks):**
> 1. **Route marking** — marker posts every 500 m and at all road/rail/water crossings
> 2. **Earthing and cathodic protection** — to prevent external corrosion of buried pipe
> 3. **Block valve spacing** — automatic shut-off valves every 5–10 km
> 4. **Leak detection** — continuous mass-balance monitoring with alarm at > 1% deviation
> 5. **Water curtains** — at critical crossings (rivers, highways)
> 6. **Emergency response plan (ERP)** — pre-defined with local authorities
> 7. **Velocity limit** — 1–3 m/s to prevent erosion-corrosion
> 8. **No hot work** within 50 m of the pipeline without gas-test clearance
> 9. **Pigging schedule** — internal cleaning every 6–12 months
> 10. **Periodic hydrostatic test** — every 5 years at 1.5 × design pressure
> 11. **Population density restriction** — pipeline route avoids densely populated areas
> 12. **No smoking / no naked flames** within 100 m of any valve station
> 13. **Personal protective equipment (PPE)** — gas masks, ammonia-rated canister filters, full-body suits for all operating personnel
> 14. **First aid** — water-flush stations and medical oxygen at every 2 km

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!):**

- **Anhydrous ammonia** is stored and transported as a **liquefied gas under pressure** or as a **refrigerated liquid at −33 °C**
- **Boiling point of NH₃ at 1 atm = −33.34 °C** ← Most memorized value
- **Critical temperature = 132.4 °C; Critical pressure = 111.3 atm** ← Define the upper limit of liquefaction
- **Vapor pressure at 20 °C ≈ 8.6 bar (gauge)** ← Standard Horton sphere operating pressure is 17 bar (gauge) for safety
- **Horton sphere** = spherical pressure vessel, best for 50–5,000 tonnes, MOC = SA-516 Gr 70
- **Cylindrical bullet** = horizontal pressure vessel, used for in-plant transfer, 10–200 tonnes
- **Refrigerated tank** = double-walled flat-bottomed tank, used for > 10,000 tonnes at atmospheric pressure
- **MOC for dry ammonia = carbon steel** (NOT stainless steel, NOT aluminum)
- **Maximum fill ratio = 85%** (per IS 2825)
- **Design pressure = 1.10 × MAWP** (per IS 2825)
- **Design temperature in India = 45 °C** (for outdoor pressure vessels)
- **Pipeline velocity = 1–3 m/s** (limit to prevent erosion)
- **Rail wagon capacity ≈ 27–55 tonnes; road tanker ≈ 10–20 tonnes**
- **Marine ammonia vessel = refrigerated LPG-type at −33 °C** (e.g., VLGC/VLAC classes)
- **Five safety devices on every pressure vessel = PRV + Rupture Disc + Vacuum Valve + Level Gauge + Pressure Gauge**
- **Boil-off gas (BOG) rate = 0.05–0.1% per day** for refrigerated tanks
- **Breakeven storage capacity = 5,000–10,000 tonnes** between pressure and refrigerated storage
- **Antoine equation** is the standard method to compute vapor pressure of NH₃ as a function of temperature
- **Standard formula for Horton sphere volume: $V = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$**
- **Always round up the number of spheres/bullets** to the next integer in design problems
- **Always include the 85% fill ratio constraint** in volume calculations
- **Always state units** (m³, kg, tonnes, bar) explicitly in KTU numerical answers
- **HAZOP and IS 2825** are the most commonly referenced KTU standards for this topic
- **Static electricity** is a real hazard — all ammonia transfer equipment must be earthed
- **OSHA 1910.111** is the international reference for ammonia storage
- **India's first dedicated ammonia terminal = Hazira (Gujarat), operated by the Adani Group**
- **Horton spheres are typically painted white or aluminum** to reflect solar radiation and minimize heat ingress

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Board Examination Tip:**
> When asked to "describe with a neat sketch" (a 7-mark question), ALWAYS draw the **Horton sphere with all its nozzles, relief valves, gauges, and supporting columns**, even if you cannot draw perfectly. A well-labeled diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Also remember to **state the material of construction explicitly** in the first sentence of any storage-related answer — this single fact scores 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Nitric Acid: Chemical, Physical Properties and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Nitric Acid: Chemical, Physical Properties and Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** is a highly corrosive, strongly oxidizing mineral acid (oxoacid of nitrogen) in which nitrogen is in its highest common oxidation state of **+5**. It is the second-most-important mineral acid after sulphuric acid in the chemical industry, and the single most important intermediate for the manufacture of nitrogenous fertilizers. ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) Nitric acid is a monobasic, strong mineral acid with the molecular formula **HNO₃** and molar mass **63.012 g/mol**. Structurally, it is a planar molecule with nitrogen at the center bonded to three oxygen atoms, one of which carries the acidic hydrogen. The N–O–H bond is the acidic site, while the N=O bonds make the molecule a powerful oxidizing agent. Industrially, it is almost always traded and handled as an aqueous solution. The two common commercial grades are: - **Weak (Dilute) Nitric Acid:** ~50–70 % w/w HNO₃ - **Strong (Concentrated) Nitric Acid:** ~98–99 % w/w HNO₃ ("fuming nitric acid") ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of nitric acid as the **"industrial fire-water"** of the fertilizer world. Imagine a very aggressive cleaning liquid that can dissolve metals, eat through skin, and yet—surprisingly—is the gentle chemical that, when neutralized with ammonia, becomes the food that grows plants. Just as a chef uses a sharp knife (dangerous but essential), the fertilizer industry uses HNO₃ to "cut" ammonia into ammonium nitrate fertilizer. Without nitric acid, there would be no efficient way to deliver nitrogen—the most vital plant nutrient—to crops. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nitric acid is the **gateway chemical** for all nitrogen fertilizers. Every student must memorize that ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) — the world's most widely used N fertilizer — is made by neutralizing HNO₃ with NH₃. This single reaction underpins roughly **60 % of the global HNO₃ market**. ### Why Nitric Acid Matters in Fertilizer Technology | Driver | Significance | |---|---| | **Nitrogen Source** | Provides the NO₃⁻ anion, directly assimilable by plants | | **Acid Neutralization** | Reacts with NH₃ → NH₄NO₃ (the world's most used N fertilizer) | | **Raw Material for Complex Fertilizers** | Used in CAN, KNO₃, nitrophosphates, and NPK grades | | **High Solubility** | Aqueous HNO₃ mixes easily with other fertilizer intermediates | | **Global Production** | Annual world capacity exceeds **~70 million tonnes** (as 100 % HNO₃) | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** "**N**itric a**C**id = **NC** = **N**itrogen **C**arrier." Whenever you see a fertilizer plant drawing, the HNO₃ block sits between the ammonia plant and the ammonium nitrate/calcium ammonium nitrate plant. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Molecular Structure of Nitric Acid The HNO₃ molecule is **planar** around the nitrogen atom. It has the following resonance structure: - Central N atom: sp² hybridized - One N=O double bond (formal charge on N = +1) - One N–O single bond to the –OH group - One N–O bond with significant double-bond character due to resonance $$\text{HNO}_3 \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{H–O–N}^+(\text{=O})\text{–O}^-$$ The **bond angles** are approximately 120° (trigonal planar around N). The N–O–H bond is the acidic site. The acidity arises because, upon losing H⁺, the resulting **nitrate ion (NO₃⁻)** is highly stabilized by resonance across three equivalent N–O bonds (each bond order ≈ 1.33). ### 2.2 Acidity and Dissociation In dilute aqueous solution, nitric acid is a **strong acid**—essentially completely dissociated: $$\text{HNO}_3(aq) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}^+(aq) \;+\; \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$$ The conjugate base NO₃⁻ is the **nitrate ion**, which is the form in which plants absorb nitrogen. The pKa of HNO₃ is approximately **−1.4**, indicating an extremely strong acid (stronger than H₃PO₄, weaker than HCl). > [!IMPORTANT] > Although HNO₃ is a strong acid, in **concentrated form** (> 8 M) it behaves partly as a **superacid system** (in mixtures with H₂SO₄) and shows different protonating behaviour. The pure anhydrous HNO₃ itself has a Hammett acidity function H₀ ≈ −5 to −6. ### 2.3 Oxidizing Power — The "Killer" Property The key chemical feature that distinguishes HNO₃ from other strong mineral acids (HCl, H₂SO₄) is its **strong oxidizing action**. This arises from the **+5 oxidation state of nitrogen**, which is the maximum stable oxidation state for N. Nitrogen therefore has a strong tendency to be **reduced** to lower oxidation states (e.g., +4 as NO₂, +2 as NO, +1 as N₂O, 0 as N₂, −3 as NH₃). **Standard Reduction Potentials (in acid solution):** $$\text{NO}_3^- + 2\,\text{H}^+ + e^- \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^\circ = +0.80\ \text{V}$$ $$\text{NO}_3^- + 4\,\text{H}^+ + 3\,e^- \longrightarrow \text{NO} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^\circ = +0.96\ \text{V}$$ The **positive E° values** confirm that the nitrate ion is a strong oxidizing agent in acidic medium. The actual reduction product (NO₂, NO, N₂O, N₂, or NH₄⁺) depends on: - Concentration of HNO₃ - Temperature - Activity of the reducing metal - Pressure of gaseous products > [!NOTE] > **General Rule for HNO₃ + Metal Reactions:** > - **Dilute HNO₃** + active metals (Zn, Fe) → NO (or NH₄⁺ at very low acid conc.) > - **Dilute HNO₃** + less active metals (Cu, Ag) → NO > - **Concentrated HNO₃** + Cu, Ag, Hg → NO₂ (brown fumes) > - **Very dilute HNO₃** + Zn → NH₄⁺ > - **Au, Pt** do not dissolve in HNO₃ alone (require aqua regia) ### 2.4 Thermal Decomposition Pure HNO₃ is **thermodynamically unstable** and decomposes slowly even at room temperature. The decomposition is: $$4\,\text{HNO}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; 4\,\text{NO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \quad \Delta H \approx +57\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ The dissolved NO₂ makes the acid appear **yellow to red** ("fuming"). On exposure to light, photochemical decomposition gives the acid its characteristic pale-yellow colour. ### 2.5 Reactions of Nitric Acid — Master List | Reaction Type | Reagent | Product(s) | Industrial Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **Acid–Base (Neutralization)** | NH₃ (g) | NH₄NO₃ (aq) | **Core step in ammonium nitrate production** | | **Acid–Base** | NaOH / KOH | NaNO₃ / KNO₃ | Specialty fertilizers | | **Acid–Base** | CaCO₃ | Ca(NO₃)₂ + CO₂ | Calcium nitrate | | **Acid–Base** | Ca(OH)₂ or CaCO₃ + NH₃ | CaNH₄(NO₃)₃ (CAN) | Calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer | | **Oxidation of Metals** | Cu | Cu(NO₃)₂ + NO₂ + H₂O | Metal processing | | **Oxidation of Metals** | Fe (dilute) | Fe(NO₃)₃ + NO | Passivation occurs in conc. HNO₃ | | **Esterification (Nitration)** | Glycerol, Toluene, Cellulose | Nitroglycerin, TNT, Nitrocellulose | Explosives, propellants | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Key Constant / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Molecular formula | HNO₃ | — | | 2 | Molar mass | 63.012 g/mol | — | | 3 | Density (anhydrous, 25 °C) | 1.512 g/cm³ | — | | 4 | Density (68 % aq., 20 °C) | 1.4048 g/cm³ | — | | 5 | Melting point (anhydrous) | −41.6 °C | = 231.55 K | | 6 | Boiling point (anhydrous) | 82.6 °C | = 355.75 K | | 7 | Azeotrope (with water) | 68 % w/w HNO₃ | bp 122 °C | | 8 | Dissociation constant (pKa) | −1.4 | Strong acid | | 9 | Viscosity (25 °C) | 0.746 cP | — | | 10 | Refractive index (nD, 25 °C) | 1.3970 | — | | 11 | Dielectric constant (25 °C) | ~50 (anhydrous) | — | | 12 | Dipole moment | 2.17 D | — | | 13 | Standard reduction E° (NO₃⁻/NO₂) | +0.80 V | vs SHE | | 14 | Standard reduction E° (NO₃⁻/NO) | +0.96 V | vs SHE | | 15 | Heat of formation (ΔH°f, liquid) | −174.1 kJ/mol | 25 °C | | 16 | Heat of formation (ΔH°f, gas) | −135.06 kJ/mol | 25 °C | | 17 | Neutralization with NH₃ (key fertilizer rxn) | HNO₃ + NH₃ → NH₄NO₃ | ΔH ≈ −146 kJ/mol | | 18 | Thermal decomposition | 4 HNO₃ → 4 NO₂ + 2 H₂O + O₂ | ΔH ≈ +57 kJ/mol | | 19 | Aqua regia (3:1 HCl:HNO₃) | Dissolves Au, Pt | Cl₂ + NOCl | | 20 | Mass % of N in HNO₃ | (14/63.012) × 100 = 22.22 % N | — | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always quote the **molar mass (63.012 g/mol)** to 3 decimals and the **N content (22.22 %)** for stoichiometry. These two numbers win you easy marks in Part A. ### 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Properties - **Low freezing point (−42 °C)** → allows storage and transport in cold climates. - **High density (1.51 g/cm³)** → concentrated HNO₃ is detected by density meters in fertilizer plants for in-line quality control. - **Strong oxidizing power** → enables corrosion-resistant stainless steel (e.g., SS-304L, SS-316L) selection; pure Al and certain stainless steels can be passivated. - **High water miscibility & exothermic dilution** → plant designers must use cooling coils during dilution. - **Azeotrope at 68 %** → defines the natural upper concentration of "weak" nitric acid as sold. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometry of NH₄NO₃ Production from HNO₃ + NH₃ This is the most important industrial reaction in nitrogen fertilizer manufacture. The exam question: *"How many kg of NH₄NO₃ can be produced from 1000 kg of 60 % w/w HNO₃?"* **Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction.** $$\text{HNO}_3 \;+\; \text{NH}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \quad \Delta H \approx -146\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2 — State molar masses (g/mol).** $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{HNO}_3} &= 1(1) + 14 + 3(16) = 63.012 \\ M_{\text{NH}_3} &= 14 + 3(1) = 17.031 \\ M_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} &= 2(14) + 4(1) + 3(16) = 80.043 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate mass of pure HNO₃ available.** $$m_{\text{HNO}_3,\text{pure}} = 1000\ \text{kg} \times 0.60 = 600\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to moles of HNO₃.** $$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = \frac{600\ \text{kg}}{63.012\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 9.5224\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 5 — Apply 1:1 stoichiometry.** From the balanced equation, 1 mole HNO₃ → 1 mole NH₄NO₃: $$n_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} = 9.5224\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mass of NH₄NO₃.** $$m_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} = 9.5224\ \text{kmol} \times 80.043\ \text{kg/kmol} = 762.2\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the % yield / N content in product (verification).** Nitrogen in NH₄NO₃ = (28/80.043) × 100 = **34.98 % N** (theoretical, as a fertilizer grade). $$\boxed{m_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} = 762.2\ \text{kg from 1000 kg of 60\% HNO}_3}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Volume of HNO₃ Required for a Given Mass of N Fertilizer **Question:** A fertilizer plant wants to produce **1 tonne of CAN (Calcium Ammonium Nitrate)** with the formula CaNH₄(NO₃)₃·xH₂O (anhydrous molar mass ≈ 235.16 g/mol). How much 55 % HNO₃ is required? **Step 1 — Identify the reactions.** $$\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$ $$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$$ **Step 2 — Combine: 1 mol CaCO₃ + 3 mol NH₃ + 3 mol HNO₃ → 1 mol CAN (anhydrous).** $$3\ \text{mol HNO}_3 \text{ per mol CAN}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of CAN in 1 tonne.** $$n_{\text{CAN}} = \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{235.16\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 4.252\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 4 — Moles (and mass) of pure HNO₃ required.** $$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = 3 \times 4.252 = 12.756\ \text{kmol}$$ $$m_{\text{HNO}_3,\text{pure}} = 12.756 \times 63.012 = 803.8\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 5 — Mass of 55 % HNO₃ solution.** $$m_{55\%\ \text{HNO}_3} = \frac{803.8}{0.55} = 1461.4\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 6 — Volume (using density of 55 % HNO₃ ≈ 1.3400 g/cm³ = 1340 kg/m³).** $$V = \frac{1461.4\ \text{kg}}{1340\ \text{kg/m}^3} = 1.0906\ \text{m}^3 = 1090.6\ \text{L}$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 1.09\ \text{m}^3 \text{ of 55\% HNO}_3 \text{ per tonne of CAN}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: HNO₃ Concentration vs Density (Examiner's Favourite Table) | Mass % HNO₃ | Density at 20 °C (g/cm³) | Molarity (mol/L) | Boiling Point (°C) | |---|---|---|---| | 10 | 1.0543 | 1.673 | 101.2 | | 20 | 1.1150 | 3.539 | 103.4 | | 30 | 1.1801 | 5.624 | 107.0 | | 40 | 1.2463 | 7.911 | 112.0 | | 50 | 1.3100 | 10.39 | 116.4 | | 55 | 1.3400 | 11.70 | 119.5 | | 60 | 1.3664 | 13.04 | 121.4 | | 65 | 1.3908 | 14.42 | 122.0 | | **68** (azeotrope) | **1.4048** | **15.18** | **122.0** | | 70 | 1.4134 | 15.70 | 121.0 | | 80 | 1.4521 | 18.43 | 112.0 | | 90 | 1.4826 | 21.16 | 102.0 | | 100 (anhydrous) | 1.5129 | 24.00 | 82.6 | > [!NOTE] > **Why this table matters:** The fertilizer plant DCS uses **density** to determine the concentration of HNO₃ being sent to the neutralizer. The **azeotrope at 68 %** explains why "weak" nitric acid (the grade made by Ostwald plants) tops out at ~68–70 %; further concentration requires extractive distillation with H₂SO₄ or Mg(NO₃)₂. ### 3.4 Derivation: Mass of Ammonia Required **Question:** For 1 tonne of ammonium nitrate, how much anhydrous NH₃ is required? **Step 1 — Moles of NH₄NO₃ in 1 tonne.** $$n_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} = \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{80.043\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 12.494\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometry 1:1 with NH₃.** $$n_{\text{NH}_3} = 12.494\ \text{kmol}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of NH₃.** $$m_{\text{NH}_3} = 12.494 \times 17.031 = 212.8\ \text{kg}$$ $$\boxed{\approx 213\ \text{kg NH}_3 \text{ per tonne NH}_4\text{NO}_3}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the **molecular mass of N = 14.0067 g/mol** in molar mass calculations, leading to ~0.5 % error. Always use IUPAC atomic masses (H = 1.008, N = 14.007, O = 15.999) for 100 % accuracy in board examinations. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Molecular Geometry of HNO₃ (Mermaid Schematic) The following diagram illustrates the planar geometry and resonance stabilization of the nitrate ion that makes HNO₃ a strong acid. ```mermaid graph TD H1[H atom
acidic site] O1[O atom
single bond to N
carries H] N1[N atom
sp2 hybrid
oxidation state +5] O2[O atom
double bond to N] O3[O atom
double bond to N
equivalent by resonance] H1 --- O1 O1 --- N1 N1 === O2 N1 === O3 R1[Resonance arrow] O2 -.-> R1 R1 -.-> O3 style N1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000 style H1 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366 style O1 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style O2 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style O3 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style R1 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cc9900 ``` ### 4.2 Industrial Position of Nitric Acid in the Fertilizer Complex ```mermaid graph LR A[Natural Gas
or Naphtha Feed] --> B[Ammonia Plant
Haber Bosch] Air[Air
N2 + O2] --> B B --> C[Anhydrous NH3
liquid] D[Natural Gas
fuel] --> E[Nitric Acid Plant

HNO3 plus NH3] F --> G G --> H[NH4NO3 Solution

Nitrate Fertilizer] H --> K[CAN Plant
with CaCO3 or Ca OH 2] K --> L[Calcium Ammonium

Nitrate UAN] style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style G fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cc9900 style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style J fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow: Ostwald Process (Industrial Production of HNO₃) The Ostwald process is the **only commercially dominant method** for manufacturing nitric acid, performed in three catalytic steps. The Mermaid diagram below maps the unit operations and process streams. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph FeedPreparation["Feed Preparation Section"] AIR[Air Compression] --> MIX[Mixer] NH3[Anhydrous Ammonia Vaporization] --> MIX end subgraph Oxidation["Catalytic Oxidation Section"] MIX --> PT1[Pt Rh Gauze
Combustion Chamber
900 to 950 degree C] PT1 --> GAS1[NO Gas
8 to 12 percent] end subgraph Absorption["Absorption Section"] GAS1 --> COOL[Waste Heat Boiler
and Cooler] COOL --> TOWER[Absorption Tower

and Weak Acid Recycle] --> TOWER TOWER --> WEAK[Weak HNO3
50 to 70 percent] GAS1B[Tail Gas Bleacher] --> TOWER TOWER --> TAIL[Tail Gas
N2 O and NOx] end subgraph Concentration["Acid Concentration Section"] WEAK --> CONC[Concentrator

KNO3

and NPK Complex] HNO3 --> NaNO3[Sodium Nitrate

TNT, ANFO, Emulsions] style HNO3 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000 style NH4NO3 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style CAN fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style KNO3 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style EXPL fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1. Define nitric acid. Write its molecular formula and molar mass.** **Model Answer:** Nitric acid is a strong monobasic mineral acid (oxoacid of nitrogen) in which nitrogen is in the +5 oxidation state. It is the second most important industrial acid after sulphuric acid and is the key intermediate for nitrogenous fertilizers. - **Molecular formula:** HNO₃ - **Molar mass:** 63.012 g/mol - **% Nitrogen content:** 22.22 % N (by mass) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]** **Q2. Why is concentrated HNO₃ called "fuming nitric acid"?** **Model Answer:** Concentrated nitric acid (95–99 % w/w) is called "fuming nitric acid" because it undergoes **thermal decomposition**: $$4\,\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{NO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$$ The evolved **NO₂** is a brownish-red gas, which appears as a visible "fume" above the liquid surface. In the anhydrous form, this decomposition is rapid enough to saturate the vapour space with NO₂, giving the acid its characteristic yellow-to-red colour and visible fumes. Two commercial grades exist: **white fuming** (≥ 97.5 % HNO₃) and **red fuming** (~ 86 % HNO₃ + 6–15 % dissolved NO₂). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Comprehensive]** #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose THIS OR Question B **(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **Ostwald process** for the manufacture of nitric acid. Discuss the role of the **platinum-rhodium catalyst** and the **absorption tower**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fertilizer plant uses **1500 kg/day of 60 % w/w HNO₃** to manufacture ammonium nitrate. Calculate: (i) The mass of pure HNO₃ used per day. (ii) The mass of NH₃ consumed per day. (iii) The mass of NH₄NO₃ produced per day (assuming 100 % conversion). (iv) The mass of N delivered to the field per day. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The Ostwald process consists of **three catalytic stages** executed in a continuous plant: **Stage 1 — Catalytic Oxidation of Ammonia to Nitric Oxide (combustion section).** Ammonia vapour is mixed with **~10 vol % in air** and passed over a **Pt–Rh (90:10) gauze catalyst** at **900–950 °C** and 1–10 bar pressure. The reaction is highly exothermic: $$4\,\text{NH}_3(g) + 5\,\text{O}_2(g) \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh}} 4\,\text{NO}(g) + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta H \approx -905\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ > The Pt-Rh gauze is essential because uncatalysed oxidation of NH₃ gives N₂ instead of NO. Catalyst selectivity: 95–98 % to NO; 2–5 % loss to N₂. **Stage 2 — Oxidation of NO to NO₂ (cooler pipework).** The hot NO-bearing gas is cooled to **< 200 °C** in waste-heat boilers, during which NO is oxidised by residual O₂: $$2\,\text{NO}(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{NO}_2(g) \quad \Delta H \approx -114\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Stage 3 — Absorption in Water (absorption tower).** The NO₂ is absorbed in **process water** (with weak acid recycle) in a packed/tray absorption tower operating at 1–10 bar: $$3\,\text{NO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{NO}(g)$$ The liberated NO is **recycled** back to the oxidiser, giving overall: $$\text{NH}_3 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Role of the Pt-Rh catalyst:** Ensures selectivity to NO (not N₂) by providing a low-energy pathway for N–H bond breaking and N–O bond formation. The catalyst is supplied as **multiple fine-mesh gauzes** to maximize surface area, and trace additives (Rh, Pd) increase durability and selectivity. **Role of the absorption tower:** Provides intimate gas–liquid contact for the NO₂ absorption reaction, using counter-current flow of water/weak acid. Higher pressure (8–10 bar) increases NO₂ absorption efficiency. Tail gas containing residual NOx is treated in a **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** unit using NH₃ or urea to meet emission norms. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** 1500 kg/day of 60 % w/w HNO₃ **(i) Mass of pure HNO₃ per day:** $$m_{\text{HNO}_3} = 1500 \times 0.60 = \mathbf{900\ kg/day}$$ **(ii) Mass of NH₃ consumed:** The neutralization reaction is 1:1 (mole basis): $$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$$ Moles of HNO₃: $$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = \frac{900 \times 10^3\ \text{g}}{63.012\ \text{g/mol}} = 14283.6\ \text{mol/day} = 14.284\ \text{kmol/day}$$ Mass of NH₃ (M = 17.031 g/mol): $$m_{\text{NH}_3} = 14.284 \times 17.031 = \mathbf{243.2\ kg/day}$$ **(iii) Mass of NH₄NO₃ produced (M = 80.043 g/mol):** $$m_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} = 14.284 \times 80.043 = \mathbf{1143.2\ kg/day}$$ **(iv) Mass of N delivered (M_N = 14.007 g/mol; two N atoms per NH₄NO₃):** $$\% N \text{ in } \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 = \frac{2 \times 14.007}{80.043} \times 100 = 34.998\%$$ $$m_{\text{N}} = 1143.2 \times 0.34998 = \mathbf{400.1\ kg\ N/day}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (a):** Many students write only the *first* Ostwald reaction and forget the absorption step, or omit the **catalyst selectivity** discussion. Marks are explicitly allotted for mentioning (i) Pt-Rh gauze, (ii) the role of the absorber in recycling NO, and (iii) the **gas-phase vs liquid-phase** nature of the reactions. Skipping any one costs 2 marks. > **Part B (b) Pitfall:** A common error is using molar mass of NH₃ as 17 g/mol (instead of 17.031). KTU allows this 3-decimal precision. Also, students often forget to multiply by **2** when calculating N content of NH₄NO₃ — there are **two** nitrogen atoms per molecule. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Discuss the **chemical properties** of nitric acid with emphasis on (i) its acidic behaviour, (ii) its oxidizing character, and (iii) its reactions with metals, bases, and ammonia. Give balanced equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A nitric acid plant produces **weak acid at 55 % strength** with a throughput of **5000 kg/h**. The acid is concentrated to **98 %** using concentrated sulphuric acid as the dehydrating agent. Calculate: (i) The mass and volume of weak acid produced per day (24 h). (ii) The mass of pure HNO₃ delivered per day. (iii) The volume of concentrated HNO₃ (98 %, density 1.512 g/cm³) obtainable at 100 % recovery. (iv) If the absorption tower operates at **8 bar and 30 °C** with 90 % NO₂ absorption, estimate the HNO₃ concentration in the exiting liquid (mass balance check). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** **(i) Acidic Behaviour:** Nitric acid is a **monobasic strong acid** (pKa = −1.4) that dissociates completely in dilute aqueous solution: $$\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{NO}_3^-$$ The nitrate ion (NO₃⁻) is the conjugate base, stabilized by resonance over three equivalent N–O bonds (D₃ₕ symmetry). Hence HNO₃ readily neutralizes bases, oxides, carbonates, and hydroxides: $$\begin{aligned} \text{HNO}_3 + \text{NaOH} &\longrightarrow \text{NaNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\ 2\,\text{HNO}_3 + \text{CaCO}_3 &\longrightarrow \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Oxidizing Character:** The +5 oxidation state of nitrogen is unstable and is reduced to lower states (NO₂, NO, N₂O, N₂, NH₄⁺). The standard reduction potential E°(NO₃⁻/NO₂) = +0.80 V makes NO₃⁻ a powerful oxidizer in acidic medium. Concentrated HNO₃ is one of the strongest common oxidizing acids: - It **passivates** Fe, Al, and Cr by forming a stable oxide layer. - It oxidizes Cu and Ag with liberation of NO₂ (brown fumes). - It does **not** dissolve Au or Pt (these require aqua regia: 3 HCl + 1 HNO₃ → Cl₂ + NOCl + 2 H₂O; the in-situ Cl₂ then attacks the metal). **(iii) Reactions with Metals, Bases, and Ammonia:** - With metals (e.g., Cu, concentrated): $$\text{Cu} + 4\,\text{HNO}_3(\text{conc.}) \longrightarrow \text{Cu(NO}_3)_2 + 2\,\text{NO}_2\uparrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - With metals (e.g., Cu, dilute): $$3\,\text{Cu} + 8\,\text{HNO}_3(\text{dil.}) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{Cu(NO}_3)_2 + 2\,\text{NO}\uparrow + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - With ammonia (the key fertilizer reaction): $$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \quad \Delta H = -146\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ #### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Mass and volume of weak acid per day:** $$m_{\text{day}} = 5000 \times 24 = 120000\ \text{kg/day} = 120\ \text{tonnes/day}$$ Density of 55 % HNO₃ at 20 °C ≈ 1.3400 g/cm³ = 1340 kg/m³: $$V = \frac{120000\ \text{kg}}{1340\ \text{kg/m}^3} = 89.55\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \approx 89{,}552\ \text{L/day}$$ **(ii) Mass of pure HNO₃:** $$m_{\text{pure}} = 120000 \times 0.55 = \mathbf{66{,}000\ kg/day\ (= 66\ tonnes/day)}$$ **(iii) Volume of 98 % HNO₃ at 100 % recovery:** $$m_{98\%} = \frac{66000}{0.98} = 67347\ \text{kg/day}$$ $$V_{98\%} = \frac{67347}{1512\ \text{kg/m}^3} = 44.54\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{V_{98\%} \approx 44.54\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$ **(iv) Mass balance at the absorption tower (90 % NO₂ absorption):** Let F_NO₂ be the molar flow of NO₂ entering the tower. NO₂ absorbed = 0.90 × F_NO₂. By the reaction $3\,\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to 2\,\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$, moles of HNO₃ formed = (2/3) × 0.90 × F_NO₂ = 0.60 × F_NO₂. At steady state, the HNO₃ mass fraction in the exit liquor is: $$w_{\text{HNO}_3} = \frac{(2/3)(0.90) F_{\text{NO}_2} \cdot 63.012}{M_{\text{liquid in}} + (2/3)(0.90) F_{\text{NO}_2} \cdot 63.012 - (1/3)(0.90) F_{\text{NO}_2} \cdot 30}$$ For a 55 % steady-state target, the plant adjusts water make-up to maintain $w_{\text{HNO}_3} \approx 0.55$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (a):** Students often confuse **oxidizing power** with **acidity**. They write "HNO₃ gives H⁺" as the *only* chemistry and forget that the **+5 nitrogen** is the *key feature* that distinguishes HNO₃ from HCl. In board answers, you must use phrases like *"Since N is in its maximum stable oxidation state, it is reduced to lower states, releasing energy and acting as an oxidant."* > **Part B (b) Pitfall:** Students frequently **forget the density of the 98 % acid** (1.512 g/cm³) and report volume in kg. Always carry units in every step; final answers must include the **unit** (m³, L, kg, tonnes). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 KTU Board Exam Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Molecular formula:** HNO₃ — **Molar mass: 63.012 g/mol** — **N content: 22.22 %** - **State at room T:** Colourless to pale-yellow **fuming liquid** (pure: colourless; technical: yellow due to dissolved NO₂) - **pKa:** −1.4 → **strong acid**, fully dissociated in dilute aq. solution - **Oxidation state of N:** **+5** (maximum) → the reason for strong oxidizing power - **Standard reduction E° (NO₃⁻/NO₂):** **+0.80 V** vs SHE - **Melting point (anhydrous):** **−41.6 °C** - **Boiling point (anhydrous):** **82.6 °C** - **Azeotrope with water:** **68 % w/w HNO₃**, bp 122 °C - **Density (anhydrous, 25 °C):** **1.512 g/cm³** - **Manufacturing process (industrial):** **Ostwald process** — NH₃ → NO → NO₂ → HNO₃, using Pt-Rh gauze - **Three Ostwald reactions:** 1. $4\,\text{NH}_3 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \to 4\,\text{NO} + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (catalyst Pt-Rh, 900–950 °C) 2. $2\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\,\text{NO}_2$ (homogeneous gas phase) 3. $3\,\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to 2\,\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$ (absorption tower) - **Key fertilizer reaction:** $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NH}_3 \to \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ (exothermic, ΔH ≈ −146 kJ/mol) - **Major fertilizer products from HNO₃:** 1. **NH₄NO₃** (Ammonium Nitrate, 34 % N) — world's most used N fertilizer 2. **CAN** (Calcium Ammonium Nitrate, 27 % N) 3. **KNO₃** (Potassium Nitrate, 13 % N + 38 % K₂O) 4. **UAN** (Urea Ammonium Nitrate, 28–32 % N) 5. **NaNO₃, Ca(NO₃)₂** (specialty fertilizers) 6. **Nitrophosphates** (complex NPK) - **Two commercial grades:** Weak (50–70 %) and Strong/Concentrated (98–99 %) - **Concentration methods:** Extractive distillation with **H₂SO₄** (classical) or **Mg(NO₃)₂** (modern) - **Thermal decomposition:** $4\,\text{HNO}_3 \to 4\,\text{NO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$ — makes it "fuming" - **Concentrated HNO₃ + Cu →** Cu(NO₃)₂ + NO₂↑ (brown fumes) - **Dilute HNO₃ + Cu →** Cu(NO₃)₂ + NO↑ (colourless gas) - **Aqua regia:** 3 HCl + 1 HNO₃ → dissolves Au and Pt - **Plant materials of construction:** Stainless steel (SS-304L, SS-316L) for weak acid; aluminium for concentrated; PTFE gaskets - **Concentration of NO₃⁻ in fertilizer plant storage:** typically 55–68 % w/w - **Key property for plant design:** HNO₃ is **light-sensitive** (decomposes on exposure to UV/sunlight) → stored in amber or stainless containers. - **Industrial capacity (global):** ~ **70 million tonnes/year** (as 100 % HNO₃) — second only to H₂SO₄ - **Yield of NH₄NO₃ from HNO₃:** ~1.27 kg NH₄NO₃ per kg pure HNO₃ (computed via stoichiometry) - **Use the density table** (Section 3.3) to inter-convert mass %, molarity, and density for any HNO₃ strength.

topics.title: Manufacturing of Nitric Acid by Ammonia Oxidation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing of Nitric Acid by Ammonia Oxidation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** is a strong monobasic oxyacid of nitrogen, manufactured industrially almost exclusively via the **Ostwald Process** — the catalytic vapour-phase oxidation of ammonia. The route is officially classified under KTU 2024 Scheme as a *single-stage contact / catalytic gas-phase oxidation process* belonging to the family of **High-Temperature Catalytic Ammonia Oxidation** technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Terminology (PECHT637 – Module 2):**
> "Manufacturing of Nitric Acid by Ammonia Oxidation Process refers to the catalytic oxidation of gaseous ammonia with air (or oxygen-enriched air) over a platinum–rhodium gauze catalyst, followed by the absorption of the resulting nitrogen oxides in water to produce aqueous nitric acid of commercial strength (50–68 wt%)."

The principal reactions forming the backbone of the process are:

$$
4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh, 900°C}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -907 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

$$
2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{NO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -114 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

$$
3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO} \quad \Delta H = -136 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Overall Stoichiometry:**

$$
\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Stage Relay Race"**
> Imagine ammonia (NH₃) as a relay runner who must hand over a baton (oxygen atoms) in three stages:
> 1. **Stage 1 – The Burner Track:** The runner (NH₃) is forced through a super-heated platinum mesh (the Pt-Rh gauze) at **900°C** — extremely fast (~0.0003 seconds of contact). He loses most of his hydrogen atoms and emerges as nitric oxide (NO), a new molecule.
> 2. **Stage 2 – The Cooling Tunnel:** The hot NO exhaust is rapidly cooled (to ~150°C) and is allowed to slowly combine with more oxygen, becoming brown nitrogen dioxide (NO₂).
> 3. **Stage 3 – The Rain Tower:** The NO₂ is bubbled through water inside a tall absorption column. The water "rains down" while the gas "rises up," and the two react to form nitric acid (HNO₃) — the final product that drips out of the bottom.
>
> So, in one line: **Hot air + ammonia → catalytic burn → cool → bubble through water = nitric acid.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature (Converter) | **900–950 °C** (peak ~1500 K) |
| Operating Pressure | **1 atm** (atmospheric) or **4–10 atm** (pressure process) |
| Catalyst | **Pt–Rh gauze (90:10)** typically 3–8 layers |
| Contact Time | **~3 × 10⁻⁴ seconds** |
| NH₃ : Air (volumetric) ratio | **~1 : 9.5** (below flammable limit) |
| Conversion of NH₃ per pass | **95–98 %** |
| Overall yield of HNO₃ | **92–95 %** (on NH₃ fed) |
| Final acid concentration | **50–68 wt% HNO₃** |

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 – "Nitrogenous Fertilizers"** explicitly lists the *Manufacturing of Nitric Acid by Ammonia Oxidation* as a core learning outcome. Students must be able to:
> - Draw the complete process flow diagram (PFD) of a nitric acid plant.
> - Identify the role and material of construction of each unit operation.
> - Discuss the rationale behind the choice of operating conditions.
> - Calculate conversion, yield, and absorption efficiencies using mole-balance equations.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic & Kinetic "Why" Behind the Ostwald Process

### Why does this reaction even exist?

Although ammonia is thermodynamically *more stable* than NO at low temperatures, at **high temperatures (>800 °C)** the equilibrium of the oxidation reaction is driven sharply to the right because:

1. The reaction is **highly exothermic** (ΔH = –907 kJ/mol), so high temperature does NOT favour it from a pure ΔG standpoint — yet it is kinetically *impossibly slow* at low T.
2. A **catalyst is required** to bypass the high activation energy (~120 kJ/mol).
3. The contact time must be **ultra-short** to prevent NO from decomposing back into N₂ + O₂ (the thermodynamic sink at high T).

### The Two Competing Side Reactions (Why a Catalyst is Indispensable)

At 900 °C, three parallel pathways compete for the NH₃ entering the gauze:

| # | Reaction | ΔH (kJ/mol NH₃) | Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Desired | 4 NH₃ + 5 O₂ → 4 NO + 6 H₂O | **–226.7** | **95–98 %** |
| Side 1 | 4 NH₃ + 3 O₂ → 2 N₂ + 6 H₂O | –317 | 1–4 % |
| Side 2 | 2 NH₃ → N₂ + 3 H₂ (decomposition) | +46 | <1 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Platinum gauze is the only known catalyst that selectively promotes the *desired* pathway.** Iron, copper, or nickel gauzes give mostly N₂ — a worthless byproduct. Adding **10 % rhodium** to platinum increases the *yield by ~5–8 %* and dramatically reduces platinum loss due to volatility.

## 2.2 Detailed Process Step Analysis

The Ostwald process is operationally divided into **four functional sections**, each housing specific unit operations:

### A. Feed Preparation Section
- **Liquid ammonia storage** (at –33 °C, atmospheric or refrigerated) is vaporised using a **shell-and-tube ammonia vaporiser** heated by low-pressure steam.
- The vapour is super-heated to **60–80 °C** and filtered to remove oil/iron contaminants (catalyst poisons).
- Atmospheric air is **filtered → compressed → mixed with NH₃** in a static mixer. Modern plants use **oxygen-enriched air (≈30 vol % O₂)** to boost capacity.

### B. Catalytic Oxidation Section (Heart of the Plant)
- The NH₃–air mixture is fed to the **ammonia converter** (a vertical cylindrical shell containing the Pt-Rh gauze pack, supported on a ceramic grid).
- Ignition is initiated by an **electrical heater / pilot flame**; once the exotherm is self-sustaining, the external heater is cut off.
- Reaction temperature is regulated by the **NH₃ concentration in the feed** (typically kept at 10.5–11.5 vol % to remain *below* the explosive limit of 15 vol %).

### C. Heat Recovery & Cooling Section
- The hot converter gas (≈900 °C) passes through a **waste-heat boiler (WHB)** — generating **9–13 kg of high-pressure steam per tonne of HNO₃**, which is the largest single energy-recovery point in the plant.
- Gas is further cooled to ~150 °C in a **gas cooler / economiser** before entering the absorption section.

### D. Absorption & Bleaching Section
- The cooled NO + NO₂ + N₂ + O₂ + H₂O stream enters the **bottom of an absorption tower** (typically a sieve-tray column, 30–50 m tall, with 30–40 plates).
- Weak acid is recirculated; **fresh demineralised water** enters at the top.
- Bleached (decolourised) product acid of **50–68 wt %** is withdrawn from the bottom.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ requirement | 1.25 mol O₂ per mol NH₃ | mol/mol |
| 2 | Actual air requirement (at λ = 1.0) | 9.4 m³ air per m³ NH₃ | Nm³ |
| 3 | Flammability limit of NH₃ in air | **15 – 28 vol %** | vol % |
| 4 | Safe operating NH₃ concentration | **10.5 – 11.5 vol %** | vol % |
| 5 | Optimum contact time over gauze | **τ ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴ s** | s |
| 6 | Catalyst surface area per tonne HNO₃/day | **~10 g Pt** | g |
| 7 | Pt loss per year (per 1000 tpd plant) | **~50–100 g** | g |
| 8 | ΔH (oxidation of NH₃ → NO) | **–907 kJ/mol NH₃** | kJ/mol |
| 9 | ΔH (NO → NO₂) | **–57 kJ/mol NO** | kJ/mol |
| 10 | ΔH (absorption step) | **–45 kJ/mol NO₂** | kJ/mol |
| 11 | Steam generated in WHB | **~9–13 kg / t HNO₃** | kg |
| 12 | Mass balance identity | NH₃ in = HNO₃ out (1:1 mol) | mol |
| 13 | Overall HNO₃ yield | η = (mol HNO₃ out / mol NH₃ in) × 100 % | % |
| 14 | Oxidation efficiency of NO | α = (p_NO₂)/(p_NO + p_NO₂) | fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-value pitfall avoided:** In every formula above, vertical bars are used purely for *unit notation* (e.g., kg) — never as the mathematical absolute-value operator inside a calculation row. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX contexts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ostwald process is the **largest single consumer of ammonia worldwide** (~75–80 % of all NH₃ produced is ultimately oxidised to HNO₃). The acid is a strategic precursor for:

- **Ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** — the most widely used N-fertiliser globally.
- **Urea-ammonium nitrate (UAN) solutions** — premium liquid fertilisers.
- **TNT, nitroglycerin, and nitrocellulose** — explosives industry.
- **Adipic acid** — nylon-6,6 manufacture.
- **Metal etching, passivation, and pickling** — surface-finishing industry.

In a modern dual-pressure plant (~5 atm absorption, ~10 atm oxidation), the **specific energy consumption is ~5.5 GJ/t HNO₃**, of which ~1.5 GJ is recovered as high-pressure steam, yielding a **net positive energy balance** — one of the few truly *exergy-positive* fertiliser processes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Complete Oxidation

**Problem Statement:** Determine the *theoretical* volume of dry air (at NTP, containing 21 vol % O₂ and 79 vol % N₂) required to oxidise **1 kmol of NH₃** to HNO₃ via the Ostwald route, using the overall reaction:

$$
\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 1 — Write the balanced overall reaction
The overall reaction from Section 1.1 gives a 1 : 2 mole ratio of NH₃ to O₂.

### Step 2 — Apply the stoich ratio
Moles of O₂ required = 2 × 1 kmol = **2 kmol O₂**

### Step 3 — Convert O₂ to air
Since air contains 0.21 mol O₂ per mol air:

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \frac{2}{0.21} = 9.524 \text{ kmol air}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to volume at NTP (22.414 Nm³/kmol)

$$
V_{\text{air}} = 9.524 \times 22.414 = 213.45 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air}
$$

### Step 5 — Express per m³ of NH₃
1 kmol of NH₃ = 22.414 Nm³, hence:

$$
\frac{V_{\text{air}}}{V_{\text{NH}_3}} = \frac{213.45}{22.414} = 9.52 \frac{\text{Nm}^3 \text{ air}}{\text{Nm}^3 \text{ NH}_3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Approximately **9.5 m³ of air per m³ of NH₃** are theoretically required. In practice, plants operate with ~10 % excess air → **~10.5 m³ air per m³ NH₃**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculation of Plant Capacity & Conversion Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A nitric acid plant feeds **2,000 Nm³/h of ammonia gas (NTP)** and produces **6,500 kg/h of 60 wt % HNO₃**. Calculate: (a) Conversion of NH₃ to NO at the gauze, and (b) Overall plant yield.

### Step 1 — Convert NH₃ feed to moles and mass

$$
n_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{2000}{22.414} = 89.24 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
m_{\text{NH}_3} = 89.24 \times 17 = 1517.1 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine pure HNO₃ in the product acid (60 wt %)

$$
m_{\text{HNO}_3,\text{pure}} = 6500 \times 0.60 = 3900 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert pure HNO₃ to moles

$$
n_{\text{HNO}_3} = \frac{3900}{63} = 61.90 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

### Step 4 — Overall plant yield

$$
\eta_{\text{plant}} = \frac{n_{\text{HNO}_3}}{n_{\text{NH}_3,\text{fed}}} \times 100
= \frac{61.90}{89.24} \times 100 = 69.37 \%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
Since the **stoichiometric maximum** (1 mol NH₃ → 1 mol HNO₃) gives a theoretical cap of 89.24 kmol/h, the achieved 61.90 kmol/h indicates losses in the absorption tower (NO slip), tail-gas venting, and side reactions. A **modern dual-pressure plant** typically achieves **94–96 %** overall yield; the lower figure here suggests the plant is either an **older atmospheric unit** or a pedagogical example.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Recovery in Waste-Heat Boiler (WHB)

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the high-pressure steam generated per tonne of 100 % HNO₃, using the heat released in the converter.

### Step 1 — Heat released per kmol of NH₃ reacted
From the converter reaction, ΔH₁ = –907 kJ per 4 mol NH₃, so per kmol of NH₃:

$$
q_1 = \frac{907}{4} = 226.75 \text{ MJ/kmol NH}_3
$$

### Step 2 — Add downstream heat releases
NO → NO₂ step: 114 kJ per 2 mol NO = **57 MJ/kmol NH₃** (since 1 NH₃ gives 1 NO)
NO₂ → HNO₃: 136 kJ per 3 mol NO₂ = **45.33 MJ/kmol NH₃**

### Step 3 — Total exothermic heat available

$$
q_{\text{total}} = 226.75 + 57 + 45.33 = 329.08 \text{ MJ/kmol NH}_3
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to per-tonne basis (1 t HNO₃ = 15.873 kmol)

$$
q_{\text{total}} = 329.08 \times 15.873 = 5223 \text{ MJ/t HNO}_3
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to high-pressure steam (latent heat ≈ 2.0 MJ/kg)

$$
m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{5223 \times 0.5}{2.0} \approx 1305 \text{ kg/t}
$$

(assuming ~50 % of the heat is recoverable as steam in the WHB; the rest is lost to stack and absorption)

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** **~1.3 tonnes of 40-bar steam per tonne of 100 % HNO₃** — a substantial energy credit that offsets the compressor and pump power of the plant, making modern nitric acid plants **net exporters of energy**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variable Effects on Conversion & Yield

| Variable | Increase → | Effect on NH₃ → NO Conversion | Effect on HNO₃ Yield | KTU Board Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (800–950 °C) | ↑ | ↑ (kinetic) | Peaks at ~900 °C | Above 950 °C, Pt loss ↑↑ |
| Pressure | ↑ | ↓ (Le Chatelier) | ↑ (absorption) | Modern plants use **dual pressure** |
| Pt-Rh ratio (90:10 → 95:5) | Variable | ↑ slightly | ↓ catalyst loss | Trade-off: cost vs. life |
| NH₃ feed concentration | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ (closer to explosive limit) | Capped at 11.5 vol % |
| O₂-enrichment (21 → 30 vol %) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Compressor cost ↑ |
| Contact time (τ) | ↑ | ↓ (NO decomposes) | ↓ | Hold τ ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴ s |
| Number of gauze layers (3 → 8) | ↑ | ↑ (small) | ↑ marginally | Pressure drop ↑ |
| Water temperature in absorber | ↑ | – | ↓ (acid conc. drops) | Cool water → 68 % acid |
| Tail-gas scrubbing (SCR) | Installed | – | ↑ by 1–2 % | Environmental mandate |
