<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**List the three major stages involved in the manufacture of ammonium nitrate by the prilling process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Key Pattern):**

The three stages are:

1. **Neutralization Stage** — Ammonia gas reacts with nitric acid (≈ 60 % w/w) in a stainless-steel Bos-type neutralizer to give a hot aqueous ammonium nitrate solution of strength 70 – 82 %.

$$
\mathrm{NH_3(g) + HNO_3(aq) \longrightarrow NH_4NO_3(aq)} \quad \Delta H = -148.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

2. **Concentration / Evaporation Stage** — The dilute AN solution is concentrated to a near-anhydrous melt (95 – 99.8 % AN) using multi-effect evaporators followed by a vacuum finishing evaporator.

3. **Prilling Stage** — The 99.5 % AN melt at 170 °C is sprayed as fine droplets from the top of a tall prilling tower (30 – 70 m high) and solidified by counter-current ambient air into spherical prills of 1.5 – 3.0 mm diameter.

**(1 mark each for naming + brief logic = 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Why is ammonium nitrate coated with kaolin or diatomaceous earth before bagging?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Ammonium nitrate is **highly hygroscopic** and has multiple **solid-state phase transitions** below its melting point (notably at 32 °C and 84 °C). The IV → III transition at 84 °C is accompanied by **≈ 4 % volume change** and release of **≈ 33.6 kJ/kg latent heat**, causing:
- Caking of prills during bag storage
- Dust formation
- Loss of free-flowing character
- Potential detonation hazard under confinement

Coating with **kaolin, dolomite, or diatomaceous earth** acts as an **anti-caking agent** by:
1. Physically separating adjacent prills (1 mark)
2. Absorbing surface moisture (1 mark)
3. Desensitizing the AN against detonation (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 2 Sample]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of ammonium nitrate by the Prilling Process**, including the chemistry of neutralization, evaporation, and the prilling tower operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plant produces **1500 tonnes/day** of ammonium nitrate using **60 % w/w nitric acid** and **anhydrous ammonia**. Calculate:
  - (i) The mass of NH₃ required per day.
  - (ii) The mass of 60 % HNO₃ required per day.
  - (iii) The total heat liberated per hour.
  - **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**1. Neutralization (2 marks):**
The reaction is carried out in a Bos-type neutralizer at 150 – 180 °C. Gaseous NH₃ and preheated 60 % HNO₃ are mixed continuously. pH is maintained slightly acidic (4 – 6) using a pH controller to avoid NH₃ slippage.

$$
\mathrm{NH_3 + HNO_3 \longrightarrow NH_4NO_3} \quad \Delta H = -148.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

The exothermic heat generates LP steam in the integrated waste-heat boiler.

**2. Evaporation (2 marks):**
The 82 % AN solution is concentrated in two stages:
- **Forward-feed double-effect evaporator** → 92 – 95 % AN
- **Vacuum finishing evaporator** (0.05 bar) → 99.5 – 99.8 % AN

This melt is stored in a surge tank at 170 °C.

**3. Prilling Tower (3 marks):**
The melt is pumped to the top of the 40 – 50 m prill tower. It flows through a perforated spray header containing 2000 – 8000 nozzles (2 – 4 mm orifices). Droplets fall under gravity counter-current to an upward stream of ambient air supplied by an axial fan at the base. Each droplet solidifies into a spherical prill of 1.5 – 3.0 mm. Prills collect on a conical hopper at the base, are lifted by a bucket elevator, screened, crushed if oversized, cooled in a rotary cooler, coated with kaolin, and bagged.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn on answer paper):**
Reference the Block Flow Diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:** Production = 1500 t/day = 1500 × 10³ kg/day = 62,500 kg/h

**Step 1 — Moles of NH₄NO₃ per hour**

$$
n \;=\; \frac{62500}{80.04} \;=\; 780.91 \;\text{kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of NH₃ per day**

$$
m_{\text{NH}_3} \;=\; 1500 \times 10^3 \times \frac{17.03}{80.04} \;\approx\; 319.13 \;\text{tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of 60 % HNO₃ per day**

$$
m_{\text{HNO}_3 (100\%)} \;=\; 1500 \times 10^3 \times \frac{63.01}{80.04} \;\approx\; 1180.84 \;\text{tonnes/day (as 100\%)}
$$

$$
m_{\text{HNO}_3 (60\%)} \;=\; \frac{1180.84}{0.60} \;\approx\; 1968.07 \;\text{tonnes/day (as 60 \% w/w)}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat liberated per hour**

$$
Q \;=\; 780.91 \times 148.6 \;\approx\; 116{,}043 \;\text{MJ/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q \;\approx\; 116.04 \;\text{GJ/h} \;\approx\; 32.23 \;\text{MW (thermal)}\;}
$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- (i) NH₃ = 2 marks
- (ii) HNO₃ = 2.5 marks
- (iii) Heat = 2.5 marks

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a Prill Tower** with a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of counter-current air flow, residence time, and the operational parameters affecting prill quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **causes of caking of ammonium nitrate prills during storage** and the **preventive measures (both internal and external)** adopted industrially. Why is fertilizer-grade AN coated with inert materials before dispatch? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) — Prill Tower Construction & Working

**Construction (3 marks):**
A modern prill tower is a **hollow vertical cylindrical structure**, 30 – 70 m tall and 6 – 15 m in diameter, constructed of reinforced concrete or carbon-steel lined with stainless steel. The top houses the **melt distribution system** — a circular pipe ring with thousands of fine perforations (orifice size 2 – 4 mm). The bottom is a conical hopper for prill collection, fitted with a **variable-speed axial-flow fan** capable of 200,000 – 300,000 m³/h air delivery.

**Working (3 marks):**
Molten AN at **170 °C** is pumped to the top header. Through each nozzle orifice, the melt emerges as a fine droplet (driven by gravity + slight pressure head). The droplet free-falls against an upward column of ambient or chilled air. Sensible heat + latent heat of fusion are extracted by the air. By the time the droplet reaches the conical base, it has cooled from 170 °C to ≈ 50 °C and is fully solidified as a **spherical prill** of 2 mm diameter.

**Operational Parameters Affecting Prill Quality (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Effect on Prill Quality |
|---|---|
| Melt temperature | Too low → viscous, large irregular prills; Too high → fines/dust |
| Melt concentration | < 99 % → hollow, porous, weak prills |
| Air flow rate | Too low → hot, soft prills; Too high → fines, dust carry-over |
| Orifice diameter | Smaller → smaller prills + more dust |
| Tower height | Taller → better solidification, harder prills |

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) — Caking & Anti-Caking

**Causes of Caking (3.5 marks):**

1. **Hygroscopic nature** — CRH of AN at 30 °C is only **59 %**, so in coastal Kerala humidity (often > 75 % RH), prills absorb surface moisture.
2. **Phase transition (IV → III at 84 °C)** — causes 4 % volumetric expansion + 33.6 kJ/kg exothermic release, generating localized heating that "weld" prills together.
3. **Mechanical compaction** — bottom prills in bags deform plastically.
4. **Crystal bridging** — recrystallization across prill surfaces in moist air forms salt bridges.

**Preventive Measures (3.5 marks):**

**Internal Additives:**
- **Magnesium nitrate** (0.5 – 1 %) — stabilizes crystal lattice and prevents IV → III transition.
- **Calcium nitrate** — alternative phase stabilizer.
- **Diammonium phosphate (DAP) micro-additions** — alters crystallization habit.

**External Surface Treatments:**
- **Coating with kaolin / china clay** (1 – 2 % by mass) — inert barrier.
- **Coating with dolomite powder** — also desensitizes against detonation (DRAN technology).
- **Surface-active agents** — minimize surface tension of adsorbed moisture.
- **Bag conditioning at controlled humidity** — packaging rooms at RH < 55 %.

**Why coating is mandatory:**
Coating with inert diluents converts explosive-grade AN into a **detonation-resistant fertilizer grade**, satisfying transportation safety codes (UN 1942, Class 5.1 oxidizer) and preventing accidental explosions during bulk storage — a critical public-safety requirement after the 2020 Beirut port tragedy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "prilling" with "granulation"** — Prilling uses **molten AN** sprayed as droplets; granulation uses **AN solution sprayed onto seed particles** in a tumbling drum/pan. Many students write the same answer for both — examiners deduct 2 – 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the upstream neutralization** — A 14-mark question on "prilling" *also* expects discussion of the neutralizer and evaporators (typically 4 – 5 marks are reserved for upstream sections).
> 3. **Numerical errors in acid strength conversion** — When HNO₃ is given as 60 %, students forget to **divide by 0.60** to get actual acid mass, instead using HNO₃ × 60 / 100 wrongly. Always write the equation explicitly: $m_{60\%} = m_{100\%}/0.60$.
> 4. **Ignoring CRH and phase transitions** — The crystal transitions at 32 °C and 84 °C and the CRH of 59 % are *high-frequency* KTU board questions, often worth 2 – 3 marks.
> 5. **No flow diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners expect at least one block diagram. Skipping it = guaranteed loss of 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Ammonium Nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** — Molecular weight **80.04**; nitrogen content **35 % pure / 33.5 % commercial**; **CRH = 59 % at 30 °C**; melting point **169.6 °C**.
- **Manufacturing route**: **Neutralization → Evaporation → Prilling** (continuous, melt-based).
- **Master equation:** $\mathrm{NH_3 + HNO_3 \longrightarrow NH_4NO_3 + 148.6 \;\text{kJ/mol}}$.
- **Stoichiometric mass ratio** per tonne AN: **NH₃ = 212.8 kg**, **HNO₃ (100 %) = 787.2 kg**, **HNO₃ (60 %) = 1312 kg**.
- **Heat recovered** ≈ **1.86 GJ per tonne AN** — drives evaporation with no external fuel.
- **Prill tower** is **30 – 70 m tall**, made of RCC/SS-lined, with **2000 – 8000 spray nozzles** at top.
- **Counter-current air flow** is essential — air enters at base, exits at top.
- **Prill diameter** = **1.5 – 3.0 mm**; residence time ≈ **5 – 7 s**.
- **Phase transitions to remember**: 32 °C (V → IV), 84 °C (IV → III, **most critical**), 125 °C, 169.6 °C (melt).
- **Caking prevention**: internal (Mg(NO₃)₂) + external (kaolin coating) + conditioned packaging.
- **Detonation safety**: coated AN = DRAN = UN 1942 Class 5.1 (oxidizer).
- **Difference between Prilling & Granulation** — Prilling = molten droplet + air cooling; Granulation = solution + seed recycle in tumbling bed.
- **Same prilling technology is used for urea, sodium nitrate, and CAN** — generalized fertilizer platform.
- **Key process flow words for board exams**: Neutralizer → Evaporators → Surge tank → Spray nozzles → Counter-current tower → Screens → Crusher → Cooler → Coater → Bagging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ammonium Sulphate from Ammonium Carbonate and Gypsum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ammonium Sulphate from Ammonium Carbonate and Gypsum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ammonium Sulphate [(NH₄)₂SO₄] from Ammonium Carbonate and Gypsum** is an **indirect (double-decomposition / metathesis)** industrial process in which a slurry of finely ground natural gypsum (calcium sulphate dihydrate, CaSO₄·2H₂O) is reacted with a concentrated solution of **ammonium carbonate [(NH₄)₂CO₃]** to yield **ammonium sulphate** in solution and an insoluble **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** precipitate. The process is a classical route to manufacture a **21% N / 24% S nitrogenous fertilizer**, in which the ammonia value from ammonium carbonate is transferred to sulphuric acid value already locked in gypsum, recovering it as a useful plant nutrient.

The governing stoichiometric reaction is:

$$(NH_4)_2CO_3 \;+\; CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; (NH_4)_2SO_4 \;+\; CaCO_3 \downarrow \;+\; 2H_2O$$

| Process Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular weight of gypsum | **172.17 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of (NH₄)₂CO₃ | **96.09 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of (NH₄)₂SO₄ | **132.14 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of CaCO₃ | **100.09 g/mol** |
| Product nitrogen content | **~21.0 % N (w/w)** |
| Product sulphur content | **~24.0 % S (w/w)** |
| Standard reaction temperature | **~60 – 70 °C** |
| Gypsum slurry concentration | **~15 – 25 % solids** |
| Product purity (technical grade) | **> 98.5 %** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Partner-Swap Dance"**
> Imagine four students holding hands in two pairs: **Ammonia-and-Ammonia paired with Carbonate**, and **Calcium paired with Sulphate (with two water molecules tagging along)**. When the music starts, the dancers are encouraged to swap partners in a controlled ballroom. **Ammonia prefers Sulphate** because it makes a highly soluble, plant-friendly salt; meanwhile **Calcium prefers Carbonate** because it forms an insoluble, harmless chalk that drops out of solution. The water molecules are simply spectators that get released into the liquid. The "ballroom" is a stirred reactor at ~60 °C; the "DJ" is the engineer who adjusts concentration, temperature and residence time so the swap is complete and clean.

This "swap" is called a **metathesis reaction** — two compounds exchange partners to form two new compounds, one of which (here CaCO₃) precipitates, driving the equilibrium to the right.

## 1.3 Why This Process Exists (Industrial Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical & Economic Significance:**
> 1. **Phosphoric-acid plants** generate huge quantities of by-product gypsum (e.g., from the dihydrate process, ~5 tonnes of gypsum per tonne of P₂O₅). Disposing of it is an environmental headache — converting it into a marketable fertilizer solves the waste problem and creates value.
> 2. **Coke-oven and coal-gas plants** historically had cheap ammonium carbonate liquor (from NH₃ scrubbers); reacting it with gypsum was a logical outlet.
> 3. **India & China** still operate gypsum-route (NH₄)₂SO₄ plants adjacent to phosphoric-acid / cement complexes, even though the dominant modern route is **caprolactam by-product** and **synthetic-from-ammonia + sulphuric-acid** routes.

The ammonium sulphate produced via this route is termed **"by-product gypsum route (NH₄)₂SO₄"** or the **"Gypsum Process"** in older KTU textbooks.

## 1.4 Product Specifications (FCO / Indian Standard Mark)

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Total nitrogen (as N) | **≥ 20.6 %** (min.) |
| Sulphur (as S) | **23.0 – 24.0 %** |
| Free acidity (as H₂SO₄) | **≤ 0.05 %** |
| Moisture | **≤ 0.5 %** |
| Crystal size | Coarse crystals preferred (less caking) |
| Colour | White to off-white |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Reaction Works

The metathesis reaction is governed by **Le Chatelier's Principle**: one of the products (CaCO₃) is highly insoluble, so its precipitation continuously removes product from the aqueous phase, pulling the equilibrium toward (NH₄)₂SO₄ formation.

### 2.1.1 Solubility Considerations

| Compound | Solubility in water at 25 °C (g/100 g H₂O) | Behaviour in reaction |
|---|---|---|
| (NH₄)₂CO₃ | Highly soluble (decomposes) | Stays in solution; source of NH₄⁺ and CO₃²⁻ |
| CaSO₄·2H₂O | ~0.21 (sparingly soluble) | Slowly dissolves; source of Ca²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ |
| (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 76.4 (very soluble) | Stays in solution — desired product |
| CaCO₃ | 0.0014 (practically insoluble) | **Precipitates → drives equilibrium** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving Force:** The **solubility product K_sp of CaCO₃ (≈ 3.3 × 10⁻⁹)** is many orders of magnitude lower than that of CaSO₄ (K_sp ≈ 4.93 × 10⁻⁵). This huge difference in solubility is what makes the swap thermodynamically favourable.

### 2.1.2 Ionic Picture of the Reaction

When (NH₄)₂CO₃ dissolves it yields 2 NH₄⁺ and CO₃²⁻. When CaSO₄·2H₂O dissolves it yields Ca²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ (slowly, because gypsum is sparingly soluble). The ionic swap is:

$$Ca^{2+} + CO_3^{2-} \longrightarrow CaCO_3 \downarrow$$

$$2\,NH_4^{+} + SO_4^{2-} \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 \text{ (in solution)}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Chemistry Logic

1. **Step 1 — Preparation of (NH₄)₂CO₃ liquor:** Gaseous ammonia and CO₂ are absorbed in water to give ammonium carbonate solution:
$$\;2\,NH_3 + CO_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2CO_3\;$$
2. **Step 2 — Grinding of gypsum:** Gypsum is ground to **60 – 80 mesh (≈ 180 – 250 µm)** to maximise surface area for dissolution.
3. **Step 3 — Reaction in stirred tank:** The gypsum slurry is fed into a stirred reactor along with (NH₄)₂CO₃ solution at **60 – 70 °C** and **slightly ammoniacal pH (8 – 8.5)**. CO₂ evolution is minimised by holding NH₃ in slight excess.
4. **Step 4 — Filtration:** The CaCO₃ slurry is filtered (rotary vacuum filter); the cake is washed and sent to a cement kiln or sold as limestone substitute.
5. **Step 5 — Evaporation:** The clear (NH₄)₂SO₄ filtrate is evaporated in **multiple-effect evaporators** until saturation (~40 – 42 % w/w).
6. **Step 6 — Crystallisation:** The hot saturated liquor is sent to **vacuum crystallisers** or **swenson crystallisers**; crystals form at ~50 °C.
7. **Step 7 — Centrifugation & Drying:** Crystals are centrifuged, washed with a little water, then dried in a rotary / fluidised-bed dryer to < 0.5 % moisture.
8. **Step 8 — Bagging:** Cooled crystals are sieved and packed in 50 kg HDPE bags.

## 2.3 Process Parameters That Matter (Why & How)

| Parameter | Why it matters | Typical value |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Higher T speeds dissolution of gypsum but loses NH₃; too low slows reaction | **60 – 70 °C** |
| **pH** | Must be alkaline (8–8.5) to keep NH₃ as NH₄⁺ and avoid CO₂ loss | **8 – 8.5** |
| **Particle size of gypsum** | Finer particles → faster dissolution | **< 250 µm** |
| **Reaction time** | Need complete conversion; CaSO₄ is sparingly soluble | **2 – 4 hours** |
| **Agitation** | Improves mass transfer between solid gypsum and liquid | **300 – 500 rpm** |
| **Excess NH₃** | Slight excess prevents CaCO₃ dissolution back into solution | **2 – 5 % over stoichiometric** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(NH_4)_2CO_3 + CaSO_4\cdot 2H_2O \to (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 + 2H_2O$ | Main metathesis reaction |
| 2 | $\text{Theoretical yield of } (NH_4)_2SO_4 = \dfrac{132.14 \times m_{CaSO_4\cdot 2H_2O}}{172.17}$ | From gypsum input |
| 3 | $\text{N content} = \dfrac{2 \times 14}{132.14} \times 100 = 21.19\,\%$ | Fixed by stoichiometry |
| 4 | $\text{S content} = \dfrac{32.06}{132.14} \times 100 = 24.26\,\%$ | Fixed by stoichiometry |
| 5 | $\% \text{ Conversion} = \dfrac{m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \text{ obtained}}}{m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \text{ theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Reaction efficiency |
| 6 | $\text{Plant efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Actual output}}{\text{Theoretical output from limiting reactant}} \times 100$ | Overall plant performance |
| 7 | $K_{sp}(CaCO_3) = [Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}] = 3.3 \times 10^{-9}$ | Solubility equilibrium |
| 8 | $K_{sp}(CaSO_4) = [Ca^{2+}][SO_4^{2-}] = 4.93 \times 10^{-5}$ | Solubility equilibrium |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{rxn}^{298} \approx -32.6 \; kJ/mol$ | Mildly exothermic |
| 10 | $\text{Heat of crystallisation of } (NH_4)_2SO_4 \approx 47 \; kJ/kg$ | Needed for evaporator design |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Phosphoric acid industry integration:** Every tonne of phosphoric acid produces ~4.5–5 tonnes of phosphogypsum. Converting this to (NH₄)₂SO₄ eliminates a waste liability and creates a marketable co-product.
* **Sulphur recovery loop:** The process is essentially a route to **fix sulphur from waste gypsum** into a bioavailable form for crops.
* **CO₂ sequestration bonus:** For every tonne of (NH₄)₂SO₄, ~0.43 tonnes of CaCO₃ are also produced — useful as **cement raw meal or flue-gas desulphurisation agent**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical (NH₄)₂SO₄ Yield per Tonne of Gypsum

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked numerical** in KTU exams on this topic. Master it.

**Given:** 1000 kg of pure CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum) is reacted completely with stoichiometric (NH₄)₂CO₃.

**Molecular weights (already verified):**
* $M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = 40.08 + 32.06 + 4(16.00) + 2(18.02) = 172.17 \; g/mol$
* $M_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 2(14.01 + 4 \times 1.008) + 32.06 + 4(16.00) = 132.14 \; g/mol$

**Step 1 — Moles of gypsum available:**

$$n_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1000 \; g}{172.17 \; g/mol}$$

$$n_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = 5808.20 \; mol \; \text{(per tonne)}$$

**Step 2 — From stoichiometry, 1 mol gypsum → 1 mol (NH₄)₂SO₄:**

$$n_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 5808.20 \; mol$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles to mass:**

$$m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 5808.20 \; mol \times 132.14 \; g/mol$$

$$m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 7.6749 \times 10^{5} \; g = 767.49 \; kg \; \text{per tonne of gypsum}$$

**Final Generalised Formula:**

$$\boxed{\;Y_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} \;=\; \dfrac{132.14}{172.17} \times m_{gypsum} \;=\; 0.7675 \times m_{gypsum}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield ratio = 132.14 / 172.17 = 0.7675 (≈ 76.75 %).** Always remember this number — it is the **KTU examiner's favourite**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Required (NH₄)₂CO₃ for Stoichiometric Conversion

For every **172.17 kg gypsum**, **96.09 kg (NH₄)₂CO₃** is needed.

Per tonne of gypsum:

$$m_{(NH_4)_2CO_3} = \dfrac{96.09}{172.17} \times 1000 = 558.06 \; kg$$

**With 5 % excess (industrial practice):**

$$m_{(NH_4)_2CO_3, \; actual} = 558.06 \times 1.05 = 585.96 \; kg$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Exam Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2022, Module 2, 14-mark variant]:**
> *A fertilizer plant produces ammonium sulphate by reacting gypsum with ammonium carbonate solution. If 1500 kg/day of gypsum of 92 % purity is fed, and the overall plant yield efficiency is 88 %, calculate:*
> *(a) The actual mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄ produced per day.*
> *(b) The mass of CaCO₃ by-product formed per day.*

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Pure gypsum fed per day:**

$$m_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O, \; pure} = 1500 \times 0.92 = 1380 \; kg/day$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical (NH₄)₂SO₄ from pure gypsum (using 3.1):**

$$m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \; theoretical} = 0.7675 \times 1380 = 1059.15 \; kg/day$$

**Step 3 — Apply plant efficiency (88 %):**

$$m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \; actual} = 1059.15 \times 0.88 = 932.05 \; kg/day$$

$$\boxed{m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \; actual} \approx 932.05 \; kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of CaCO₃ by-product:**

Stoichiometry: 1 mol gypsum → 1 mol CaCO₃, so:

$$\dfrac{m_{CaCO_3}}{100.09} = \dfrac{m_{CaSO_4\cdot 2H_2O, \; pure}}{172.17}$$

$$m_{CaCO_3, \; theoretical} = \dfrac{100.09}{172.17} \times 1380 = 802.32 \; kg/day$$

Applying same efficiency:

$$m_{CaCO_3, \; actual} = 802.32 \times 0.88 = 706.04 \; kg/day$$

$$\boxed{m_{CaCO_3, \; actual} \approx 706.04 \; kg/day}$$

## 3.4 Mass Balance Table (Per 1000 kg Pure Gypsum Fed)

| Stream | Compound | Mass in (kg) | Mass out (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed | CaSO₄·2H₂O | 1000.00 | – |
| Feed | (NH₄)₂CO₃ | 558.06 | – |
| Product | (NH₄)₂SO₄ | – | 767.49 |
| By-product | CaCO₃ | – | 581.40 |
| By-product | H₂O (released + feed) | – | 209.17 |
| Total | | **1558.06** | **1558.06** |

> [!NOTE]
> Mass is balanced. Note that 209.17 kg of water appears on the product side — this comes from 2 × 18.02 g per mole of gypsum × 5808 mol = **209.27 kg** (small rounding).

## 3.5 Energy Balance (Quick Estimate)

For 1 tonne gypsum/day at 88 % efficiency:

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 767.5 kg |
| Actual (NH₄)₂SO₄ | 675.4 kg |
| Heat released by reaction | $-32.6 \; kJ/mol \times 5808 \; mol = -189.3 \; MJ$ |
| Heat needed for evaporation (water to remove from filtrate) | ~3.5 – 4.0 GJ per tonne product |
| Net heat demand | **Supplied by 1.0 – 1.2 tonne of steam** per tonne of gypsum |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables on Yield

| Variable ↑ (increase) | Reaction rate | (NH₄)₂SO₄ yield | Purity | Energy cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature (up to 80 °C) | ↑ faster | ↑ slightly | ↓ if NH₃ loss | ↑ (heating) |
| Particle fineness | ↑ much faster | ↑ | ≈ unchanged | ↑ (grinding) |
| Agitation speed | ↑ faster | ↑ | ↑ better washing | ↑ (power) |
| (NH₄)₂CO₃ excess | ↑ faster | ↑ marginally | ↑ | ↑ (reagent cost) |
| Residence time (up to 4 h) | ≈ plateau | ↑ to ~98 % | ↑ | ↑ (vessel size) |
| pH (8 – 8.5 optimum) | ↑ at optimum | ↑ at optimum | ↑ | neutral |
