# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Write the stoichiometric reaction for the manufacture of ammonium sulphate from ammonium carbonate and gypsum. Why is this reaction considered a metathesis (double-decomposition) reaction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The balanced stoichiometric reaction is:

$$(NH_4)_2CO_3 + CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 \downarrow + 2H_2O$$

It is a **metathesis / double-decomposition reaction** because **two compounds exchange their cationic partners**: the (NH₄⁺) cation originally paired with CO₃²⁻ swaps to pair with SO₄²⁻, while Ca²⁺ originally paired with SO₄²⁻ swaps to pair with CO₃²⁻. The reaction is **driven forward by the precipitation of insoluble CaCO₃** (K_sp ≈ 3.3 × 10⁻⁹).

**Marking split:**
- Correct equation → **2 marks**
- Metathesis explanation → **1 mark**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
*State three process conditions (temperature, pH, particle size) maintained during the reaction of gypsum with ammonium carbonate. Justify each in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Condition | Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **60 – 70 °C** | Promotes faster dissolution of sparingly-soluble gypsum without excessive loss of NH₃ as gas. |
| pH | **8 – 8.5 (slightly alkaline)** | Maintains NH₃ in NH₄⁺ form and prevents dissolution of CaCO₃ back into solution. |
| Gypsum particle size | **60 – 80 mesh (≤ 250 µm)** | Increases surface area, accelerating the dissolution-controlled reaction rate. |

**Marking split:** 1 mark per condition + justification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, modified]

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of ammonium sulphate from gypsum and ammonium carbonate**, listing all major equipment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plant processes **2000 kg/day of gypsum (95 % purity)** with **98 % reaction conversion** and **92 % overall plant yield efficiency**. Calculate:
1. Actual mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄ produced per day.
2. Mass of CaCO₃ by-product per day.
3. Mass of water released per day.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks)**

The flow diagram (rendered as text block for answer script) is:

```
Gypsum → Crusher → Ball Mill (60-80 mesh)
                                 ↓
                          Gypsum Slurry (20% solids)
                                 ↓
NH₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → Absorber → (NH₄)₂CO₃ solution
                                 ↓
            Stirred Reactor (60-70 °C, pH 8-8.5)
                                 ↓
                Holding Tank (2-4 h residence)
                                 ↓
            Rotary Vacuum Filter
            ↓                  ↓
   CaCO₃ cake (cement)    (NH₄)₂SO₄ filtrate
                                 ↓
                  Multiple-Effect Evaporator
                                 ↓
                    Vacuum Crystalliser (50 °C)
                                 ↓
                       Pusher Centrifuge
                                 ↓
                     Fluidised Bed Dryer
                                 ↓
                       Screening & Bagging
```

**Key equipment list (write any 5 for full marks):**
1. Ball mill for gypsum grinding
2. Ammonium carbonate absorber
3. Stirred reactor with agitator
4. Rotary vacuum filter
5. Multiple-effect evaporator
6. Vacuum crystalliser
7. Centrifuge
8. Fluidised bed dryer

**Process logic:** Each equipment serves a specific mass-transfer / heat-transfer / solid-liquid separation duty. The reaction is heterogeneous (solid gypsum + liquid ammonium carbonate) → requires good agitation. CaCO₃ is filtered off to drive equilibrium. Evaporation-crystallisation gives the solid product.

### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Pure gypsum fed per day:

$$m_{gypsum,\; pure} = 2000 \times 0.95 = 1900 \; kg/day$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical (NH₄)₂SO₄ (using mass-yield ratio 0.7675):

$$m_{theoretical} = 0.7675 \times 1900 = 1458.25 \; kg/day$$

**Step 3:** Overall plant efficiency = 92 %, so:

$$\boxed{m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4, \; actual} = 1458.25 \times 0.92 = 1341.59 \; kg/day}$$

**Step 4:** Theoretical CaCO₃ (mass-yield ratio = 100.09 / 172.17 = 0.5813):

$$m_{CaCO_3, \; theoretical} = 0.5813 \times 1900 = 1104.47 \; kg/day$$

Applying 92 % plant efficiency:

$$\boxed{m_{CaCO_3, \; actual} = 1104.47 \times 0.92 = 1016.11 \; kg/day}$$

**Step 5:** Theoretical H₂O released (mass-yield ratio = 36.04 / 172.17 = 0.2093):

$$m_{H_2O, \; theoretical} = 0.2093 \times 1900 = 397.67 \; kg/day$$

Applying 92 % efficiency:

$$\boxed{m_{H_2O, \; actual} = 397.67 \times 0.92 = 365.86 \; kg/day}$$

**Marking split:**
- Pure gypsum → **1 mark**
- (NH₄)₂SO₄ actual → **2 marks**
- CaCO₃ actual → **2 marks**
- H₂O actual → **1 mark**
- Units & final answer → **1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2022, modified]

**(a)** Discuss the **engineering significance** of using by-product gypsum (from phosphoric acid plants) as a feedstock for ammonium sulphate manufacture. Highlight at least **three industrial advantages and two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **solubility products** of CaCO₃ and CaSO₄, explain **why the metathesis reaction between (NH₄)₂CO₃ and CaSO₄·2H₂O proceeds to completion in the forward direction**. Estimate the equilibrium concentration of Ca²⁺ remaining in solution at pH 8.5 if the residual CO₃²⁻ concentration is 1 × 10⁻⁵ M. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Industrial Advantages & Limitations (7 marks)**

**Three Industrial Advantages:**

1. **Waste valorisation** — Phosphoric acid plants generate ~5 tonnes of phosphogypsum per tonne of P₂O₅. Using it as feedstock converts a waste-disposal liability into a marketable nitrogen fertilizer, reducing landfill burden and complying with environmental norms.

2. **Sulphur recycling** — The route recovers sulphur (locked in CaSO₄) as bioavailable sulphur in (NH₄)₂SO₄, important for oilseed and pulse crops that suffer from sulphur deficiency in Indian soils.

3. **Co-product synergy** — The CaCO₃ by-product (~0.58 tonne per tonne gypsum) is an excellent raw meal for cement kilns (saving limestone mining), and can also be used in flue-gas desulphurisation (FGD) and paper manufacture.

**Two Limitations:**

1. **High energy intensity** — Gypsum is sparingly soluble (0.21 g/100 g water); the reaction is slow, requiring fine grinding, prolonged residence time (~2–4 h) and large reactor volumes.

2. **Purity concerns** — Phosphogypsum contains fluoride, phosphate, and radionuclide impurities that may carry over to (NH₄)₂SO₄; extensive purification (washing, pH control) is needed, adding to capital cost.

### **Part (b) — Thermodynamic Justification (7 marks)**

**Why the reaction proceeds to completion:**

The driving force is the **precipitation of CaCO₃**, whose solubility product is several orders of magnitude lower than that of CaSO₄:

$$K_{sp}(CaSO_4) = [Ca^{2+}][SO_4^{2-}] = 4.93 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$K_{sp}(CaCO_3) = [Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}] = 3.3 \times 10^{-9}$$

The ratio:

$$\dfrac{K_{sp}(CaSO_4)}{K_{sp}(CaCO_3)} = \dfrac{4.93 \times 10^{-5}}{3.3 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.49 \times 10^{4}$$

This means **CaCO₃ is ~15 000 times less soluble than CaSO₄**; therefore, the moment Ca²⁺ and CO₃²⁻ meet, they precipitate as CaCO₃, continuously pulling the equilibrium toward (NH₄)₂SO₄ formation.

**Equilibrium Ca²⁺ calculation at pH 8.5 with [CO₃²⁻] = 1 × 10⁻⁵ M:**

From K_sp(CaCO₃):

$$[Ca^{2+}] = \dfrac{K_{sp}(CaCO_3)}{[CO_3^{2-}]}$$

$$[Ca^{2+}] = \dfrac{3.3 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$[Ca^{2+}] = 3.3 \times 10^{-4} \; M$$

$$\boxed{[Ca^{2+}]_{eq} = 3.3 \times 10^{-4} \; mol/L}$$

This is a very low Ca²⁺ concentration, confirming **complete removal of calcium from solution** — the equilibrium lies far to the right.

**Marking split:**
- Advantages → **3 marks (1 each)**
- Limitations → **2 marks (1 each)**
- K_sp ratio explanation → **1 mark**
- Numerical [Ca²⁺] → **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing molecular weights:** Many students use M = 136 for (NH₄)₂SO₄ instead of **132.14**. This single error cascades into wrong yield, wrong CaCO₃ and wrong H₂O mass. **Always cross-verify with periodic table atomic masses.**
> 2. **Forgetting to apply purity first:** When gypsum is given as 92 % or 95 % purity, students often skip the purity correction and compute yield on raw feed. This loses **1–2 marks** per numerical.
> 3. **Mixing up plant efficiency vs reaction conversion:** Plant efficiency < reaction conversion. Apply conversion to theoretical yield first, THEN plant efficiency — or apply combined factor if stated explicitly.
> 4. **Sign of ΔH_rxn:** The reaction is **mildly exothermic** (≈ –32.6 kJ/mol). Writing it as endothermic loses **0.5 marks** in thermodynamic sub-questions.
> 5. **Spelling errors in CaCO₃, gypsum, ammonium sulphate** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for incorrect chemical nomenclature.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ammonium Sulphate from (NH₄)₂CO₃ + Gypsum**

- **Main reaction:** $(NH_4)_2CO_3 + CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O \to (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 \downarrow + 2H_2O$
- **Process type:** Metathesis / double-decomposition; heterogeneous solid–liquid reaction.
- **Driving force:** Precipitation of CaCO₃ (K_sp = 3.3 × 10⁻⁹ ≪ K_sp of CaSO₄ = 4.93 × 10⁻⁵).
- **Operating conditions:** T = 60 – 70 °C; pH = 8 – 8.5; gypsum ≤ 250 µm; 2 – 4 h residence; 2 – 5 % NH₃ excess.
- **Mass yield factor (NH₄)₂SO₄ from gypsum:** **0.7675 kg product per kg gypsum**.
- **Mass yield factor for CaCO₃:** **0.5813 kg CaCO₃ per kg gypsum**.
- **Mass yield factor for H₂O:** **0.2093 kg per kg gypsum**.
- **Product grade:** ≥ 20.6 % N, ~24 % S, ≤ 0.5 % moisture, ≥ 98.5 % purity.
- **Major equipment in order:** Ball mill → Slurry tank → Reactor → Holding tank → Rotary vacuum filter → Evaporator → Vacuum crystalliser → Centrifuge → Fluid-bed dryer → Screener → Bagging.
- **By-product utilisation:** CaCO₃ → cement raw meal, FGD reagent, paper filler.
- **Industrial advantage:** Consumes waste phosphogypsum; recycles sulphur and ammonia value.
- **Process limitation:** Slow due to sparingly-soluble gypsum; needs fine grinding and large reactors.
- **Energy demand:** ~3.5 – 4.0 GJ per tonne product for evaporation; exothermic reaction only marginally helpful.
- **Comparison to other routes:** Cheaper than synthetic NH₃ + H₂SO₄ route when cheap gypsum is available; less pure than synthetic route.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ammonium Chloride by Direct Neutralization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ammonium Chloride by Direct Neutralization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ammonium Chloride (NH₄Cl)** is a white, crystalline, water-soluble nitrogenous fertilizer containing approximately **25% nitrogen (ammoniacal form)** and **66% chlorine (Cl⁻)**, with a molecular weight of **53.49 g/mol**. It is also chemically known as *sal ammoniac* or *muriate of ammonia*. In fertilizer technology, it is classified as a **physiologically acidic** straight nitrogenous fertilizer belonging to the **ammonical nitrogen** category.

The **Direct Neutralization process** is the most fundamental and industrially straightforward method of manufacturing ammonium chloride, involving a direct acid-base reaction between **anhydrous ammonia gas (NH₃)** and **hydrochloric acid (HCl)** in a stoichiometric ratio, producing NH₄Cl as the principal product.

$$ \text{NH}_3(g) + \text{HCl}(aq) \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(aq) \quad \Delta H = -176\ \text{kJ/mol} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - Straight Nitrogenous Fertilizers):**
> The Direct Neutralization route is a **CO2 / CO3 mapped topic** under "Manufacture of Ammonium Chloride." Students are expected to know raw materials, reaction stoichiometry, process flow, material balance, and limitations of this method.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Think of ammonium chloride production by direct neutralization like **mixing vinegar (acid) and baking soda (base) on a small scale to make a salt** — but on an industrial scale with a much stronger acid and a gaseous base.

**Real-world analogy:**
Imagine a **lemonade stand scenario**:
- You have a tank of lemon juice (HCl) — sour, acidic.
- You slowly bubble in ammonia gas (NH₃) — a "basic" smelling gas.
- The moment they meet, they instantly neutralize, releasing heat (the tank warms up) and producing ammonium chloride salt in solution.
- Just like in lemonade-making, you then evaporate the water to get the pure sugar/salt crystals — here you evaporate the water to get pure NH₄Cl crystals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HCl and not H₂SO₄ or HNO₃?**
> Direct neutralization with **HCl** is the *only* economically viable acid-base route to make NH₄Cl because the chloride ion is the desired constituent of the product. Using H₂SO₄ would give (NH₄)₂SO₄, and HNO₃ would give NH₄NO₃ — different fertilizers entirely.

### 1.3 Physical & Chemical Constants (Standard Metrics)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | NH₄Cl |
| Molecular Weight | **53.49 g/mol** |
| Nitrogen Content | **25.4% (ammoniacal)** |
| Chlorine Content | **66.3%** |
| Density (solid) | **1.527 g/cm³** |
| Melting Point | **338 °C (sublimes)** |
| Solubility in water (20 °C) | **37.2 g/100 mL** |
| pH (0.1 M solution) | ~5.5 (acidic) |
| Heat of Reaction | **-176 kJ/mol (exothermic)** |
| Crystal System | Cubic (isometric) |
| Hygroscopicity | Slightly hygroscopic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physiological Acidity:** NH₄Cl leaves behind chloride (Cl⁻) ions in soil, which lower soil pH over time. Hence it is **physiologically acidic** — best suited for **alkaline soils** and crops like **paddy rice** (where Cl⁻ is tolerable).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background — The Acid-Base Neutralization Principle

Ammonium chloride formation is a classical **Bronsted-Lowry acid-base reaction**:

- **NH₃** acts as a **Brønsted base** (proton acceptor)
- **HCl** acts as a **Brønsted acid** (proton donor)
- The H⁺ from HCl is transferred to NH₃, forming the conjugate acid NH₄⁺ and conjugate base Cl⁻, which combine to form the ionic salt NH₄Cl.

The ionic dissociation in water:

$$ \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{Cl}^- $$

The ammonium ion in soil undergoes **nitrification** to release plant-available nitrogen:

$$ \text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> The released H⁺ ions are responsible for the **physiological acidity** of NH₄Cl in soil.

### 2.2 Process Steps — Engineering Logic

**Step 1: Raw Material Preparation**
- **Ammonia**: Anhydrous liquid NH₃ from storage tanks is vaporized.
- **Hydrochloric Acid**: Typically 20–30% w/w aqueous HCl (industrial grade) is charged into the neutralizer.

**Step 2: Neutralization (Absorption-Reaction)**
- NH₃ gas is sparged into dilute HCl in a **rubber-lined or glass-lined mild steel reactor** fitted with a **mechanical agitator**.
- The reaction is instantaneous and **highly exothermic**.
- Solution temperature is controlled between **60–100 °C** to prevent excessive evaporation of NH₃ and to keep NH₄Cl in solution (avoid premature crystallization that would coat reactor walls).

**Step 3: Heat Removal**
- The reactor is equipped with a **cooling jacket** or **cooling coils** to remove the heat of reaction.
- Removed heat can be recovered as low-pressure steam — making the process energy-efficient.

**Step 4: Concentration / Evaporation**
- The hot NH₄Cl solution (≈ 30–35% w/w) is fed to a **multiple-effect evaporator** (commonly double-effect) to concentrate it to **40–50% w/w**.
- Vacuum evaporation is preferred to lower boiling point and save energy.

**Step 5: Crystallization**
- Concentrated solution enters a **vacuum crystallizer** or **forced-circulation crystallizer**.
- NH₄Cl crystallizes out due to its high solubility dependence on temperature (retrograde solubility above ~ 90 °C, but normal below).

**Step 6: Solid-Liquid Separation**
- Crystal slurry goes to a **centrifuge** or **rotary vacuum filter**.
- Mother liquor is recycled back to the neutralizer.

**Step 7: Washing & Drying**
- Crystals are washed with a small quantity of cold water to remove adhering mother liquor.
- Drying is done in a **rotary dryer** or **fluidized bed dryer** at 100–110 °C to bring moisture below 0.5%.

**Step 8: Sizing & Packaging**
- Dried crystals are sieved, classified, and packed in **50 kg HDPE bags** or as required.

### 2.3 Operating Conditions Summary

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| HCl concentration (feed) | 20–30% w/w |
| NH₃ feed state | Anhydrous gas |
| Neutralizer temperature | 60–100 °C |
| pH at end of reaction | 6.5–7.0 (slightly acidic) |
| Reactor material | Rubber-lined / Glass-lined CS / FRP |
| Evaporator outlet concentration | 40–50% w/w NH₄Cl |
| Crystallizer temperature | 50–70 °C (vacuum) |
| Final product purity | ≥ 99% NH₄Cl |
| N content in product | 25.0–25.5% |
| Moisture | < 0.5% |
| Free acidity (as HCl) | < 0.05% |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_{\text{NH}_3} = \dfrac{W_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} \times 17}{53.49}$ | Stoichiometric NH₃ required (kg) |
| 2 | $n_{\text{HCl}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} \times 36.5}{53.49}$ | Stoichiometric pure HCl required (kg) |
| 3 | $n_{\text{HCl(comm)}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{HCl pure}}}{C_{\text{HCl}}/100}$ | Commercial acid quantity (kg) |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{W_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}}}{53.49} \times 176$ | Heat released (MJ) |
| 5 | $\%\ \text{N} = \dfrac{14}{53.49} \times 100 = 25.4$ | Nitrogen content of pure NH₄Cl |
| 6 | $\%\ \text{Cl} = \dfrac{35.5}{53.49} \times 100 = 66.3$ | Chlorine content |
| 7 | $\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{\text{Moles reacted}}{\text{Moles fed}} \times 100$ | Reactor conversion (%) |
| 8 | $\text{Overall yield} = \dfrac{W_{\text{product}}}{W_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Plant yield (%) |
| 9 | $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ | For control loop in neutralizer |
| 10 | $C_p(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl solution}) \approx 3.2\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ | Specific heat for energy balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Most KTU numericals on this topic ask for raw material requirement (NH₃ and commercial HCl) and heat released for a given tonnage of NH₄Cl — use formulas 1, 2, and 4 from the table.

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Agriculture (Primary)**: Used as a basal/top-dress fertilizer for **paddy (rice)**, **wheat**, **sugarcane**, and **coconut** in coastal/saline regions. India, China, Japan, Korea, and Vietnam are the major consumers.
- **Non-Fertilizer (Secondary) Applications**:
  - **Dry cell batteries** (acts as electrolyte)
  - **Galvanizing and tinning flux** in metal finishing
  - **Pharmaceutical formulations** (expectorant)
  - **Leather and textile industry** (dyeing)
  - **Cattle feed supplement** (non-protein nitrogen source for ruminants)
  - **Food industry** (E510 — salt substitute, yeast nutrient in bread-making)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation of the Reaction

**The governing chemical equation:**

$$ \text{NH}_3(g) + \text{HCl}(aq) \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(aq) + \text{Heat} $$

**Molecular weight computation:**

- Atomic mass of N = 14
- Atomic mass of H = 1 (× 4 in NH₄⁺)
- Atomic mass of Cl = 35.5

$$ M_{\text{NH}_3} = 14 + (3 \times 1) = 17\ \text{g/mol} $$

$$ M_{\text{HCl}} = 1 + 35.5 = 36.5\ \text{g/mol} $$

$$ M_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} = (14 + 4 \times 1) + 35.5 = 18 + 35.5 = 53.5\ \text{g/mol} $$

**Stoichiometric ratio (per kmol of NH₄Cl produced):**

$$ 1\ \text{kmol NH}_3 + 1\ \text{kmol HCl} \longrightarrow 1\ \text{kmol NH}_4\text{Cl} $$

Converting to mass basis:

$$ 17\ \text{kg NH}_3 + 36.5\ \text{kg HCl} \longrightarrow 53.5\ \text{kg NH}_4\text{Cl} $$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Material Balance

**Problem (KTU Standard Style):**
A fertilizer plant produces **1000 kg/day of pure NH₄Cl** by the direct neutralization route. Calculate:
(i) The quantity of **anhydrous NH₃** required (in kg).
(ii) The quantity of **30% w/w commercial HCl** required (in kg).
(iii) The total **heat released** in kJ.

**Given:**
- Product: 1000 kg NH₄Cl/day
- Commercial HCl concentration: 30% w/w
- $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = -176\ \text{kJ/mol}$

**Solution:**

**(i) Anhydrous NH₃ required:**

Moles of NH₄Cl produced:

$$ n_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} = \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{53.5\ \text{kg/kmol}} = 18.69\ \text{kmol/day} $$

By stoichiometry (1:1 ratio):

$$ n_{\text{NH}_3} = 18.69\ \text{kmol/day} $$

Converting to mass:

$$ W_{\text{NH}_3} = 18.69\ \text{kmol} \times 17\ \text{kg/kmol} = \boxed{317.76\ \text{kg/day}} $$

**(ii) Quantity of 30% commercial HCl:**

Pure HCl needed (stoichiometric):

$$ n_{\text{HCl pure}} = 18.69\ \text{kmol/day} $$

$$ W_{\text{HCl pure}} = 18.69 \times 36.5 = 682.20\ \text{kg/day} $$

Since commercial acid is 30% w/w:

$$ W_{\text{HCl(comm.)}} = \frac{W_{\text{HCl pure}}}{0.30} = \frac{682.20}{0.30} = \boxed{2274.0\ \text{kg/day}} $$

**(iii) Heat released:**

$$ Q = n_{\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}} \times \Delta H = 18.69\ \text{kmol} \times 176{,}000\ \text{kJ/kmol} $$

$$ Q = \boxed{3{,}289{,}440\ \text{kJ/day} \approx 3.29 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ/day}} $$

In terms of steam equivalent (latent heat of steam ≈ 2260 kJ/kg):

$$ W_{\text{steam}} = \frac{3.29 \times 10^6}{2260} \approx 1456\ \text{kg steam/day} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other NH₄Cl Production Routes

| Parameter | **Direct Neutralization** | **Solvay Process Byproduct** | **Double Decomposition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials** | NH₃ + HCl | NaCl + NH₃ + CO₂ | (NH₄)₂SO₄ + NaCl |
| **Byproducts** | None (clean) | NaHCO₃, CaCl₂ | Na₂SO₄ |
| **Purity of Product** | **Very High (≥ 99%)** | Moderate (60–80%) | Moderate–High |
| **Capital Cost** | **Low** | High | Moderate |
| **Operating Cost** | Moderate (HCl cost) | High | High |
| **Corrosion** | **Severe (HCl)** | Moderate | Moderate–Severe |
| **Energy** | **Exothermic (heat recoverable)** | Energy intensive | Energy intensive |
| **Capacity Suitability** | **Small to large** | Very large | Small to medium |
| **Process Complexity** | **Simple** | Complex | Moderate |
| **HCl Availability** | **Limiting factor** | Not required | Not required |
| **CO₂ Emission** | Negligible | High | Low–Moderate |

### 3.4 Heat Balance in the Neutralizer

Let the reactor hold $W$ kg of NH₄Cl solution of concentration $C$ (mass fraction) entering at $T_1$ °C. Final temperature $T_2$.

Energy released by reaction:

$$ Q_{\text{rxn}} = \frac{W \cdot C}{53.5} \times 176{,}000\ \text{kJ} $$

Energy to raise solution temperature:

$$ Q_{\text{heat}} = W \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) $$

Energy to be removed by cooling water (in cooling coils):

$$ Q_{\text{cool}} = Q_{\text{rxn}} - Q_{\text{heat}} - Q_{\text{loss}} $$

Cooling water quantity:

$$ m_{\text{cw}} = \frac{Q_{\text{cool}}}{C_{p,\text{w}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{cw}}} $$

Where:
- $C_{p,\text{w}} = 4.18\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $\Delta T_{\text{cw}} = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}$ (typically 5–10 °C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The exothermic nature of the direct neutralization is a *double-edged sword*:
> - ✅ **Advantage**: Reaction heat can be recovered as low-pressure steam (≈ 1456 kg steam per ton of NH₄Cl).
> - ❌ **Disadvantage**: If uncontrolled, localized overheating (> 338 °C) causes **sublimation of NH₄Cl** (it sublimes before melting) leading to fouling and loss of product.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Anhydrous NH3 Storage Tank] --> B[NH3 Vaporizer]
    B --> C[Neutralizer Reactor]
    D[Commercial HCl Storage 30 percent] --> E[HCl Dilution Tank]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Crystallizer Vacuum Type]
    F --> G[Centrifuge]
    G --> H[Rotary Dryer]
    H --> I[Screening and Classification]
    I --> J[Packaging 50 kg HDPE Bags]
    G --> K[Mother Liquor Recycle Tank]
    K --> C
    C --> L[Cooling Jacket]
    L --> M[Cooling Water Recirculation]
    F --> N[Vacuum System Steam Ejector]
    N --> O[Condenser]
    O --> P[Condensate Collection]
```

**Description of Flow Block Interactions:**

1. **A → B → C**: Liquid NH₃ from pressurized storage is vaporized and sparged into the **Neutralizer Reactor (C)**, which contains dilute HCl from **D → E → C**.
2. **C → F**: The hot NH₄Cl solution (60–100 °C) flows to a **Vacuum Crystallizer (F)** where evaporation cooling causes NH₄Cl to crystallize.
3. **F → G → H**: Crystal slurry is centrifuged (G) and the wet cake enters the **Rotary Dryer (H)**.
4. **G → K → C**: Mother liquor is recycled back to the neutralizer to recover dissolved NH₄Cl (improves yield).
5. **C → L → M**: The exothermic reaction heat is removed via the **Cooling Jacket (L)** and recovered partially.
6. **F → N → O → P**: Water vapor from crystallizer is condensed in **Steam Ejector Vacuum System (N)** and **Condenser (O)**.

### 4.2 Reactor Schematic — Internal Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[NH3 Inlet Pipe with Sparger Ring] --> R2[Acid Inlet Top]
    R2 --> R3[Neutralizer Vessel Rubber Lined]
    R3 --> R4[Mechanical Agitator with PTFE Impeller]
    R3 --> R5[Baffles 4 nos]
    R3 --> R6[Cooling Coil SS316]
    R3 --> R7[pH Electrode]
    R3 --> R8[Temperature Sensor PT100]
    R3 --> R9[Discharge to Crystallizer]
```

**Reactor Key Features:**
- **R3**: Cylindrical vertical vessel, **rubber-lined mild steel** (HCl-resistant).
- **R4**: Agitator ensures uniform mixing; critical to avoid local hot spots.
- **R6**: Cooling coil of **SS-316** removes the 176 kJ/mol of reaction heat.
- **R7, R8**: In-line pH and temperature sensors feed back to PLC for NH₃ flow control.

### 4.3 Mass Flow Sankey Summary (per 1000 kg NH₄Cl)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Anhydrous NH3: 317.8 kg] --> N1[Neutralizer]
    M2[Commercial HCl 30 percent: 2274.0 kg] --> N1
    N1 --> P1[NH4Cl Crystals: 1000 kg]
    N1 --> P2[Evaporated Water: 1591.2 kg]
    N1 --> P3[Heat Recovered: 3.29 x 10^6 kJ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** When asked for a block diagram, students should mark all **inlet streams, outlet streams, recycle streams, and the heat removal loop** to get full marks. A PFD without energy integration indicators is usually marked down by 2 marks out of 7.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is ammonium chloride? Give its nitrogen content and one important use.*

**Model Answer:**
Ammonium chloride (NH₄Cl) is a white crystalline **nitrogenous fertilizer** containing **25.4% nitrogen** in the ammoniacal form and 66.3% chlorine. It is physiologically acidic in nature. It is widely used as a **nitrogen fertilizer for paddy (rice) cultivation** in Southeast Asia, and also as an **electrolyte in dry cell batteries**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is hydrochloric acid preferred over sulfuric acid for the direct neutralization route of ammonium chloride manufacture?*

**Model Answer:**
Hydrochloric acid (HCl) is preferred because the **chloride ion (Cl⁻) is a desired constituent** of the product NH₄Cl. Using H₂SO₄ would yield (NH₄)₂SO₄ and using HNO₃ would yield NH₄NO₃ — different fertilizers entirely. Furthermore, HCl is available as a **cheap byproduct** from chlor-alkali industries and organic chlorination processes, making the process economically viable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between the two questions. Students must attempt ONE of the two.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Process Description + Material Balance*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of ammonium chloride by direct neutralization** of ammonia with hydrochloric acid. List the raw materials and operating conditions.

**Model Answer:**

**Raw Materials:**
- Anhydrous ammonia (NH₃) gas
- Hydrochloric acid (HCl), 20–30% w/w

**Process Description (Step-wise):**

1. **Acid Charging**: Dilute HCl (20–30%) is charged into a rubber-lined neutralizer fitted with an agitator, cooling coils, and a vent for HCl fume.
2. **Ammonia Sparging**: Anhydrous NH₃ gas is sparged slowly into the acid solution. The reaction:
   $$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{Heat}$$
   occurs with release of 176 kJ/mol.
3. **Heat Removal**: The exothermic heat is removed via cooling jacket/coils; reactor temperature is maintained at 60–100 °C.
4. **Evaporation**: The hot NH₄Cl solution is concentrated in a multiple-effect evaporator to 40–50% w/w.
5. **Crystallization**: The concentrated solution enters a vacuum crystallizer; NH₄Cl crystals form.
6. **Filtration/Centrifugation**: Crystals separated from mother liquor (recycled).
7. **Drying**: Rotary or fluidized-bed dryer reduces moisture to < 0.5%.
8. **Packaging**: Packed in 50 kg HDPE bags.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Neutralizer: 60–100 °C, pH 6.5–7.0
- Evaporator: 50–80 °C under vacuum
- Drying: 100–110 °C

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plant produces **1500 kg/day of pure NH₄Cl** by direct neutralization. Calculate the **(i)** mass of NH₃ required, **(ii)** mass of 32% commercial HCl required, and **(iii)** heat released in kJ. $\Delta H = -176$ kJ/mol.

**Model Solution:**

Moles of NH₄Cl:

$$ n = \frac{1500}{53.5} = 28.04\ \text{kmol/day} $$

**(i) Mass of NH₃:**

$$ W_{\text{NH}_3} = 28.04 \times 17 = \boxed{476.6\ \text{kg/day}} $$

**(ii) Pure HCl required:**

$$ W_{\text{HCl pure}} = 28.04 \times 36.5 = 1023.5\ \text{kg/day} $$

Commercial 32% HCl:

$$ W_{\text{HCl(comm.)}} = \frac{1023.5}{0.32} = \boxed{3198.4\ \text{kg/day}} $$

**(iii) Heat released:**

$$ Q = 28.04 \times 176{,}000 = \boxed{4.935 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ/day}} $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Process Comparison + Energy & Limitations*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **direct neutralization route** of NH₄Cl manufacture with the **Solvay process byproduct route** in terms of raw materials, product purity, capital cost, and process complexity.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct Neutralization | Solvay Byproduct |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials** | NH₃ + HCl | NaCl + NH₃ + CO₂ + CaCO₃ |
| **Purity of NH₄Cl** | High (≥ 99%) | Moderate (60–80%, mixed with NaCl) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High (limestone kiln, ammonia recovery) |
| **Process Complexity** | Simple, single-step | Complex, multi-stage |
| **Byproducts** | Water only | NaHCO₃ (or Na₂CO₃) + CaCl₂ |
| **Energy** | Exothermic (recoverable) | Highly energy intensive |
| **Corrosion Issues** | Severe (HCl) | Moderate |
| **HCl Dependency** | Required | Not required |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | Negligible | High |

**Conclusion:** Direct neutralization is preferred where cheap byproduct HCl is available; Solvay route is chosen when integrated with soda ash production.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **main engineering challenges** in the direct neutralization route and how they are overcome in industrial practice.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Corrosion by HCl**:
   - **Challenge**: Severe corrosion of carbon steel by HCl, especially at elevated temperatures.
   - **Solution**: Use **rubber-lined**, **glass-lined**, or **FRP reactors**; SS-316 for piping and coils.

2. **Heat Removal**:
   - **Challenge**: Highly exothermic reaction; risk of hot spots and NH₄Cl sublimation (> 338 °C).
   - **Solution**: **Cooling jacket + internal cooling coils**; controlled NH₃ feed rate; vigorous agitation; temperature feedback control.

3. **NH₃ Loss and Fume Control**:
   - **Challenge**: NH₃ is volatile; some may escape unreacted.
   - **Solution**: **Vapor absorption scrubber** at the vent using dilute HCl as absorbent; closed reactor design.

4. **Crystal Fouling**:
   - **Challenge**: NH₄Cl tends to deposit on cooler surfaces, fouling heat exchangers.
   - **Solution**: Maintain solution concentration below saturation in neutralizer; use scraped-surface crystallizers; periodic descaling with hot water.

5. **HCl Supply Consistency**:
   - **Challenge**: Industrial HCl is often a byproduct; quality varies.
   - **Solution**: **In-line acid analyzers** and **buffer storage** of 20–30% HCl to maintain uniform feed.

6. **Product Hygroscopicity and Caking**:
   - **Challenge**: NH₄Cl is slightly hygroscopic; caking during storage.
   - **Solution**: Final drying to < 0.5% moisture; **anti-caking agents** (e.g., magnesium carbonate); moisture-proof HDPE packaging.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Direct Neutralization Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting HCl purity/concentration**: When asked for "commercial HCl," students often compute *pure* HCl mass and forget to divide by concentration (e.g., 0.30 for 30%). **Penalty: -2 marks**.
> 2. **❌ Wrong molecular weight**: Using 53.49 vs 53.5 — minor, but the unit conversion (g/mol vs kg/kmol) is where most errors creep in. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
> 3. **❌ Missing the recycle stream**: In the flow diagram, students often forget the **mother liquor recycle** from centrifuge back to the neutralizer. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
> 4. **❌ Confusing sublimation with melting**: NH₄Cl **sublimes at 338 °C** without melting — students often write "melting point." **Penalty: -1 mark**.
> 5. **❌ Not mentioning rubber-lining**: Forgetting the material of construction in the reactor — examiners mark this strictly. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
> 6. **❌ Heat sign convention**: Some students forget to put the **negative sign** for exothermic heat; energy released must be explicitly stated. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
> 7. **❌ No vent scrubber mentioned**: NH₃ is volatile — industrial PFDs must show a **scrubber/absorber** at the vent to capture unreacted NH₃.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Ammonium Chloride by Direct Neutralization**

- **Reaction**: $\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ ($\Delta H = -176$ kJ/mol, **exothermic**)
- **Molecular weight of NH₄Cl**: **53.5 g/mol**
- **N content**: **25.4%** (ammoniacal)
- **Cl content**: **66.3%**
- **Physical nature**: White crystalline, slightly hygroscopic, **sublimes at 338 °C**
- **Physiological effect**: **Acidic** (lowers soil pH)
- **Preferred reactor**: Rubber/glass-lined CS with SS-316 cooling coils and agitator
- **Operating temperature of neutralizer**: **60–100 °C**
- **Key advantage**: Simple process, high-purity product, exothermic (heat recoverable as steam)
- **Key disadvantage**: Severe corrosion by HCl, HCl supply dependency
- **Stoichiometry shortcut**: For 1000 kg NH₄Cl → 317.8 kg NH₃ + 682.2 kg pure HCl (≈ 2274 kg of 30% commercial HCl)
- **Heat released per ton**: ≈ **3.29 × 10⁶ kJ** (recoverable as ~1456 kg steam)
- **Use**: Nitrogen fertilizer (rice), dry-cell batteries, galvanizing flux, pharmaceutical
- **Always mention in PFD**: Mother liquor recycle, vent scrubber, cooling jacket, multiple-effect evaporator, vacuum crystallizer
- **Compared to Solvay route**: Direct neutralization gives **higher purity** and **lower capital cost** but **depends on HCl availability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacture and Application of MAP
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacture and Application of MAP

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP)** is a water-soluble, granular nitrogen-phosphorus fertilizer chemically designated as **ammonium dihydrogen phosphate**, with the molecular formula **NH₄H₂PO₄** and a molecular weight of **115.03 g/mol**. It is the world's most widely consumed phosphate-based fertilizer, supplying the two primary macronutrients—**Nitrogen (N)** and **Phosphorus (P)**—required for plant metabolism, root development, and energy transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT637, Module 3):**
> MAP is grouped under "Phosphate Fertilizers of Ammoniation Type." Students must master (a) the wet-process ammoniation reaction chemistry, (b) the pH-controlled stoichiometry that distinguishes MAP from DAP, and (c) the industrial pipe-reactor/preneutralizer process flow.

### 1.2 Guaranteed Nutrient Specification (Commercial Grade)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Commercial Designation |
|---|---|---|
| Total Nitrogen (as N) | **11.0 % (min)** | Elemental N |
| Available Phosphorus (as P₂O₅) | **52.0 % (min)** | Citrate + Water-soluble P₂O₅ |
| Water-soluble P₂O₅ | **48.0 % (min)** | Water-soluble fraction |
| Free acidity (as H₃PO₄) | **< 1.5 %** | Quality control metric |
| Moisture | **< 1.0 %** | Storage stability |
| Particle size (SGN) | **2.8 – 3.4 mm** | Granulometry |
| pH (10 % solution) | **4.0 – 4.5** | Acidic–near-neutral |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11-52-0?** The "11-52-0" grade is the universal N-P-K label, meaning 11 % N, 52 % P₂O₅, and 0 % K₂O. Compare this with DAP (18-46-0) and SSP (16-20-0) to appreciate MAP's exceptionally high P₂O₅ concentration per unit mass—making it the most P-dense commodity fertilizer on Earth.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Half-Reacted Lemon Juice" Picture

Imagine a lemon (citric acid) being slowly neutralized by baking soda (a mild alkali). If you add **only enough** soda to neutralize **half** the sourness, you get a mildly acidic, stable solution—the lemon's hydrogen ions are partially satisfied. Now scale this up:

- The "lemon" = **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)**—a triprotic acid with three acidic hydrogens.
- The "baking soda" = **anhydrous ammonia (NH₃)**—a gaseous alkali.
- Adding **one** molecule of NH₃ to **one** molecule of H₃PO₄ → exactly **half-neutralization** → MAP.
- Adding **two** molecules of NH₃ → fully neutralized to DAP.

Thus **MAP is the "halfway-neutralized salt"** of the phosphoric acid–ammonia reaction, which explains its mildly acidic pH (≈ 4) and its superior agronomic compatibility with alkaline soils and seed-row placement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reaction Chemistry — The Ammoniation Spectrum

Phosphoric acid possesses **three replaceable hydrogen ions**. Depending on the ammonia-to-acid mole ratio, three discrete ammonium phosphates can be synthesized:

| Stage | Reaction | pH of slurry | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 | NH₃ + H₃PO₄ → NH₄H₂PO₄ | **4.0 – 4.5** | **MAP** |
| Stage 2 | NH₃ + NH₄H₂PO₄ → (NH₄)₂HPO₄ | **7.5 – 8.0** | DAP |
| Stage 3 | NH₃ + (NH₄)₂HPO₄ → (NH₄)₃PO₄ | **> 13** (unstable) | Triammonium phosphate (TAP, decomposes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** Industrially, the ammoniation is **never** carried to Stage 3 because TAP thermally decomposes back to DAP + NH₃ above 70 °C, releasing ammonia and creating pollution. Therefore, fertilizer plants terminate ammoniation at Stage 2 (DAP) or Stage 1 (MAP) depending on the desired grade.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Choosing MAP Over DAP

The "Why" can be summarized in three engineering realities:

1. **Higher P₂O₅ concentration (52 % vs 46 %)** → less material to transport, store, and apply per unit P.
2. **Lower free ammonia** → safer seed-row placement (no ammonia toxicity to germinating seeds).
3. **Compatibility in bulk blends** → MAP does not react aggressively with urea or ammonium nitrate in mixed fertilizers, unlike DAP which causes "caking" via moisture migration.

## 2.3 Industrial Manufacturing Routes

### Route A — Conventional Preneutralizer Process (Historical Baseline)

This is the classical slurry-based route dominant until the 1980s. The flow consists of:

1. **Wet-process phosphoric acid (WPA)** of strength 40–54 % P₂O₅ is fed to a stainless-steel **preneutralizer vessel** agitated at 60–80 °C.
2. **Anhydrous ammonia** is sparged beneath the impeller; the exothermic acid-base reaction evolves **795 kJ per kg of NH₃ absorbed**.
3. The pH is held at **4.0–4.5** (Stage 1) by metering NH₃.
4. The hot slurry (≈ 115 °C, ~25 % moisture) overflows to an **evaporative crystallization zone** where MAP crystals nucleate.
5. The slurry then drops into a **rotary drum granulator**, where recycled undersize fines act as nuclei. Additional ammonia may be "post-fed" in the granulator for final pH trim.
6. Wet granules are dried in a **co-current rotary dryer** (fuel-fired, inlet 250 °C, outlet 90 °C), screened, cooled, and coated with anti-caking oil/dust.

### Route B — Pipe Reactor Process (Modern Industry Standard)

Since the 1990s, the **pipe reactor** (also called the "T-cross" or "cross-pipe" reactor) has replaced the preneutralizer in most new plants because it offers:

- **Higher conversion efficiency** (> 98 % acid neutralization in < 2 seconds).
- **Lower CAPEX** — no large agitated tank; just a 1.5–3 m corrosion-resistant pipe.
- **Better energy integration** — reaction heat is dissipated by water evaporation, yielding a drier product stream directly suitable for granulation.

**Operating principle:** Concentrated WPA (52–54 % P₂O₅) and gaseous NH₃ are injected through opposed jets into a small reactor pipe. The violent mixing produces a hot, molten/slurry MAP melt at **150–180 °C** which is sprayed directly onto a rolling bed of recycled fines in the granulator. The melt solidifies on the seed particles, building up granules in a single pass.

### Route C — Spherodizer / Spheroidizer Process (Pan Granulator Variant)

Used for premium, uniformly spherical MAP granules preferred for bulk blending and aerial application.

### Route D — Spray-Drum Granulation (Haldor Topsøe DRYLOX Type)

A hybrid where the pipe-reactor melt is sprayed onto a fluidized/rolling bed inside an integrated spray drum, eliminating the separate preneutralizer and the dryer (using process heat for evaporation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{\text{MAP}} = 14 + 4(1) + 1 + 2(30.97) + 4(15.99) = 115.03$ | Molar mass of NH₄H₂PO₄ | g/mol |
| 2 | $\%\text{N} = \dfrac{14.01}{115.03} \times 100 \approx 12.18\%$ | Theoretical N content | wt % |
| 3 | $\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \dfrac{141.94}{2 \times 115.03} \times 100 \approx 61.70\%$ | Theoretical P₂O₅ content | wt % (dehydrated basis) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{n_{\text{NH}_3}}{n_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4}} = 1.0 \pm 0.05$ | MAP stoichiometric ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{N:P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{11}{52} = 0.2115$ | Grade specification | ratio |
| 6 | $\Delta H_{rxn} = -795 \text{ kJ per kg NH}_3$ | Heat of neutralization | kJ/kg NH₃ |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{conversion}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{NH}_4^+ \text{ reacted}}}{n_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \text{ fed}}} \times 100\%$ | Acid neutralization efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{RVR} = \dfrac{\text{recycled fines}}{\text{product output}}$ | Recycle-to-product ratio | dimensionless (typ. 1.5–3.0) |
| 9 | $\%\text{availability of P}_2\text{O}_5 = \dfrac{\text{(WA + Citrate-soluble P}_2\text{O}_5)}{\text{Total P}_2\text{O}_5} \times 100$ | Regulatory quality metric | % (must be ≥ 90 %) |
| 10 | $\text{Acid requirement per ton MAP} = \dfrac{1000 \times 98}{115.03 \times 0.527} \approx 1618 \text{ kg H}_3\text{PO}_4$ | H₃PO₄ feed per ton MAP | kg/ton |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (8) — the recycle-to-product ratio (RVR) — is the single most important process-control lever. A high RVR (> 3) means oversized granulator, low throughput, and high crushing losses; a low RVR (< 1) means fines overload, granulator flooding, and poor granule strength. KTU questions frequently ask: "Why is RVR ≈ 2 optimal for MAP granulation?" (Answer: it balances nuclei density against granule growth kinetics.)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agronomy:** MAP is the **P-source of choice for starter fertilizers** because its acidic pH (4.5) liberates P from calcareous soils (pH > 7), counteracting "P-fixation" by Ca²⁺.
- **Fire Retardancy:** MAP is a key ingredient in **ABC dry-chemical fire extinguishers** (monoammonium phosphate powder) because it melts at 190 °C to form a glassy char that excludes oxygen.
- **Food Industry:** Food-grade MAP (E-number E342i) is used as a **leavening agent, buffer, and yeast nutrient** in bread, cheese, and wine.
- **Animal Feed:** Feed-grade MAP supplies both N and P to ruminants.
- **Metal Finishing:** MAP is used as a **rust converter and phosphating agent** for steel surface treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical N and P₂O₅ Content of Pure MAP

### Step 1: Confirm Molecular Formula and Molar Mass

The molecular formula of monoammonium phosphate is **NH₄H₂PO₄**. The constituent atomic masses (IUPAC 2021 standard) are:
- N = 14.007 g/mol
- H = 1.008 g/mol
- P = 30.974 g/mol
- O = 15.999 g/mol

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{MAP}} &= (1 \times 14.007) + (4 \times 1.008)_{\text{NH}_4} + (1 \times 1.008)_{\text{H}} \\
&\quad + (1 \times 30.974) + (4 \times 15.999) \\
&= 14.007 + 4.032 + 1.008 + 30.974 + 63.996 \\
&= 115.017 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

The convention **rounded to 115.03 g/mol** is used in fertilizer handbooks.

### Step 2: Theoretical % N

One molecule of MAP contains **1 nitrogen atom** (mass 14.007):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{N}_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{\text{mass of N per mole MAP}}{\text{molar mass of MAP}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{14.007}{115.017} \times 100 \\
&= 12.178 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Theoretical % P₂O₅ (Conversion from Elemental P)

Two molecules of MAP contain **2 phosphorus atoms**, equivalent to **one P₂O₅ unit**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ per 2 moles MAP} &= 2 \times 30.974 + 5 \times 15.999 \\
&= 61.948 + 79.995 \\
&= 141.943 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5\,_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{141.943}{2 \times 115.017} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{141.943}{230.034} \times 100 \\
&= 61.704 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Bridging Theory to Commercial Grade (11-52-0)

The theoretical 12.18 % N and 61.70 % P₂O₅ correspond to **anhydrous, 100 % pure MAP**. Commercial fertilizer-grade MAP contains:
- 1–2 % moisture,
- 1–3 % inert impurities from the WPA (Fe, Al, Ca, Mg, F, SiO₂),
- 1–2 % free H₃PO₄ or (NH₄)₂SO₄ from upstream.

These diluents lower the assay to the guaranteed **11 % N and 52 % P₂O₅** specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** The expression $\dfrac{2 \times 115.017}{141.943} = 1.6206$ is the *P₂O₅-to-MAP stoichiometric mass factor*. Hence to deliver 1 kg of P₂O₅ as MAP, the plant must dispense $1.6206$ kg of product. This single factor appears in virtually every fertilizer blending problem and is a **direct question item in KTU university exams**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Stoichiometric Phosphoric Acid Consumption per Ton of MAP

### Step 1: Define Production Basis

Basis: **1 metric ton (1000 kg) of fertilizer-grade MAP at 52 % P₂O₅.**

### Step 2: Equivalent P₂O₅ Mass to Deliver

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5} &= 1000 \times 0.52 = 520 \;\text{kg P}_2\text{O}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert P₂O₅ → Equivalent MAP Mass

Using the factor derived in Step 4 of §3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MAP theoretical}} &= m_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5} \times \frac{2 \times 115.017}{141.943} \\
&= 520 \times 1.6206 \\
&= 842.7 \;\text{kg MAP (anhydrous)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Account for Process Losses (3 % typical)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MAP gross production}} &= 842.7 \times 1.03 \\
&= 868.0 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

The shortfall (132 kg) comes from inert impurities in the wet-process acid, which means we must feed more acid than the stoichiometric minimum.

### Step 5: Back-Calculate H₃PO₄ Feed (assuming 54 % P₂O₅ merchant-grade acid)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4} &= m_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5} \times \frac{98.00}{71.00 \times \text{acid strength}} \\
&= 520 \times \frac{98.00}{71.00 \times 0.54} \\
&= 520 \times 2.557 \\
&= 1329.6 \;\text{kg H}_3\text{PO}_4 (100\%\text{ basis}) \\
&= 2462 \;\text{kg of 54 \% P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ merchant acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Ammonia Feed

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NH}_3} &= m_{\text{MAP}} \times \frac{17.03}{115.03} \\
&= 868.0 \times 0.1480 \\
&= 128.5 \;\text{kg NH}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Route Comparison

| Parameter | Preneutralizer (Old) | Pipe Reactor (Modern) | Spherodizer (Premium) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acid strength requirement | 40–50 % P₂O₅ | 52–54 % P₂O₅ | 50–54 % P₂O₅ |
| Reaction time | 5–15 min | **< 2 s** | 3–8 min |
| Conversion efficiency | 92–95 % | **98–99 %** | 95–97 % |
| CAPEX (relative) | 1.0 (baseline) | **0.6–0.7** | 1.3 |
| Granule shape | Irregular | Rounded | **Perfect sphere** |
| Cracking tendency | Moderate | Low | **Very low** |
| Energy consumption | 35–45 kWh/ton | **20–25 kWh/ton** | 40 kWh/ton |
| Product P₂O₅ | 52 % | **52–53 %** | 52 % |
| Suitability for bulk blends | Good | Good | **Excellent** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A fertilizer plant produces **120 tons/day of MAP (11-52-0)** using 54 % P₂O₅ wet-process phosphoric acid and anhydrous ammonia. Calculate: (a) the daily H₃PO₄ feed (as 100 % basis), (b) the daily NH₃ feed, and (c) the heat liberated per hour, given $\Delta H_{rxn} = -795$ kJ/kg NH₃.

### Solution

**(a) Daily H₃PO₄ feed**

Each ton of MAP contains 520 kg P₂O₅. The 100 %-basis H₃PO₄ equivalent for 520 kg P₂O₅ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4} &= 520 \times \frac{98.00}{71.00} \\
&= 520 \times 1.3803 \\
&= 717.8 \;\text{kg (100 \% basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For 120 tons/day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4}^{24\text{h}} &= 717.8 \times 120 \\
&= 86{,}136 \;\text{kg/day} = 86.14 \;\text{tons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Daily NH₃ feed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NH}_3} &= 120{,}000 \times \frac{17.03}{115.03} \\
&= 120{,}000 \times 0.14805 \\
&= 17{,}766 \;\text{kg/day} = 17.77 \;\text{tons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Hourly heat release**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \frac{17{,}766 \times 795}{24 \times 3600} \;\text{kW} \\
&= \frac{14{,}123{,}970}{86{,}400} \\
&= 163.5 \;\text{kW (continuous)} \\
&= 163.5 \times 3600 / 1000 = 588.5 \;\text{MJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovered heat is partly used to evaporate slurry moisture and partly to preheat the dryer inlet air, making MAP manufacture energy-positive on the heat balance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — MAP vs DAP vs SSP vs TSP Comparison

| Property | MAP | DAP | SSP | TSP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | NH₄H₂PO₄ | (NH₄)₂HPO₄ | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ + CaSO₄ | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ |
| % N | **11** | **18** | 0 | 0 |
| % P₂O₅ | **52** | 46 | 16–20 | 44–48 |
| N:P₂O₅ | 1 : 4.7 | 1 : 2.55 | – | – |
| pH (1:10) | **4.0–4.5** | 7.5–8.0 | < 4 (very acidic) | < 4 |
| Seed-row safety | **Excellent** | Moderate | Poor (acidic) | Poor |
| Ammonia volatilization | Negligible | **Yes, significant** | None | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Compare MAP and DAP as phosphate fertilizers. Why is MAP preferred for alkaline soils and for bulk blending?"* — The answer must invoke (i) higher P₂O₅ concentration, (ii) lower free ammonia, (iii) acidic pH counteracting soil alkalinity, and (iv) compatibility with urea in bulk blends.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pipe Reactor MAP Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Wet Process Phosphoric Acid 54 percent P2O5 Tank] --> nodeB[Acid Preheater]
    nodeC[Anhydrous Ammonia Vaporizer] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Pipe Reactor T-Cross Mixer 150 to 180 C]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Granulator Rolling Bed]
    nodeF[Recycled Fines Hopper] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG[Rotary Dryer Co-current 250 C inlet]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Vibrating Screen 3 deck]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Oversize Crusher]
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Product Cooler Fluidized Bed]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Anti Caking Coating Drum]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Bagging and Dispatch 11-52-0 MAP]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Undersize Fines Conveyor]
    nodeM --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeN[Off Gas Scrubber Fluorine Ammonia Recovery]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Stack Emission 30 mg per Nm3 F limit]
    nodeG --> nodeP[Cyclone Dust Collector]
    nodeP --> nodeF
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

- **nodeA → nodeD:** Phosphoric acid is preheated and meets ammonia in the pipe reactor where the violent exothermic reaction forms MAP melt in < 2 seconds.
- **nodeD → nodeE:** The molten MAP is sprayed onto a rolling bed of recycled fines (nodeF), causing the particles to agglomerate into granules.
- **nodeG:** Wet granules are dried in a co-current rotary dryer; dust from the dryer is captured by the cyclone (nodeP) and returned as recycle.
- **nodeH:** Three-deck vibrating screen classifies granules into **oversize**, **product**, and **undersize** streams.
- **nodeI:** Oversize material is crushed and returned to the granulator as nuclei.
- **nodeJ, nodeK:** Product is cooled and coated with oil/anti-caking agent to prevent moisture absorption during storage.
- **nodeN:** Off-gas from the pipe reactor contains fluorine (SiF₄, HF) and unreacted ammonia; a wet scrubber recovers these as fluosilicic acid (a saleable by-product) and ammonium sulfate.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reaction Chemistry of Ammoniation Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[H3PO4 Triprotic Acid] -->|NH3 Stage 1 pH 4 to 4.5| node2[NH4H2PO4 MAP 11-52-0]
    node2 -->|NH3 Stage 2 pH 7.5 to 8.0| node3[(NH4)2HPO4 DAP 18-46-0]
    node3 -->|NH3 Stage 3 unstable above 70 C| node4[(NH4)3PO4 TAP Decomposes]
    node4 -->|Back reaction 70 C| node3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Implication:** Industrial plants must terminate ammoniation at **Stage 1** for MAP and **Stage 2** for DAP. Beyond Stage 2 the product reverts, releasing free NH₃ — a safety hazard and an economic loss.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Quality Assurance & Off-Spec Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Pipe Reactor Outlet Slurry pH 4 to 4.5] --> nodeB[Inline pH Probe]
    nodeB --> nodeC{pH within 4.0 to 4.5?}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Proceed to Granulator]
    nodeC -->|No too acidic below 4.0| nodeE[Increase NH3 Flow]
    nodeC -->|No too alkaline above 4.5| nodeF[Reduce NH3 Flow Add H3PO4 Trim]
    nodeG[Finished MAP Storage Silo] --> nodeH[Hourly Sampling Lab]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Spec 11 N 52 P2O5 met?}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Release for Dispatch]
    nodeI -->|No low N below 11| nodeK[Reprocess via Re-ammoniation]
    nodeI -->|No low P2O5 below 52| nodeL[Blend with Higher Grade Batch]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT637]**
**Define MAP. Write its molecular formula, theoretical nutrient composition, and one industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**
Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP) is a water-soluble nitrogen-phosphorus fertilizer, chemically ammonium dihydrogen phosphate with the formula **NH₄H₂PO₄** (molar mass 115.03 g/mol). Its theoretical nutrient content is **12.18 % N and 61.70 % P₂O₅**; the commercial grade is **11-52-0**. One industrial application is its use as a **fire-retardant chemical in ABC dry-powder fire extinguishers**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT637]**
**Why is MAP preferred over DAP for seed-row placement in alkaline soils?**

**Model Answer:**
MAP has a **pH of 4.0–4.5** (acidic) compared to DAP's pH of **7.5–8.0** (alkaline). When placed in the seed row of alkaline soils, MAP locally **acidifies the rhizosphere**, mobilizing fixed phosphorus (Ca-bound P) and supplying P in plant-available form. Additionally, MAP releases negligible **free NH₃**, preventing ammonia toxicity to germinating seeds, whereas DAP releases free ammonia that damages seedling roots.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECHT637]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a flow diagram the **manufacture of MAP by the pipe-reactor process**. List the operating parameters (pH, temperature, mole ratio) and state the function of each unit.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A plant produces **200 tons/day of MAP (11-52-0)** from 52 % P₂O₅ wet-process acid and anhydrous ammonia. Calculate the daily feed requirement of (i) phosphoric acid (100 % basis) and (ii) ammonia. Also calculate the heat released per hour if $\Delta H_{rxn} = -795$ kJ/kg NH₃.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Pipe Reactor Process**

The pipe reactor process is the modern industry-standard route for MAP manufacture. The flow consists of the following units:

| Unit | Function | Operating Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Phosphoric acid storage & preheater | Buffers feed and raises acid temperature to reduce viscosity | 60–80 °C |
| 2. Anhydrous ammonia vaporizer | Converts liquid NH₃ to gas at controlled pressure | 1.5–3 bar, 30–50 °C |
| 3. **Pipe reactor (T-cross)** | Mixes NH₃ and H₃PO₄ violently in < 2 s; forms MAP melt at 150–180 °C | NH₃:H₃PO₄ mole ratio = 1.0 ± 0.05; pH = 4.0–4.5 |
| 4. Granulator (rotary drum or spherodizer) | Sprays melt onto recycled fines to build up granules | 90–115 °C bed; 4–6 rpm drum |
| 5. Rotary dryer (co-current) | Reduces moisture from ~5 % to < 1.5 % | Inlet 220–260 °C, outlet 85–95 °C |
| 6. Vibrating screen (3-deck) | Classifies into oversize, product, undersize | Cut points 2 mm and 4 mm |
| 7. Oversize crusher | Pulverizes oversize for recycle as nuclei | Hammer mill, < 6 mm discharge |
| 8. Fluidized-bed cooler | Cools product from 90 °C to < 45 °C | Air flow 1.5 m/s |
| 9. Coating drum | Applies anti-caking oil/dust | 0.3–0.5 % oil by mass |
| 10. Off-gas scrubber | Captures F (SiF₄, HF) and fugitive NH₃; produces fluosilicic acid by-product | pH 5–6, water-spray |

**Key operational advantages:**
- Conversion efficiency > 98 %.
- Lower CAPEX (no preneutralizer tank).
- Lower energy consumption (reaction heat self-evaporates slurry moisture).

#### **Part (b) — Mass & Energy Balance**

**Given:**
- Production = 200 tons MAP/day at 52 % P₂O₅ grade.
- Acid strength = 52 % P₂O₅.
- $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = −795 kJ/kg NH₃.

**(i) Daily H₃PO₄ feed (100 % basis):**

Total P₂O₅ delivered per day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5} &= 200{,}000 \times 0.52 = 104{,}000 \;\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent 100 % H₃PO₄:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4\,(100\%)} &= 104{,}000 \times \frac{98.00}{71.00} \\
&= 104{,}000 \times 1.3803 \\
&= 143{,}548 \;\text{kg/day} = 143.55 \;\text{tons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily NH₃ feed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NH}_3} &= 200{,}000 \times \frac{17.03}{115.03} \\
&= 200{,}000 \times 0.14805 \\
&= 29{,}610 \;\text{kg/day} = 29.61 \;\text{tons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Hourly heat release:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= \frac{29{,}610 \times 795}{24 \times 3600} \\
&= \frac{23{,}540{,}000}{86{,}400} \\
&= 272.5 \;\text{kW (continuous)} \\
&\approx 981 \;\text{MJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Set:** H₃PO₄ feed = 143.55 tons/day (100 % basis), NH₃ feed = 29.61 tons/day, Heat release = 272.5 kW ≈ 981 MJ/h.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PECHT637]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **chemistry of ammoniation of phosphoric acid**. How does the pH control differentiate MAP from DAP and why is Stage 3 (triammonium phosphate) avoided industrially?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare MAP and DAP with respect to (i) nutrient grade, (ii) pH, (iii) free ammonia content, (iv) seed-row safety, (v) compatibility with urea in bulk blends, and (vi) typical industrial manufacturing route.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Ammoniation Chemistry**

Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is a triprotic acid possessing **three replaceable hydrogen ions**. Neutralization with ammonia proceeds sequentially through three stoichiometric stages:

**Stage 1 — MAP formation (pH 4.0–4.5):**

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4
$$

At this pH, only the **first** proton of H₃PO₄ has been replaced, yielding the **acidic salt MAP**. One mole of NH₃ per mole of acid is required. The slurry temperature is held at 60–80 °C to avoid MAP crystallization in the reactor.

**Stage 2 — DAP formation (pH 7.5–8.0):**

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4
$$

A second mole of NH₃ replaces the second proton. The product is mildly alkaline. Industrially, ammoniation is stopped here because further reaction yields an unstable salt.

**Stage 3 — TAP formation (pH > 13, unstable above 70 °C):**

$$
\text{NH}_3 + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_3\text{PO}_4
$$

**TAP is NOT produced commercially** for two reasons:

1. **Thermal instability:** Triammonium phosphate decomposes at temperatures above 70 °C, releasing NH₃ gas:
$$
(\text{NH}_4)_3\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta > 70^\circ\text{C}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 + \text{NH}_3\uparrow
$$

2. **Economic loss:** Free NH₃ represents both a pollution hazard and a loss of nitrogen value; recovery is uneconomical.

**Industrial practice:** Plants select MAP or DAP by simply **metering the NH₃ flow** to stop at the desired pH endpoint — making the same plant flexible for both products.

#### **Part (b) — MAP vs DAP Comparison**

| Parameter | MAP (11-52-0) | DAP (18-46-0) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Nutrient grade | 11 % N, **52 % P₂O₅** | **18 % N**, 46 % P₂O₅ |
| (ii) pH (10 % solution) | 4.0–4.5 (acidic) | 7.5–8.0 (alkaline) |
| (iii) Free NH₃ content | Negligible | 1–2 % (significant) |
| (iv) Seed-row safety | Excellent (no NH₃ damage) | Moderate (NH₃ phytotoxic) |
| (v) Compatibility with urea | Excellent — no reaction | Poor — promotes caking |
| (vi) Typical process | Pipe reactor (modern); preneutralizer (old) | Pipe reactor; preneutralizer; slurry process |

**Engineering conclusion:** MAP is the preferred choice when (a) high P₂O₅ delivery is needed, (b) the crop is P-demanding, (c) the soil is alkaline, or (d) the fertilizer must be bulk-blended with urea. DAP is preferred when a high N:P ratio (closer to 1:2.5) is required, as in rice paddies.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in MAP Manufacture Questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing "HPO₄²⁻" for MAP.** MAP contains **H₂PO₄⁻** (dihydrogen phosphate). Markers deduct ½ mark for this ion confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometric factor.** Using 17/115 = 0.148 for NH₃ is correct; using 17/97 = 0.175 (DAP factor) for MAP loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the recycle stream.** A MAP plant flow diagram without the **fines return loop** is incomplete — markers expect 3-deck screen → crusher → recycle to granulator.
> 4. **Confusing MAP (52 % P₂O₅) with DAP (46 % P₂O₅).** State the comparative number explicitly; vague "high-phosphorus" answers lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning fluorine/silica scrubber.** Wet-process acid contains F that must be scrubbed as SiF₄/HF; omitting the **scrubber unit** in plant diagrams costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing "available P₂O₅" with "total P₂O₅".** Regulatory assay requires **water-soluble + citrate-soluble** P₂O₅ ≥ 90 % of total. Marks awarded only if this is correctly framed.
> 7. **Skipping the exotherm heat-recovery discussion.** Failure to mention that the **795 kJ/kg NH₃** reaction heat is recovered for evaporation/drying is a ½-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MAP** = NH₄H₂PO₄, molar mass **115.03 g/mol**, grade **11-52-0**, theoretical 12.18 % N and 61.7 % P₂O₅.
- The reaction is **NH₃ + H₃PO₄ → NH₄H₂PO₄** with $\Delta H = -795$ kJ/kg NH₃.
- MAP forms at **pH 4.0–4.5**; DAP at **pH 7.5–8.0**; **TAP (Stage 3) is industrially avoided** because of thermal decomposition above 70 °C.
- **Mole ratio NH₃:H₃PO₄ = 1.0 ± 0.05** for MAP; **2.0 ± 0.05** for DAP.
- **Pipe reactor process** is the modern industry standard — reaction time **< 2 s**, conversion efficiency **> 98 %**, energy consumption **20–25 kWh/ton**.
- **P₂O₅-to-MAP mass factor** = **1.6206** (every kg P₂O₅ needs 1.6206 kg MAP).
- **H₃PO₄-to-P₂O₅ mass factor** = **98/71 = 1.3803** (every kg P₂O₅ needs 1.3803 kg 100 % H₃PO₄).
- **Ammonia-to-MAP mass factor** = **17.03/115.03 = 0.1480**.
- **Recycle-to-product ratio (RVR) ≈ 1.5–3.0** is the key granulator control variable.
- **MAP advantages over DAP:** higher P₂O₅, lower N, no free NH₃, seed-row safe, bulk-blend compatible, alkaline-soil-friendly.
- **Industrial uses:** N-P fertilizer (major), fire retardant (ABC powder), yeast nutrient (brewing/baking), animal feed supplement, metal phosphating.
- **Quality specs:** % N ≥ 11, % P₂O₅ ≥ 52, free acidity < 1.5 %, moisture < 1 %, granule size 2.8–3.4 mm, pH 4.0–4.5.
- **Mandatory plant units:** acid preheater → pipe reactor → granulator → dryer → 3-deck screen → crusher (oversize) → recycle (undersize) → cooler → coating drum → bagging.
- **Mandatory environmental unit:** wet scrubber for F (SiF₄, HF) and fugitive NH₃; produces fluosilicic acid by-product.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Tag:** this topic maps to **CO₂ (Understand fertilizer manufacturing processes)** and **CO₃ (Apply stoichiometric calculations to fertilizer plants)** in PECHT637.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**M**AP = **M**ildly **A**cidic **P**hosphate"* — remember the **M-A-P** initials = **M**ild / **A**cid / **P**hosphate. Its pH (~4.5) is the "mild acid" signature that distinguishes it from DAP (alkaline) and SSP (strongly acidic). This single mnemonic has rescued students in viva-voce and 2-mark definition questions repeatedly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SINGLE SUPERPHOSPHATE (SSP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single Superphosphate (SSP)** is the oldest, historically significant, and most widely used phosphatic fertilizer in the world. It is produced by the **acidulation of finely ground phosphate rock (primarily fluorapatite or hydroxyapatite) with sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) of commercial grade concentration**. The resulting product is a **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate** mixed with calcium sulfate (gypsum) as a co-product.

The official IUPAC name for the principal nutrient compound is **Calcium dihydrogen phosphate monohydrate**, and the grade is standardized at approximately **16% available P₂O₅** (water-soluble), making it the world's most economically accessible phosphate fertilizer for smallholder and developing-economy agriculture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT637 - Module 3)**
> Single Superphosphate occupies a foundational position in the fertilizer technology syllabus because it introduces students to the **principle of acidulation**, which is the unifying chemical concept behind **all** phosphate fertilizer manufacture (SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP, NPK complexes).

### 1.2 Standard Commercial Specifications

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Nutrient content (as P₂O₅) | **16%** minimum (water-soluble) |
| Equivalent P content | **~7%** elemental P |
| Free phosphoric acid (as H₃PO₄) | **< 4%** |
| Free moisture (H₂O) | **< 5%** |
| Sulfur content (as S) | **11% – 12%** (from co-product CaSO₄) |
| Calcium content (as Ca) | **19% – 21%** |
| Physical form | Granular / Powdered |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Limestone + Vinegar" Analogy**
> Imagine dropping vinegar (a weak acid) onto a piece of limestone (calcium carbonate, CaCO₃). The acid attacks the rock, producing bubbles of CO₂ and a chemical transformation. **Single Superphosphate manufacturing is the industrial equivalent of this reaction**, except we use **sulfuric acid** (a much stronger acid) and attack **phosphate rock** (an even more stubborn mineral) inside a mechanical mixer. The acid "cracks open" the insoluble phosphate mineral, converting the locked-in phosphorus into a **water-soluble, plant-digestible form**.

> **Real-world impact:** SSP is essentially the agricultural equivalent of "pre-digesting" rocks so that plants can absorb the phosphorus without having to break the mineral lattice themselves.

### 1.4 Historical Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SSP was first manufactured commercially in **1842** by **Sir John Bennet Lawes** at his Rothamsted Experimental Station in England, marking the birth of the chemical fertilizer industry. It remained the world's dominant phosphate fertilizer until **Triple Superphosphate (TSP, ~46% P₂O₅)** and **Diammonium Phosphate (DAP, ~46% P₂O₅)** were commercialized in the mid-20th century.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raw Materials Required

1. **Phosphate Rock** – Naturally occurring mineral deposits of **fluorapatite** [Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂] or **hydroxyapatite** [Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂]. Key sources: Morocco, USA (Florida), Russia, China, Jordan, Tunisia, Togo, Senegal.
2. **Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** – Commercial grade, typically **93% – 98% concentration** (commonly called **"Bunker Acid"** or **"Chamber Acid"**).
3. **Water / Steam** – For temperature control and reaction initiation.

### 2.2 Principal Chemical Reactions

The acidulation reaction proceeds in **two sequential stages**, governed by temperature control:

#### Stage 1: Initial Acid Attack (The "Hot Mix" Stage)

$$
\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{HF} \uparrow
$$

At temperatures above **100 °C**, the free **phosphoric acid** generated initially reacts with additional phosphate rock:

$$
\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 5\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + \text{CaF}_2 + 5\text{HF} \uparrow
$$

#### Stage 2: Final Cure / Den Stage (The "Slow Cure" Stage)

The hot mix is transferred to a curing den ("**denning**") where the reaction completes over **3 – 6 weeks** at ambient temperature. The unreacted phosphate rock continues to react with the monocalcium phosphate and free phosphoric acid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Den?**
> If all the acid is added at once, the reaction becomes violently exothermic (heat release up to **~120 °C**), causing:
> - Excessive fluorine evolution (HF, toxic)
> - Reverted (insoluble) phosphate formation
> - Poor physical product quality
>
> The **den** allows slow, controlled completion of the reaction.

### 2.3 Side Reactions & Impurities

$$
\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow
$$

$$
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Iron and Aluminum impurities** are undesirable because they form water-insoluble **FePO₄** and **AlPO₄** compounds, a phenomenon known as **"phosphate reversion"**, which reduces the water-soluble P₂O₅ content.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Variable | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric H₂SO₄ requirement** | $m_{acid} = 7 \times M_{H_2SO_4} \times \dfrac{m_{rock}}{M_{rock}}$ | Mass of acid needed per mole of fluorapatite |
| **Molar mass (fluorapatite)** | $M_{rock} = 1008 \text{ g/mol}$ | Used for stoichiometry |
| **Molar mass (H₂SO₄)** | $M_{acid} = 98 \text{ g/mol}$ | Used for stoichiometry |
| **P₂O₅ conversion factor** | $\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \%\text{P} \times 2.2913$ | Convert elemental P to oxide equivalent |
| **Acidulation efficiency** | $\eta_{acid} = \dfrac{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ water-soluble}}{\text{Total P}_2\text{O}_5} \times 100\%$ | Reverted phosphate indicator |
| **Gypsum co-product yield** | $m_{CaSO_4} = \dfrac{7 \times 136}{1008} \times m_{rock}$ | Mass of CaSO₄ generated |
| **Specific heat of mix** | $c_p \approx 1.05 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ | For heat balance calculations |
| **Exothermic heat of reaction** | $\Delta H \approx -1260 \text{ kJ/kg P}_2\text{O}_5$ | Heat release during acidulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor 2.2913** comes from: $\dfrac{2 \times 31 + 8 \times 16}{2 \times 31} = \dfrac{142}{62} = 2.2913$ — converting mass of P to mass of P₂O₅.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SSP manufacturing plants are **decentralized** because sulfuric acid is expensive to transport long distances and phosphate rock is bulky. SSP plants are typically located **near the port of phosphate rock import** or near a **sulfuric acid plant**. India has a vast network of SSP plants due to the availability of imported rock from **Morocco, Jordan, and Egypt** via Kandla, Mundra, and Vizag ports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Acid Requirement per Tonne of Rock

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the theoretical mass of sulfuric acid required to completely react with **1 metric tonne (1000 kg)** of pure fluorapatite.

**Given:**
- Mass of rock = $m_{rock} = 1000 \text{ kg}$
- Molar mass of fluorapatite = $M_{rock} = 1008 \text{ g/mol}$
- Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = $M_{acid} = 98 \text{ g/mol}$
- Stoichiometric coefficient of acid = 7 (from balanced equation)

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of moles of fluorapatite in 1 tonne.

$$
n_{rock} = \dfrac{m_{rock}}{M_{rock}} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}}{1008 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{rock} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{1008} = 992.06 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the moles of H₂SO₄ required (from balanced equation, 7 moles of acid per mole of rock).

$$
n_{acid} = 7 \times n_{rock} = 7 \times 992.06
$$

$$
n_{acid} = 6944.44 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the mass of H₂SO₄ required.

$$
m_{acid} = n_{acid} \times M_{acid} = 6944.44 \times 98
$$

$$
m_{acid} = 680{,}555.56 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kilograms and tonnes.

$$
m_{acid} = 680.56 \text{ kg} = 0.6806 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Interpretation:**

> **To process 1 tonne of pure fluorapatite rock, approximately 0.68 tonnes of pure H₂SO₄ is theoretically required.** In practice, due to impurities in commercial rock (carbonates, silicates, iron oxides) and acid losses, the actual requirement is **~0.75 – 0.85 tonnes** of 100% H₂SO₄ equivalent per tonne of rock.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical P₂O₅ Content of SSP

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the theoretical maximum P₂O₅ content in SSP produced from pure fluorapatite.

**Step 1:** From the balanced equation, 1 mole of fluorapatite (1008 g) produces 3 moles of monocalcium phosphate monohydrate [Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O].

$$
M_{MCP} = 40 + 2(1 + 31 + 64) + 18 = 252 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass of monocalcium phosphate produced.

$$
m_{MCP} = 3 \times 252 = 756 \text{ g per mole of rock}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of P₂O₅ equivalent in monocalcium phosphate.

Each mole of Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O contains 2 moles of P (atomic mass 31), equivalent to 1 mole of P₂O₅ (molar mass 142).

$$
m_{P_2O_5} = 3 \times 142 = 426 \text{ g per mole of rock}
$$

**Step 4:** Total mass of product mixture (excluding HF evolved).

$$
m_{product} = 3 \times 252 + 7 \times 136 = 756 + 952 = 1708 \text{ g}
$$

where $M_{CaSO_4} = 136 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 5:** Calculate the percentage P₂O₅.

$$
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \dfrac{m_{P_2O_5}}{m_{product}} \times 100 = \dfrac{426}{1708} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = 24.94\%
$$

**Step 6:** Adjust for impurities. With ~30% impurities (carbonates, silicates, etc.) in commercial phosphate rock, the realistic P₂O₅ content drops to:

$$
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \approx 0.70 \times 24.94 \approx 17.5\%
$$

**Final Commercial Grade:** **~16% – 18% P₂O₅** (consistent with the marketed **"16% SSP"** grade).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material Balance for 1000 kg/h SSP Plant

| Stream | Component | Inlet (kg/h) | Outlet (kg/h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phosphate Rock (30% P₂O₅) | Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂ | 700 | — |
| Phosphate Rock | Inerts (SiO₂, CO₃²⁻) | 300 | 300 |
| Sulfuric Acid (98% H₂SO₄) | H₂SO₄ | 700 | — |
| Sulfuric Acid | H₂O | 14 | 14 |
| Acidulation Reactor | Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O | — | 525 |
| Acidulation Reactor | CaSO₄ | — | 680 |
| Acidulation Reactor | HF (evolved, scrubbed) | — | 81 |
| Acidulation Reactor | Unreacted rock | — | 70 |
| Moisture (added H₂O + reaction H₂O) | H₂O | 30 | 30 |
| **Total** | | **1744** | **1700** (+ 44 kg loss as HF + heat) |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **the foundation of process design** for SSP plants and is regularly asked in KTU university examinations.

---

### 3.4 Heat Balance Summary

The acidulation reaction is highly **exothermic**:

$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = -1260 \text{ kJ per kg of P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ formed}
$$

For a plant producing 1000 kg/h of 16% P₂O₅ SSP:

$$
Q_{released} = 1000 \times 0.16 \times 1260 = 201{,}600 \text{ kJ/h} = 56 \text{ kW}
$$

This heat is removed by **cooling coils** and **water spray** to maintain the reactor temperature at **100 °C – 120 °C** during acidulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SSP Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
