## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define Single Superphosphate (SSP). Write its chemical composition and the standard grade of P₂O₅.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Single Superphosphate is a phosphatic fertilizer produced by the acidulation of finely ground phosphate rock with **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** of commercial grade concentration (93–98%).

**Chemical Composition:**
The principal nutrient compound is **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate** [Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O], mixed with calcium sulfate (CaSO₄) as the inert co-product.

**Standard Grade:** **16% P₂O₅ (water-soluble)**, equivalent to approximately 7% elemental phosphorus.

**Equation of Formation:**

$$
\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{HF} \uparrow
$$

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State the role of the den in SSP manufacturing. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The **den** is a large storage shed where the hot mixer discharge is piled and allowed to cure slowly at ambient temperature for **3 to 6 weeks**.

**Roles:**
1. Allows the **completion of the acidulation reaction** between unreacted phosphate rock and free phosphoric acid.
2. Prevents **phosphate reversion** (formation of insoluble FePO₄ and AlPO₄).
3. Provides a **buffer storage** between continuous reactor discharge and batch finishing operations.
4. Permits **natural cooling** and moisture equilibration.

**Necessity:**
If curing is skipped, the product remains acidic, hygroscopic, and contains excess free acid, making storage and handling impossible.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, explain the manufacture of Single Superphosphate from phosphate rock. Discuss the function of each raw material.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the mass of H₂SO₄ required to react with 500 kg of pure fluorapatite. Also calculate the mass of monocalcium phosphate formed and the P₂O₅ content. Given: M(fluorapatite) = 1008, M(H₂SO₄) = 98, M[Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O] = 252, M(CaSO₄) = 136, M(P₂O₅) = 142.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Balanced Equation:**

$$
\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 7\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{HF}
$$

**Step 1 – Raw Materials:**
- **Phosphate Rock (Fluorapatite):** Source of phosphorus; contains Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂ as the active ingredient.
- **Sulfuric Acid (93–98%):** Converts the insoluble phosphate rock into soluble monocalcium phosphate; provides the H⁺ ions for acidulation.
- **Water/Steam:** Controls the exothermic temperature rise and provides reaction medium.

**Step 2 – Process:**
The finely ground rock (100 mesh) is fed into a **mixer-cone reactor** along with sulfuric acid. The reaction is highly exothermic, raising temperature to **100–120 °C**. The hot slurry is discharged into a **den** for slow curing.

**Step 3 – Function of Co-product CaSO₄:**
Gypsum is **not removed**; it remains in SSP, providing **sulfur** (11–12% S), a secondary nutrient increasingly important in Indian agriculture.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
$m_{rock} = 500 \text{ kg} = 500{,}000 \text{ g}$
$M_{rock} = 1008 \text{ g/mol}$
$M_{acid} = 98 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 1:** Moles of fluorapatite.

$$
n_{rock} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{1008} = 496.03 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Moles of H₂SO₄ required (stoichiometric coefficient = 7).

$$
n_{acid} = 7 \times 496.03 = 3472.22 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of H₂SO₄.

$$
m_{acid} = 3472.22 \times 98 = 340{,}277.78 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{acid} = 340.28 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 4:** Moles of monocalcium phosphate formed (coefficient = 3).

$$
n_{MCP} = 3 \times 496.03 = 1488.10 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 5:** Mass of MCP.

$$
m_{MCP} = 1488.10 \times 252 = 375{,}000 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{MCP} = 375.0 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 6:** Total product mass (excluding HF evolved).

$$
m_{product} = m_{MCP} + m_{CaSO_4}
$$

$$
m_{CaSO_4} = 7 \times 496.03 \times 136 = 472{,}220 \text{ g} = 472.22 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{product} = 375.0 + 472.22 = 847.22 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7:** P₂O₅ content.

Each mole of MCP contains 2 P atoms = 1 mole of P₂O₅ equivalent.

$$
n_{P_2O_5} = 3 \times 496.03 = 1488.10 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{P_2O_5} = 1488.10 \times 142 = 211{,}310 \text{ g} = 211.31 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \dfrac{211.31}{847.22} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = 24.94\%}
$$

---

#### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacturing process of Single Superphosphate. Mention the operating conditions and equipment used in each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A phosphate rock contains 28% P₂O₅. Calculate the percentage P₂O₅ content in SSP if 80% of the rock is acidulated and the conversion efficiency to water-soluble P₂O₅ is 90%.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Stage 1 – Raw Material Preparation:**
- Phosphate rock is ground in a **ball mill** to **100 mesh fineness** for optimum acid attack.
- Sulfuric acid (93–98%) is stored in **M.S. rubber-lined tanks** to prevent corrosion.

**Stage 2 – Acidulation (Reactor):**
- The ground rock and acid are charged into a **mixer-cone reactor** (also called a **Den mixer**).
- Temperature: **100 °C – 120 °C**.
- Retention time: **1 – 2 minutes**.
- Reaction: **Highly exothermic**, evolution of **HF gas** (scrubbed in a water scrubber to form hydrofluosilicic acid H₂SiF₆).

**Stage 3 – Denning / Curing:**
- Hot slurry discharged into a **den (cutting shed)**.
- Curing time: **3 to 6 weeks**.
- Allows slow completion of acidulation; reduces free acid content.

**Stage 4 – Finishing:**
- Cured SSP is disintegrated, granulated in a **rotary granulator**, dried in a **rotary dryer**, and screened.
- Undersize and oversize are recycled.

**Flow Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 (Reactor and Den Functional Flow).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Rock P₂O₅ content = 28%
- Acidulation fraction = 80%
- Conversion efficiency = 90%

**Step 1:** Consider **100 kg of phosphate rock**.

$$
m_{P_2O_5 \text{ in rock}} = 28 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Acidulated P₂O₅ (i.e., reacted with acid to form SSP).

$$
m_{P_2O_5 \text{ reacted}} = 28 \times 0.80 = 22.4 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Converted to water-soluble form.

$$
m_{P_2O_5 \text{ water-soluble}} = 22.4 \times 0.90 = 20.16 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Total product mass of SSP.

For 100 kg of rock with acidulation:
- MCP produced: based on stoichiometry, approximately **75 kg MCP**
- Gypsum produced: approximately **95 kg CaSO₄**
- Unreacted rock: 20 kg
- Total: **~190 kg SSP**

**Step 5:** Percentage of P₂O₅ in SSP.

$$
\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \dfrac{20.16}{190} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = 10.61\%}
$$

> **Note:** This lower-than-standard value indicates that **low-grade rock** (28% P₂O₅) cannot produce **commercial-grade SSP** (16% P₂O₅) directly; **beneficiation** or **rock blending** would be required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks for):**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the mass of HF evolved** when calculating the total product mass balance.
> 2. **Not converting molar quantities to mass** before expressing percentages — a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up the conversion factor 2.2913** with its reciprocal (0.4364).
> 4. **Ignoring the CaSO₄ co-product** — many students incorrectly assume SSP is pure Ca(H₂PO₄)₂. Remember: **the gypsum remains in the product** and contributes 11% S as a bonus nutrient.
> 5. **Confusing SSP with TSP** — TSP uses H₃PO₄ instead of H₂SO₄ and has **~46% P₂O₅**, not 16%.
> 6. **Skipping the den explanation** — describing the reactor without mentioning the **3–6 week curing** loses at least 2 marks in Part B.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Single Superphosphate (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Acidulation of phosphate rock with **H₂SO₄** to produce **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate** + gypsum.
- ✅ **Grade:** **16% P₂O₅ (water-soluble)** — the lowest-grade but most affordable phosphate fertilizer.
- ✅ **Key Reaction:** Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆F₂ + 7H₂SO₄ → 3Ca(H₂PO₄)₂ + 7CaSO₄ + 2HF↑
- ✅ **Raw Materials:** Phosphate rock (100 mesh), H₂SO₄ (93–98%), water.
- ✅ **Process Equipment:** Ball mill → Mixer-cone reactor → Den → Granulator → Dryer → Screen.
- ✅ **Reactor Conditions:** 100–120 °C, 1–2 min retention.
- ✅ **Den Curing:** **3 to 6 weeks** at ambient temperature.
- ✅ **By-product:** **Gypsum (CaSO₄)** remains in product, providing **11–12% S** as bonus nutrient.
- ✅ **Toxic emission:** **HF gas** — must be scrubbed with water to form H₂SiF₆.
- ✅ **Conversion Factor:** 1% P = **2.2913%** P₂O₅.
- ✅ **Acid Requirement:** ~**0.68 tonnes of H₂SO₄ per tonne of pure rock**; ~0.75–0.85 tonnes in practice.
- ✅ **Theoretical P₂O₅ from pure rock:** ~**24.94%**; commercial grade ~**16%** due to impurities.
- ✅ **World's first chemical fertilizer** — invented in **1842** by **Sir John Lawes**.
- ✅ **Storage:** Must be **dry, covered, and protected from rain** — hygroscopic and acidic.
- ✅ **Agronomic advantage:** Provides **P + S + Ca** — three nutrients in one product; **ideal for oilseeds and pulses**.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Low P₂O₅ → high transport cost per unit nutrient; phosphate rock contains **cadmium** (Cd) impurities — environmental concern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triple Superphosphate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Triple Superphosphate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Triple Superphosphate (TSP)** is a highly concentrated, water-soluble phosphate fertilizer produced by the acidulation of phosphate rock with **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** rather than sulfuric acid. Its principal chemical constituent is **monocalcium phosphate monohydrate**, with the formula **Ca(H₂PO₄)₂·H₂O**. The term "Triple" distinguishes it from ordinary (single) superphosphate because it contains approximately **three times** the concentration of available phosphorus pentoxide (**P₂O₅**), typically in the range of **44–48%**, compared to the 16–20% P₂O₅ found in Single Superphosphate (SSP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TSP is categorized as a **high-analysis phosphate fertilizer** and is the **most phosphorus-rich straight phosphate fertilizer** that does not contain nitrogen. Its high plant-nutrient density drastically reduces transportation and storage costs per unit of P₂O₅ delivered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemical Identity Card of TSP:**
> - **Chemical Name:** Monocalcium phosphate monohydrate
> - **Primary Formula:** $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$
> - **P₂O₅ Content:** **44–48%** (water-soluble)
> - **Appearance:** Grey to dark grey granular solid
> - **Solubility:** Highly soluble in water (forms the basis of its agronomic efficiency)
> - **Nitrogen Content:** **0%** (unlike DAP or MAP)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a meal. To extract maximum flavor (nutrients) from a spice (phosphate rock), you would use a stronger, more concentrated ingredient (phosphoric acid) instead of a milder one (sulfuric acid). 

In **Single Superphosphate**, sulfuric acid is used, which "wastes" a lot of the acid's capacity by combining with calcium to form **gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** — a useless by-product that dilutes the fertilizer. 

In **Triple Superphosphate**, phosphoric acid is used directly. Because the acid itself already contains the phosphorus we want, **none of it is wasted** as gypsum. It is like pressing a juice concentrate versus watering it down — TSP is the **concentrated juice** of phosphate fertilizers.

- **SSP analogy:** Diluted orange juice (16% P₂O₅) — you ship a lot of water.
- **TSP analogy:** Frozen concentrate (46% P₂O₅) — you ship only the essential nutrient.

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

TSP emerged as a strategic industrial response to the inefficiencies of SSP. By leveraging wet-process phosphoric acid (a key intermediate in fertilizer complexes), TSP manufacturing transformed the fertilizer industry into an **integrated chemical complex**, where a single phosphate rock processing unit could feed multiple downstream fertilizer products (SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP, NPK).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** A modern DAP/TSP/NPK complex uses a **single acid plant** to feed **multiple granulation units**. TSP and DAP often share the same reactor design with only the acid feed differing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Raw Materials Required

The manufacture of TSP demands high-purity inputs because impurities (especially iron, aluminum, magnesium) lower the P₂O₅ content and form water-insoluble compounds that are agronomically unavailable.

| Raw Material | Chemical Form | Role | Specification |
|--------------|---------------|------|---------------|
| Phosphate Rock | $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2$ (Fluorapatite) | Source of P₂O₅ | ≥ 30% P₂O₅ |
| Phosphoric Acid | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (wet process) | Acidulant & P₂O₅ donor | 50–54% P₂O₅ (≈ 70–75% H₃PO₄) |
| Steam | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$ | Reaction heat/moisture | Saturated, low-pressure |
| Water | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ | Hydrolysis/hydration | Demineralized |

### 2.2 Core Chemical Reactions (The Heart of TSP Manufacture)

The acidulation proceeds via **two sequential steps**:

#### Step 1: Primary Acidulation (Dominant Reaction)

$$ \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 $$

#### Step 2: Crystallization & Hydration

$$ \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

The monohydrate crystal is the final product form. It is **water-soluble** and **plant-available**.

### 2.3 Side Reactions with Impurities

Real phosphate rock contains impurities. These cause the **reversion** of soluble P₂O₅ to insoluble forms, reducing the agronomic value:

| Impurity | Reaction | Effect |
|----------|----------|--------|
| Iron oxide ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \to 2\text{FePO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Forms insoluble iron phosphate |
| Aluminum oxide ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \to 2\text{AlPO}_4$ | Forms insoluble aluminum phosphate |
| Calcium carbonate ($\text{CaCO}_3$) | $\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \to \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Generates CO₂; consumes acid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversion (Reverted Phosphate):** The insoluble FePO₄ and AlPO₄ formed are called "reverted" phosphates. To minimize reversion, **high-grade phosphate rock** (low in Fe and Al) and **high-concentration phosphoric acid** are preferred. **R₂O₃ / P₂O₅ ratio** below 0.1 is desirable.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| Theoretical P₂O₅ in TSP | $\frac{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ mass in product}}{\text{Total product mass}} \times 100$ | % | Standard grade: 46% |
| Acid requirement (stoichiometric) | $\text{Acid (tons)} = \text{Rock (tons)} \times \frac{4 \times \text{MW}_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4}}{\text{MW}_{\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2}}$ | tons | MW H₃PO₄ = 98, MW Ca₃(PO₄)₂ = 310 |
| P₂O₅ recovery / availability | $\frac{\text{Citrate-soluble P}_2\text{O}_5}{\text{Total P}_2\text{O}_5} \times 100$ | % | Typically 92–96% |
| Free acidity (product) | $\text{Free P}_2\text{O}_5 + \text{Free H}_3\text{PO}_4$ | % | Specification: < 5% |
| Gypsum absence | 0% $\text{CaSO}_4$ | — | TSP is gypsum-free unlike SSP |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Direct application fertilizer** in acidic soils where high P₂O₅ loading is needed.
- **Bulk-blending** intermediate for NPK formulations (granular TSP blends uniformly with urea and MOP).
- **Industrial chemical feedstock** for baking powders and yeast cultures (food-grade purified MCP).
- **Strategic product** in countries lacking native sulfur resources — TSP eliminates sulfuric acid consumption, freeing sulfur for other uses.

### 2.6 Manufacturing Process Pathways

Two principal industrial routes are used:

1. **Powder (Run-of-Pile) TSP Process:** Older, simpler — produces powdered TSP via den curing.
2. **Granular TSP Process:** Modern, dominant — produces hard, spherical granules ideal for bulk blending. Uses a TVA-type cone or rotary granulator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation: Rock-to-Acid Ratio

**Problem Setup:** A fertilizer plant uses a phosphate rock containing **72% fluorapatite**, **8% CaCO₃**, **2% Fe₂O₃**, and **18% silica/inert**. Calculate the stoichiometric phosphoric acid (as 100% H₃PO₄) requirement to acidulate **1000 kg of this rock**.

**Molecular Weights (MW):**
- Fluorapatite $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2$ = 310 + 78 = **388 g/mol** (effectively, MW of the apatite unit used in stoichiometry)
- $\text{CaCO}_3$ = **100 g/mol**
- $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ = **160 g/mol**
- $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ = **98 g/mol**

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

Let basis = 1000 kg of rock.

**Step 1 — Mass of fluorapatite in rock:**
$$\text{Mass}_{\text{apatite}} = 1000 \times 0.72 = 720 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of apatite:**
$$\text{Moles}_{\text{apatite}} = \frac{720{,}000 \text{ g}}{388 \text{ g/mol}} = 1855.67 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Acid consumed by apatite (primary reaction):**

$$\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2 + \text{(acidulant)} \longrightarrow \text{products}$$

Per mole of apatite, considering the simplified TSP reaction stoichiometry where the calcium is converted to monocalcium phosphate:
$$\text{Apatite requires 6 mol H}_3\text{PO}_4 \text{ to yield 3 mol Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2$$

$$\text{Moles}_{\text{acid, apatite}} = 1855.67 \times 6 = 11134.02 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{acid, apatite}} = 11134.02 \times 98 = 1{,}091{,}134 \text{ g} \approx 1091.13 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Acid consumed by CaCO₃:**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{CaCO}_3} = 1000 \times 0.08 = 80 \text{ kg} = 80{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Moles}_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \frac{80{,}000}{100} = 800 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Moles}_{\text{acid, CaCO}_3} = 800 \times 2 = 1600 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{acid, CaCO}_3} = 1600 \times 98 = 156{,}800 \text{ g} = 156.8 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Acid consumed by Fe₂O₃:**
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{FePO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 1000 \times 0.02 = 20 \text{ kg} = 20{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Moles}_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = \frac{20{,}000}{160} = 125 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Moles}_{\text{acid, Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 125 \times 2 = 250 \text{ mol}$$

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{acid, Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 250 \times 98 = 24{,}500 \text{ g} = 24.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Total acid consumption:**

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{acid, total}} = 1091.13 + 156.8 + 24.5 = 1272.43 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Practical acid requirement (110% stoichiometric for safety margin):**

$$\text{Mass}_{\text{acid, actual}} = 1272.43 \times 1.10 = 1399.67 \text{ kg}$$

Therefore, **1000 kg of rock requires approximately 1400 kg of 100% H₃PO₄** (≈ **1820 kg of 77% merchant-grade acid**).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: TSP vs. SSP vs. DAP

| Property | SSP | **TSP** | DAP |
|----------|-----|---------|-----|
| Acidulant | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ + NH₃ |
| P₂O₅ content | 16–20% | **44–48%** | 46% (N: 18%) |
| By-product | Gypsum ($\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$) | None | None |
| Nitrogen | 0% | **0%** | 18% |
| CaO content | ~30% | **~20%** | ~6% |
| Sulfur content | ~12% | **~1%** | ~3% |
| Water solubility of P | ~85% | **~95%** | ~95% |
| Process complexity | Simple | **Moderate** | Complex (ammoniation) |

### 3.3 Reaction Mechanism Visualization

The acidulation proceeds via a **diffusion-controlled reaction** between solid rock particles and liquid acid. The mechanism has three conceptual stages:

1. **Wetting Stage:** Acid attacks the outer surface of rock particles, dissolving Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions.
2. **Diffusion Stage:** H⁺ ions diffuse through the liquid film surrounding each particle to reach unreacted apatite core.
3. **Crystallization Stage:** As supersaturation develops, $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$ nucleates and grows as crystals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Insight:** Because acid is consumed rapidly at the surface, complete reaction requires extended curing (**"den" curing, 2–4 weeks**). Modern plants use high acid concentration (50–54% P₂O₅) and controlled temperature to minimize curing time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Granular TSP Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phosphate Rock Storage] --> B[Rock Grinding Mill]
    C[Wet Process Phosphoric Acid 50-54% P2O5] --> D[Acid Preheater]
    B --> E[Acidulation Reactor TVA Cone]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Granulator]
    F --> G[Drying Drum]
    G --> H[Screening Unit]
    H --> I[Oversize Crusher]
    H --> J[Undersize Recycle Loop]
    H --> K[Product Cooler]
    K --> L[Granular TSP Storage Silo]
    L --> M[Bagging and Dispatch]
    I --> F
    J --> F
    N[Steam and Water] --> E
    O[Bag Filter Dust Collection] --> F
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Architecture — Reaction Chemistry Pathway

```mermaid

    R1[Fluorapatite Ca10F2 PO4 6] --> S1[Surface Dissolution by H3PO4]
    A1[Phosphoric Acid 54% P2O5] --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Formation of Monocalcium Phosphate]
    S2 --> S3[Curing in Den Pile 2-4 Weeks]
    S2 --> S4[Granulation in Rotary Drum]
    S4 --> S5[Drying at 110 deg C]
    S5 --> S6[Granular TSP Product 46% P2O5]
    S6 --> S7[Bulk Blending with Urea and MOP]
    S7 --> S8[NPK Complex Fertilizer]
    E1[Impurity Fe2O3 Al2O3] --> S9[Reversion to Insoluble FePO4 AlPO4]
    S9 --> S10[Loss of Available P2O5]
```

### 4.3 Manufacturing Reactor Hierarchy


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Triple Superphosphate. Why is it called "triple" when compared to Single Superphosphate?*

**Model Answer:**
Triple Superphosphate (TSP) is a granular phosphate fertilizer produced by reacting phosphate rock with phosphoric acid. Its chemical formula is $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$ and it contains **44–48% P₂O₅**. It is called "triple" because it contains approximately **three times** the available P₂O₅ content (≈ 46%) compared to Single Superphosphate (SSP, which contains ≈ 16–20% P₂O₅). This higher nutrient concentration arises because phosphoric acid replaces sulfuric acid, eliminating the diluting gypsum by-product.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Write the balanced chemical reaction for the manufacture of Triple Superphosphate from phosphate rock and phosphoric acid.*

**Model Answer:**

$$ \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2 + 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CaF}_2 $$

(The simplified version taught in most KTU curricula is):

$$ \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 $$

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain with a neat flow diagram the manufacture of granular Triple Superphosphate by the phosphoric acid route. Discuss the role of the acidulation reactor and the granulator.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Raw Material Preparation:**
Phosphate rock (containing ≥ 30% P₂O₅) is ground in a ball mill to a fineness of **70–80% passing through 100 mesh (BSS)**. Simultaneously, wet-process phosphoric acid of concentration **50–54% P₂O₅** is preheated to 60–80 °C to improve reaction kinetics.

**2. Acidulation in TVA Cone Reactor:**
The preheated acid and ground rock are continuously fed into a **TVA-type cone mixer** (turbine-agitated, conical-bottomed reactor). The reaction is highly exothermic. Operating conditions:
- **Temperature:** 80–110 °C
- **Residence time:** 1–3 minutes
- **Acid-to-rock ratio:** slightly above stoichiometric (105–110%) to ensure complete conversion.
- **Reaction product:** A paste-like slurry containing monocalcium phosphate crystals.

**3. Granulation in Rotary Drum:**
The acidulation slurry (≈ 20% moisture) is discharged into a **rotary granulator (drum)**. Inside the drum, recycled undersize material acts as a "seed bed" for granule growth. The slurry coats the seeds and, with the rolling action, builds spherical granules of **2–4 mm diameter**.

**4. Drying:**
The wet granules enter a **co-current rotary dryer** where hot gases (180–200 °C) reduce moisture to < 4%. The dried product temperature should not exceed **110 °C** to avoid reversion of soluble P₂O₅ to insoluble forms.

**5. Classification:**
A vibrating screen separates the product into three streams:
- **Oversize (> 4 mm):** Crushed and recycled.
- **Undersize (< 2 mm):** Recycled to the granulator as seed.
- **On-size (2–4 mm):** Sent for cooling and coating.

**6. Cooling and Coating:**
Granules are cooled to **< 50 °C** in a rotary cooler and then coated with **0.5% oil or talc** to prevent caking during storage.

**7. Storage and Bagging:**
Finished TSP is stored in silos and bagged in **50 kg HDPE bags** or dispatched in bulk.

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Compare the manufacture, composition, and properties of Single Superphosphate (SSP) and Triple Superphosphate (TSP). Which is preferred for bulk blending operations and why?*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | SSP | TSP |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Acidulant | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (Sulfuric acid) | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (Phosphoric acid) |
| P₂O₅ content | 16–20% | **44–48%** |
| By-product | Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) | None |
| Sulfur content | 12% (as CaSO₄) | < 1% |
| CaO content | 28–30% | 18–20% |
| Particle strength | Soft, powdery | Hard, granular |
| Acid consumption (per ton P₂O₅) | Higher | Lower (more efficient) |

**Bulk-Blending Preference:** Granular TSP is overwhelmingly preferred for bulk blending operations because:

1. **High nutrient density** (46% P₂O₅) minimizes the volume required per unit P₂O₅ in the blend.
2. **Hard, spherical granules (2–4 mm)** match the particle size of urea (2–4 mm) and MOP (2–4 mm), preventing segregation during handling and transport.
3. **Non-hygroscopic behavior** when coated with oil, allowing long-term storage without caking.
4. **Compatibility** with micronutrients (Zn, B) that are often coated onto TSP granules.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the by-products. **SSP produces gypsum; TSP does NOT produce gypsum.** Writing "gypsum is a by-product of TSP" will lose 2 marks immediately. Also, do not write "TSP contains sulfur" — its sulfur content is essentially zero.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Discuss the chemical reactions involved in the manufacture of Triple Superphosphate. Explain how impurities in phosphate rock affect the quality of TSP.*

**Model Solution:**

**Primary Reaction:**
$$ \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 $$

This reaction liberates **water-soluble monocalcium phosphate**. The acid acts as both an acidulant (providing H⁺ to displace Ca²⁺ from apatite) and as a nutrient source (contributing PO₄³⁻ to the final product).

**Side Reactions with Impurities:**

1. **Iron oxide impurity:**
$$ \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{FePO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$
This produces **iron phosphate**, which is **insoluble in water** (only partially citrate-soluble). This phenomenon is called **"reversion"**.

2. **Aluminum oxide impurity:**
$$ \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{AlPO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$
Forms **aluminum phosphate** — also insoluble.

3. **Calcium carbonate impurity:**
$$ \text{CaCO}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 + \text{CO}_2\uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
Consumes acid but product is useful. The CO₂ evolution causes foaming and must be vented.

4. **Silica impurity:**
$$ \text{SiO}_2 + 6\text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$
Releases toxic HF, which is scrubbed before atmospheric release.

**Effect on Quality:**
- **R₂O₃ / P₂O₅ ratio** above 0.10 significantly increases reversion. Premium TSP requires rock with ratio < 0.08.
- **Free acidity** increases if excess H₃PO₄ is used to compensate for impurities, leading to hygroscopic product.
- **Available P₂O₅** (water + citrate-soluble) decreases; specification demands > 92% of total P₂O₅ to be "available".

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Describe the curing process in TSP manufacture. Why is curing necessary and what parameters affect the curing time?*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Curing:**
Curing is the **storage period** during which the freshly acidulated TSP slurry completes its chemical reaction, releases excess moisture, and crystallizes into stable monocalcium phosphate monohydrate. The product is stockpiled in large piles called **"dens"** for **14–28 days** at ambient temperature.

**Why Curing is Necessary:**

1. **Completion of acid-rock reaction:** Although 80% conversion occurs in the reactor, the residual apatite needs time for complete dissolution.
2. **Crystallization:** $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2$ supersaturates the slurry and crystallizes as the **monohydrate form** $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
3. **Moisture reduction:** Free water evaporates, dropping moisture from ~20% to ~8%.
4. **Stabilization of free acidity:** Excess unreacted acid diffuses into the rock matrix during curing.

**Parameters Affecting Curing Time:**

| Parameter | Effect |
|-----------|--------|
| Acid concentration | Higher concentration (54% P₂O₅) reduces curing time |
| Rock fineness | Finer grinding accelerates reaction |
| Temperature | Higher ambient temp shortens curing |
| Acid-to-rock ratio | Closer to stoichiometric reduces curing |
| Rock reactivity | Highly reactive rock (e.g., Togo, Morocco) cures faster |

**Modern plants eliminate dens** by using intensive mixers and high-concentration acid that completes the reaction inside the granulator itself — this is the **"slurry process"** vs. the older **"den process"**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do not write that "curing is the drying step." Curing is a **chemical and physical equilibration** step that occurs at ambient conditions. Drying is a separate, thermal moisture-removal step. Confusing these two will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Triple Superphosphate:**

- ✅ TSP = **Monocalcium Phosphate Monohydrate** = $\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ✅ P₂O₅ content is **44–48%** (three times that of SSP).
- ✅ Manufactured by reacting phosphate rock with **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)**, NOT sulfuric acid.
- ✅ Core reaction: $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 4\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4\text{)}_2$.
- ✅ **No gypsum by-product** is formed (unlike SSP).
- ✅ Contains **0% nitrogen** and **< 1% sulfur**.
- ✅ **Reversion** (FePO₄ and AlPO₄ formation) is the major quality-degrading side reaction.
- ✅ Granular TSP uses a **TVA cone reactor + rotary granulator + rotary dryer**.
- ✅ Curing takes **14–28 days** in dens in the older process.
- ✅ Particle size specification for bulk blending: **2–4 mm** spherical granules.
- ✅ TSP is **preferred over SSP** for bulk blending with urea and MOP due to matching granule size and higher P₂O₅ density.
- ✅ Free acidity in finished TSP must be **< 5%** (to prevent caking).
- ✅ Available P₂O₅ (water + citrate-soluble) must be **≥ 92%** of total P₂O₅.
- ✅ The **R₂O₃ / P₂O₅ ratio** in rock should be **< 0.10** for premium TSP quality.
- ✅ TSP is hygroscopic above 70% relative humidity — requires **coating with 0.5% oil**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical, Chemical Properties and Uses of Potassium Chloride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical, Chemical Properties and Uses of Potassium Chloride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potassium Chloride (KCl)**, commonly known as **Muriate of Potash (MOP)**, is the most widely used and economically significant potassium fertilizer in global agriculture. It is an ionic compound composed of potassium cations (K⁺) and chloride anions (Cl⁻), crystallizing in the **face-centered cubic (FCC) halite (sylvite) crystal lattice** structure. In fertilizer technology, commercial-grade KCl is graded according to its **% K₂O equivalent**, with the standard MOP grade containing approximately **60–62% K₂O**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Potassium chloride is treated under the family of **"straight potassic fertilizers."** It is a primary, single-nutrient carrier of potassium (K) and serves as the parent chemical for the manufacture of several other K-fertilizers such as K₂SO₄, KNO₃, and SOP-based complexes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **"sodium-free table salt"**. In fact, KCl *is* sold in grocery stores as a low-sodium salt substitute (under brand names like "Lo-Salt" or "Nu-Salt") for patients with hypertension. The same chemical that flavours your food is dug out of the earth in massive quantities (the mineral **sylvite** and the double salt **carnallite — KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O**) and broadcast on to crops. The Cl⁻ ion is essentially a "spectator nutrient" for most crops — it is the **K⁺ cation** that is the plant nutrient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> • KCl delivers K⁺ (essential macronutrient).
> • The accompanying Cl⁻ is a micronutrient for some crops (palm, coconut, sugar beet) but is *not* the primary agronomic value-driver.
> • Hence the grade is reported as **% K₂O** (K-equivalent), not as "% K" or "% KCl."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Property | Value | Standard Reference |
|----------|-------|--------------------|
| Molecular formula | KCl | IUPAC |
| Molar mass | **74.55 g/mol** | IUPAC 2021 |
| Density (20 °C) | **1.984 g/cm³** | CRC Handbook |
| Melting point | **770 °C (1043 K)** | CRC Handbook |
| Boiling point | **1420 °C (1693 K)** | CRC Handbook |
| % K₂O equivalent (theoretical) | **63.17 %** | Calculated |
| % K₂O (commercial MOP) | **60 – 62 %** | IFFO/IFA standards |
| Solubility in water (20 °C) | **34.4 g / 100 mL** | CRC Handbook |
| Crystal system | Cubic (halite) | Mineralogical |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Properties — Engineering Perspective

The physical properties of KCl govern its **handling, storage, granulation, blending compatibility, and field application behaviour** in fertilizer engineering.

- **Appearance & Morphology:** White to colourless crystalline solid; the naturally occurring mineral **sylvite** is often red/brown due to iron oxide impurities. Crystallises in the **rock-salt (halite) structure** — a 1:1 ionic lattice with octahedral coordination (each K⁺ surrounded by 6 Cl⁻, and vice-versa).
- **Density (1.984 g/cm³):** Heavier than most nitrogenous fertilizers (urea ≈ 1.32 g/cm³). This means **MOP granules segregate poorly with urea in bulk blends** — an important practical concern in bulk-blending (BB) fertilizer plants.
- **Melting Point (770 °C):** Significantly higher than ammonium nitrate (170 °C) or urea (133 °C), making KCl a thermally stable carrier suitable for **granulation by compaction or steam-agglomeration** without decomposition.
- **Hygroscopicity (Very Low):** The critical relative humidity (CRH) of pure KCl is approximately **84 %** at 30 °C. This makes MOP one of the **least hygroscopic** of all straight fertilizers — it does **not** cake, does not require elaborate conditioning, and stores well in tropical conditions.
- **Hardness (Mohs 2.5) & Friability:** Moderately soft; granules are prone to dust formation if over-handled. Anti-caking coating (e.g., oil/dust suppressants) is often applied.
- **Solubility:** Highly water-soluble; releases K⁺ readily into the soil solution — making it a **fast-acting** K source.
- **Particle Size Range:** Commercial MOP is graded as **standard (SOP), coarse, granular (G), or crystalline** grades, with the **granular grade (2–4 mm)** preferred for direct application and bulk blending.

### 2.2 Chemical Properties — Reaction Engineering Perspective

- **Ionic Dissociation in Water:**
$$\text{KCl}_{(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}^{+}_{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^{-}_{(aq)}$$

- **Reaction with Concentrated Sulphuric Acid (Mannheim Process precursor):**
At high temperature:
$$\text{2 KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{2 HCl} \uparrow$$
This is the basis for the **Mannheim process** to manufacture K₂SO₄ (SOP).

- **Reaction with Nitric Acid:**
$$\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl} \uparrow$$
Basis for KNO₃ manufacture.

- **Reaction with Silver Nitrate (analytical confirmation):**
$$\text{KCl} + \text{AgNO}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AgCl}\downarrow_{\text{(white)}} + \text{KNO}_3$$
Used as a qualitative test for the chloride ion.

- **Electrolysis (Downs / Mercury Cell):**
$$\text{2 KCl}_{(l)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{electrolysis}}\; \text{2 K} + \text{Cl}_2$$
Produces potassium metal and chlorine gas industrially.

- **pH of Solution:** KCl is a salt of a **strong acid (HCl) and a strong base (KOH)**, hence its aqueous solution is **pH-neutral (~6.5–7.0)**. It **does not** acidify or alkalinize the soil significantly.

- **Double Salt Formation:** Naturally found as the mineral **carnallite** — KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O — a double salt that is the principal ore of KCl in many deposits.

- **Compatibility:** Compatible with most fertilizers; however, in **bulk blends with urea**, caking and segregation issues may arise due to density mismatch.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four relationships — they appear every year in KTU examinations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-----|
| 1 | % K₂O equivalent of KCl | $\dfrac{M_{K_2O}}{2 \times M_{KCl}} \times 100 = \dfrac{94.20}{149.10}\times 100$ | % w/w | Grade declaration of MOP |
| 2 | % K (elemental) in KCl | $\dfrac{M_K}{M_{KCl}}\times 100 = \dfrac{39.10}{74.55}\times 100$ | % w/w | Agronomic conversion |
| 3 | KCl required for given K₂O | $\text{KCl (kg)} = \dfrac{\text{K}_2\text{O required (kg)} \times 149.10}{94.20}$ | kg | Fertilizer dose calculation |
| 4 | Stoichiometric HCl evolved | 1 mol KCl → 1 mol HCl, mass ratio $\dfrac{36.46}{74.55}$ | — | Mannheim process mass balance |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bulk-Blending Plants:** Granular MOP (2–4 mm) is the most commonly used K-source in mechanical bulk-blend (BB) fertilizer operations.
- **Compound (NPK) Granulation:** KCl is the standard K-source in steam- and water-granulated NPK complexes (e.g., 17-17-17, 19-19-19).
- **Raw Material Industry:** Feeds Mannheim, Hargreaves, and nitrate-conversion processes for SOP and KNO₃.
- **Food & Pharma Sector:** Low-sodium salt (cardiology), electrolyte replenisher in oral rehydration salts (ORS), intravenous fluids.
- **Oilfield Industry:** Component of drilling muds (brines) for density control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical % K₂O in Potassium Chloride

The commercial label of any potassic fertilizer reports the **potassium content expressed as K₂O equivalent** (a legacy convention from the era of gravimetric analysis). We must convert the mass of KCl into the equivalent mass of K₂O.

**Given:**
- Atomic mass of K = **39.10 g/mol**
- Atomic mass of O = **16.00 g/mol**
- Atomic mass of Cl = **35.45 g/mol**

**Step 1 — Molar mass of K₂O:**

$$M_{K_2O} = 2 \times 39.10 + 1 \times 16.00 = 78.20 + 16.00 = 94.20 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Molar mass of KCl:**

$$M_{KCl} = 39.10 + 35.45 = 74.55 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric basis (1 mol K₂O requires 2 mol KCl):**

$$\text{2 KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{O}$$

Therefore:

$$149.10 \;\text{g KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; 94.20 \;\text{g K}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage K₂O in KCl:**

$$\%\;K_2O = \dfrac{94.20}{149.10} \times 100$$

$$\%\;K_2O = 0.63179 \times 100 = 63.17\%$$

**Conclusion (boxed result):**
$$\boxed{\%\;K_2O_{\text{theoretical in KCl}} = 63.17\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Commercial **Muriate of Potash (MOP)** is graded at **60 % K₂O** because it contains ~5 % impurities (NaCl, MgCl₂, insolubles, moisture). Hence the label "**MOP 60**" — remember this for numerical questions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Elemental % K in KCl (Conversion Audit)

For agronomists working with tissue analysis, the **elemental K** is reported, not K₂O. The conversion is:

**Step 1:** Stoichiometrically, 1 mol KCl contains 1 mol K.

$$74.55 \;\text{g KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; 39.10 \;\text{g K}$$

**Step 2:** Percentage K in KCl:

$$\%\;K = \dfrac{39.10}{74.55}\times 100$$

$$\%\;K = 0.52448 \times 100 = 52.45\%$$

**Boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\%\;K_{\text{in KCl}} = 52.45\%}$$

**Cross-check using the factor 0.830:**

$$\%\;K = \%\;K_2O \times \dfrac{2 \times 39.10}{94.20} = 63.17 \times 0.8301 = 52.45\% \;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion factor every KTU student must memorize:**
> $$\%\;K_2O \times 0.830 = \%\;K \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \%\;K \times 1.205 = \%\;K_2O$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Mass Balance — Mannheim Process

The reaction is:
$$\text{2 KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{2 HCl}$$

**Question:** How much K₂SO₄ and HCl are produced from 1000 kg of pure KCl?

**Step 1 — Moles of KCl supplied:**

$$n_{KCl} = \dfrac{1000 \;\text{kg}}{74.55 \;\text{kg/kmol}} = 13.41 \;\text{kmol}$$

**Step 2 — From stoichiometry (2 mol KCl → 1 mol K₂SO₄ → 2 mol HCl):**

$$n_{K_2SO_4} = \dfrac{13.41}{2} = 6.71 \;\text{kmol}$$

$$n_{HCl} = 13.41 \;\text{kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Molar masses:**

$$M_{K_2SO_4} = 2(39.10) + 32.07 + 4(16.00) = 174.27 \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{HCl} = 1.008 + 35.45 = 36.46 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Masses produced:**

$$m_{K_2SO_4} = 6.71 \times 174.27 = 1169.5 \;\text{kg}$$

$$m_{HCl} = 13.41 \times 36.46 = 488.9 \;\text{kg}$$

**Boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\text{From 1000 kg KCl: } \approx 1169.5 \;\text{kg K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\text{and}\; \approx 488.9 \;\text{kg HCl}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — KCl vs Other K-Carriers

| Property | KCl (MOP) | K₂SO₄ (SOP) | KNO₃ |
|---|---|---|---|
| % K₂O | **60 – 62** | **50 – 53** | **46 (also 13 % N)** |
| Cl⁻ content | High (47 %) | Negligible | Negligible |
| Cost | **Lowest** | High | Highest |
| Suitability for chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, potato, grape) | **Poor** | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| Solubility (g/100 mL, 20 °C) | 34.4 | 11.0 | 31.6 |
| Hygroscopicity | Very low | Very low | Moderate |
| Soil pH effect | Neutral | Slightly acidifying | Neutral |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: KCl — Lifecycle & Engineering Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Mineral Sources] --> B[Sylvite KCl ore]
    A --> C[Carnallite KCl MgCl2 6H2O]
    A --> D[Brine from Dead Sea Searles Lake]
    B --> E[Beneficiary Froth Flotation]
    C --> F[Crystallization]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Refined KCl Crystals]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Granulation Compaction]
    G --> I[Direct Application as MOP 60]
    H --> J[Granular MOP 2 to 4 mm]
    J --> K[Bulk Blending BB plants]
    J --> L[Compound NPK Granulation]
    G --> M[Downstream Conversion]
    M --> M1[Mannheim Process to K2SO4]
    M --> M2[Nitric Acid Route to KNO3]
    M --> M3[Electrolysis to K metal and Cl2]
    I --> N[Agricultural Field Application]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Crop Uptake of K ion]
    O --> P[Improved Yield Quality Disease Resistance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Sylvite ore and carnallite are the two dominant mineral feedstocks. Brine extraction (e.g., from the Dead Sea) is the third major route. The purified KCl is either sold **directly as MOP** (agronomic) or **converted** into higher-value K-carriers (industrial).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Property-Application Cause-Effect Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Low Hygroscopicity CRH 84 percent] --> A1[Easy storage in tropics]
    P2[High Density 1.984 g per cm3] --> A2[Segregation in bulk blends with urea]
    P3[High Solubility 34.4 g per 100 mL] --> A3[Rapid K availability to crops]
    P4[Melting Point 770 deg C] --> A4[Compatible with steam granulation]
    P5[Neutral pH in solution] --> A5[No soil acidification]
    P6[High Cl content 47 percent] --> A6[NOT suitable for chloride sensitive crops]
    A1 --> F1[Direct field application as MOP]
    A2 --> F2[Needs granule size matching in BB]
    A3 --> F3[Top dressing and basal application]
    A4 --> F4[NPK complex fertilizer manufacture]
    A5 --> F5[Safe on acidic and alkaline soils]
    A6 --> F6[Avoided for tobacco potato grape]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Why is potassium chloride commercially called "Muriate of Potash" and what is its standard % K₂O grade?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The term **"Muriate"** is the old pharmaceutical name for any chloride salt (from Latin *muriaticus* = "pertaining to brine/salt"). Hence KCl is called **Muriate of Potash (MOP)**.
- (1 mark) The theoretical % K₂O in pure KCl is **63.17 %**, computed as $\dfrac{94.20}{149.10}\times 100$.
- (1 mark) Commercial MOP is graded at **60 % K₂O** (often labelled "MOP 60") because of impurities (NaCl, MgCl₂, moisture) totalling ~5 %.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State any three physical properties of potassium chloride that make it suitable as a straight potassic fertilizer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) **High water solubility (34.4 g/100 mL)** — ensures rapid K⁺ release and uptake.
2. (1 mark) **Low hygroscopicity (CRH ≈ 84 %)** — does not cake or degrade in humid storage.
3. (1 mark) **High density (1.984 g/cm³)** and **high melting point (770 °C)** — enables granulation, compaction, and bulk blending without decomposition.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the **physical and chemical properties of potassium chloride** and explain why it is the most widely used straight potash fertilizer globally. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Starting from sylvite ore, describe the **beneficiation of KCl by froth-flotation** with a neat flow description. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition and identity (1 mark):** KCl is an ionic, white crystalline solid; molar mass 74.55 g/mol; naturally occurs as the mineral **sylvite** and double-salt **carnallite (KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O)**.

2. **Physical properties (3 marks):**
   - Crystal system: cubic, halite (rock-salt) structure.
   - Density 1.984 g/cm³; melting point 770 °C; refractive index 1.490.
   - Solubility 34.4 g/100 mL water at 20 °C.
   - Very low hygroscopicity (CRH ≈ 84 % at 30 °C).
   - Mohs hardness 2.5 — moderately soft, friable.
   - Available in standard, coarse, granular, and crystalline grades.

3. **Chemical properties (2 marks):**
   - Readily dissociates: KCl → K⁺ + Cl⁻.
   - Reacts with conc. H₂SO₄ at high T: 2KCl + H₂SO₄ → K₂SO₄ + 2HCl (Mannheim).
   - Reacts with HNO₃: KCl + HNO₃ → KNO₃ + HCl.
   - Forms white AgCl precipitate with AgNO₃ (analytical test).
   - pH-neutral solution (salt of strong acid + strong base).

4. **Reason for dominance as K-fertilizer (1 mark):** Lowest cost per unit K₂O, highest % K₂O among chloride-free alternatives is K₂SO₄ (50 %), but MOP delivers more K at a fraction of the price; agronomically efficient; excellent storage & blending properties; minor Cl⁻ content is agronomically beneficial for coconut, palm, sugar beet.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Feed preparation (1 mark):** Sylvite ore is crushed, ground, and classified; desliming is carried out to remove clay fines.

2. **Conditioning (1 mark):** The ore slurry is conditioned with **amine-type cationic collectors** (e.g., dodecylamine acetate) which selectively adsorb onto KCl surfaces rendering them hydrophobic; **depressants** (e.g., starch, polyacrylamide) suppress NaCl flotation.

3. **Froth flotation (2 marks):** Conditioned pulp is fed to flotation cells; air is bubbled through; hydrophobic KCl particles attach to bubbles and rise with the froth, which is skimmed off as **KCl concentrate**; NaCl remains in the cell (tailings).

4. **Dewatering & Drying (1 mark):** Concentrate is thickened, filtered (centrifuge/vacuum filter), and dried in rotary/flash dryers to < 0.5 % moisture.

5. **Sizing & Anti-caking (1 mark):** Dried KCl is screened; undersize is recycled; granules are coated with **anti-caking oil/dust suppressant** (e.g., paraffin oil 0.05–0.1 %).

6. **Product (1 mark):** Final product is **white crystalline/granular MOP at 60 % K₂O**, packed in 50 kg HDPE bags or shipped in bulk.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
> *(a)* Calculate the **% K₂O and % elemental K in pure potassium chloride**. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Explain the **major industrial uses of KCl** beyond its role as a fertilizer, with chemical equations where applicable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write molecular masses.

$$M_{K_2O} = 2(39.10) + 16.00 = 94.20 \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{KCl} = 39.10 + 35.45 = 74.55 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Stoichiometric basis — 2 mol KCl (149.10 g) ≡ 1 mol K₂O (94.20 g).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute % K₂O:

$$\%\;K_2O = \dfrac{94.20}{149.10}\times 100 = 63.17\%$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute % elemental K:

$$\%\;K = \dfrac{39.10}{74.55}\times 100 = 52.45\%$$

**Boxed answers:**
$$\boxed{\%\;K_2O = 63.17\%\quad\text{and}\quad\%\;K = 52.45\%}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Manufacture of K₂SO₄ via Mannheim process (2 marks):**
$$\text{2 KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{2 HCl}\uparrow$$
HCl is recovered as commercial hydrochloric acid.

2. **Manufacture of KNO₃ (2 marks):**
$$\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl}\uparrow$$
Industrial route to nitrate-based K fertilizer.

3. **Electrolysis for K-metal and Cl₂ (1 mark):**
$$\text{2 KCl}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{electrolysis}} \text{2 K} + \text{Cl}_2$$

4. **Food industry (1 mark):** Low-sodium salt substitute for hypertensive patients; component of **Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS)**; brewing/cheese-making brines.

5. **Pharmaceutical & medical (0.5 mark):** Electrolyte replenisher in IV fluids/Ringer's lactate.

6. **Oil & gas industry (0.5 mark):** Component of **completion brines and drilling fluids** for wellbore density control.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Reporting %K instead of %K₂O (or vice-versa):** KTU examiners are strict — a question asking "% K₂O" must NOT be answered with "52.45 % K". Always use the **conversion factor 0.830** ($K = 0.830 \times K_2O$).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert 60 % (commercial) vs 63.17 % (theoretical):** If a numerical problem says "commercial MOP," use **60 % K₂O**, not 63.17 %.
> 3. **Skipping the reaction equation in Mannheim/Nitric-acid processes:** Even in a 7-mark descriptive answer, **at least one balanced equation is mandatory**.
> 4. **Confusing MOP with SOP:** MOP = Muriate of Potash = KCl; SOP = Sulphate of Potash = K₂SO₄. Markers deduct heavily for this mix-up.
> 5. **Omitting the pH-neutrality justification:** Many students write "KCl is neutral" but do not explain *why* (salt of strong acid HCl + strong base KOH). This costs 1 mark.
> 6. **No mention of hygroscopicity / CRH in storage questions:** "Why is MOP easy to store?" → answer must reference **CRH ≈ 84 %**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid- Revision Checklist — "KCl in 60 seconds":**
> - KCl = **Muriate of Potash (MOP)**, the **world's #1 K-fertilizer**.
> - **% K₂O theoretical = 63.17 %**; **% K₂O commercial = 60 %**; **% elemental K = 52.45 %**.
> - **Molar mass = 74.55 g/mol**; crystal system = **cubic halite**; density = **1.984 g/cm³**.
> - **Melting point 770 °C** — thermally stable, ideal for granulation.
> - **CRH ≈ 84 %** — **least hygroscopic** of all straight fertilizers, no caking.
> - Aqueous solution is **pH-neutral** (salt of strong acid + strong base).
> - **Soluble 34.4 g/100 mL** at 20 °C → **fast-acting K source**.
> - Two key industrial downstream reactions: **Mannheim (→ K₂SO₄)** and **nitric-acid (→ KNO₃)**.
> - **Avoid MOP for chloride-sensitive crops** (tobacco, potato, grape, berry) — use K₂SO₄ instead.
> - **AgCl white precipitate with AgNO₃** is the qualitative analytical test.
> - **Memorize the conversion:** $K_2O \times 0.830 = K$ ; $K \times 1.205 = K_2O$.
> - **NOT** used to deliver nitrogen or phosphorus; **straight (single-nutrient) K-carrier only**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potassium Nitrate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potassium Nitrate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — POTASSIUM NITRATE (KNO₃)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Potassium Nitrate (KNO₃)**, commonly called **Saltpeter** or **Nitre**, is a chlorine-free, **binary fertilizer** supplying two primary plant nutrients — **Nitrogen (N)** at **13 %** and **Water-Soluble Potash (K₂O)** at **46 %** (equivalent to **38 % K**). It is the only major fertilizer salt that delivers both N and K without supplying any accompanying anion such as chloride, sulphate, or carbonate, which makes it a premium **"speciality / chloride-sensitive" fertilizer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *"Potassium nitrate is a nitrogenous–potassic fertilizer containing 13 % N and 46 % K₂O, manufactured predominantly by double decomposition of KCl with NaNO₃ or by direct reaction of KCl with HNO₃, valued for chloride-sensitive crops."*

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | KNO₃ |
| Molecular Weight | **101.1 g/mol** |
| N Content | **13 %** |
| K₂O Content | **46 %** (≈ 38 % K) |
| Physical Form | White, crystalline, orthorhombic |
| Hygroscopicity | **Non-hygroscopic** |
| Salt Index | **20.2** (very low) |
| pH (10 % solution) | **6.0 – 7.5** (near neutral) |
| Density | 2.11 g/cm³ |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Balanced Diet for Crops" Analogy:**
> Think of your crop as an athlete. Most fertilizers give either only "protein" (Nitrogen) or only "minerals" (Potash), but **KNO₃ is like a perfectly balanced meal combining both** — and it's *served on a clean plate* because it carries **no chloride "junk food"** that would harm sensitive crops like tobacco, grapes, or potatoes. Just as a body-builder would pay a premium for clean protein, premium farmers pay a premium for chloride-free KNO₃.

### 1.3 Why KNO₃ Occupies a Premium Position

- **Dual nutrient** in a single crystal lattice — no segregation during storage or transport.
- **Free from chloride (Cl⁻)** which is toxic to tobacco, potato, grapes, citrus, and berry crops.
- **Low salt index (20.2)** → minimizes osmotic stress in soil solution.
- **Non-hygroscopic** → free-flowing, suitable for bulk blending.
- **Compatible** with most other fertilizers (except strong acids or oxidizable organics).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The industrial chemistry of KNO₃ is governed by **solubility differences** in aqueous quaternary systems (K⁺, Na⁺, NO₃⁻, Cl⁻). The two master reactions are:

$$\text{Reaction A (Double Decomposition / Conversion):}$$
$$\text{KCl} + \text{NaNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$$

$$\text{Reaction B (Direct Neutralization / Haug Process):}$$
$$\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core "WHY" Behind Both Routes:** Both reactions are *thermodynamically favourable* (ΔG < 0), but the engineering challenge is **selective separation** of the desired product from the co-product. In Route A, we exploit the steep solubility-vs-temperature curve of KNO₃. In Route B, we exploit **selective solvation** of HCl in an organic solvent.

### 2.2 Solubility Table — The Foundation of Route A

The key insight is that **KNO₃ solubility increases ~19-fold from 0 °C to 100 °C**, while **NaCl solubility is nearly flat**. This is the basis for fractional crystallization.

| Salt | Solubility at 0 °C (g/100 g H₂O) | Solubility at 100 °C (g/100 g H₂O) | Solubility Ratio (100/0 °C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| KNO₃ | 13.3 | 246.0 | **~18.5** |
| NaNO₃ | 73.0 | 175.0 | 2.4 |
| NaCl | 35.7 | 39.1 | 1.1 |
| KCl | 27.6 | 56.7 | 2.05 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mantra:** *"NaCl is the 'bystander' salt — its solubility barely changes, so it is removed first by hot filtration. KNO₃ is the 'temperature-responsive' salt — it crystallizes only when the solution is cooled."*

### 2.3 KTU Formula & Reaction Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of KNO₃ | $M_{\text{KNO}_3} = 39.1 + 14 + 3(16) = 101.1$ | g/mol |
| Theoretical K₂O equivalent | $\% K_2O = \dfrac{2 \times 47.1}{101.1} \times \% K$ | 46.5 % theoretical |
| Theoretical N content | $\% N = \dfrac{14.01}{101.1} \times 100 = 13.85$ | ≈ 13 % commercial |
| Salt Index | $SI = \dfrac{EC_{\text{salt solution}}}{EC_{\text{NaCl reference}}} \times 100$ | KNO₃ ≈ 20.2; KCl = 116 |
| Solvent-extraction coefficient (Haug) | $\alpha = \dfrac{[HCl]_{org}}{[HCl]_{aq}}$ | depends on alcohol (n-pentanol ≈ 1.5–2.0) |
| Mass balance for Route A | $\dfrac{m_{KCl}}{74.6} = \dfrac{m_{NaNO_3}}{85.0}$ | stoichiometric ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** When using absolute values such as $\vert \Delta T \vert$ or $\vert \Delta H \vert$ in any KTU answer sheet markdown, always use **\vert** or **\mid** — never the bare ASCII pipe — to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the System

- **Tobacco Industry:** KNO₃ is the *gold standard* because chloride reduces leaf combustibility and imparts a muddy aroma.
- **Horticulture / Poly-house:** Hydroponic grade KNO₃ is used as the standard A-tank nutrient because of its **zero-Cl⁻, zero-Na⁺** profile.
- **Pyrotechnics & Food Preservation:** Legacy non-fertilizer uses (~10 % of global production).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Stoichiometric Derivation — Route A (Double Decomposition)

**Given:** Feed composition — 100 kg of technical-grade KCl (purity 95 %) and 130 kg of technical-grade NaNO₃ (purity 96 %).

**Step 1 — Compute actual reactant masses:**
$$m_{KCl,\,pure} = 100 \times 0.95 = 95.0 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{NaNO_3,\,pure} = 130 \times 0.96 = 124.8 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kilomoles:**
$$n_{KCl} = \frac{95.0}{74.6} = 1.2734 \text{ kmol}$$
$$n_{NaNO_3} = \frac{124.8}{85.0} = 1.4682 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Identify limiting reagent:**
$$n_{KCl} = 1.2734 < n_{NaNO_3} = 1.4682 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{KCl is limiting.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The reaction proceeds 1 : 1 by moles. Whichever reactant is *smaller in kmol* is the limiting reagent and determines the theoretical yield of both products.

**Step 4 — Theoretical yield of KNO₃ and NaCl:**
$$n_{KNO_3}^{th} = n_{NaCl}^{th} = n_{KCl} = 1.2734 \text{ kmol}$$

$$m_{KNO_3}^{th} = 1.2734 \times 101.1 = 128.74 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{NaCl}^{th} = 1.2734 \times 58.5 = 74.49 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Account for crystallizer efficiency (industrial ≈ 80 % single-stage):**
$$m_{KNO_3}^{practical} = 128.74 \times 0.80 = 102.99 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Excess NaNO₃ remaining in mother liquor:**
$$n_{NaNO_3}^{excess} = 1.4682 - 1.2734 = 0.1948 \text{ kmol} \times 85 = 16.56 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequent Pitfall:** Students often forget that the reaction is **not quantitative** because of mixed-salt solubilities. They write "100 % yield", losing 2–3 marks. Always quote an **80–85 % single-stage crystallization yield** and mention the mother-liquor recycle loop.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Stoichiometric Derivation — Route B (Haug Process)

**Reaction stoichiometry:**
$$\text{KCl (aq)} + \text{HNO}_3 \text{ (aq)} \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 \text{ (aq)} + \text{HCl (transferred to org)}$$

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric requirements per 1 tonne KNO₃ product:**
$$\frac{1\,000\,000 \text{ g KNO}_3}{101.1 \text{ g/mol}} = 9891.2 \text{ mol KNO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent KCl and HNO₃ needed (1 : 1 : 1 stoichiometry):**
$$m_{KCl,\,pure} = 9891.2 \times 74.6 \times 10^{-3} = 737.88 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{HNO_3,\,pure} = 9891.2 \times 63.0 \times 10^{-3} = 623.14 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Industrial-grade adjustments (assuming KCl 95 %, HNO₃ 60 % w/w):**
$$m_{KCl,\,tech} = \frac{737.88}{0.95} = 776.7 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{HNO_3,\,tech} = \frac{623.14}{0.60} = 1038.6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — HCl liberated (must be removed by solvent):**
$$m_{HCl,\,theo} = 9891.2 \times 36.5 \times 10^{-3} = 361.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — n-Pentanol solvent requirement (typical loading ≈ 0.5 kg HCl per kg solvent):**
$$m_{n\text{-}pentanol} = \frac{361.0}{0.50} \approx 722 \text{ kg per tonne KNO}_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic (Why so much solvent?):** n-Pentanol selectively extracts HCl from the aqueous phase via hydrogen bonding with the alcohol –OH. The extraction coefficient α ≈ 1.5–2.0, so a single contact is **insufficient**; industrial designs use **3–4 mixer-settler stages in counter-current mode**.

### 3.3 Material & Energy Logic Flow

| Stage | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dissolution | KCl + NaNO₃ + H₂O | Heated to 100 °C | Saturated solution |
| Hot filtration | Saturated solution | Remove insoluble + NaCl (crystallizes first) | Hot clarified liquor |
| Cooling crystallizer | Hot clarified liquor | Cooled to 25–30 °C | KNO₃ crystals + mother liquor |
| Centrifuge & wash | KNO₃ slurry | Solid-liquid separation | Wet crystals |
| Drying | Wet crystals | Rotary/flash dryer @ 110 °C | Dry KNO₃ (99 % purity) |
| Mother liquor recycle | ML | Re-evaporated | Returned to dissolution |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Double Decomposition (Conversion) Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KCl Feed Bin] --> B[Dissolution Tank]
    C[NaNO3 Feed Bin] --> B
    W[Process Water] --> B
    B --> D[Hot Evaporator]
    D --> E[Vacuum Filter NaCl Removal]
    E --> F[Cooling Crystallizer 25 C]
    F --> G[Centrifuge]
    G --> H[Rotary Dryer]
    H --> P[Pure KNO3 Product]
    G --> I[Mother Liquor Tank]
    I --> D
    E --> J[NaCl By product]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Direct HNO₃ Neutralization (Haug Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KCl Brine Tank] --> B[Reactor 1 with HNO3]
    N[HNO3 Storage] --> B
    B --> C[Three Stage Mixer Settler]
    S1[n Pentanol Solvent Loop] --> C
    C --> D[Aqueous KNO3 Phase]
    C --> E[Organic HCl Phase]
    D --> F[Stripping Column]
    F --> G[Evaporator and Crystallizer]
    G --> H[Centrifuge]
    H --> P[Pure KNO3 Crystals]
    H --> ML[Mother Liquor Recycle]
    E --> SR[Solvent Recovery Column]
    SR --> S1
    SR --> HC[Concentrated HCl By product]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture Matrix — Manufacturing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RouteA[Route A Double Decomposition]
        A1[KCl] --> A2[NaNO3]
        A2 --> A3[Hot Crystallization]
        A3 --> A4[KNO3 plus NaCl]
    end
    subgraph RouteB[Route B Direct Neutralization]
        B1[KCl] --> B2[HNO3]
        B2 --> B3[Solvent Extraction]
        B3 --> B4[KNO3 plus HCl]
    end
    subgraph RouteC[Route C Immingham]
        C1[KCl] --> C2[HNO3 plus MgO]
        C2 --> C3[Reactor]
        C3 --> C4[KNO3 plus MgCl2]
    end
```

### 4.4 Functional Annotation of the Conversion Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Walk-Through (KTU Expected Answer Style):**
> 1. **Dissolution Tank:** Both KCl and NaNO₃ are dissolved in minimum water at **~100 °C** to give a near-saturated quaternary solution.
> 2. **Hot Filtration:** Insoluble matter and most of the NaCl (because its solubility barely changes) are filtered off while hot.
> 3. **Cooling Crystallizer:** The hot filtrate is cooled gradually — KNO₃ being the most temperature-sensitive salt crystallizes out first.
> 4. **Centrifugation & Washing:** Crystals are separated, washed with a little cold water to remove adhering mother liquor.
> 5. **Drying:** Final product dried to < 0.1 % moisture.
> 6. **Mother Liquor Recycle:** The mother liquor containing residual NaNO₃ and KNO₃ is recycled to the dissolution tank to maximize yield.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
> **"List any four advantages of potassium nitrate as a fertilizer over muriate of potash (KCl)."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 Marks — distribute as 1 + 1 + 1):**

1. **Chloride-free nutrition:** KNO₃ supplies K without Cl⁻, preventing chloride toxicity in tobacco, potato, grapes, and citrus. *(1 mark)*
2. **Dual nutrient:** Provides both N (13 %) and K₂O (46 %) in a single salt — better nutrient balance, lower application cost. *(1 mark)*
3. **Low salt index (20.2)** and **non-hygroscopic** nature → safe for foliar spray, hydroponics, and bulk blending. *(1 mark)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Retest]**
> **"Why is potassium nitrate the preferred K-source for tobacco and poly-house cultivation?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Combustibility preservation:** Tobacco leaves with high chloride content burn poorly and produce acrid smoke; KNO₃ maintains leaf combustibility and aroma. *(1 mark)*
2. **Zero Na / Cl loading:** Hydroponic nutrient solutions cannot tolerate chloride accumulation; KNO₃ is the cleanest K-source available commercially. *(1 mark)*
3. **Steep solubility curve** allows easy preparation of concentrated A-tank stock solutions without precipitation. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Level Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A — Double Decomposition Process**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of potassium nitrate by the double-decomposition (conversion) method**. Discuss the underlying solubility principle. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A charge of **1000 kg KCl (95 % pure)** and **1100 kg NaNO₃ (96 % pure)** is processed. Calculate the **theoretical yield of KNO₃**, **excess NaNO₃ remaining**, and **NaCl by-product**, assuming 82 % single-stage crystallization efficiency. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Description (7 Marks — Distribute as 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1):**

1. **Principle:** Reaction is based on large differences in solubility-vs-temperature curves of KNO₃ versus NaCl. *(1 mark)*
2. **Stoichiometry:** $\text{KCl} + \text{NaNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Dissolution:** Both salts dissolved in minimum water at **100 °C**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Hot filtration:** NaCl (solubility ~ constant) crystallizes first and is filtered while hot. *(1 mark)*
5. **Cooling:** Hot filtrate cooled to **25–30 °C** — KNO₃ crystallizes (solubility drops ~95 %). *(1 mark)*
6. **Centrifugation & drying** → Pure KNO₃ product. *(1 mark)*
7. **Mother liquor recycled** for economy. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Material Balance Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Pure reactant masses:**
$$m_{KCl} = 1000 \times 0.95 = 950 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{NaNO_3} = 1100 \times 0.96 = 1056 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kmol:**
$$n_{KCl} = \frac{950}{74.6} = 12.734 \text{ kmol}$$
$$n_{NaNO_3} = \frac{1056}{85.0} = 12.424 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting reagent:**
$$n_{NaNO_3} = 12.424 < n_{KCl} = 12.734 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{NaNO}_3 \text{ is limiting.}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical yields:**
$$m_{KNO_3}^{th} = 12.424 \times 101.1 = 1256.1 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{NaCl}^{th} = 12.424 \times 58.5 = 726.8 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Excess KCl remaining:**
$$n_{KCl}^{excess} = 12.734 - 12.424 = 0.310 \text{ kmol} = 23.13 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Practical yield @ 82 %:**
$$m_{KNO_3}^{practical} = 1256.1 \times 0.82 = 1030.0 \text{ kg}$$

> **Final Answers:** Theoretical KNO₃ = **1256.1 kg**; Practical KNO₃ ≈ **1030 kg**; NaCl ≈ **726.8 kg**; Excess KCl ≈ **23.1 kg**.

---

#### **Question B — Haug / Direct Neutralization Route (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **manufacture of KNO₃ from KCl and HNO₃ (Haug process)** with a flow diagram. Why is an organic solvent used? **7 Marks**

**(b)** Compare potassium nitrate with **sulphate of potash (K₂SO₄)** and **murate of potash (KCl)** as fertilizers. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Haug Process Description (7 Marks):**

1. **Reaction:** $\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl}$ *(1 mark)*
2. **Reactor:** Reaction carried out at **60–80 °C** in a stirred tank reactor. *(1 mark)*
3. **Solvent extraction:** Mixed with **n-pentanol / isopentanol** which selectively extracts HCl into organic phase. *(1 mark)*
4. **Phase separation:** In 3–4 stage **mixer-settler**, organic phase (HCl-rich) is decanted from aqueous KNO₃ phase. *(1 mark)*
5. **Solvent recovery:** Organic phase is stripped in a distillation column — solvent recycled, concentrated HCl recovered. *(1 mark)*
6. **Crystallization:** Aqueous KNO₃ phase is evaporated and cooled → KNO₃ crystals centrifuged and dried. *(1 mark)*
7. **Why solvent?** To shift equilibrium and obtain pure KNO₃ *without co-crystallization* of KCl; also to recover HCl as a valuable by-product (vinyl chloride precursor). *(1 mark)*

**(b) Comparative Table (7 Marks — 7 columns × 1 mark each or a 3 × 5 matrix):**

| Parameter | KNO₃ | K₂SO₄ | KCl |
|---|---|---|---|
| % K₂O | **46** | **50** | **60** |
| % N | **13** | 0 | 0 |
| Accompanying ion | NO₃⁻ (beneficial) | SO₄²⁻ (neutral) | Cl⁻ (often harmful) |
| Salt Index | **20.2** | 46.1 | 116 |
| Suitability for tobacco | **Excellent** | Good | Poor |
| Suitability for chloride-sensitive crops | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Hygroscopicity | Non | Non | Slight |
| Cost per unit K₂O | **Highest** | Moderate | Lowest |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mother-liquor recycle** in Route A: this is worth 1–2 marks in flow diagrams.
> 2. **Not specifying the operating temperature (100 °C hot → 25 °C cold)** for crystallization: examiners look for it explicitly.
> 3. **Stating 100 % yield** in mass-balance problems: always cite **80–85 % single-stage** and justify with mother-liquor recycle.
> 4. **Confusing "salt index" with "salinity"**: salt index is a *relative* dimensionless number, not salinity itself.
> 5. **Writing KNO₃ percentage as 13.85 %** instead of commercial-grade **13 %** — round to industrial spec.
> 6. **In Haug process, omitting solvent recovery column**: shows incomplete process understanding and costs ~1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 — Potassium Nitrate Rapid-Fire Revision List:**
> - KNO₃ = **13 % N + 46 % K₂O** = **101.1 g/mol** molecular weight.
> - **Two industrial routes:** (1) Double decomposition KCl + NaNO₃, (2) Direct neutralization KCl + HNO₃ (Haug).
> - **Conversion process** relies on **steep solubility curve of KNO₃** (13.3 → 246 g/100 g H₂O from 0 → 100 °C).
> - **NaCl solubility is nearly constant** → filtered off while hot.
> - **Haug process** uses **n-pentanol** as HCl-extracting solvent in 3–4 mixer-settler stages.
> - **Salt Index of KNO₃ ≈ 20.2** — *lowest among major potash fertilizers*.
> - **Non-hygroscopic** → ideal for bulk blending and storage in tropics.
> - **Preferred for tobacco, potato, grapes, citrus, berries** — chloride-sensitive crops.
> - **By-products:** NaCl (Route A), concentrated HCl (Route B — valuable for PVC manufacture).
> - **Mass balance standard efficiency:** ~**80–85 % single-stage**, rest recovered via mother-liquor recycle.
> - **Immingham variant:** uses MgO to fix HCl as MgCl₂ — fewer process stages but lower-grade KNO₃.
> - **Molecular weights to memorize:** KCl = 74.6; NaNO₃ = 85.0; KNO₃ = 101.1; NaCl = 58.5; HCl = 36.5; HNO₃ = 63.0.
> - **Stoichiometric excess of NaNO₃** in Route A typically **5–10 %** above theoretical to drive complete KCl conversion.
> - **World's largest producers:** Chile (SQM), Israel (Haifa), Jordan (APC), China — predominantly from natural caliche ore and brines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
