<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"What is Potassium Nitrate? Why is it preferred over KCl for certain crops?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Potassium Nitrate (KNO₃) is a binary fertilizer with the chemical formula KNO₃, containing 13 % N (nitrate form) and 46 % K₂O.*

It is preferred over KCl for:
1. **Chloride-sensitive crops** such as tobacco, potato, grapes, citrus, and berries — excess Cl⁻ reduces yield and quality (e.g., tobacco burn, potato dry-matter loss).
2. **Nitrate form of N** is directly absorbed by plants without undergoing nitrification, giving rapid green-up.
3. **Neutral pH** (~7) does not acidify the soil, unlike KCl which can be mildly acidic over time.
4. **High K₂O concentration (46 %)** compared to KCl (≈ 60 % K₂O equivalent but with 47 % Cl).

*(Examiner's distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for reasons.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"State the solubility of KNO₃ in water at 0 °C and 100 °C. Explain the industrial significance of this difference."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Temperature | Solubility of KNO₃ |
|---|---|
| **0 °C** | **13.3 g / 100 g H₂O** |
| **100 °C** | **246 g / 100 g H₂O** |

**Industrial significance:** The solubility changes by a factor of **~ 18.5×** between 0 °C and 100 °C. This **steep positive solubility gradient** is the basis of the **Conversion Process** for KNO₃ manufacture — a hot saturated solution on cooling precipitates almost the entire dissolved KNO₃, allowing easy, low-energy crystallization. This is in contrast to NaCl whose solubility changes only marginally (35.7 → 39.2), enabling it to crystallize out by *evaporation* at high temperature.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **industrial manufacture of Potassium Nitrate from sodium nitrate and potassium chloride** with a neat flow diagram. Explain the **principle of selective crystallization** based on solubility data.

**(b)** [7 marks] A plant produces KNO₃ from 1500 kg/day of NaNO₃ and 1300 kg/day of KCl. Calculate (i) the limiting reactant, (ii) theoretical yield of KNO₃, (iii) mass of NaCl co-product, and (iv) actual production at 90 % conversion efficiency.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q-A (a):**

**Definition (½ mark):** The Conversion Process (Konversionsverfahren) is a **double-decomposition (metathesis)** reaction between sodium nitrate and potassium chloride, driven to completion by **selective crystallization** exploiting the differential solubility of NaCl and KNO₃ at different temperatures.

**Reaction:**

$$\text{NaNO}_3\,(85) + \text{KCl}\,(74.5) \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3\,(101.1) + \text{NaCl}\,(58.5)$$

**Process Steps (1½ marks per step, total 5 marks):**

1. **Dissolution:** NaNO₃ and KCl are dissolved in hot (80–100 °C) **recycled mother liquor** inside a steam-heated dissolver.

2. **Evaporation & NaCl crystallization:** The solution is evaporated at 100–115 °C. Because **NaCl solubility is nearly constant (35.7 → 39.2 g/100 g H₂O)** between 0–100 °C, while KNO₃ is highly soluble at 100 °C, **NaCl crystallizes first** on water removal. The Jänecke diagram at 100 °C shows NaCl has the largest crystallization field.

3. **Hot filtration:** The NaCl slurry is filtered at > 90 °C (to keep KNO₃ in solution) in a rotary vacuum filter. NaCl is sent to chlor-alkali plant.

4. **Cooling crystallization of KNO₃:** The hot filtrate is cooled in **swenson-walker or vacuum crystallizers** from 100 °C → 5 °C. KNO₃ solubility drops from 246 → 13.3 g/100 g, giving ~ 90 % yield.

5. **Centrifugation, washing & drying:** KNO₃ crystals are separated, washed with **saturated KNO₃ brine**, dried at 120–150 °C in fluidized-bed dryer to < 0.1 % moisture, then coated with 0.1–0.3 % oil for anti-caking and bagged.

6. **Mother liquor recycle:** All mother liquor is recycled to the dissolver for zero-liquid discharge.

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.1 (block diagram with hot dissolver → evaporator → NaCl filter → crystallizer → centrifuge → dryer).

**Principle of Selective Crystallization (1 mark):**

The reaction itself is reversible with **Keq ≈ 1** in dilute solution. The separation is therefore **NOT** chemical but **physical**: by choosing a temperature where one product (NaCl) has minimum solubility relative to the other (KNO₃), we precipitate it out. At 100 °C, NaCl is the **least soluble relative to KNO₃**; at 5 °C, KNO₃ becomes the **least soluble**. This **field inversion** in the Jänecke phase diagram is the engineering lever.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q-A (b):**

**Given:** 1500 kg/day NaNO₃; 1300 kg/day KCl. Molar masses: NaNO₃ = 85.0, KCl = 74.5, KNO₃ = 101.1, NaCl = 58.5.

**Step 1 — Moles of each reactant (1 mark):**

$$n_{\text{NaNO}_3} = \dfrac{1500}{85.0} = 17.647 \text{ kmol/day}$$

$$n_{\text{KCl}} = \dfrac{1300}{74.5} = 17.450 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 2 — Limiting reactant (1 mark):**

Reaction stoichiometry is **1:1**; hence KCl (17.450) < NaNO₃ (17.647) → **KCl is limiting**. Excess NaNO₃ = 17.647 − 17.450 = 0.197 kmol/day → recycled with mother liquor.

**Step 3 — Theoretical yield of KNO₃ (2 marks):**

$$m_{\text{KNO}_3,\,\text{theo}} = 17.450 \times 101.1 = 1764.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of NaCl co-product (1 mark):**

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = 17.450 \times 58.5 = 1020.8 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Actual production at 90 % efficiency (2 marks):**

$$m_{\text{KNO}_3,\,\text{actual}} = 1764.2 \times 0.90 = 1587.8 \text{ kg/day} \approx 1588 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Tabular Result (½ mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Limiting reactant | KCl |
| Theoretical KNO₃ | 1764.2 kg/day |
| NaCl co-product | 1020.8 kg/day |
| Actual KNO₃ (90 %) | 1587.8 kg/day |

---

### **QUESTION B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a **Jänecke phase diagram** for the system KNO₃–NaCl–KCl–NaNO₃–H₂O, explain how the **field inversion between 100 °C and 5 °C** governs the Conversion Process for KNO₃ manufacture.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **alternative industrial methods** for KNO₃ preparation (other than the NaNO₃–KCl route) with their **reactions, merits, and limitations**. Compare them with the conversion process on energy and purity.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q-B (a):**

**Jänecke Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The Jänecke diagram is a **square (or diamond) representation** of the **quaternary reciprocal salt system** K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻, NO₃⁻, H₂O. The four salt corners are:

- Top: KNO₃
- Bottom: NaCl
- Left: KCl
- Right: NaNO₃

The water is implicitly represented by the saturation isotherm. At any temperature, the interior of the square is divided into **four crystallization fields** by the **invariant (eutonic) point** where two salts saturate simultaneously.

**At 100 °C (2 marks):**
- NaCl crystallization field is **largest** (occupies ~ 60–70 % of the diagram).
- KCl, KNO₃, NaNO₃ fields are small.
- **Implication:** As water is evaporated, the composition moves toward the NaCl corner, and NaCl is the **first salt to saturate and crystallize** — the basis of *hot* separation.

**At 5 °C / 25 °C (2 marks):**
- The NaCl field **shrinks dramatically**.
- The KNO₃ field **expands** to dominate the diagram (~ 50–60 %).
- **Implication:** On cooling, the KNO₃-corner side becomes the saturated phase, and **KNO₃ crystallizes first** — the basis of *cold* separation.

**Field Inversion and Process Logic (1 mark):**

The **swap of dominant fields** between 100 °C and 5 °C is the *physical lever* of the Conversion Process. The two-step temperature swing exploits this inversion:
- Hot stage → strip out NaCl (unwanted).
- Cold stage → harvest KNO₃ (wanted).

This double-step eliminates the need for an organic solvent, ion-exchange resin, or membrane, making the process **highly energy-efficient and elegant**.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q-B (b):**

**Alternative Method 1 — Direct Neutralization of HNO₃ with KCl (2 marks):**

$$\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl}$$

- *Merits:* High-purity KNO₃; uses cheap HNO₃; HCl is a saleable co-product.
- *Limitations:* Corrosion (HCl + HNO₃ mix); KCl competes for HNO₃ — equilibrium favors *unchanged* KCl in solution. **Conversion ≈ 60 %** per pass; needs multiple stages or extractive distillation.

**Alternative Method 2 — Solvent Extraction with Amines (2 marks):**

Uses tertiary amines (e.g., Alamine 336) in kerosene to selectively extract HCl, shifting equilibrium:
$$\text{KCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{KNO}_3 + \text{HCl (extracted by amine)}$$

- *Merits:* > 95 % KNO₃ yield, food/fertilizer grade, modern.
- *Limitations:* High capex, solvent loss, amine degradation.

**Alternative Method 3 — Ion-Exchange Resin Method (1.5 marks):**

K-form cation resin loaded with K⁺ is contacted with HNO₃:
$$\text{R-K}^+ + \text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{R-H}^+ + \text{KNO}_3$$

Regenerated with KCl. Continuous, very pure (electronic-grade) KNO₃.
- *Limitations:* Resin cost, regeneration chemistry, low throughput.

**Alternative Method 4 — From K₂CO₃ + HNO₃ (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{KNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

- *Merits:* Lab/analytical grade KNO₃.
- *Limitations:* K₂CO₃ is expensive; unsuitable for bulk fertilizer.

**Comparison Table (½ mark):**

| Method | Energy | Purity | Cost | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conversion (NaNO₃+KCl)** | Low | Good (95 %) | Low | **Dominant** |
| HNO₃ + KCl direct | Medium | High | Medium | Niche |
| Solvent extraction | High | Very high | High capex | Modern |
| Ion exchange | Medium | Ultra-high | High | Pharma only |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The **Conversion Process** remains the **workhorse of global KNO₃ production** (~ 70 % of world capacity) due to raw-material availability (NaNO₃ from natural caliche, KCl from sylvinite) and low energy footprint.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing the reaction WITHOUT state symbols or molar masses** → lose 1 mark. Always write `NaNO₃(85) + KCl(74.5) → KNO₃(101.1) + NaCl(58.5)`.
> 2. **Confusing "limiting reactant" determination** → in the KTU 2024 scheme, ALWAYS show mole calculation, not just mass ratio.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention Jänecke diagram** in the principle of selective crystallization → this is a *mandatory* diagram; missing it = –2 marks.
> 4. **Mentioning "mother liquor recycle"** earns a bonus ½ mark — examiners love it.
> 5. **Not specifying wash liquor composition** (saturated KNO₃ brine, NOT water) — students often write "water wash" → incorrect, causes crystal dissolution losses.
> 6. **Skipping the actual yield calculation** at the end of numerical problems — even if theoretical yield is asked, ALWAYS state the % conversion assumption explicitly.
> 7. **Mixing up KNO₃ solubilities** at 0 °C and 100 °C — memorize 13.3 and 246, and 35.7 and 39.2 for NaCl.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **KNO₃ = Saltpeter = Nitre = 13-0-46 grade fertilizer**; 13 % N (nitrate) + 46 % K₂O; chloride-free.
- [x] **Molar masses (must memorize):** NaNO₃ = 85, KCl = 74.5, KNO₃ = 101.1, NaCl = 58.5.
- [x] **Core reaction:** NaNO₃ + KCl ⇌ KNO₃ + NaCl (metathesis, driven by solubility).
- [x] **Solubility at 0 °C → 100 °C:** KNO₃ jumps from **13.3 → 246 g** (factor ~ 18.5×); NaCl barely changes **35.7 → 39.2 g** (factor ~ 1.1×).
- [x] **Key principle = Selective crystallization based on Jänecke phase diagram field inversion** between 100 °C and 5 °C.
- [x] **NaCl crystallizes during hot evaporation (100 °C); KNO₃ crystallizes during cold cooling (5 °C).**
- [x] **Process order:** Dissolution → Evaporation → Hot NaCl filtration → Cooling crystallization → Centrifugation → Washing with saturated KNO₃ brine → Drying → Anti-caking → Bagging.
- [x] **Wash liquor is NOT water** — must be saturated brine of the crystal being washed.
- [x] **Mother liquor is recycled** to dissolver (zero-liquid discharge, KTU favourite point).
- [x] **Co-product NaCl** is sold to chlor-alkali plant (economic bonus).
- [x] **Alternative methods:** HNO₃ + KCl direct, Solvent extraction, Ion exchange, K₂CO₃ + HNO₃.
- [x] **Yield calculation steps:** Balanced equation → Moles → Limiting reactant → Theoretical yield → Actual yield = Theo × (η/100).
- [x] **Stoichiometric mass ratio NaNO₃ : KCl = 85 : 74.5 = 1.141 : 1.**
- [x] **Application memory:** Tobacco, potato, grapes, citrus, solar thermal storage, gunpowder, food-grade E252.
- [x] **Drying temperature** = 120 – 150 °C; **final moisture** < 0.1 %; **anti-caking oil** = 0.1 – 0.3 %.
- [x] **Industrial leaders** in KNO₃ production: Haifa Group (Israel), SQM (Chile), K+S (Germany), RCF (India).
- [x] **KNO₃ is mildly hygroscopic** — store in sealed HDPE bags with inner liner.
- [x] **Comparison with KCl:** KNO₃ has lower K₂O (46 vs 60) but is chloride-free; hence more expensive but mandatory for sensitive crops.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Strategy (2024 Scheme):**
> - In 14-mark questions, allocate **2 marks for flow diagram**, **2 marks for reaction + molar masses**, **6 marks for stepwise process description**, **2 marks for principle (Jänecke)**, **2 marks for yields/numerical**.
> - Always draw the **Jänecke square diagram** in principle-type questions.
> - For numericals, the **balanced equation with molar masses** in the FIRST line is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potassium Sulphate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potassium Sulphate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potassium Sulphate (K₂SO₄)**, commercially marketed as **Sulphate of Potash (SOP)**, is a white, crystalline, water-soluble salt that serves as a dual-nutrient fertilizer supplying both **potassium (K)** and **sulphur (S)** to crops. It is the preferred K-source for crops sensitive to chloride (e.g., tobacco, potatoes, grapes, citrus, and several horticultural crops).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PECHT637 / Module 3**
> Potassium Sulphate is studied under the unit dealing with **manufacture of nitrogen-free straight / compound fertilizers**. Students are expected to master (i) chemistry, (ii) the principal industrial manufacturing routes (Mannheim, Hargreaves, double-decomposition, alunite, and natural brine processes), and (iii) the engineering of crystallization, drying, and granulation.

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | K₂SO₄ |
| Molar Mass | **174.26 g·mol⁻¹** |
| K₂O equivalent | **50 – 52 %** (theoretical 54.06 %) |
| Sulphur content | **17 – 18 %** (theoretical 18.39 %) |
| Density | 2.66 g·cm⁻³ |
| Melting Point | 1069 °C |
| Solubility in water (20 °C) | **≈ 11.0 g per 100 mL** |
| pH of saturated solution | **≈ 7 (neutral)** |
| Crystal System | Orthorhombic |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Salty-Ionic Snack for Plants"**
> Imagine a chocolate bar (KCl — Muriate of Potash, MOP) that is deliciously rich in potassium but has an unavoidable "side-dish" of **chloride ions**. For most people the salt is fine, but a few people (chloride-sensitive plants) develop a stomach-ache — leaf burn, poor tuber quality, or reduced sugar content.
> Potassium sulphate is essentially the **same potassium but with a much friendlier side-dish** — the sulphate ion (SO₄²⁻), which itself is a useful plant nutrient (sulphur) and is far less harsh. So K₂SO₄ is the **"premium, mild-flavoured, chloride-free"** version of potash fertilizer.
> The challenge, however, is *making* it cheaply — because nature mostly gives us KCl, and chemists must "swap" the chloride for sulphate through clever reactions. That is what the manufacturing routes below achieve.

### 1.3 Importance in Indian & Global Agriculture

- K₂SO₄ is the **second most consumed potassium fertilizer** worldwide after KCl.
- Global production capacity exceeds **8 million tonnes per year**, with China, Belgium, Germany, and the USA being major producers.
- In India, K₂SO₀₄ is imported in significant quantities as India has **virtually no indigenous reserves of soluble potash minerals**; domestic production is limited to small units using the **double-decomposition (gypsum / Epsom salt) route**.
- Used heavily for **cash crops**: tobacco, sugarcane, fruits, vegetables, tea, coffee, oilseeds, and in hydroponics.

### 1.4 Forms, Grades, and Specifications (FCO, India)

| Grade / Specification | K₂O (%) min. | S (%) min. | Moisture (%) max. | Free Acid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Granular SOP | 50 | 17 | 1.5 | < 0.5 % |
| Powdered / Crystalline | 50 | 17 | 1.0 | < 0.5 % |
| Water-soluble SOP (fertigation) | 52 | 18 | 0.5 | < 0.1 % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Chemical Reactions — All Major Manufacturing Routes

The industrial manufacture of K₂SO₄ centres on **reacting a potassium salt (almost always KCl or K₂CO₃) with a sulphate source**. Each route trades off economics, raw-material availability, and purity.

#### 2.1.1 Mannheim Process (HCl co-product, dominant global route)

$$2\,\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; 2\,\text{HCl}\uparrow$$

- Operated in a **Mannheim furnace** — a refractory-lined, gas-fired, hemispherical cast-iron muffle stirred by a central shaft with two arms.
- Two-stage endothermic → exothermic progression, with peak wall temperatures of **≈ 700 – 800 °C**.
- The liberated HCl gas is absorbed in adiabatic or isothermal absorbers to produce 30 – 33 % hydrochloric acid — a **valuable co-product** that subsidises the fertilizer cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HCl must be recovered:** If vented, it violates environmental norms. Recovery makes Mannheim economically viable because **two saleable products** are obtained from one furnace.

#### 2.1.2 Hargreaves Process (no acid plant required)

$$4\,\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{SO}_3 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; 2\,\text{HCl}$$

- In practice, SO₃ is *generated in situ* by burning sulphur in air:
$$ \text{S} \;+\; \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SO}_2;\quad 2\,\text{SO}_2 \;+\; \text{O}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\,\text{SO}_3 $$
- The SO₃ + steam mixture is passed over a bed of KCl pellets in a **Hargreaves reactor** (a vertical, cylindrical, multi-tray tower).
- HCl obtained is dilute (≈ 18 – 20 %) and is typically absorbed to produce commercial-grade acid.

#### 2.1.3 Double-Decomposition (Gypsum Route) — popular in India

$$\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Wait — that is the wrong direction. The actual route is:

$$\underbrace{\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4}_{\text{desired}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{CaCl}_2}_{\text{cheap by-product}}$$
is *not* what is done. The reverse — reacting **KCl with CaSO₄ or MgSO₄** — is what is industrially practiced.

**With Epsom salt (MgSO₄·7H₂O):**
$$2\,\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{MgSO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{MgCl}_2$$

**With gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) in presence of ammonia — the "Krystal" / gypsum-ammonia process:**
$$2\,\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{CaSO}_4 \;+\; 2\,\text{NH}_3 \;+\; \text{CO}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow \;+\; 2\,\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

- CaCO₃ precipitates (driving force); K₂SO₄ stays in solution and is crystallized; NH₄Cl is recovered as a **bonus fertilizer** (1.0 t K₂SO₄ yields ≈ 0.55 t NH₄Cl).

#### 2.1.4 From Alunite (KAl₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆)

$$\text{KAl}_3(\text{SO}_4)_2(\text{OH})_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{calcination @ 600 °C}}\; \text{KAl(SO}_4)_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}\uparrow$$

$$\text{KAl(SO}_4)_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\;(\text{hydrated alumina})$$

- Pilot plants in USSR / Hungary have demonstrated feasibility; not commercially dominant today.

#### 2.1.5 From Natural Brines (Great Salt Lake, Dead Sea)

- Mixed brines of K, Mg, Na, Cl, SO₄ are evaporated in staged solar ponds.
- Carnallite (KCl·MgCl₂·6H₂O) crystallizes first, then kainite (KCl·MgSO₄·3H₂O), then Schoenite (K₂SO₄·MgSO₄·6H₂O).
- Schoenite is further processed:
$$\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \cdot \text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \text{KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \downarrow \;+\; \text{MgCl}_2 \;+\; 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.2 Mannheim Process — Detailed Engineering Discussion

The Mannheim furnace is the work-horse of SOP manufacture. Understanding its geometry, energy balance, and kinetics is a **frequently asked KTU question**.

**Geometry:** Refractory-lined, gas-fired, domed cast-iron pan of diameter **3 – 6 m**, fitted with a vertical shaft holding 2 cast-iron stirring arms fitted with replaceable ploughs / scrapers.

**Operating Steps (logic chain):**

1. **Charging**: Solid KCl is fed continuously at the centre.
2. **Acid dosing**: 96 – 98 % H₂SO₄ is sprayed through a lance. Initial reaction:
$$\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{KHSO}_4 \;+\; \text{HCl}$$
   — mildly exothermic, gives a pasty mass.
3. **Main reaction**: Temperature rises; ploughs push the mass outward. The KHSO₄ reacts with more KCl:
$$\text{KHSO}_4 \;+\; \text{KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; \text{HCl}\uparrow$$
   — strongly exothermic, solid K₂SO₄ forms.
4. **Discharge**: Finished K₂SO₄ falls off the rim into a screw conveyor / cooler.
5. **HCl recovery**: Gases pass through a cyclone → tail-scrubber → adiabatic absorber.

**Operating Parameters (typical):**
- Furnace temperature: **700 – 800 °C**
- Acid strength: **96 %** minimum
- KCl purity: **≥ 95 %** (industrial grade)
- H₂SO₄ excess: **3 – 5 %** over stoichiometric
- Residence time: **15 – 30 minutes**
- HCl yield: **98 – 99 %** of theoretical
- Energy consumption: **≈ 1.8 – 2.2 GJ per tonne of K₂SO₄** (fired mostly by natural gas or fuel oil).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these — they are the high-yield items examiners test.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Use / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{K}_2\text{O equivalent (\%)} = \dfrac{94.20}{174.26}\times\%K_2SO_4 \times 100 \approx 0.5406\times\%K_2SO_4$ | Convert K₂SO₄ to K₂O for fertilizer grade | wt % |
| 2 | $\%S = \dfrac{32.06}{174.26}\times\%K_2SO_4 \times 100$ | Sulphur content in fertilizer | wt % |
| 3 | Stoichiometric KCl : H₂SO₄ = $2 \times 74.55 : 98.08 = 1.520 : 1$ by mass | Mannheim raw-material ratio | kg / kg |
| 4 | Stoichiometric KCl : MgSO₄·7H₂O = $2 \times 74.55 : 246.51 = 0.605 : 1$ by mass | Epsom-salt route | kg / kg |
| 5 | Theoretical HCl yield = $\dfrac{2 \times 36.46}{174.26} = 0.4185$ kg per kg K₂SO₄ | Acid co-product accounting | kg / kg |
| 6 | Energy for Mannheim ≈ 2.0 GJ t⁻¹ K₂SO₄ | Utility calculation | GJ / t |
| 7 | Crystallization yield $Y = \dfrac{C_s - C_f}{C_s - C_0}\times 100$ | Recovery of K₂SO₄ from mother liquor | % |
| 8 | Solubility at 25 °C: **12.0 g / 100 g water**; at 100 °C: **24.1 g / 100 g water** | Crystallizer sizing | g/100g |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{abs}} = \dfrac{\text{HCl in acid}}{\text{HCl in furnace gas}}\times 100$ | Absorption efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\text{Purity} = \dfrac{\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ in product}}{\text{Total mass}}\times 100$ | Product specification | wt % |

**Where $\;C_s$ = saturation concentration, $\;C_f$ = final mother-liquor concentration, $\;C_0$ = initial concentration.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae are used in (i) **material-balance** problems, (ii) **heat-load** calculations for the Mannheim furnace, (iii) **crystallizer** design (MSMPR — Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal), and (iv) **environmental compliance** (HCl stack emissions).

### 2.4 KTU Common Mistake — K₂O vs. K confusion

| Ion | Atomic / Molecular Mass | Contribution per K |
|---|---|---|
| K | 39.10 | 1.000 |
| K₂O | 94.20 | 2.000 × 39.10 / 94.20 = 0.830 |

Fertilizer grades are universally expressed as **% K₂O**, *not* elemental K. Always divide by 0.830 to convert K → K₂O; multiply by 0.830 to go K₂O → K.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Material Balance on a Mannheim Furnace

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A Mannheim furnace produces **1500 kg h⁻¹** of K₂SO₄ containing **98 %** K₂SO₄ by mass (remainder is moisture and trace Na₂SO₄). Industrial-grade KCl of **95 % purity** and 96 % H₂SO₄ are used. Calculate:
(a) The hourly feed rates of KCl and H₂SO₄.
(b) The hourly production of 33 % HCl acid (assuming 96 % HCl recovery efficiency).

---

**Step 1 — Identify the principal reaction.**

$$2\,\text{KCl} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 \;+\; 2\,\text{HCl}$$

- Molecular masses: KCl = 74.55; H₂SO₄ = 98.08; K₂SO₄ = 174.26; HCl = 36.46.

**Step 2 — Purity-corrected K₂SO₄ in product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pure K}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ produced per hour} &= 1500 \times 0.98 \\
&= 1470 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric requirement of pure KCl.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KCl required (pure)} &= 1470 \times \dfrac{2 \times 74.55}{174.26} \\
&= 1470 \times \dfrac{149.10}{174.26} \\
&= 1470 \times 0.8557 \\
&= 1257.9 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for KCl purity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Industrial KCl feed} &= \dfrac{1257.9}{0.95} \\
&= 1324.1 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric requirement of pure H₂SO₄.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ required (pure)} &= 1470 \times \dfrac{98.08}{174.26} \\
&= 1470 \times 0.5629 \\
&= 827.5 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjust for acid strength (96 %).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{96 \% H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ feed} &= \dfrac{827.5}{0.96} \\
&= 861.9 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Theoretical HCl generated.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HCl (theoretical)} &= 1470 \times \dfrac{2 \times 36.46}{174.26} \\
&= 1470 \times 0.4185 \\
&= 615.2 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply 96 % absorption efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HCl actually recovered} &= 615.2 \times 0.96 \\
&= 590.6 \text{ kg h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to 33 % commercial acid.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{33 \% HCl acid} &= \dfrac{590.6}{0.33} \\
&= 1789.7 \text{ kg h}^{-1} \approx 1.79 \text{ t h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Final Answers (boxed for KTU valuation):**

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Industrial KCl feed | **1324 kg h⁻¹** |
| 96 % H₂SO₄ feed | **862 kg h⁻¹** |
| 33 % HCl acid produced | **≈ 1790 kg h⁻¹** |

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — K₂O Equivalent Calculation

**Problem:** A fertilizer sample is **98.5 % pure K₂SO₄**. Find its %K₂O and %K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{K}_2\text{O} &= 98.5 \times \dfrac{94.20}{174.26} \times 100 \\
&= 98.5 \times 0.5406 \\
&= 53.25 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{K (elemental)} &= 98.5 \times \dfrac{2 \times 39.10}{174.26} \times 100 \\
&= 98.5 \times 0.4487 \\
&= 44.20 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using the conversion ratio:**
$$
\% \text{K} = \% \text{K}_2\text{O} \times 0.830 = 53.25 \times 0.830 = 44.20 \,\% \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Crystallizer Yield (K₂SO₄ from Epsom-Salt Route)

**Problem:** A solution containing **150 g of K₂SO₄ per 1000 g of water** at 80 °C is cooled to **20 °C** for crystallization. Solubility data:
- at 80 °C → 21.4 g K₂SO₄ / 100 g water
- at 20 °C → 11.0 g K₂SO₄ / 100 g water

Find the **% crystallization yield**.

**Step 1 — Identify states.**
- $C_s$ (saturation at 80 °C) = 21.4 g/100 g water — *but our solution is below saturation (150 g/1000 g = 15.0 g/100 g); so heat step not needed, simply cool the as-prepared solution.*

Actually, re-interpret: We dissolve 150 g K₂SO₄ in 1000 g water at 80 °C (15 g/100 g — unsaturated, OK). Then cool to 20 °C.

**Step 2 — Crystallized mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Concentration at 20 °C (C}_f\text{)} &= 11.0 \text{ g}/100\text{ g water} \\
\text{In 1000 g water, that is} &= 110.0 \text{ g K}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ remaining in solution}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass crystallized} &= 150.0 - 110.0 = 40.0 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Crystallization yield (using the formula from §2.3 row 7):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \dfrac{C_s^{80} - C_f^{20}}{C_s^{80} - C_0}\times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{21.4 - 11.0}{21.4 - 15.0}\times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{10.4}{6.4}\times 100 \\
&= 162.5 \,\% \quad ???
\end{aligned}
$$

This is impossible — yield > 100 % indicates the chosen formula needs re-application here (the simpler form gives the correct result).

**Simpler (correct) approach — recovery from feed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \dfrac{\text{Mass crystallized}}{\text{Mass initially dissolved}}\times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{40.0}{150.0}\times 100 \\
&= 26.7 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the two formulas differ:** Formula 7 in §2.3 is used when initial concentration *equals* saturation at the higher temperature (i.e., the solution is saturated at T_high). Here it is *unsaturated*, so we use the simpler mass balance.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Route Selection

| Criterion | Mannheim | Hargreaves | Gypsum-NH₃ | Alunite | Brine / Schoenite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw material cost | Medium | High | Low | Very low (ore) | Low |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Medium | Very high | Very high |
| By-product value | HCl (high) | HCl (medium) | CaCO₃ + NH₄Cl | Alumina | MgCl₂ |
| Product purity | Very high | High | Medium | High | High |
| Energy intensity | High | Medium | Low | Very high | Solar |
| Environmental load | Acid handling | SO₂ slip | Ammonia slip | Red mud | Brine disposal |
| Global capacity share | ≈ 50 % | ≈ 15 % | ≈ 10 % | < 1 % | ≈ 25 % |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mannheim Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KCl Silo Hopper] --> B[Rotary Feeder]
    B --> C[Mannheim Furnace Dome]
    H1[96 percent H2SO4 Storage Tank] --> P1[Acid Pump]
    P1 --> C
    F1[Natural Gas Burner] --> C
    C --> D[Hot K2SO4 Discharge Screw]
    C --> E[Hot HCl Gas Stream]
    D --> F[Rotary Cooler]
    F --> G[Crusher and Screener]
    G --> J[Finished SOP Bagging]
    E --> K[Cyclone Dust Collector]
    K --> L[Adiabatic HCl Absorber]
    L --> M[Isothermal HCl Absorber]
    L --> N[33 percent HCl Acid Storage]
    M --> N
    G --> R[Recycled Fines Return]
    R --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid KCl enters at the top of the furnace dome; 96 % H₂SO₄ is sprayed through a lance; natural gas is fired through the peripheral burner ring. Reaction yields hot K₂SO₄ and HCl gas. The K₂SO₄ is cooled, crushed, screened, and bagged; the HCl gas passes through a cyclone (removes entrained K₂SO₄ dust), then through a two-stage absorber producing 33 % commercial acid.

### 4.2 Hargreaves Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sulphur Melter] --> B[Sulphur Burner]
    B --> C[SO2 + SO3 Gas Cooler]
    K[KCl Hopper] --> D[Hargreaves Reactor Tower]
    S[Steam Superheater] --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E[K2SO4 Pellet Discharge]
    D --> F[HCl Gas Outlet]
    F --> G[Absorber Train]
    G --> H[Dilute HCl Acid Storage]
    E --> I[Screener]
    I --> J[Finished SOP]
    I --> K2[Oversize Crusher]
    K2 --> I
```

### 4.3 Gypsum–Ammonia Double-Decomposition — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Gypsum Crusher] --> M1[Mix Tank 1 Slurry]
    K[KCl Dissolver] --> M1
    M1 --> R1[Reactor with NH3 and CO2 Spargers]
    R1 --> F1[Filter CaCO3 Cake]
    F1 --> F2[CaCO3 Wash and Discard]
    R1 --> C1[Crystallizer K2SO4]
    C1 --> F3[Centrifuge]
    F3 --> P1[Wet K2SO4 Crystals]
    P1 --> DR[Dryer]
    DR --> S1[Screener]
    S1 --> BP[Bagging Plant]
    F3 --> ML[Mother Liquor NH4Cl]
    ML --> EV[Evaporator]
    EV --> CR[NH4Cl Crystallizer]
    CR --> F4[Centrifuge 2]
    F4 --> NH4P[NH4Cl Byproduct Packing]
```

### 4.4 Crystal Morphology Comparison (SOP vs. MOP)

| Feature | K₂SO₄ (SOP) | KCl (MOP) |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal habit | Hard, dense, orthorhombic prisms | Cubic, often agglomerated |
| Hygroscopicity | Very low | Moderate |
| Caking tendency | Low (with anti-caking agent) | Medium |
| Bulk density (kg m⁻³) | **1200 – 1400** | **1000 – 1200** |
| Storage stability | Excellent (no caking, no leaching) | Good |

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q.A.1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*List the chemical reactions involved in the manufacture of potassium sulphate by the **Mannheim process**. Write the molecular formula and molecular weight of K₂SO₄.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reaction**: $2\,\text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{HCl}\uparrow$ **(2 marks)**
2. **Molecular formula**: K₂SO₄; **Molecular weight** = 174.26 g mol⁻¹; contains 54.06 % K₂O equivalent **(1 mark)**

**Mark split:** Equation = 2 M; Formula + Mol. wt. = 1 M.

---

**Q.A.2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*State any three **advantages** of potassium sulphate over muriate of potash (KCl) as a fertilizer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks, one mark per point):**
1. **Chloride-free** — safe for chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, potato, grape, citrus). **(1 M)**
2. Supplies **secondary nutrient sulphur (≈ 18 % S)** along with potash. **(1 M)**
3. **Lower salt index** and **near-neutral pH**, hence does not acidify soils and is suitable for foliar / fertigation use. **(1 M)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat flow-sheet, describe the manufacture of **potassium sulphate by the Mannheim process**. Discuss the operating conditions and the recovery of the by-product HCl. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Mannheim furnace is to produce **2000 kg h⁻¹** of K₂SO₄ of 98 % purity using industrial KCl (95 % purity) and 98 % H₂SO₄. Calculate:
> - (i) Mass of KCl feed per hour.
> - (ii) Mass of 98 % H₂SO₄ feed per hour.
> - (iii) Mass of anhydrous HCl gas liberated (theoretical).
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Chemistry (2 marks):**
$$\text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{KHSO}_4 + \text{HCl}\quad\text{(mildly exothermic)}$$
$$\text{KHSO}_4 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{HCl}\uparrow\quad\text{(strongly exothermic)}$$
Overall: $2\,\text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{HCl}$.

**Step 2 — Equipment (3 marks):**
- **Mannheim furnace**: refractory-lined cast-iron dome, internal diameter 3 – 6 m, central shaft with two counter-rotating stirring arms fitted with replaceable ploughs. Gas-fired from a peripheral burner ring.
- **Cooling & size reduction**: rotary cooler → crusher → screener (oversize returns to crusher; fines returned to furnace).
- **HCl absorption train**: cyclone → tail scrubber → adiabatic absorber (falling-film, graphite or PVDF) → isothermal absorber → 33 % HCl storage.

**Step 3 — Operating conditions (1 mark):** Furnace dome 700 – 800 °C; acid strength 96 – 98 %; KCl feed ≥ 95 % purity; residence time 15 – 30 min; 3 – 5 % stoichiometric excess H₂SO₄.

**Step 4 — By-product HCl recovery (1 mark):** Hot HCl gas is quenched and absorbed in adiabatic + isothermal columns using demineralised water, yielding 33 % commercial hydrochloric acid. Tail gas scrubbed with dilute alkali to meet pollution norms (< 5 mg Nm⁻³ HCl).

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** K₂SO₄ output = 2000 kg h⁻¹ × 0.98 = **1960 kg h⁻¹ pure K₂SO₄**.

**(i) KCl feed (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KCl (pure)} &= 1960 \times \dfrac{2 \times 74.55}{174.26} = 1960 \times 0.8557 = 1677.2 \text{ kg h}^{-1}\\
\text{KCl (95 \%)} &= \dfrac{1677.2}{0.95} = \boxed{1765.5 \text{ kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) 98 % H₂SO₄ feed (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (pure)} &= 1960 \times \dfrac{98.08}{174.26} = 1960 \times 0.5629 = 1103.3 \text{ kg h}^{-1}\\
\text{98 \% H}_2\text{SO}_4 &= \dfrac{1103.3}{0.98} = \boxed{1125.8 \text{ kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Anhydrous HCl (theoretical) (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HCl} &= 1960 \times \dfrac{2 \times 36.46}{174.26} = 1960 \times 0.4185 \\
&= \boxed{820.3 \text{ kg h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** Compare the **Mannheim process** and the **Hargreaves process** for the manufacture of potassium sulphate with respect to raw materials, operating conditions, by-products, and economics. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **double-decomposition method** of manufacturing potassium sulphate using **gypsum and ammonia**. Give the reactions, process flow, and the by-products obtained. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) (7 marks)

| Aspect | Mannheim Process | Hargreaves Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw materials** | KCl + 96 % H₂SO₄ | KCl + Sulphur + Steam (SO₃ in situ) |
| **Reaction** | $2\text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}$ | $4\text{KCl} + \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HCl}$ |
| **Reactor** | Mannheim furnace (dome muffle) | Hargreaves tower (multi-tray vertical) |
| **Temperature** | 700 – 800 °C | 350 – 450 °C |
| **HCl concentration** | 30 – 33 % (concentrated) | 18 – 20 % (dilute) |
| **Acid handling** | Bulk liquid acid needed | Elemental sulphur burned — no liquid acid handling |
| **By-product value** | High (conc. HCl) | Lower (dilute HCl) |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (furnace + absorbers) | Lower (no H₂SO₄ plant) |
| **Energy** | High (≈ 2 GJ t⁻¹) | Medium |
| **Best suited** | Coastal plants with HCl market | Inland plants with sulphur availability |

**Mark split:** Tabular comparison = 5 marks; concluding preference remark = 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) (7 marks)

**Reaction (2 marks):**
$$2\,\text{KCl} + \text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$$

- Driving force: precipitation of **insoluble CaCO₃**.
- Reaction medium: aqueous; mild conditions (40 – 60 °C, atmospheric pressure).

**Process flow (4 marks):**
1. **Slurry preparation**: Crushed gypsum + saturated KCl solution charged to a stirred reactor.
2. **Ammoniation & carbonation**: NH₃ and CO₂ (often from a captive ammonia plant or ammonium-carbonate solution) sparged in. pH maintained at 9 – 10.
3. **Filtration**: CaCO₃ cake filtered off, washed, and used as a soil conditioner / cement raw material.
4. **Crystallization**: Filtrate contains K₂SO₄ + NH₄Cl. On evaporation and cooling, K₂SO₄ crystallizes first (lower solubility at high T). Centrifuged, washed, dried.
5. **NH₄Cl recovery**: Mother liquor is further evaporated to crystallize NH₄Cl — a **bonus nitrogenous fertilizer**.

**By-products (1 mark):**
- **CaCO₃** — cement / lime industry / soil amendment.
- **NH₄Cl** — 25 % N fertilizer, especially used for paddy.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE MARKS on Potassium Sulphate questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to purity-correct the KCl feed** — Examiners expect you to divide by 0.95 (or whatever purity is given). Skipping this costs 1 – 2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Writing K₂O content as K content** (or vice-versa). Always state the conversion factor 0.830 explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the HCl recovery section** in the Mannheim description. KTU examiners *always* ask about by-product handling — losing 2 – 3 marks if missed.
> 4. **Drawing the Mannheim furnace** without labelling the **stirring arms** and **acid lance** — the central shaft and ploughs are visual markers the examiner looks for.
> 5. **Writing unbalanced equations** for the gypsum-ammonia process — students frequently forget to balance NH₃ and CO₂; the balanced form earns full marks.
> 6. **Confusing K₂SO₄ with K₂S** (potassium sulphide) — a silly but surprisingly common slip that costs the "chemical correctness" mark.
> 7. **Not stating the % K₂O** when describing the product — examiners deduct if the grade is missing.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Potassium Sulphate (PECHT637 / M3)**
> - K₂SO₄ = Sulphate of Potash (SOP); **chloride-free** dual-nutrient (K + S) fertilizer.
> - Theoretical: **54.06 % K₂O**, **18.39 % S**; commercial grade ≥ 50 % K₂O.
> - **Molecular weight 174.26 g mol⁻¹**; solubility 11 g/100 g at 20 °C, 24 g/100 g at 100 °C.
> - Conversion factors: K → K₂O multiply by **1.205**; K₂O → K multiply by **0.830**.
> - **Mannheim process**: 2 KCl + H₂SO₄ → K₂SO₄ + 2 HCl↑ (700 – 800 °C, by-product 33 % HCl).
> - **Hargreaves process**: 4 KCl + SO₃ + H₂O → 2 K₂SO₄ + 2 HCl (350 – 450 °C, uses elemental S).
> - **Gypsum-ammonia process**: 2 KCl + CaSO₄ + 2 NH₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → K₂SO₄ + CaCO₃↓ + 2 NH₄Cl (low-temperature, low-cost, popular in India).
> - **Epsom-salt route**: 2 KCl + MgSO₄·7H₂O → K₂SO₄ + MgCl₂ (limited by MgSO₄ availability).
> - **Alunite route**: Calcination of KAl₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆ → KAl(SO₄)₂ → K₂SO₄ + hydrated alumina (historical only).
> - **Schoenite / brine route**: Solar evaporation of Great Salt Lake / Dead Sea brines (K₂SO₄·MgSO₄·6H₂O + KCl → K₂SO₄↓).
> - Mannheim furnace components: **dome muffle, central shaft, two stirring arms with ploughs, peripheral gas burner, acid lance, peripheral discharge lip**.
> - HCl recovery: **cyclone → adiabatic absorber → isothermal absorber → 33 % acid storage**.
> - **Salt index** of K₂SO₄ ≈ 46 (vs. 116 for KCl) — reason for "mildness".
> - **Crops**: tobacco, potato, grape, citrus, tomato, onion, oilseeds, sugarcane, tea, coffee.
> - **Salt-out crystallization** exploited due to steep rise of solubility with temperature.
> - **India** has no indigenous soluble potash; K₂SO₄ is mostly manufactured locally by the **gypsum–ammonia route** and imported in bulk.
> - Key specifications (FCO): **≥ 50 % K₂O, ≤ 1.5 % moisture, ≤ 0.5 % free acidity**.
> - **Energy benchmark**: Mannheim ≈ 2.0 GJ t⁻¹ K₂SO₄; gypsum route ≈ 0.5 GJ t⁻¹.
> - Material balance identity: **mass-in = mass-out**; track Cl, K, S, H atoms separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miscellaneous Fertilizer and Bio Fertilizers: Introduction to Complex and Mixed Fertilizers, Manufacturing of NPK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Miscellaneous Fertilizer and Bio Fertilizers: Introduction to Complex and Mixed Fertilizers, Manufacturing of NPK # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Straight vs. Mixed vs. Complex Fertilizers — The Big Picture Before we dive in, let us clear the three confusing categories KTU loves to test on: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Straight Fertilizer** — Contains **only one primary nutrient** (N, P, or K). Example: **Urea (46% N)**, **SSP** (Single Super Phosphate, 16% P₂O₅), **MOP** (Muriate of Potash, 60% K₂O). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complex Fertilizer** — A multi-nutrient fertilizer produced **chemically in a single process**, where the three primary nutrients (N, P, K) are present in a **chemically combined form** (e.g., ammonium phosphate nitrate, diammonium phosphate). The manufacturer *cannot* independently alter nutrient ratios because the chemistry locks them together. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mixed Fertilizer** — A physical **blend of two or more straight (or complex) fertilizers**, mechanically mixed to give the desired N–P–K ratio. The grade can be **arbitrarily adjusted** at the blending plant. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake Analogy" 🎂 Imagine you are baking a cake: - A **Straight fertilizer** is like buying only **flour**, only **sugar**, or only **eggs** — a single ingredient. - A **Complex fertilizer** is like buying a **pre-mixed cake batter from a factory** where flour, sugar, eggs, and milk have already reacted together into a homogeneous dough during manufacture. You cannot separate them back. - A **Mixed fertilizer** is like **you, the baker, personally mixing** flour, sugar, eggs, and milk on your kitchen counter in the proportions your recipe demands. The baker (blending plant) has full freedom over the recipe (NPK ratio). The factory-batter (complex) comes in fixed grades decided by chemistry. --- ## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus-Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Complex Fertilizer (FCO / KTU Definition):** > A complex fertilizer is a **multi-nutrient fertilizer containing two or three primary plant nutrients (N, P₂O₅, K₂O)** which are obtained by **chemical reaction during manufacture**. The nutrients are present in a **combined/compound form** (e.g., $\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4$, $\text{(NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$, $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \cdot \text{CaCO}_3$). > [!NOTE] > **Mixed Fertilizer (FCO / KTU Definition):** > A mixed fertilizer is a **mechanical mixture of two or more straight or complex fertilizers** to supply **two or three primary nutrients**, the proportions of which can be **varied at will** at the mixing plant. No chemical reaction occurs between the components. --- ## 1.3 Key Differentiating Metrics | Parameter | Complex Fertilizer | Mixed Fertilizer | |---|---|---| | **Mode of formation** | Chemical reaction in situ | Physical/Mechanical blending | | **Nutrient flexibility** | Fixed by chemical stoichiometry | Arbitrarily adjustable | | **Granule uniformity** | Chemically homogeneous granules | Granules may differ in colour, size, density | | **Segregation risk** | Negligible | High (needs conditioning) | | **Typical process** | Nitrophosphate, Mixed Acid | Bulk blending, Steam granulation | | **Example** | DAP (18-46-0), 20-20-0 | 17-17-17, 19-19-19 from bulk blender | --- ## 1.4 Introduction to Bio-Fertilizers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bio-fertilizer (KTU Definition):** > A bio-fertilizer is a **biological preparation containing living or latent cells of efficient strains of nitrogen-fixing, phosphate-solubilizing, or cellulose-decomposing microorganisms** which, when applied to seed, soil, or composting pits, increase the availability of nutrients to plants through biological processes rather than chemical supply. | Type | Function | Example Organism | |---|---|---| | **N₂-fixer (symbiotic)** | Root nodules of legumes | *Rhizobium* spp. | | **N₂-fixer (asymbiotic)** | Free-living in soil | *Azotobacter*, *Azospirillum* | | **P-solubilizer** | Solubilizes insoluble phosphates | *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus megaterium* | | **Mycorrhiza (VAM)** | Enhances P & water uptake | *Glomus* spp. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of NPK Manufacturing Processes KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on **three industrially dominant routes** to manufacture NPK complex/mixed fertilizers: ### Route A — Nitrophosphate Route (Odda Process) This is the **classic European route** (originally Norsk Hydro Odda, Norway). The central chemistry involves **digestion of phosphate rock with nitric acid** followed by **partial removal of calcium nitrate** (which is a useless ballast nutrient for most soils). #### Step-by-Step Logic: 1. **Acidulation** — Phosphate rock ($\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot \text{CaF}_2$, fluorapatite) is dissolved in **65–70% nitric acid** in stainless-steel reactors at **55–75 °C**: $$ \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 20\,\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 10\,\text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 6\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{HF} $$ 2. **Cooling & Crystallization (Odda step)** — The acidulate is cooled to **−15 to −20 °C** so that **calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (CNTH)** crystallizes out: $$ \text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \downarrow $$ 3. **Filtration / Centrifugation** — Crystals separated; mother liquor contains **phosphoric acid + HNO₃ + residual Ca(NO₃)₂**. 4. **Ammoniation** — Mother liquor is ammoniated with **gaseous NH₃**: $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \quad (\text{MAP}) $$ $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 \quad (\text{DAP}) $$ $$ \text{HNO}_3 + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 $$ 5. **Potash Addition** — KCl (MOP) or K₂SO₄ (SOP) is added to fix the desired **K₂O content**. 6. **Granulation, Drying, Cooling, Coating** — Final product is 20-20-20, 15-15-15, etc. > [!NOTE] > **Why remove Ca(NO₃)₂?** > Calcium nitrate is highly hygroscopic, increases caking, and adds **no agronomic value** to most non-acid soils. Removing ~70–80% Ca improves the NPK grade and storage stability. --- ### Route B — Mixed-Acid (Urea-Based MAP-MKP) Route The **dominant Indian and global route** (e.g., IFFCO, Tata Chemicals). It uses a mixture of **sulfuric, phosphoric, and nitric acids** together with **ammonia** and **potash**. #### Key Reactions: - **Ammoniation of phosphoric acid** (DAP manufacture): $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 \quad \Delta H \approx -148 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ - **Prilling tower / Granulator addition of urea**: $$ \text{CO(NH}_2)_2 + \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 + \text{KCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{NPK melt/granule} $$ > [!TIP] > In this route, **urea is the nitrogen carrier** (highest N at 46%) and **monoammonium phosphate (MAP)** supplies P₂O₅; KCl supplies K₂O. The acid mix is **balanced** to give the target NPK grade. --- ### Route C — Bulk Blending Route The simplest route — just weigh and mix straight fertilizers in a **rotary drum, pugmill, or vertical blender**. **Pre-conditions for a good bulk blend:** - Granule size match (within ±10% mesh) - Density match (avoid segregation in transport) - Low-moisture components - Optional **coating oil** (0.2–0.5% by mass) to reduce dust and segregation > [!WARNING] > **Segregation is the #1 enemy of bulk blending.** If urea (low density, 0.75 g/cm³) is blended with DAP (high density, 1.6 g/cm³), vibration during transport separates them by density, producing a non-uniform field application. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **examination-tested** for KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize the bolded ones. | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use / Meaning** | |---|---|---| | **Nutrient-grade formula (Total)** | $\%\,\text{N} + \%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 + \%\,\text{K}_2\text{O} = \text{Total grade}$ | Identifies whether a fertilizer is "high" (>30) or "low" (<30) analysis | | **P₂O₅ conversion** | $\%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \%\,\text{P} \times \frac{M_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5}}{2 \cdot M_{\text{P}}} = \%\,\text{P} \times 2.2913$ | Convert elemental P to oxide form | | **K₂O conversion** | $\%\,\text{K}_2\text{O} = \%\,\text{K} \times \frac{M_{\text{K}_2\text{O}}}{2 \cdot M_{\text{K}}} = \%\,\text{K} \times 1.2046$ | Convert elemental K to oxide form | | **N equivalent of urea** | $1\text{ kg urea} = 0.46\text{ kg N}$ | Urea is 46% N | | **Sulphuric acid for SSP** | $1\text{ t SSP} \approx 0.35\text{ t H}_2\text{SO}_4$ | Acidulation ratio | | **NPK equivalent calculation** | $\text{Units of nutrient} = \text{Tons of fert.} \times \text{grade fraction}$ | Inventory & sales accounting | | **Bulk density criterion** | $\Delta \rho \le 0.2\text{ g/cm}^3$ for bulk blends | To prevent segregation | | **Granule size match** | $-6+10\,\text{BSS} \approx 2\text{ to }4\,\text{mm}$ | Compatible blending range | | **P₂O₅ in DAP** | $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5\,\% = \frac{M_{\text{P}_2\text{O}_5}}{M_{(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4}} \times 100 = 53.8$% (theoretical) | KTU favourite | | **N in DAP** | $\text{N}\,\% = \frac{2 \times 14}{132.06} \times 100 = 21.2$% (theoretical) | KTU favourite | | **Ammonia required for DAP** | $2\text{ mol NH}_3$ per mol $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ | Stoichiometry | | **CN yield in Odda** | $\frac{\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O mass}}{\text{acidulate mass}} \approx 0.55\text{–}0.65$ | Yield % per cycle | > **Convention rule (KTU):** All grades are reported as **percent N – percent P₂O₅ – percent K₂O**. Example: 17-17-17 means 17% N, 17% P₂O₅, 17% K₂O. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Agricultural Extension** | Farmers select grade based on soil-test-crop-recommendation (STCR) | | **Fertilizer Industry** | IFFCO Kandla, FACT Cochin, Tata Chemicals Babrala — bulk NPK plants | | **Inventory accounting** | Government of India tracks nutrient tonnes (N + P₂O₅ + K₂O) under Nutrient-Based Subsidy (NBS) | | **Export trade** | International trade grades expressed in N–P₂O₅–K₂O format | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Theoretical Composition of DAP (18-46-0) This is the **most asked derivation** in KTU exams. > **Given:** Reaction: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$ > **Molecular weights:** $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 = 98$, $\text{NH}_3 = 17$, $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 = 132.06$ ### Step 1 — Moles of DAP formed per mole of acid From stoichiometry: **1 mol H₃PO₄ produces 1 mol DAP**, consuming **2 mol NH₃**. ### Step 2 — Mass of DAP produced from 98 kg H₃PO₄ $$ \text{Mass of DAP} = 1 \times M_{(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4} = 132.06 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 3 — Nitrogen content in DAP Each mole of $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$ contains **2 nitrogen atoms**. $$ \%\,\text{N} = \frac{2 \times 14}{132.06} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\,\text{N} = \frac{28}{132.06} \times 100 = 21.20\,\% $$ ### Step 4 — P₂O₅ content in DAP Each mole of DAP contains **1 mol P** = **0.5 mol P₂O₅** (since 1 P₂O₅ = 2 P). $$ \%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \frac{0.5 \times 142}{132.06} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \frac{71}{132.06} \times 100 = 53.77\,\% $$ ### Step 5 — Comparison with commercial grade > [!NOTE] > Theoretical (21.2 - 53.8 - 0) is **never achieved** in practice because commercial DAP uses **technical-grade phosphoric acid** (~54% P₂O₅) containing impurities (Fe, Al, SO₄, F). Commercial DAP is reported as **18-46-0**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Formulating a 17-17-17 NPK from Straight Fertilizers (Bulk Blend) > **Given:** > Available straight fertilizers: **Urea (46% N)**, **DAP (18% N, 46% P₂O₅)**, **MOP (60% K₂O)**. > **Target:** 1000 kg of 17-17-17 NPK. ### Step 1 — Set up the matrix Let $U$, $D$, $M$ = kg of urea, DAP, MOP respectively. $$ U + D + M = 1000 \quad \text{(mass balance)} $$ $$ 0.46\,U + 0.18\,D = 170 \quad \text{(nitrogen balance)} $$ $$ 0.46\,D = 170 \quad \text{(phosphate balance)} $$ $$ 0.60\,M = 170 \quad \text{(potash balance)} $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for D from P-balance $$ D = \frac{170}{0.46} = 369.57 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for M from K-balance $$ M = \frac{170}{0.60} = 283.33 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for U from mass balance $$ U = 1000 - 369.57 - 283.33 = 347.10 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 5 — Verify nitrogen balance $$ \text{N from Urea} = 0.46 \times 347.10 = 159.67 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \text{N from DAP} = 0.18 \times 369.57 = 66.52 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \text{Total N} = 159.67 + 66.52 = 226.19 \text{ kg} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Mismatch detected!** Total N = 226.19 kg, but target N = 170 kg. The blend is **over-nitrogenated** because DAP *also* contributes N. ### Step 6 — Re-solve using simultaneous equations Re-arrange: let U = a, D = b. $$ 0.46\,a + 0.18\,b = 170 \quad \text{...(i)} $$ P-balance still gives b independently: $$ b = D = 369.57 \text{ kg (fixed by P)} $$ Substitute into (i): $$ 0.46\,a + 0.18 \times 369.57 = 170 $$ $$ 0.46\,a + 66.52 = 170 $$ $$ 0.46\,a = 103.48 $$ $$ a = 224.96 \text{ kg Urea} $$ Now total mass: $$ \text{Total} = U + D + M = 224.96 + 369.57 + 283.33 = 877.86 \text{ kg} $$ This is **less than 1000 kg**, so we need a **filler** (gypsum or sand or kaolin) of: $$ \text{Filler} = 1000 - 877.86 = 122.14 \text{ kg} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU answer-writing tip:** Always state the **filler requirement** explicitly and mention its purpose (to prevent caking and ensure free-flow). --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CN Removal in Odda Process (Mass Balance) > **Given:** 1000 kg of phosphate rock (75% fluorapatite, 25% gangue). Reacted with stoichiometric 65% HNO₃. CN crystallizer removes 70% of the Ca as CNTH. ### Step 1 — Mass of fluorapatite $$ \text{Apatite} = 1000 \times 0.75 = 750 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 2 — Moles of apatite $$ n_{\text{apatite}} = \frac{750}{M_{\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2}} = \frac{750}{1008} = 0.744 \text{ kmol} $$ ### Step 3 — HNO₃ required From stoichiometry: 20 mol HNO₃ per mol apatite. $$ n_{\text{HNO}_3} = 0.744 \times 20 = 14.88 \text{ kmol} $$ $$ \text{Mass HNO}_3 \text{ (100% basis)} = 14.88 \times 63 = 937.4 \text{ kg} $$ For 65% acid: $$ \text{Mass of 65\% acid} = \frac{937.4}{0.65} = 1442.2 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 4 — Ca(NO₃)₂ produced 10 mol Ca(NO₃)₂ per mol apatite. $$ n_{\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2} = 0.744 \times 10 = 7.44 \text{ kmol} $$ $$ \text{Mass Ca(NO}_3)_2 = 7.44 \times 164 = 1220.2 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 5 — CNTH removed (70%) $$ \text{CNTH mass} = 0.70 \times 7.44 \times M_{\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.70 \times 7.44 \times 236.15 = 1230.0 \text{ kg} $$ > [!NOTE] > CNTH (tetrahydrate) MW = 164 + 4 × 18 = 236 g/mol. ### Step 6 — Ca(NO₃)₂ remaining in mother liquor $$ \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 \text{ remaining} = 0.30 \times 1220.2 = 366.1 \text{ kg} $$ This residual Ca(NO₃)₂ will be **ammoniated later** along with phosphoric acid. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Nitrophosphate (Odda) Route for NPK ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Phosphate Rock
Crushed 100 Mesh"] --> B["Nitric Acid Reactor
65 percent HNO3 55 to 75 C"] B --> C["Acidulate
H3PO4 plus Ca NO3 2 plus HF"] C --> D["Crystallizer
Cooling to minus 15 C"] D --> E["Filter Centrifuge
CNTH Crystals"] E --> F["Mother Liquor
H3PO4 plus HNO3 plus Ca NO3 2"] E --> G["CNTH Product
Sold or Converted to CAN"] F --> H["Ammoniator
NH3 Gas Addition"] H --> I["Ammonium Phosphates
MAP plus DAP plus AN"] I --> J["Potash Addition
KCl or K2SO4"] J --> K["Granulator
Pugmill or Drum"] K --> L["Dryer Cooler"] L --> M["Screening"] M --> N["Coating and Bagging
Final NPK 15 15 15"] G --> O["Calcium Ammonium Nitrate
CAN 25 percent N"] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a unit operation in the Odda process. The two product streams (NPK and CAN) are deliberately separated to maximize agronomic value of every calcium atom. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mixed-Acid NPK Route ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["H3PO4 54 percent"] --> AM["Ammoniator"] P2["H2SO4 98 percent"] --> AM P3["HNO3 65 percent"] --> AM AM --> G["Granulator
Drum Type"] U["Urea Melt 140 C"] --> G K["MOP or SOP
KCl Powder"] --> G G --> D["Dryer"] D --> C["Cooler"] C --> S["Screens"] S --> W["Crushed Oversize
Recycled"] S --> PR["Final Product NPK"] REC["Recycled Fines"] --> G W --> REC ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative Block Matrix — Three Manufacturing Routes ```mermaid flowchart TD R["NPK Manufacturing Routes"] --> R1["Nitrophosphate Odda"] R --> R2["Mixed Acid Route"] R --> R3["Bulk Blending"] R1 --> R1A["Acidulate with HNO3 only"] R1A --> R1B["CN Crystallization"] R1B --> R1C["Ammoniation of Mother Liquor"] R1C --> R1D["Co-product CAN"] R2 --> R2A["Acid Mix H3PO4 H2SO4 HNO3"] R2A --> R2B["Pugmill or Slurry Granulator"] R2B --> R2C["Urea and Potash Addition"] R3 --> R3A["Weigh and Blend Straight Fert"] R3A --> R3B["Rotary Drum or Vertical Blender"] R3B --> R3C["Optional Oil Coating"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Bio-Fertilizer Production — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Mother Culture
Pure Strain"] --> A["Inoculum Build-up
Lab Scale"] A --> B["Seed Fermenter
Working Volume Bank"] B --> C["Production Fermenter
Aerated Stirred Tank"] C --> D["Carrier Mixing
Peat or Lignite"] D --> E["Curing 7 to 14 Days"] E --> F["Quality Check
CFU per g"] F --> G["Packaging Sterile Pouch"] G --> H["Field Application
Seed or Soil"] ``` > **KTU Tip:** Memorize the sequence **Mother → Inoculum → Seed → Production → Carrier → Cure → Pack**. Examiners love 2-3 mark questions asking for "steps in bio-fertilizer manufacture." # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between **complex** and **mixed** fertilizers with two examples each. **Model Answer:** | Basis | Complex Fertilizer | Mixed Fertilizer | |---|---|---| | **Formation** | Produced by chemical reaction between constituents | Produced by physical blending | | **Nutrient flexibility** | Fixed by stoichiometry | Adjustable at will | | **Examples** | DAP (18-46-0), Nitrophosphate 20-20-0 | 17-17-17 bulk blend, 19-19-19 | | **Cost** | Higher (capital-intensive) | Lower (simple blender) | --- ### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **bio-fertilizer**. Name two nitrogen-fixing bio-fertilizer organisms. **Model Answer:** > **Bio-fertilizer** is a biological preparation containing **living/latent cells of efficient strains of microorganisms** (e.g., bacteria, fungi, algae) which when applied to seed, soil, or composting pits, augment the availability of nutrients (especially N and P) to crops through biological activity. **Two N₂-fixing organisms:** 1. ***Rhizobium* spp.** — symbiotic, forms root nodules on legumes (e.g., *R. meliloti* on alfalfa). 2. ***Azotobacter chroococcum*** — asymbiotic (free-living) N₂ fixer in non-legume soils. --- --- ## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** #### (a) Explain the manufacture of **NPK fertilizer by the Nitrophosphate (Odda) route** with a neat flow diagram. (7 marks) #### (b) Calculate the theoretical composition (% N and % P₂O₅) of **diammonium phosphate (DAP)**. State two reasons why commercial DAP grade is 18-46-0 instead of 21-53. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution (a): The Odda route is a **chemical complex-fertilizer process** that uses **nitric acid to acidulate phosphate rock**, followed by **removal of calcium nitrate as crystals** and **ammoniation of the mother liquor**. **Steps:** 1. **Acidulation** — Ground phosphate rock (–100 mesh) reacts with 55–70% HNO₃ at 55–75 °C in a stainless-steel reactor: $$ \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 20\,\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 10\,\text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2 + 6\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{HF} $$ 2. **Cooling & Crystallization** — Acidulate cooled to –15 °C; **Ca(NO₃)₂·4H₂O (CNTH)** crystallizes. 3. **Filtration** — Crystals separated; mother liquor contains H₃PO₄ + HNO₃ + residual Ca(NO₃)₂. 4. **Ammoniation** — Gaseous NH₃ bubbled in stages (to control pH): - pH 4.5 → MAP ($\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4$) - pH 8.0 → DAP ($(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$) - Excess HNO₃ → $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ 5. **Potash addition** — KCl added to fix K₂O content (15–20%). 6. **Granulation, drying, cooling, screening, coating**. 7. **Co-product:** CNTH crystals are sold as **Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN, 25% N)** after reaction with ammonia and CO₂. **Final grade:** 15-15-15, 20-20-0, 12-32-16 etc. **Flow Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram). --- #### Model Solution (b): **Reaction:** $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 $$ **Molecular weights:** - $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ = 98 g/mol - $\text{NH}_3$ = 17 g/mol - $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$ = 132.06 g/mol **Step 1 — Nitrogen content:** 1 mole DAP contains 2 N atoms. $$ \%\,\text{N}_{\text{DAP}} = \frac{2 \times 14}{132.06} \times 100 = \frac{28}{132.06} \times 100 = 21.20\,\% $$ **Step 2 — P₂O₅ content:** 1 mole DAP contains 1 mol P = 0.5 mol P₂O₅. $$ \%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \frac{0.5 \times 142}{132.06} \times 100 = \frac{71}{132.06} \times 100 = 53.77\,\% $$ **Theoretical grade:** **21.2 – 53.8 – 0**. **Two reasons why commercial grade is 18-46-0:** 1. **Wet-process phosphoric acid** (used industrially) contains impurities — Fe₂O₃, Al₂O₃, SO₄²⁻, F⁻, SiO₂ — that bind part of the P₂O₅ as insoluble Fe/Al phosphates, **reducing available P₂O₅ to ~46%**. 2. **Process losses** during ammoniation, granulation, drying, and recycling of fines reduce the actual N content from 21.2% to ~18%, and P₂O₅ from 53.8% to ~46%. Over-ammoniation beyond DAP stoichiometry leads to ammonia loss and a controlled ratio. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** #### (a) Discuss **bulk blending** as a method of NPK manufacture. What are the **necessary conditions** for a good bulk blend? (7 marks) #### (b) A farmer needs **500 kg of 12-32-16 NPK**. Calculate the **kilograms of Urea (46% N), DAP (18-46-0), and MOP (60% K₂O)** required. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution (a): **Bulk blending** is the **simplest, lowest-capital method** of producing multi-nutrient fertilizers by **dry-mixing straight (or complex) fertilizers** in the desired proportions. **Process sequence:** 1. **Storage** — Urea, DAP, MOP stored separately in bins near blender. 2. **Weighing** — Each material weighed as per formulation (using load cells). 3. **Blending** — Mixed in **rotary drum, ribbon blender, or vertical auger mixer** for 1–3 minutes. 4. **Optional conditioning** — Anti-caking agent (0.2–0.5% oil) sprayed. 5. **Bagging or bulk loading**. **Necessary conditions for a good bulk blend (5 rules):** | # | Condition | Rationale | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Particle size match** (±10% mesh) | Prevents size segregation | | 2 | **Density match** ($\Delta\rho \le 0.2$ g/cm³) | Prevents density segregation | | 3 | **Low moisture content** (<1.5%) | Prevents caking & urea hydrolysis | | 4 | **Chemical compatibility** (no urea + SSP = reaction) | Avoids ammonia loss | | 5 | **Conditioner coating** | Reduces dust & segregation | --- #### Model Solution (b): Let $U$, $D$, $M$ = kg of urea, DAP, MOP respectively to make **500 kg** of 12-32-16. **Nutrient targets (per 500 kg):** - N required = 12% × 500 = 60 kg - P₂O₅ required = 32% × 500 = 160 kg - K₂O required = 16% × 500 = 80 kg **Equations:** $$ 0.46\,U + 0.18\,D = 60 \quad \text{...(i) N-balance} $$ $$ 0.46\,D = 160 \quad \text{...(ii) P-balance} $$ $$ 0.60\,M = 80 \quad \text{...(iii) K-balance} $$ **From (ii):** $$ D = \frac{160}{0.46} = 347.83 \text{ kg DAP} $$ **From (iii):** $$ M = \frac{80}{0.60} = 133.33 \text{ kg MOP} $$ **From (i):** $$ 0.46\,U = 60 - 0.18 \times 347.83 = 60 - 62.61 = -2.61 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative N deficit detected!** This means **DAP alone is over-supplying N** (62.61 kg > 60 kg required). We must **not add urea**. We must instead use a **filler to dilute**. **Revised strategy:** - DAP supplies both N (62.61 kg) and P₂O₅ (160 kg) ✅ - MOP supplies K₂O (80 kg) ✅ - Total N supplied = 62.61 kg (which exceeds 60 kg by 2.61 kg) - Total solid mass = 347.83 + 133.33 = 481.16 kg - Filler required = 500 − 481.16 = **18.84 kg** (gypsum or kaolin) **Final formulation:** | Material | Mass (kg) | % by mass | |---|---|---| | DAP (18-46-0) | 347.83 | 69.57 | | MOP (0-0-60) | 133.33 | 26.67 | | Gypsum filler | 18.84 | 3.77 | | **Total** | **500.00** | **100.00** | **Verification:** - N% = (0.18 × 347.83) / 500 × 100 = 62.61 / 500 × 100 = **12.52%** (≈ 12%) ✅ - P₂O₅% = (0.46 × 347.83) / 500 × 100 = 160 / 500 × 100 = **32.00%** ✅ - K₂O% = (0.60 × 133.33) / 500 × 100 = 80 / 500 × 100 = **16.00%** ✅ --- --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in NPK calculations:** > > 1. **Forgetting that DAP contributes BOTH N and P₂O₅** — they solve for urea as if N comes only from urea. Always check the N-balance *after* fixing DAP from P-balance. > > 2. **Not reporting the filler mass** — a correct formulation must sum to the requested total mass. Mismatch between calculated component mass and target mass is a 2-mark penalty. > > 3. **Confusing "P" with "P₂O₅"** — agricultural grades **always use P₂O₅**. If a problem states "12% P", you must convert: $\%\,\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = 12 \times 2.2913 = 27.5$%. > > 4. **Writing "mixed acid route" without naming the acids** — KTU expects **H₃PO₄ + H₂SO₄ + HNO₃** (or specifically H₃PO₄ + HNO₃) — vague answers lose a mark. > > 5. **Skipping the block-flow diagram in 7-mark questions** — examiners are mandated to deduct 1 mark if no diagram is drawn. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Definition Cluster - **Complex fertilizer** → chemical reaction in manufacture → grades fixed. - **Mixed fertilizer** → physical blending → grades flexible. - **Straight fertilizer** → only one primary nutrient. ### Manufacturing Routes (Three Pillars) 1. **Nitrophosphate (Odda)** — HNO₃ acidulation → CN crystallization → ammoniation → co-product CAN. 2. **Mixed-Acid** — H₃PO₄/H₂SO₄/HNO₃ → pugmill/granulator with urea & KCl. 3. **Bulk Blending** — dry-mixing of straight fertilizers; needs particle size, density, moisture match. ### Critical Formulas - $\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5 = \%\text{P} \times 2.2913$ - $\%\text{K}_2\text{O} = \%\text{K} \times 1.2046$ - DAP theoretical = **21.2% N, 53.8% P₂O₅**; commercial = **18-46-0**. - MAP theoretical = **12.2% N, 61.7% P₂O₅**; commercial = **11-52-0**. ### Bio-Fertilizer Quick Notes - **N₂-fixers** — *Rhizobium* (symbiotic), *Azotobacter* & *Azospirillum* (asymbiotic), *Blue-Green Algae* (paddy). - **P-solubilizers** — *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*, *Aspergillus*. - **Mycorrhiza (VAM)** — *Glomus*; enhances P & water uptake. - **Production chain** — Mother → Inoculum → Seed → Production → Carrier (peat) → Cure → Pack. ### Units & Conventions - **Grade format** = N – P₂O₅ – K₂O (always oxides for P & K). - **Filler** = gypsum or kaolin (when straight fertilizers sum less than target mass). - **Coating oil** = 0.2–0.5% by mass in bulk blends. ### KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis - Heavy weightage on **flow diagrams** — practice drawing Odda and Mixed-Acid routes from memory. - **Numerical problems on blending calculations** appear every semester — memorize the 3-equation 3-unknown approach. - **Definitions** of complex vs. mixed fertilizers are mandatory for Part A.

topics.title: Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate (ASP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate (ASP) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate (ASP)** is a **compound / complex nitrogenous fertilizer** manufactured by the chemical reaction of **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** with **ammonium sulphate ((NH₄)₂SO₄)**, often with subsequent ammoniation. It is a **granular, free-flowing, two-nutrient (N + P) plus secondary nutrient (S)** carrier, classified under the **Group A – Ammoniated Phosphate fertilizers** category of KTU Module 4 (Phosphate & Complex Fertilizers). **Typical Guaranteed Specifications (FCO India / KTU Reference Standard):** | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | Total Nitrogen (as N) | **16.0 % min** | | Available Phosphate (as P₂O₅) | **20.0 % min** | | Water-soluble P₂O₅ | **18.0 % min** (≥ 90 % of available) | | Sulphur (as S) | **12–15 %** | | Moisture | **1.5 % max** | | Free acidity (as H₃PO₄) | **2.0 % max** | | Granule size | **1–4 mm (≥ 90 %)** | The grade notation in India is widely written as **16-20-0 (S 13)**, meaning **16 % N, 20 % P₂O₅, 0 % K₂O, and 13 % S**. > [!NOTE] > **Why ASP is a "Complex" Fertilizer?** > A *complex fertilizer* is one in which **two or more of the primary nutrients (N, P, K) are chemically combined in the same granule**, not merely physically mixed. In ASP, the ammonium and phosphate ions are present in the same crystal matrix — guaranteeing **uniform nutrient distribution** and preventing the **segregation problem** seen in bulk-blended (BB) fertilizers during transport. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Bar" View Imagine you are preparing a **packed lunch (a complete meal)** instead of carrying three separate tiffins of rice, dal, and sabzi. The three tiffins (a urea bag + a DAP bag + an ammonium sulphate bag) represent **straight fertilizers**. The packed lunch is **ASP** — every bite (every granule) carries all the nutrients your crop needs. Just as a chef (the chemical engineer) **cooks** these ingredients together at controlled temperature and pH to form a uniform product, the fertilizer manufacturer reacts phosphoric acid with ammonium sulphate in a slurry phase and then granulates it. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** **A**mmonium **S**ulphate **P**hosphate = **A.S.P.** — think of it as the *"All-in-one Sulphur + Phosphorus"* carrier. ## 1.3 Role of Each Nutrient in ASP - **Nitrogen (16 % N)** → as **NH₄⁺ ion** (ammonium form). Ammoniacal N is **non-leaching** and **non-volatile** under normal soil conditions, making it superior to urea-N in paddy soils. - **Phosphorus (20 % P₂O₅)** → as **mono-ammonium phosphate (MAP, NH₄H₂PO₄)** form, which is the **most plant-available P carrier** with high water solubility. - **Sulphur (13 % S)** → as **SO₄²⁻** ion. Sulphur is the **4th major plant nutrient** and is critical for oilseed crops (mustard, groundnut) and pulses. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of ASP Formation — Step-by-Step Logic ASP is **not** formed by simply mixing (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals with MAP. It is the product of a controlled **slurry-phase reaction**, followed by **granulation**. ### Step 1: Primary Acid-Base Reaction Phosphoric acid is neutralized by ammonium sulphate in the presence of additional ammonia (or sometimes directly by ammonia gas bubbled into the slurry): $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_4\text{HSO}_4 $$ The reaction is **exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –115 kJ/mol of H₃PO₄), so the reactor must have **cooling jackets** to maintain slurry temperature at **110–125 °C**. ### Step 2: Ammoniation (to Convert Acidic Salts to Neutral MAP) Ammonia is sparged to neutralize the residual acidity and the bisulphate: $$ \text{NH}_4\text{HSO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 $$ $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4} \quad (\text{MAP}) $$ The **NH₃ : H₃PO₄ molar ratio** (called the **"ammonia mole ratio"**) is the **single most critical process variable**. It is typically maintained at **1.0–1.2** for ASP grade. If the ratio exceeds 1.5, free NH₃ losses increase and the product becomes sticky. ### Step 3: Slurry Drying and Granulation The hot slurry (≈ 18–22 % moisture) is fed to a **rotary drum granulator** along with **recycled fines** (undersize product). Steam and water sprays control granule growth. The wet granules then pass through a **rotary dryer** (inlet gas ≈ 200 °C, outlet ≈ 90 °C) to bring moisture below **1.5 %**. ### Step 4: Screening, Crushing, and Coating Oversize → crusher → recycle. Undersize → recycle. On-size (1–4 mm) → **cooler → anti-caking coating (oil + clay) → bagging**. ## 2.2 Two Industrial Manufacturing Routes ### Route A: Merchant Acid Route (Most Common in India) **Phosphoric acid (from a dedicated plant)** is reacted with **imported or captive ammonium sulphate** and **anhydrous ammonia** in a **stainless-steel lined Trough reactor (PAMCO / TVA-type pre-neutralizer)**. Feedstocks: H₃PO₄ (52–54 % P₂O₅), (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals, NH₃ gas. ### Route B: Mixed Acid (ODDA / Nitrophosphate) Route **Phosphate rock is attacked simultaneously by nitric acid and sulphuric acid** in a single reactor. The slurry is then ammoniated. This route is adopted by plants that already have a **nitrophosphate** complex (e.g., GNFC, Gujarat State Fertilizers). Key reactions: $$ \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 10\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 10\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{HF}\uparrow $$ $$ \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 7\text{CaSO}_4 + 3\text{Ca(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\text{HF}\uparrow $$ The Ca(NO₃)₂ formed is **partially removed by cooling crystallization** (the Odda process) or **fully converted** to CaSO₄ by adding (NH₄)₂SO₄, simultaneously producing NH₄NO₃ that joins the ASP slurry. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Ammonia Mole Ratio (AMR)** | $\text{AMR} = \dfrac{n_{\text{NH}_3}}{n_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4}}$ | dimensionless | Controls MAP vs DAP formation; ASP requires AMR ≈ 1.0–1.2 | | 2 | **N:P₂O₅ Nutrient Ratio** | $\text{N:P} = \dfrac{\%\text{N}}{\%\text{P}_2\text{O}_5}$ | dimensionless | For ASP 16-20-0 → ratio = **0.80** | | 3 | **Theoretical N content of (NH₄)₂SO₄** | $\dfrac{2 \times 14}{132} = 21.21$ % | % by mass | Used to back-calculate ammonium sulphate requirement | | 4 | **Theoretical P₂O₅ in MAP (NH₄H₂PO₄)** | $\dfrac{142}{115} = 61.7$ % P₂O₅ equivalent | % by mass | Used in mass-balance for granulation | | 5 | **Slurry molality at granulation** | Typically **3.5–4.5 mol/kg water** | mol/kg | Determines granule hardness | | 6 | **Heat of neutralization** | ΔHₙ ≈ **–115 kJ/mol H₃PO₄** | kJ/mol | Sizing reactor cooling jacket | | 7 | **Acid requirement per ton of P₂O₅** | For 54 % P₂O₅ acid → **1.85 t acid / t P₂O₅** | t/t | Plant capacity calculations | | 8 | **Sulphur Nutrient Value** | S% ≈ **0.43 × (NH₄)₂SO₄ %** in product | % | Quick QC check | | 9 | **Critical Relative Humidity (CRH)** | ASP CRH ≈ **70–75 %** at 30 °C | % RH | Storage shed design limit | | 10 | **Bulk Density** | **0.85–0.95 g/cm³** | g/cm³ | Bag (50 kg) sizing, silo design | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU TIP:** Whenever a calculation asks *"find the ammonia required to produce 1000 kg of 16-20-0 ASP"*, use the **N-balance**: > Required NH₃ (as 100 % N) = 160 kg → 160/0.82 ≈ **195 kg liquid NH₃ per tonne of product**. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - Used as a **starter / basal dose** for **oilseeds** (mustard, sunflower, soybean), **pulses** (chana, tur), and **paddy** where sulphur deficiency is endemic in **Inceptisols and Alfisols** of India. - **Co-granulation** of N + P + S eliminates **3 bag-handling operations** per hectare for the farmer. - Sulphate-based nitrogen is **far safer for paddy** than urea-N, since it does not undergo nitrification-denitrification losses in flooded fields. # 3.
