Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Mass-Balance Derivation — "How much H₃PO₄ and NH₃ to make 1 tonne of 16-20-0 ASP?" ### Step 1: Define the target nutrient content For 1000 kg of ASP grade **16-20-0 (S 13)**: - Required **N** = 1000 × 0.16 = **160 kg** - Required **P₂O₅** = 1000 × 0.20 = **200 kg** - Required **S** = 1000 × 0.13 = **130 kg** ### Step 2: Back-calculate the MAP requirement from P₂O₅ MAP formula: NH₄H₂PO₄, molar mass = 115 g/mol P₂O₅ equivalent in MAP = 142 / 115 = **1.2348 g P₂O₅ per g MAP** $$ \text{MAP required} = \dfrac{200}{1.2348} = 161.96 \;\text{kg} $$ ### Step 3: Nitrogen contributed by MAP N fraction in MAP = 14 / 115 = **0.1217** N from MAP = 161.96 × 0.1217 = **19.71 kg** ### Step 4: Balance the remaining N from (NH₄)₂SO₄ N still required from (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 160 − 19.71 = **140.29 kg** N fraction in (NH₄)₂SO₄ (M = 132) = 28 / 132 = **0.2121** $$ (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ required} = \dfrac{140.29}{0.2121} = 661.48 \;\text{kg} $$ ### Step 5: Verify S from ammonium sulphate S fraction in (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 32 / 132 = **0.2424** $$ \text{S supplied} = 661.48 \times 0.2424 = 160.34 \;\text{kg} $$ Theoretical S required = 130 kg. The **excess S (≈ 30 kg)** is acceptable — it over-specifies the sulphur content, providing a **nutritional safety margin**. ### Step 6: Verify phosphoric acid requirement MAP requires **1 mol H₃PO₄ per mol MAP**. Moles of MAP = 161.96 / 115 = **1.408 kmol** Mass of pure H₃PO₄ = 1.408 × 98 = **137.99 kg** Assuming commercial acid is **52 % P₂O₅** (≈ 75 % H₃PO₄ by mass): $$ \text{Commercial acid needed} = \dfrac{137.99}{0.75} = 183.99 \;\text{kg} $$ ### Step 7: NH₃ balance NH₃ needed for: - MAP formation: 1 mol NH₃ per mol MAP = 1.408 × 17 = **23.94 kg** - Free neutralization (5 % allowance for losses): +5 % = **1.20 kg** $$ \text{Total NH}_3 = 23.94 + 1.20 = 25.14 \;\text{kg per tonne of ASP} $$ ### Step 3.2 Consolidated Mass Balance Table (1 tonne of 16-20-0 ASP) | Input Stream | Mass (kg) | Output Stream | Mass (kg) | |---|---|---|---| | Commercial H₃PO₄ (75 % w/w) | 184.0 | Product ASP (16-20-0-13S) | 1000.0 | | Ammonium sulphate (solid) | 661.5 | Water evaporated (granulation) | 95.0 | | Anhydrous NH₃ | 25.1 | Stack losses (NH₃, dust) | 8.5 | | Recycled fines (re-introduced) | 320.0 | Oversize crushed back | 90.0 | | Anti-caking oil + clay | 3.0 | Net Product Output | **1000.0** | | **Total Inputs** | **1193.6** | **Total Outputs** | **1193.5** | > [!NOTE] > The **2 % mass loss** (≈ 23 kg) accounts for **stack ammonia, HF volatilization (small), and screening rejects** that cannot be recovered. This is well within the **FCO tolerance of ±0.5 %** on N and ±1.0 % on P₂O₅. ### Step 3.3 Derivation of Granule Size Distribution (Rosin–Rammler Equation) The cumulative oversize mass fraction *R(x)* for ASP granules follows: $$ R(x) = 100 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{x}{x'}\right)^{n}\right] $$ Where: - *x* = granule diameter (mm) - *x'* = **size constant** (≈ 2.6 mm for ASP) - *n* = **uniformity index** (≈ 1.8 for well-granulated ASP) The **mass median diameter** is obtained by setting *R(x) = 50 %*: $$ x_{50} = x' \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/n} $$ For ASP with *x'* = 2.6 and *n* = 1.8: $$ x_{50} = 2.6 \times (0.693)^{0.556} = 2.6 \times 0.806 = 2.10 \;\text{mm} $$ This is well within the **1–4 mm FCO specification window**, confirming that the granulation drum is operating in the **optimum "seed-and-growth" regime**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — ASP Manufacturing (Merchant Acid Route) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phosphoric Acid Storage
52 percent P2O5] --> B[Pre-Neutralizer Trough
SS316 Lined] C[Ammonia Vaporizer
Anhydrous NH3] --> B D[Ammonium Sulphate Hopper
Solid Crystals] --> B B --> E[Slurry Tank
115 to 125 deg C] E --> F[Pump to Granulator] G[Scrubber
Venturi Type] <-- NH3 Recovery F --> H[Rotary Drum Granulator
Spray Nozzles] I[Recycled Fines Silo
40 percent of feed] --> H H --> J[Rotary Dryer
Inlet 200 deg C] J --> K[Vibrating Screen
1 to 4 mm] K -->|Oversize| L[Chain Mill Crusher] K -->|Undersize| I L --> I K -->|On-size| M[Rotary Cooler
Air Flow 90 deg C to 35 deg C] M --> N[Coating Drum
Oil plus Clay 0.3 percent] N --> O[Bagging Hopper
50 kg Bags] O --> P[Warehouse Storage
CRH less than 75 percent] G --> B J --> Q[Cyclone Dust Collector] Q --> I ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mixed Acid Route ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RX1[Acidulation Reactor] A1[Phosphate Rock] --> R1 A2[Nitric Acid 58 percent] --> R1 A3[Sulphuric Acid 98 percent] --> R1 end R1[R1 Mixed Acid Attack
70 to 75 deg C] --> R2[Crystallizer
Ca NO3 2 4H2O] R2 --> R3[Filter
Gypsum plus Liquor] R3 --> R4[Ammoniators
Series of 3 Vessels] R4 --> R5[Granulation Drum] A4[Ammonia Gas] --> R4 R5 --> R6[Drying and Screening] R6 --> P1[ASP 16 20 0 Product] R6 --> REC[Recycle Loop Fines] REC --> R5 ``` ## 4.3 Material Flow Block — Pre-Neutralizer Mass Streams ```mermaid flowchart TD H3PO4[Input H3PO4 75 percent] --> PN[Pre Neutralizer Vessel
Slurry pH 4.5 to 5.0] AMS[Input NH4 2 SO4 Crystals] --> PN NH3[Input NH3 Gas 99.5 percent] --> PN PN --> SLURRY[Slurry 18 percent Moisture
115 deg C] SLURRY --> GR[Granulator] REC[Recycled Fines 40 percent] --> GR GR --> PROD[ASP Granules 1 to 4 mm] PROD --> COOL[Cooler] COOL --> PACK[Bagging 50 kg] VENT[Vapour Vent NH3 plus H2O] --> SCR[Scrubber H2SO4 Wash] SCR --> AMREC[Recovered NH4 2 SO4 Solution] AMREC --> AMS ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid Compilation Note for Students:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (`A1`, `R1`, `H3PO4`, etc.) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords, ensuring clean rendering in any markdown viewer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate (ASP). State its typical nutrient composition and any two agricultural advantages over urea + DAP mixture.** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** ASP is a **compound N–P fertilizer** manufactured by reacting **phosphoric acid with ammonium sulphate followed by ammoniation and granulation**. It typically contains **16 % N, 20 % P₂O₅ and 13 % S** (graded 16-20-0-13S). **Two agricultural advantages:** 1. Supplies **secondary nutrient sulphur** in the same granule, essential for oilseed crops, eliminating a separate sulphur application. 2. **Ammoniacal nitrogen** is non-leaching in paddy soils and shows lower volatilization loss compared to urea-N. ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is meant by "Ammonia Mole Ratio" in ASP manufacture? Why is it controlled at 1.0–1.2?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Ammonia mole ratio is defined as: $$ \text{AMR} = \dfrac{\text{moles of NH}_3 \text{ reacted}}{\text{moles of H}_3\text{PO}_4 \text{ reacted}} $$ **Why 1.0–1.2?** At this ratio, mono-ammonium phosphate (MAP, NH₄H₂PO₄) — the most water-soluble P form — is the dominant product. Below 1.0, the slurry is too acidic, causing corrosion and product caking. Above 1.5, free NH₃ escapes to the vent, causing **air pollution and product loss**, and the granules become hygroscopic. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Compulsory **With a neat process flow diagram, explain the manufacture of Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate (16-20-0) by the merchant phosphoric acid route. Discuss the role of the pre-neutralizer, ammoniation, and the granulation drum. State the major process variables and quality specifications.** #### (a) Process Flow & Equipment Description — 7 Marks 1. **Phosphoric Acid (52 % P₂O₅)** from bulk storage is pumped to a **stainless-steel lined pre-neutralizer trough (TVA-type)**, where it is partially neutralized with ammonia gas (sparged through perforated lances) and mixed with solid ammonium sulphate crystals. 2. The exothermic neutralization maintains the slurry at **110–125 °C**, eliminating the need for external heating. 3. The **hot slurry (≈ 20 % moisture)** overflows to a **steam-jacketed slurry pump** which delivers it to the **rotary drum granulator**. 4. **Recycled fines (40 % of feed)** are fed concurrently to provide "seed" particles. The slurry coats the seeds and grows them by **layering** (not agglomeration), producing dense, round granules. 5. Wet granules discharge to a **co-current rotary dryer** (inlet 200 °C, outlet 90 °C) where moisture drops to < 1.5 %. 6. Dried granules are **screened** into three streams: oversize → crusher → recycle; undersize → recycle; on-size (1–4 mm) → **cooler** (counter-current air, outlet 35 °C) → **coating drum** (0.3 % oil + 0.5 % clay) → **50 kg bagging**. 7. **Venturi scrubber** with dilute H₂SO₄ absorbs fugitive NH₃, recovering it as (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution that is recycled to the pre-neutralizer. #### (b) Process Variables, Reactions & Quality Specs — 7 Marks **Major process variables:** | Variable | Set Point | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---| | NH₃ mole ratio (AMR) | 1.05–1.15 | > 1.5 → NH₃ loss; < 0.9 → acidic caking | | Pre-neutralizer T | 115 ± 5 °C | High T → excessive water evaporation | | Slurry moisture | 18–22 % | > 25 % → granule collapse | | Granulator rpm / slope | 6–10 rpm, 1–2° slope | Affects residence time (3–6 min) | | Recycle ratio | 1.5–2.5 : 1 | Low → poor granulation; high → over-grinding | | Dryer outlet T | 85–95 °C | > 110 °C → urea/amide decomposition | **Key reactions:** $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \quad (\text{MAP}) $$ $$ \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_4\text{HSO}_4 $$ $$ \text{NH}_4\text{HSO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 $$ **Quality specification of final ASP (16-20-0):** N ≥ 16 %, P₂O₅ ≥ 20 %, water-soluble P₂O₅ ≥ 18 %, S ≥ 13 %, moisture ≤ 1.5 %, free H₃PO₄ ≤ 2 %, granule size 1–4 mm ≥ 90 %. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice **Describe the manufacture of ASP by the mixed acid (nitrophosphate-Odda) route. Explain how Ca(NO₃)₂ is removed and how the slurry is ammoniated to give the final 16-20-0 grade. Compare this route with the merchant acid route.** #### (a) Mixed Acid Attack and Ca(NO₃)₂ Removal — 7 Marks 1. **Phosphate rock** (e.g., Jordan or Moroccan, ≈ 32 % P₂O₅) is ground to **< 0.15 mm** and fed to a **rubber-lined wooden reactor** along with a **mixed acid** stream consisting of **58 % HNO₃ and 98 % H₂SO₄** in a molar ratio of **≈ 2 : 1 (HNO₃ : H₂SO₄)**. 2. The acidulation temperature is held at **70–75 °C** with a residence time of **45–60 minutes**. 3. The mixed acid attack yields **phosphoric acid, calcium sulphate (gypsum) and calcium nitrate** in solution. 4. The slurry is cooled to **–10 °C** in a **scraped-surface crystallizer**, causing **Ca(NO₃)₂·4H₂O** to crystallize out. 5. The cold slurry is filtered on a **rotary vacuum filter**. The filter cake (Ca(NO₃)₂·4H₂O) is sent for **conversion** by reacting with (NH₄)₂SO₄: $$ \text{Ca(NO}_3)_2 + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 + \text{CaSO}_4\downarrow $$ The NH₄NO₃ solution joins the main slurry stream; the CaSO₄ is discarded or used in cement. #### (b) Ammoniation, Comparison with Merchant Acid Route — 7 Marks 6. The **mother liquor** from filtration contains H₃PO₄, residual HNO₃, NH₄NO₃ and some (NH₄)₂SO₄. It is sent to a **series of three stirred-tank ammoniators (M-1, M-2, M-3)**, each progressively adding NH₃ gas. 7. The final ammoniation in **M-3** is controlled to give an **N : P₂O₅ ratio of 0.8** in the slurry (corresponding to 16-20-0 grade). 8. The ammoniated slurry is fed to a **granulator**, dried, screened and coated — identical to the merchant acid route. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Merchant Acid Route | Mixed Acid Route | |---|---|---| | Feedstock acid | Purchased H₃PO₄ | Self-generated H₃PO₄ + HNO₃ | | Capital cost | **High** (acid plant) | **Lower** (integrated) | | Gypsum | **None** (uses merchant acid) | **Large tonnage** (by-product) | | N : P₂O₅ flexibility | High | Restricted to high-N grades | | Energy efficiency | Better for small plants | Better for integrated complexes | | Typical adopters | Small/medium units | GNFC, NFL, RCF | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT write the formula as `(NH₄)₃PO₄`** — that is **tri-ammonium phosphate**, which is **unstable** and releases NH₃. The correct form is **NH₄H₂PO₄ (MAP)**. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. > 2. **Do NOT confuse ASP grade "16-20-0" with DAP "18-46-0".** ASP has *less* N and *much less* P₂O₅ than DAP — students often incorrectly cite DAP figures in ASP questions. > 3. **Always state the AMR value** when describing the pre-neutralizer. A process description without numerical AMR = **–1 mark** as per KTU 2024 valuation key. > 4. **Don't forget the scrubber** in the PFD — ammonia recovery is a **mandatory environmental compliance step** under the **Consent to Operate** rules; omitting it costs **½ mark**. > 5. In Odda-route derivations, **always show both reactions** (acidulation + Ca(NO₃)₂ conversion) — partial answers fetch only **3–4 marks out of 7**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition**: ASP = NH₄H₂PO₄ + (NH₄)₂SO₄ co-granulated; grade **16-20-0-13S**. - **Critical control variable**: **Ammonia Mole Ratio (AMR) = 1.0–1.2**. - **Two routes**: (i) Merchant phosphoric acid route, (ii) Mixed acid (nitrophosphate) route. - **Pre-neutralizer** operates at **110–125 °C**, **pH 4.5–5.0**, **slurry moisture 18–22 %**. - **Granulation** is by **drum layering** with recycle ratio **1.5–2.5 : 1**. - **Drying**: rotary, co-current, inlet 200 °C / outlet 90 °C; final moisture ≤ **1.5 %**. - **N : P₂O₅ ratio** in ASP = **0.80** (unique fingerprint). - **Sulphur contribution** comes entirely from (NH₄)₂SO₄; theoretical S ≈ **0.43 × (NH₄)₂SO₄ %**. - **CRH (Critical Relative Humidity)** of ASP ≈ **70–75 %** — store in **dry, ventilated godowns**. - **Bulk density**: 0.85–0.95 g/cm³; **angle of repose**: 30–33°. - **Anti-caking coating**: 0.3 % oil + 0.5 % kaolin clay. - **PFD essentials to draw**: acid storage → pre-neutralizer → granulator → dryer → screen → crusher (recycle) → cooler → coating → bagging → scrubber (loop back). - **Numerical**: 1 tonne ASP needs ≈ **184 kg H₃PO₄ (75 %)** + **661 kg (NH₄)₂SO₄** + **25 kg NH₃**. - **Environmental note**: HF released in acidulation is scrubbed with **silica-coated cyclones**; modern plants use **wet phosphoric acid** with low F content. - **Agronomic edge**: ASP is the **only fertilizer supplying N + P + S in one granule** at this nutrient density, making it indispensable for the **oilseed-pulse cropping systems** of Central and Eastern India. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "SAPS":** > **S** → Sulphur (13 %) from (NH₄)₂SO₄ > **A** → Ammonia Mole Ratio controls MAP formation > **P** → Phosphoric acid is the P source > **S** → Slurry granulation in rotary drum

topics.title: Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)** is a granular, nitrogenous fertilizer manufactured by the **granulation of a homogeneous mixture of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) and finely divided calcareous material** — typically **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** or **dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂)**. The standard commercial grade contains **20.5% to 26% Nitrogen (N)**, depending on the proportion of lime/carbonate additive used. The most globally traded grade is **CAN-27** (or CAN-26), with the European standard being **CAN-27 (27% N)**, while the Indian/KTU-referenced standard is typically **CAN-25 (25% N)** containing limestone or dolomite.

The chemical structure is best represented as a **double-salt / homogeneous physical mixture** system:

$$NH_4NO_3 \cdot CaCO_3 \quad \text{(homogeneous solid solution / mixture)}$$

The most commonly accepted stoichiometric formulation of CAN is:

$$[NH_4NO_3 + CaCO_3] \rightarrow \text{CAN Granule (N\% varies from 20.5 to 27.5)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** CAN is a **physically mixed, chemically bound granular fertilizer** where the CaCO₃ (or dolomite) acts as a **"safety diluent"** — it chemically binds the moisture-sensitive and detonable ammonium nitrate, thereby reducing its **hygroscopicity, caking tendency, and explosion hazard** while simultaneously supplying **secondary nutrient Calcium (and Magnesium in dolomitic CAN)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **highly reactive, dangerous chemical** — pure **Ammonium Nitrate (AN)** — which is so powerful that it is used as an **explosive in mining and terrorist bombings** (e.g., the Oklahoma City bombing, 1995). It also absorbs moisture from air like a sponge, turning into a wet, useless lump in storage.

Now, imagine you take this "dangerous sponge" and **mix it with chalk powder (CaCO₃)** and then **bake it into hard, dry pellets** (granules). What you get is:
- A **safe, stable, dry pellet** that doesn't absorb moisture easily.
- A pellet that **cannot explode** because the chalk absorbs the energy of decomposition.
- A pellet that **slowly releases nitrogen** into the soil.
- A pellet that **adds calcium** to acidic soils.

That baked, safe pellet is **Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)** — the **"tamed and domesticated cousin" of Ammonium Nitrate**.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Properties

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Nitrogen Content (N)** | **20.5% – 27%** (CAN-25 / CAN-27 grades) |
| **Calcium Content (Ca)** | **8% – 12%** (as CaO equivalent) |
| **Magnesium (in dolomitic CAN)** | **4% – 6%** (as MgO) |
| **Molecular weight of NH₄NO₃** | **80.04 g/mol** |
| **Molecular weight of CaCO₃** | **100.09 g/mol** |
| **Bulk Density (granular)** | **0.95 – 1.05 g/cm³** |
| **Granule size** | **2 – 4 mm (90% retention)** |
| **Critical Relative Humidity (CRH)** | **~60–70%** (vs. 59% for pure AN) |
| **pH (10% solution)** | **6.0 – 7.5** (near neutral) |
| **Colour** | **White / light grey (greyish-white granules)** |
| **Solubility in water** | **Highly soluble** (≈ 190 g/100 mL at 20 °C for AN component) |
| **Detonation Sensitivity** | **Negligible** (passes UN Test Series 1 & 2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Relative Humidity (CRH)** is the RH of the atmosphere above which a fertilizer starts absorbing moisture. The **higher the CRH, the safer the storage**. CAN's CRH (~60–70%) is significantly higher than pure AN (~59%), making it a far superior storage-grade fertilizer.

### Why CAN Was Developed — The Industrial Problem

The **Green Revolution (1960s–70s)** demanded massive quantities of nitrogen fertilizers. Pure Ammonium Nitrate (AN-34.5%N) was the most concentrated dry N-source, but suffered from three fatal flaws:

1. **Hygroscopicity** — caked in storage, unusable in spreaders.
2. **Detonation hazard** — used in the Texas City Disaster (1947, 581 deaths) and many mining accidents.
3. **Soil acidification** — pure AN leaves residual acidity, lowering soil pH.

The **Norwegian Hydro / Norsk Hydro** process (1950s) pioneered the addition of **dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂)** to AN, creating **"Dolomitic Ammonium Nitrate"** — the direct ancestor of modern CAN. By the 1970s, the **IFA (International Fertilizer Association)** mandated global standardization on CAN as the **safe alternative to straight AN**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** CAN is the **direct industrial answer to AN's safety and storage failures**. It is the only major N-fertilizer that simultaneously: (a) reduces explosion risk, (b) reduces hygroscopicity, (c) reduces soil acidification, and (d) supplies a secondary macronutrient (Ca/Mg) — all in a single granule.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Core Chemical Logic of CAN

The manufacturing of CAN is governed by **three sequential chemical principles**:

#### Step 1 — Production of Ammonium Nitrate (the N-carrier)
The base material NH₄NO₃ is first synthesized from ammonia and nitric acid:

$$NH_3 \,(\text{g}) + HNO_3 \,(\text{aq}) \rightarrow NH_4NO_3 \,(\text{aq}) \quad \Delta H = -145.6 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

The solution obtained is **83–95% NH₄NO₃** (called **AN melt** or **AN concentrate**), and is sent to the CAN plant at **165–175 °C** with **< 0.5% moisture**.

#### Step 2 — Preparation of the Calcareous Additive
Either **CaCO₃ (limestone)** or **CaMg(CO₃)₂ (dolomite)** is:
- **Crushed** to < 100 µm (microscopic fineness).
- **Dried** to < 0.5% moisture.
- **Classified** to ensure uniform particle size distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why micronized CaCO₃?** Because the finer the CaCO₃, the more **homogeneous** the final CAN granule, leading to **uniform N-distribution** in every pellet. Coarse CaCO₃ produces "speckled" granules with non-uniform nutrient release.

#### Step 3 — Granulation (The Heart of the Process)
The AN melt and powdered CaCO₃ are fed into a **rotating drum granulator** or **spherodizer** at controlled ratios. The melt acts as a **liquid binder**, coating the CaCO₃ particles and solidifying into spherical granules. Hot air (110–130 °C) is blown counter-currently to:
- Evaporate excess water.
- Cool the granules.
- Prevent over-wetting and agglomeration.

The granulation reaction is **physical**, not strictly chemical — but partial ionic interaction occurs:

$$2NH_4NO_3 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow Ca(NO_3)_2 + 2NH_3\uparrow + H_2O + CO_2\uparrow$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** The above reaction looks like a "transformation", but in the **actual granulation process** it does **NOT proceed to completion** at the granulator temperature (~125 °C). The CAN granule is essentially a **physical mixture / solid solution** of NH₄NO₃ and CaCO₃. The **stability** of CAN depends on keeping the granule **dry and cool** to suppress this decomposition.

### Why CaCO₃ Stabilizes AN — The "Self-Defence" Mechanism

The thermodynamic justification for adding CaCO₃ lies in **Reaction Inhibition Kinetics**:

1. **Pure AN decomposes on heating** as:
$$NH_4NO_3 \rightarrow N_2O\uparrow + 2H_2O \quad (\text{at } 210\,°C)$$
$$2NH_4NO_3 \rightarrow 2N_2\uparrow + O_2\uparrow + 4H_2O \quad (\text{detonation, } >\,300\,°C)$$

2. **In CAN, the CaCO₃ acts as a thermal sink and CO₂-release buffer**:
$$CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2\uparrow \quad (\text{at } 825\,°C, \text{ too high for normal fires})$$

3. More importantly, the **Ca²⁺ ions** in the granule **complex with the NO₃⁻ decomposition products**, slowing the chain reaction of detonation. The **decomposition temperature of CAN is raised by ~50–80 °C** compared to pure AN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Point:** The CO₂ released by CaCO₃ during a fire **smothers the fire by displacing O₂**, providing an **inherent fire-extinguishing property** to CAN. This is why CAN is **the preferred N-fertilizer for storage near flammable materials**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N\% \text{ in CAN} = \dfrac{w_{AN} \times 0.35 - \text{losses}}{w_{AN} + w_{CaCO_3}}$ | $w_{AN}$ = wt of AN, $w_{CaCO_3}$ = wt of CaCO₃ | mass% | Calculates N-grade from mix ratio |
| 2 | $N\% \text{ (stoichiometric CAN-27)} = \dfrac{2 \times 14}{80.04 + 100.09} \times 100$ | — | % | Theoretical max N% for 1:1 AN:CaCO₃ ≈ 15.5% |
| 3 | $N\% \text{ for CAN-25} = \dfrac{x \cdot M_N}{M_{AN} + M_{CaCO_3} \cdot y}$ | $x$ = no. of N atoms, $M_N$ = 14 | % | Grade design equation |
| 4 | $CRH_{CAN} = 60\text{–}70\% > CRH_{AN} = 59\%$ | — | %RH | Hygroscopicity comparison |
| 5 | $Ca\% \text{ in CAN} = \dfrac{40.08 \cdot w_{CaCO_3}}{M_{CAN\_granule}} \times \dfrac{1}{100}$ | — | wt% | Ca content per granule |
| 6 | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{m_{CAN}}{V_{container}}$ | $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume | g/cm³ | Bulk density test (KTU lab) |
| 7 | $\text{Granule strength (kg/granule)} = \dfrac{F_{crush}}{n_{granules}}$ | $F_{crush}$ = crushing force, $n$ = no. of granules | kg | KTU lab quality test |
| 8 | $\eta_{N} = \dfrac{N_{product}}{N_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | — | % | Process efficiency |
| 9 | $H_{NH_4NO_3 \, \text{formation}} = -145.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | — | kJ/mol | Heat of reaction in AN plant |
| 10 | $\text{AN:CaCO}_3 \text{ ratio for CAN-25} \approx 76:24$ (by mass) | — | — | Plant operating ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The most tested calculation is the **N% calculation from mix ratio**. For CAN-25, the industry-standard mix is **~76% AN + ~24% CaCO₃ (or dolomite)** by mass. For CAN-27, the mix is **~80% AN + ~20% CaCO₃**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture:** CAN is the **preferred top-dressing fertilizer for wheat, paddy, sugarcane, and vegetables** in India, Europe, and China. It is especially valuable for **acidic soils (pH < 6.0)** because the CaCO₃ component **neutralizes soil acidity** simultaneously with N-supply.
- **Blended Fertilizers:** CAN is a **key ingredient in NPK blend manufacture** (e.g., 17-17-17, 20-10-10) because of its high N% and spherical granule shape (matches other granules for uniform blending).
- **Defence / Mining:** Ironically, **CAN is now legally classified as a "non-explosive" fertilizer** under the UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UN-1942 has been removed; CAN is shipped as **non-DG cargo**).
- **Green Chemistry:** Modern CAN plants use **closed-loop dust recovery** and **CO₂ sequestration** in the limestone step, making it a **lower-carbon-intensity N-fertilizer** than urea or AN.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Theoretical Nitrogen Percentage of CAN from Mix Ratio

**Problem:** A CAN plant mixes **76 kg of pure NH₄NO₃ (anhydrous, 100% concentration)** with **24 kg of pure CaCO₃ (anhydrous)**. Calculate the **theoretical nitrogen percentage** in the final CAN granule, assuming **zero process losses** and **complete homogeneous mixing**.

**Given:**
- Mass of NH₄NO₃ = 76 kg
- Mass of CaCO₃ = 24 kg
- Molecular mass of NH₄NO₃ = 80.04 g/mol
- Atomic mass of N = 14.01 g/mol
- Moles of N per mole of NH₄NO₃ = 2

**Step 1 — Calculate mass of nitrogen supplied by NH₄NO₃:**

$$M_{N} \text{ in NH}_4\text{NO}_3 = \dfrac{2 \times 14.01}{80.04} = \dfrac{28.02}{80.04} = 0.3501$$

$$\therefore \text{N in NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \text{ (mass fraction)} = 35.01\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total mass of nitrogen in 76 kg of NH₄NO₃:**

$$m_N = 76 \times 0.3501 = 26.608 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total mass of the CAN granule (assuming no losses):**

$$m_{CAN} = 76 + 24 = 100 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate N% in CAN:**

$$N\%_{CAN} = \dfrac{m_N}{m_{CAN}} \times 100 = \dfrac{26.608}{100} \times 100 = 26.61\%$$

**Step 5 — Round to industry grade:**

$$\boxed{N\% \approx 26.6\% \text{ (close to CAN-27 grade)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The standard CAN-27 is achieved with **80% AN + 20% CaCO₃**, while **CAN-25** uses **76% AN + 24% CaCO₃**. The **higher the CaCO₃ fraction, the lower the N%**, but the **higher the Ca supply and the lower the explosion risk**.

---

### Derivation 2: Material Balance for a 100 TPD CAN Plant

**Problem:** A CAN-25 plant produces **100 tonnes per day (TPD)** of CAN granules at **25% N**. The plant uses **76% AN solution (concentrated melt)** and **24% dolomite powder (CaMg(CO₃)₂)** by mass. Calculate:
- (a) Daily requirement of NH₄NO₃ (anhydrous basis)
- (b) Daily requirement of CaCO₃ equivalent (assuming dolomite has 54% CaCO₃ equivalent)
- (c) Daily output of pure nitrogen

**Given:**
- Production rate = 100 TPD CAN-25
- N% in CAN = 25%
- AN in mix = 76%, CaCO₃-eq in mix = 24%

**Part (a) — AN requirement:**

$$m_{AN} = 100 \times 0.76 = 76 \, \text{TPD}$$

**Part (b) — CaCO₃ equivalent requirement (as pure CaCO₃ basis):**

$$m_{CaCO_3 \, eq} = 100 \times 0.24 = 24 \, \text{TPD}$$

**If dolomite is used (54% CaCO₃-eq basis):**

$$m_{dolomite} = \dfrac{24}{0.54} = 44.44 \, \text{TPD}$$

**Part (c) — Daily pure nitrogen output:**

$$m_N = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \, \text{TPD}$$

**Check via AN:**

$$m_N = 76 \times 0.3501 = 26.61 \, \text{TPD (theoretical, no losses)}$$

**With realistic 6% process loss:**

$$m_N^{actual} = 26.61 \times 0.94 = 25.01 \, \text{TPD} \approx 25 \, \text{TPD} \, \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Balance Insight:** The 6% "loss" in CAN plants is mainly from: (i) **dust loss** to cyclones (1–2%), (ii) **NH₃ slip** from melt decomposition (1–3%), and (iii) **undersize recycle** (2–3%). Modern plants recover >95% via **wet scrubbers** and **sizing screens**.

---

### Derivation 3: Stoichiometry of the Stabilization Reaction

**Problem:** In a CAN granule, 1 mole of CaCO₃ is in contact with 2 moles of NH₄NO₃. If 100% of the CaCO₃ were to react (worst-case), calculate:
- (a) Volume of NH₃ gas released at STP
- (b) Volume of CO₂ gas released at STP

**Reaction:**

$$2NH_4NO_3 + CaCO_3 \rightarrow Ca(NO_3)_2 + 2NH_3\uparrow + H_2O\uparrow + CO_2\uparrow$$

**Step 1 — Moles:**
- 1 mole CaCO₃ → 1 mole CO₂ and 2 moles NH₃ (in the hypothetical full reaction)

**Step 2 — Volumes at STP (1 mole = 22.4 L):**

$$V_{NH_3} = 2 \times 22.4 = 44.8 \, \text{L per mole CaCO}_3$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 1 \times 22.4 = 22.4 \, \text{L per mole CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3 — For 24 kg CaCO₃ in 100 kg CAN:**

$$n_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{24,000}{100.09} = 239.8 \, \text{moles}$$

$$V_{NH_3} = 239.8 \times 44.8 = 10,743 \, \text{L} \approx 10.74 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 239.8 \times 22.4 = 5,372 \, \text{L} \approx 5.37 \, \text{m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-plant reality:** This is the **worst-case if 100% reaction occurs** — but in actual storage, **<1% reaction occurs per year**. Even so, plant designers must provide **adequate ventilation** in storage sheds to prevent NH₃ accumulation above **25 ppm TLV** (Threshold Limit Value).

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix: CAN vs. Other N-Fertilizers

| Property | Urea (46% N) | Ammonium Nitrate (34.5% N) | Ammonium Sulphate (21% N) | **CAN-25 (25% N)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **N%** | 46 | 34.5 | 21 | **25** |
| **Hygroscopicity (CRH)** | 75% | 59% | 79% | **60–70%** |
| **Detonation Risk** | None | **High** | None | **Negligible** |
| **Soil Acidification** | High (urease → acidity) | High | Very High | **Low (CaCO₃ buffers)** |
| **Secondary Nutrient** | None | None | S (24%) | **Ca (8–12%) / Mg (4–6%)** |
| **Granule Strength** | Medium | Poor (caking) | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Storage Stability** | Good (caking risk) | **Poor** | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Cost per kg N** | Lowest | Medium | High | **Medium** |
| **Volatilization Loss** | High (NH₃ loss) | None | None | **None** |
| **Best Use** | Top-dressing, foliar | Banned/restricted in many countries | S-deficient soils | **Acidic soils, top-dressing, blends** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Overall CAN Manufacturing Process — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[HNO3 Acid Tank] --> reactA[Neutralizer Reactor]
    rawB[NH3 Vaporizer] --> reactA
    reactA --> evap[AN Evaporator 83 to 95 percent]
    evap --> melt[AN Melt 165 to 175 deg C]
    lime[Crushed Limestone CaCO3] --> dryL[Dolomite/Limestone Dryer]
    doLo[Dolomite Mine] --> crush[Crusher]
    crush --> dryL
    dryL --> mill[Ball Mill 100 micron]
    mill --> silo[CaCO3 Storage Silo]
    silo --> granu[Rotating Drum Granulator]
    melt --> granu
    granu --> dryG[Granule Dryer 110 to 130 deg C]
    dryG --> screen[Vibrating Screens 2 to 4 mm]
    screen --> over[Oversize Crusher Recycle]
    screen --> coat[Coating Drum Oil and Clay]
    coat --> cool[Cooler 30 to 40 deg C]
    cool --> bag[Bagging and Storage 25 kg / 50 kg / 1 MT]
    screen --> under[Undersize Recycle to Granulator]
    over --> under
    granu --> scrub[Wet Scrubber NH3 Recovery]
    scrub --> reactA
    dryG --> cycl[Cyclone Dust Collector]
    cycl --> melt
```

### Diagram 2: Granule Microstructure — Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[CAN Granule Cross Section]
        A1[Outer Hard Shell 0.1 mm Clay and Oil Coating]
        A2[Inner Porous Matrix NH4NO3 plus CaCO3 2 to 4 mm]
        A3[CaCO3 Microparticles White Dots 100 micron]
        A4[NH4NO3 Crystalline Matrix Grey Background]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A2 --> A4
    A3 -.dissolves slowly.-> A4
    A4 -.releases NO3 and NH4.-> R1[Soil N Uptake]
    A3 -.supplies Ca2 .-> R2[Soil pH Buffer]
```

### Diagram 3: Reaction Pathways and Safety Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[CAN Granule Exposed to Heat 200 deg C plus]
    start --> path1[Pure AN Behaviour Detonation Risk]
    start --> path2[CAN Behaviour Stabilized by CaCO3]
    path1 --> det[2 NH4NO3 to 2 N2 plus O2 plus 4 H2O Detonation]
    path2 --> dec[Slow Decomposition N2O plus H2O]
    path2 --> co2[CaCO3 Releases CO2 at 825 deg C]
    co2 --> safe[CO2 Smothers Fire Oxygen Displacement]
    dec --> safe
    safe --> outcome[Self Extinguishing No Detonation]
```

### Diagram 4: Quality Control Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[CAN Batch Sampling] --> Q1{N percent 25 plus or minus 0.5}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{CRH greater than 60 percent}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Re-blend with AN or CaCO3]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Granule Size 2 to 4 mm 90 percent}
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Re-dry in Fluidized Bed]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Crushing Strength greater than 2 kg}
    Q3 -- No --> R3[Re-screen and Recycle]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{pH 6.0 to 7.5}
    Q4 -- No --> R4[Re-coat with Oil]
    Q5 -- Yes --> APPROVED[Batch Approved for Dispatch]
    Q5 -- No --> R5[Re-condition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block flow shows the **continuous integrated process** from raw materials (HNO₃, NH₃, limestone) to finished CAN bags. The **recycle loops** (undersize, oversize, dust) are critical to the **>95% material efficiency** of modern CAN plants. The microstructure diagram shows how the **CaCO₃ microparticles are embedded in the NH₄NO₃ matrix** — this is the **physical basis of CAN's stability**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN). State its typical nitrogen content and the role of calcium carbonate in its formulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) is a **granular nitrogenous fertilizer** manufactured by the **granulation of a homogeneous mixture of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) and finely divided calcareous material** such as **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) or dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂)**.

**Typical nitrogen content:** **20.5% to 27% N**, with the most common grades being **CAN-25 (25% N)** and **CAN-27 (27% N)**.

**Role of CaCO₃:**
1. **Acts as a diluent** — reduces the concentration of detonable NH₄NO₃.
2. **Reduces hygroscopicity** — raises the Critical Relative Humidity (CRH) from 59% (pure AN) to **60–70%** for CAN.
3. **Suppresses detonation** — Ca²⁺ ions inhibit the chain decomposition of NH₄NO₃.
4. **Supplies secondary nutrient Ca** — neutralizes soil acidity.
5. **Releases CO₂ on extreme heating** — provides inherent fire-extinguishing property.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain why Calcium Ammonium Nitrate is preferred over straight Ammonium Nitrate as a nitrogen fertilizer, listing at least four advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CAN is preferred over straight Ammonium Nitrate (AN) for the following reasons:

1. **Safety against detonation:** Straight AN is classified as an **oxidizer (UN-1942)** and has been involved in industrial disasters (Texas City 1947, Oklahoma City 1995). CAN, with CaCO₃, is classified as **non-dangerous goods** and passes UN detonation tests.

2. **Lower hygroscopicity / better storage:** CRH of AN is only **59%**, causing caking in humid climates. CAN has **CRH ~60–70%**, allowing open-bag storage in tropical conditions like Kerala.

3. **Soil-friendly pH:** Straight AN strongly **acidifies soil** (residual H⁺ from nitrification). CAN's CaCO₃ component **buffers pH**, making it suitable for **acidic laterite soils** of Kerala.

4. **Supplies secondary nutrient Ca (and Mg in dolomitic CAN):** Improves soil structure, root development, and reduces Ca-deficiency disorders like **blossom-end rot in tomatoes and tip-burn in lettuce**.

5. **Better granule strength:** CAN granules are **harder and more uniform** than AN, enabling **mechanical broadcasting** without breakage.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **manufacturing process of Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)** starting from ammonia and nitric acid, with a **neat flow diagram** and the **key process parameters** at each stage. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CAN plant produces **CAN-25** with the following raw material composition:
- NH₄NO₃ (anhydrous) feed: **76 kg per 100 kg CAN**
- CaCO₃ (anhydrous) feed: **24 kg per 100 kg CAN**

If the plant capacity is **200 TPD (tonnes per day)** of CAN, calculate:
- (i) Daily NH₄NO₃ requirement in kg.
- (ii) Daily CaCO₃ requirement in kg.
- (iii) Daily pure nitrogen output in kg, assuming 6% process loss. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CAN Manufacturing Process — 7 marks**

The CAN manufacturing is a **continuous integrated process** with the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Ammonium Nitrate Production (Neutralization):**
- **Reactors:** Stainless steel neutralizers with **pH control (5.5–6.0)**.
- **Reaction:** $NH_3 \, (\text{g}) + HNO_3 \, (\text{aq}) \rightarrow NH_4NO_3 \, (\text{aq}) + 145.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- **Temperature:** 145–155 °C; **Pressure:** 4–6 bar.
- **Concentration obtained:** 80–83% NH₄NO₃ solution.

**Stage 2 — AN Evaporation:**
- **Equipment:** Falling-film evaporator.
- **Outlet concentration:** 95–99% AN melt.
- **Outlet temperature:** 165–175 °C.
- **Moisture:** < 0.5%.

**Stage 3 — Limestone/Dolomite Preparation:**
- **Crushing:** Jaw crusher → ball mill.
- **Particle size:** < 100 µm (95% passing 100 mesh).
- **Drying:** Rotary dryer or flash dryer to < 0.5% moisture.

**Stage 4 — Granulation (The Heart of the Process):**
- **Equipment:** Rotary drum granulator (3–4 m dia, 8–12 m long, 5–8 rpm).
- **Feed ratio:** 76:24 (AN:CaCO₃) for CAN-25.
- **Granulator temperature:** 120–135 °C.
- **Granulation mechanism:** AN melt coats CaCO₃ particles; tumbling action forms spherical granules (1–4 mm).

**Stage 5 — Drying & Cooling:**
- **Drying:** Rotary/co-current dryer with hot air (110–130 °C).
- **Cooling:** Fluidized bed cooler or rotary cooler to 30–40 °C.

**Stage 6 — Screening & Recycling:**
- **Screens:** Vibratory, double-deck (4 mm top, 2 mm bottom).
- **Oversize** (> 4 mm): Crushed and recycled.
- **Undersize** (< 2 mm): Melted and recycled to granulator.
- **Product size:** 2–4 mm (90% retention).

**Stage 7 — Coating:**
- **Coating drum** with **0.3–0.5% oil (mineral/vegetable)** + **0.5–1% clay (kaolin)**.
- **Purpose:** Anti-caking, anti-dust, improved storage.

**Stage 8 — Bagging & Storage:**
- **Packing:** 50 kg HDPE bags or 1 MT bulk bags.
- **Storage:** Covered, dry warehouse, < 70% RH.

**Process parameters (key control points):**
| Parameter | Set Value |
|---|---|
| AN melt temperature | 165–175 °C |
| Granulator bed temperature | 120–135 °C |
| CaCO₃ moisture | < 0.5% |
| CaCO₃ particle size | < 100 µm (95%) |
| Granule product size | 2–4 mm |
| Product temperature | < 40 °C |
| Bag storage RH | < 70% |

**(b) Material Balance — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Plant capacity = 200 TPD CAN-25
- N% in CAN = 25%
- AN per 100 kg CAN = 76 kg
- CaCO₃ per 100 kg CAN = 24 kg
- Process loss = 6%

**(i) Daily NH₄NO₃ requirement:**

$$m_{AN} = \dfrac{200 \times 76}{100} = 152 \text{ TPD} = 1,52,000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(ii) Daily CaCO₃ requirement:**

$$m_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{200 \times 24}{100} = 48 \text{ TPD} = 48,000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Daily pure N output (with 6% loss):**

Theoretical N from 152,000 kg AN:

$$m_N^{theoretical} = 1,52,000 \times 0.3501 = 53,215.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

With 6% process loss:

$$m_N^{actual} = 53,215.2 \times (1 - 0.06) = 53,215.2 \times 0.94$$

$$m_N^{actual} = 50,022.3 \text{ kg/day} \approx 50.02 \text{ TPD}$$

**Check using N%:**

$$m_N = 200 \times 0.25 = 50 \text{ TPD} \, \checkmark$$

The 0.02 TPD discrepancy is due to **rounding** in the loss factor — both methods agree within engineering tolerance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **properties, advantages, and disadvantages** of **CAN, Urea, and Ammonium Nitrate** as nitrogen fertilizers. Which one is the **most suitable for acidic laterite soils of Kerala**, and why? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **role of CaCO₃ (or dolomite) in stabilizing Ammonium Nitrate** in CAN formulation. Discuss the **mechanism of detonation suppression** with relevant chemical equations. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis of N-Fertilizers — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Urea | Ammonium Nitrate | CAN-25 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N%** | 46 (highest) | 34.5 | 25 |
| **Form** | Prilled/granular white | Crystalline/granular | Granular white-grey |
| **Hygroscopicity** | Moderate (CRH 75%) | High (CRH 59%) | Moderate (CRH 60–70%) |
| **Detonation risk** | None | **High** | **Negligible** |
| **Soil pH effect** | Acidifies (after hydrolysis) | Strongly acidifies | **Buffers / neutralizes** |
| **Volatilization loss** | High (NH₃ loss in alkaline soil) | None | None |
| **Caking tendency** | High (forms biuret) | Very high | **Very low** |
| **Storage safety** | Good (but biuret issue) | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| **Cost per kg N** | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium |
| **Secondary nutrient** | None | None | **Ca (and Mg)** |

**Advantages & Disadvantages:**

- **Urea (46% N):**
  - ✅ Highest N concentration → **lowest transport cost per kg N**.
  - ✅ Non-explosive → safe to store.
  - ❌ Suffers **NH₃ volatilization loss up to 30–50%** when surface-applied to alkaline or hot soils.
  - ❌ Forms **biuret** (toxic to germinating seeds) if overheated during manufacture.
  - ❌ **Acidifies soil** after nitrification.

- **Ammonium Nitrate (34.5% N):**
  - ✅ High N concentration, fully water-soluble, fast-acting.
  - ❌ **Severe explosion hazard** (Oklahoma City 1995, Texas City 1947) → **banned/restricted** in many countries.
  - ❌ **Highly hygroscopic** (CRH 59%) → caking in tropical storage.
  - ❌ **Strongly acidifies** soil (2 H⁺ per NH₄⁺ nitrified).

- **CAN-25 (25% N):**
  - ✅ **Safe from explosion** (passes UN Test Series 1 & 2).
  - ✅ **Improves soil pH** — CaCO₃ component neutralizes acidity.
  - ✅ Supplies **Ca and Mg** (in dolomitic CAN) — secondary macronutrients.
  - ✅ **Better granule strength** for mechanical spreading.
  - ❌ **Lower N%** than urea or AN → higher transport/handling cost per kg N.

**Most suitable for Kerala laterite soils: CAN-25 (specifically dolomitic CAN).**

**Reasons:**
1. Kerala soils are **strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5)** due to laterite (iron + aluminium oxide) composition. CAN's CaCO₃ component **raises pH to 6.0–6.5** — optimal for most crops.
2. Kerala has **high rainfall (>2500 mm/year)** and **humidity >80%** — CAN's CRH of 60–70% is more reliable than AN's 59%.
3. **Dolomitic CAN** supplies **Mg**, critical for **coconut, arecanut, and cardamom** grown in Kerala.
4. **No NH₃ volatilization** (unlike urea) — important in the **high-temperature, high-humidity tropical climate** of Kerala where urea losses can exceed 40%.

**(b) Mechanism of CaCO₃ Stabilization — 7 marks**

The role of CaCO₃ in CAN is **multi-fold and synergistic**:

**1. Thermal Decomposition Suppression:**
Pure AN decomposes exothermically:

$$NH_4NO_3 \xrightarrow{210\,°C} N_2O\uparrow + 2H_2O \quad \text{(slow decomp.)}$$

$$2NH_4NO_3 \xrightarrow{>300\,°C, \text{ confined}} 2N_2\uparrow + O_2\uparrow + 4H_2O \quad \text{(detonation)}$$

The **detonation chain reaction** requires:
- **Confined space** (storage silo, ship's hold).
- **Heat source** (fire, friction, electrical spark).
- **Continuous fuel path** (pure AN crystals).

CaCO₃ **physically disrupts the fuel path** by introducing inert particles that:
- **Absorb heat** (CaCO₃ has high heat capacity, ~0.9 kJ/kg·K).
- **Block the chain reaction** by **scattering the decomposition front**.
- **Release CO₂** at 825 °C (above the detonation threshold) — **smothers the fire**.

**2. Ionic Stabilization:**
The Ca²⁺ ions in the granule **form weak coordination complexes** with NO₃⁻ decomposition products (NO₂⁻, NO), slowing the rate of gas evolution. This is the **chemical basis of CAN's "thermal buffering"**.

**3. Hygroscopicity Reduction:**
Pure AN's **CRH = 59%** means it absorbs moisture from air with RH > 59%. In humid climates (RH 70–90%), AN cakes into a wet mass.

CaCO₃ in the granule:
- Acts as a **drying scaffold** — provides **high surface area for moisture absorption** without forming a wet mass.
- The CaCO₃ particles **disrupt the continuity of AN crystals**, preventing the formation of a continuous moisture-absorbing film.
- **Result:** CAN's CRH rises to **60–70%** — still not as high as urea (75%) or ammonium sulphate (79%), but vastly better than pure AN (59%).

**4. Self-Extinguishing Mechanism:**
In a CAN fire, the **CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂** reaction (endothermic, requires 825 °C) is **slower than AN decomposition**. However, even partial CO₂ release, combined with **water vapour from AN decomposition**, **displaces oxygen** in the burning zone. The result:
- **Pure AN:** Burns vigorously, may detonate.
- **CAN:** Burns slowly, self-extinguishes when external heat source is removed.

**Conclusion:** CaCO₃ is **not just a diluent** but an **active stabilizer** that provides **four independent safety mechanisms** (thermal, ionic, hygroscopic, and chemical), making CAN the **safest solid N-fertilizer** in global use.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CAN with Ammonium Nitrate (AN):** Students often write "CAN contains NH₄NO₃ as the active ingredient" but forget to mention the **stabilization role of CaCO₃** — a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Writing "CAN = NH₄NO₃·CaCO₃" as a chemical compound:** This is **incorrect** — CAN is a **physical mixture/granular blend**, not a stoichiometric compound.
> 3. **N% calculation errors:** Using 14 (atomic mass) instead of 14.01, or forgetting the factor of 2 (NH₄NO₃ has 2 N atoms per molecule) — common 1-mark errors.
> 4. **Omitting the granulation step:** A full 7-mark process description **must** include the granulator, screens, and recycle loop — not just neutralization and evaporation.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention dolomitic CAN:** The Kerala context (laterite + coconut/arecanut) makes **dolomitic CAN** a KTU favourite — missing it loses 1–2 marks in the comparison question.
> 6. **Wrong CRH values:** Pure AN CRH = 59% (memorize this). CAN CRH = 60–70% (state the range, not a single value).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — CAN (Module 4):**

- ✅ **Definition:** CAN = **granular blend of NH₄NO₃ + CaCO₃ (or dolomite)**; **N% = 20.5–27%**; standard grades **CAN-25** and **CAN-27**.
- ✅ **Chemical reaction (base AN):** $NH_3 + HNO_3 \rightarrow NH_4NO_3$ (exothermic, **−145.6 kJ/mol**).
- ✅ **Standard mix ratio (CAN-25):** **76% AN + 24% CaCO₃** by mass.
- ✅ **N% in NH₄NO₃ = 35.01%** (i.e., $2 \times 14.01 / 80.04$).
- ✅ **Granulation temperature:** **120–135 °C**; **AN melt temp:** 165–175 °C; **cooling to <40 °C** before bagging.
- ✅ **CRH of pure AN = 59%**; **CRH of CAN = 60–70%**; **CRH of urea = 75%**; **CRH of ammonium sulphate = 79%**.
- ✅ **Four roles of CaCO₃:** (1) explosion suppression, (2) hygroscopicity reduction, (3) soil pH buffering, (4) secondary nutrient (Ca) supply.
- ✅ **Detonation equation of AN:** $2NH_4NO_3 \rightarrow 2N_2 + O_2 + 4H_2O$ (occurs >300 °C, confined).
- ✅ **CaCO₃ decomposition:** $CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2$ at **825 °C** (above AN's normal fire temperature — provides **self-extinguishing** property).
- ✅ **CaCO₃ in CAN is NOT a separate phase in stoichiometric sense** — it is a **physical mixture** in the granule (do not call it a "compound").
- ✅ **Dolomitic CAN** uses **CaMg(CO₃)₂** → supplies **Ca + Mg**; preferred for **Kerala laterite soils** and for **coconut/arecanut cultivation**.
- ✅ **CAN is classified as non-dangerous goods** under UN transport regulations (no UN number required for shipment, unlike pure AN which is **UN-1942**).
- ✅ **Granule size specification:** 2–4 mm, **90% retention**; crushing strength **>2 kg/granule**.
- ✅ **Coating:** **0.3–0.5% oil + 0.5–1% kaolin clay** for anti-caking.
- ✅ **Suitable for:** acidic soils, top-dressing, NPK blending, foliar (after dissolution), fertigation.
- ✅ **Not suitable for:** highly alkaline soils (pH > 8.0) — urea more efficient; flooded paddy (NH₃ volatilization not a concern, but CAN dissolves fast).
- ✅ **Process efficiency of modern CAN plants:** >95% (with dust + undersize recycle).
- ✅ **Major producers globally:** Yara International (Norway), OCI (Netherlands), CF Industries (USA), IFFCO & KRIBHCO (India — use dolomitic CAN at **Kandla, Kalol, Phulpur, Aonla** plants).
- ✅ **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Compare CAN with Urea" or "Calculate N% from mix ratio" — both are **guaranteed** in the 14-mark Part B.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for CAN's advantages:** **"S-SAFE"** — **S**afe from explosion, **S**econdary nutrient (Ca), **A**nti-caking, **F**riendly to acidic soils, **E**xcellent granule strength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofertilizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT637 - FERTILIZER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Biofertilizers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Official KTU Definition (Fertilizer Technology):** > **Biofertilizers** are biologically active formulations consisting of living or latent cells of efficient strains of beneficial microorganisms — including nitrogen-fixers, phosphate solubilizers, phosphate mobilizers, and plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria (PGPR) — which, when applied to seed, soil, rhizosphere, or composting substrates, augment the availability of primary nutrients (N, P, K) and trace elements to crops through natural biological processes such as **biological nitrogen fixation (BNF)**, **mineralization**, **solubilization**, and **mobilization**. The term was formally codified under the **Fertilizer (Inorganic, Organic or Mixed) (Control) Order, 1985** in India, which recognized biofertilizers as a distinct category of nutrient supplements, with standards prescribed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. **Key Standardized Metrics (KTU High-Yield):** - Population density at manufacture: **≥ 10⁸ CFU/g** (carrier-based) or **≥ 10⁹ CFU/mL** (liquid) - Shelf life: **6 months** (carrier) / **12 months** (liquid) - Contamination threshold: **< 0.5%** at manufacture; **< 1.0%** at expiry - pH range: **6.5 – 7.5** - Moisture content (carrier): **30 – 40%** ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Underground Workforce" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Microbial Workforce:** > Imagine your soil as a large construction site, and your crop plants as skyscrapers. Chemical fertilizers (urea, DAP, MOP) are like **cash handouts** that directly pay for bricks and cement. **Biofertilizers** are like **hiring skilled workers** — Rhizobium is a *mason* (fetches nitrogen from the air for free), *PSB (Phosphate Solubilizing Bacteria)* is a *miner* (digs locked phosphorus from rocks), and *Mycorrhiza* is an *electrician* (lays down nutrient-grabbing wires underground). The worker doesn't build the building alone, but without the worker, the cash handouts would mostly get wasted, stolen, or locked in storage. ## 1.3 Why Biofertilizers? — The Sustainability Imperative > [!NOTE] > The Haber-Bosch process consumes approximately **1.4% of global energy** and emits **> 300 million tonnes of CO₂/year** for synthetic N-fertilizer production. Biological Nitrogen Fixation (BNF) alone contributes roughly **70%** of biologically fixed N₂ on Earth (~ 2 × 10¹³ kg/year), making biofertilizers an ecologically critical technology in the KTU 2024 sustainability-focused curriculum. **The 5 Pillars of Biofertilizer Importance (KTU-Exam Favorite):** | Pillar | Engineering / Agricultural Significance | |---|---| | **Ecological** | Reduces groundwater nitrate leaching, NOₓ emissions, soil salinization | | **Economic** | Cuts farmer input cost by 30–40%; indigenously producible | | **Renewable** | Solar-driven N₂ fixation — virtually inexhaustible | | **Soil Health** | Builds SOM, enhances microbial biodiversity, improves tilth | | **Targeted** | Site-specific nutrient delivery (root-zone focused) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Biological Nitrogen Fixation (BNF) Equation — The Heart of Biofertilizers The entire science of nitrogen-fixing biofertilizers pivots around one master reaction catalyzed by the **nitrogenase enzyme complex**: $$N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrogenase}} 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this equation is critical for KTU exams:** The stoichiometry reveals that **fixing one mole of N₂ consumes 16 moles of ATP and 8 electrons**. This high energy cost (≈ 25 kcal per mole N₂) explains why *free-living* nitrogen fixers need carbohydrate-rich root exudates, while *symbiotic* fixers (Rhizobium) demand enormous photosynthate from the host plant. ## 2.2 The Nitrogenase Complex — MoFe-Protein Architecture The nitrogenase enzyme consists of two metalloproteins: - **Component I (MoFe-protein, dinitrogenase)** — contains the **FeMo-cofactor (FeMoco)** — the actual N₂ binding site. - **Component II (Fe-protein, dinitrogenase reductase)** — supplies electrons from reduced ferredoxin/flavodoxin using ATP hydrolysis. **N₂ Fixation Mechanistic Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic):** - **Step 1 — Energy Sourcing:** Heterocyst differentiation (in cyanobacteria) or nodule formation (in legumes) provides anaerobic microenvironments because nitrogenase is **O₂-sensitive**. - **Step 2 — Electron Donation:** Reduced ferredoxin (Fd_red) donates electrons to Fe-protein. - **Step 3 — ATP Hydrolysis & Conformational Change:** 2 ATP hydrolyzed per electron transferred; induces redox potential shift from −0.30 V to −0.40 V. - **Step 4 — Sequential Protonation:** N₂ is reduced stepwise via intermediates: **N₂ → HN=NH → H₂N–NH₂ → 2NH₃**. - **Step 5 — H₂ Evolution Side-Reaction:** At least **25%** of the electron flux is "wasted" as H₂ evolution — a major inefficiency exploited in modern research. ## 2.3 Classification of Biofertilizers — The Master Taxonomy > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Master Classification:** Biofertilizers are categorized by **function** (N-fixer, P-solubilizer, P-mobilizer, K-solubilizer, micronutrient) and by **ecological niche** (symbiotic, asymbiotic/free-living, associative). ### A. Nitrogen-Fixing Biofertilizers | Type | Microorganism | Host / Habitat | Symbiotic? | |---|---|---|---| | **Rhizobium** | *R. leguminosarum, R. japonicum, R. meliloti* | Legumes (pulses, soybean, groundnut) | **Symbiotic** (root nodules) | | **Azotobacter** | *A. chroococcum, A. vinelandii* | Soil, non-legumes | **Free-living / asymbiotic** | | **Azospirillum** | *A. brasilense, A. lipoferum* | Cereals (rice, maize, sorghum) | **Associative** (rhizosphere) | | **Cyanobacteria (BGA)** | *Anabaena, Nostoc, Oscillatoria, Aulosira* | Paddy fields (flooded) | **Free-living** (some symbiotic with Azolla) | | **Azolla** | *Azolla caroliniana, A. pinnata* + *Anabaena azollae* | Waterlogged rice | **Symbiotic** (water fern) | | **Frankia** | Actinomycete | Non-legumes (Casuarina, Alnus) | **Symbiotic** (root nodules) | ### B. Phosphate-Solubilizing Biofertilizers (PSB) - **Bacterial PSB:** *Bacillus megaterium, B. circulans, B. subtilis, Pseudomonas striata, Burkholderia cepacia* - **Fungal PSB:** *Aspergillus awamori, A. niger, Penicillium digitatum, Trichoderma spp.* - **Mechanism:** Production of **organic acids** (gluconic, citric, oxalic, lactic, 2-ketogluconic acid) → lowers soil pH in micro-environment → releases **HPO₄²⁻ / H₂PO₄⁻** from insoluble **tricalcium phosphate, rock phosphate, FePO₄, AlPO₄**. ### C. Phosphate-Mobilizing Biofertilizers (Mycorrhiza) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concept Distinction (Common Exam Trap):** > *Solubilization* = converting **insoluble P → soluble P** (chemical dissolution). > *Mobilization* = transporting **soluble P from distant soil zones → root surface** (physical extension via fungal hyphae). This distinction is **frequently tested** in KTU ESE. **Mycorrhizal Sub-Types:** - **Ectomycorrhiza (ECM):** Fungal hyphae form a *sheath* (Hartig net) **outside** root cells. Found in *Pinus, Quercus, Eucalyptus*. Fungi: *Boletus, Amanita, Laccaria*. - **Endomycorrhiza (VAM/AM):** Hyphae penetrate **inside** cortical cells forming *arbuscules* and *vesicles*. Found in **> 80% of land plants**. Genera: *Glomus, Gigaspora, Acaulospora, Scutellospora*. ### D. Other Specialized Biofertilizers - **PGPR (Plant Growth-Promoting Rhizobacteria):** *Pseudomonas fluorescens, Bacillus subtilis* — produce **IAA (Indole-3-Acetic Acid), gibberellins, cytokinins**, and **siderophores**. - **Potassium-Solubilizing Bacteria (KSB):** *Bacillus mucilaginosus, B. edaphicus, Frateuria aurantia* — release K from **micas, illite, orthoclase** via organic acid + enzyme action. - **Zinc-Solubilizing Bacteria:** *Bacillus subtilis, Thiobacillus thiooxidans* — mobilize Zn from ZnO, ZnCO₃, ZnS. - **Sulfur-Oxidizing Bacteria:** *Thiobacillus ferrooxidans, T. thiooxidans* — oxidize S → SO₄²⁻. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Quantitative Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All numerical constants, equations, and conversion factors required for PECHT637 ESE — Module 4 problems are consolidated here.** | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Biological N₂ fixation (master) | $N_2 + 16\,ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP$ | 16 mol ATP per mol N₂ | | Symbiotic N-fixation (legume) | Field rate | **50–200 kg N/ha/year** | | Asymbiotic N-fixation (Azotobacter) | Field rate | **5–20 kg N/ha/year** | | Azolla N contribution (paddy) | Per season | **20–40 kg N/ha** | | BGA N contribution (paddy) | Per season | **15–30 kg N/ha** | | Carrier CFU requirement | $N_{CFU}$ | **≥ 10⁸ CFU/g** | | Liquid biofertilizer CFU | $N_{liq}$ | **≥ 10⁹ CFU/mL** | | Shelf-life decay (1st order) | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | k = 0.005–0.015 /day | | P-solubilization index (PSI) | $PSI = \frac{\text{Colony diameter + Halo}}{\text{Colony diameter}}$ | Dimensionless, > 2.0 preferred | | Mycorrhizal infection % | $M\% = \frac{\text{Infected root segments}}{\text{Total segments}} \times 100$ | ≥ 70% is acceptable | | Carbon cost of N₂ fixation | $C_{cost}$ | **≈ 6–12 g C per g N fixed** | | Energy cost (ATP) | $E_{ATP}$ | **≈ 4.5 mol ATP per g N** | | Approx. molecular mass of N₂ | $M_{N_2}$ | **28 g/mol** | | Approx. molecular mass of NH₃ | $M_{NH_3}$ | **17 g/mol** | | % N in NH₃ | $w_N$ | **82.35%** | | Conversion: NH₃ ↔ N | $1\,\text{kg N} = 1.21\,\text{kg NH}_3$ | — | | Conversion: NH₃ ↔ Urea | $1\,\text{kg N (urea)} = 2.14\,\text{kg NH}_3$ | Urea = 46% N | | Moisture content (carrier) | $MC$ | **30 – 40%** | | Spore load (Trichoderma) | $S_{load}$ | **≥ 10⁶ CFU/g** | | Root colonization efficiency | $\eta_c$ | **40 – 80%** for VAM | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Deployment - **Solid-state fermentation (SSF)** for fungal biofertilizers (Aspergillus, Trichoderma) using agri-waste (wheat bran, rice husk) as substrate — directly aligns with the **KTU waste-to-wealth** module theme. - **Liquid biofertilizer technology** using cell-protectants (polyvinylpyrrolidone, glycerol, trehalose) extends shelf life and supports the **PEG-PVP-encapsulation** industrial process. - **Carrier selection engineering** — peat, lignite, charcoal, talc, vermiculite — chosen based on **water-holding capacity (WHC)**, **pH buffering**, and **sterilization compatibility** (γ-irradiation at **5 Mrad** for carrier sterilization is industrial standard). - **Bioencapsulation using alginate beads** for controlled-release PGPR formulations — a frontier research area tied to KTU Module 4 advanced topics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric N₂-Fixation Energy Balance > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Objective:** Derive the **minimum ATP requirement** and **H₂:NH₃ ratio** for biological nitrogen fixation, then compute the **theoretical maximum N₂-fixation rate** per unit biomass. **Given Data:** - Reaction: $N_2 + 8H^+ + 8e^- + 16\,ATP \rightarrow 2NH_3 + H_2 + 16\,ADP + 16\,P_i$ - ΔG° of ATP hydrolysis in cells: **−30.5 kJ/mol** (physiological) - Free energy of NH₃ formation from N₂: **ΔG° ≈ +33.4 kJ/mol** (under standard biochemical conditions the fixation is *endergonic* but driven by ATP) - Free energy of H₂ formation from 2H⁺ + 2e⁻: **ΔG° = 0** (set as reference) - Assumption: All 8 electrons pass through nitrogenase; minimum 25% wasted on H₂ evolution **Step 1 — Total ATP Energy Supplied:** $$E_{supplied} = 16 \times 30.5 = 488\,\text{kJ per mol N}_2$$ **Step 2 — Useful Work in NH₃ Formation (2 moles per mol N₂ fixed):** $$E_{useful} = 2 \times 33.4 = 66.8\,\text{kJ per mol N}_2$$ **Step 3 — Thermodynamic Efficiency of the Biological Process:** $$\eta_{thermo} = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{supplied}} = \frac{66.8}{488} \times 100\% = 13.69\%$$ **Step 4 — Why This Efficiency is So Low (Conceptual Reasoning):** The remaining **86.31%** of energy is consumed by: - ATP hydrolysis overhead (binding changes, conformational shifts in MoFe-protein) - Proton gradient maintenance across bacteroid membrane - **H₂ side-reaction** (at least 1 mol H₂ per mol N₂ is unavoidable) **Step 5 — Calculate the H₂:N₂ Molar Ratio:** Given the stoichiometry, every 1 mol N₂ fixed produces ≥ 1 mol H₂: $$\frac{n_{H_2}}{n_{N_2}} = \frac{1}{1} = 1.0 \quad \text{(theoretical minimum)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In *Rhizobium*, **uptake hydrogenase (Hup)** enzymes re-oxidize H₂ to recover energy, raising overall efficiency. Hup⁺ strains fix 4–8% more N₂ per carbon source than Hup⁻ strains — a frequent KTU numerical question. **Step 6 — Theoretical N₂ Fixation Rate per Gram Biomass:** Assume Azotobacter specific respiration rate = 15 mg O₂/(g cell·h), and N₂ fixation consumes 8% of total electron flow: $$R_{N_2} = 0.08 \times 15 \times \frac{28}{32} \times 0.5 = 0.525\,\text{mg N}_2 \text{ per g cell per hour}$$ where the factor 28/32 converts O₂ to N₂ molecular masses, and 0.5 accounts for the reduction stoichiometry. ## 3.2 Derivation: Shelf-Life Decay Kinetics **Problem:** A carrier-based Rhizobium biofertilizer has initial CFU of **5 × 10⁸ CFU/g**. After **120 days** at 30 °C, the CFU drops to **1.2 × 10⁸ CFU/g**. Find the decay constant *k* and the expiry CFU. **Step 1 — First-order decay model:** $$\frac{dN}{dt} = -kN \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(N_t) = \ln(N_0) - kt$$ **Step 2 — Solve for k:** $$k = \frac{\ln(N_0) - \ln(N_t)}{t} = \frac{\ln(5 \times 10^8) - \ln(1.2 \times 10^8)}{120}$$ $$k = \frac{\ln(5/1.2)}{120} = \frac{\ln(4.1667)}{120} = \frac{1.4271}{120} = 0.01189\,\text{day}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Compute CFU after 180 days (typical shelf life target):** $$N_{180} = (5 \times 10^8) \times e^{-0.01189 \times 180}$$ $$N_{180} = (5 \times 10^8) \times e^{-2.140}$$ $$N_{180} = (5 \times 10^8) \times 0.1175 = 5.88 \times 10^7\,\text{CFU/g}$$ > [!WARNING] > The result **5.88 × 10⁷ CFU/g is BELOW the BIS minimum of 10⁸ CFU/g** at the 180-day mark. The KTU exam implication: this formulation **fails the regulatory shelf-life requirement** — students must recommend reformulation with **cell-protectants (trehalose 1% w/w, glycerol 0.5% v/w)** to lower *k* to ~ 0.006 /day. ## 3.3 Derivation: P-Solubilization Quantitative Index **Problem:** A PSB isolate forms a colony of diameter **8 mm** on Pikovskaya's agar with a clear halo of **20 mm total diameter**. Calculate the **Phosphate Solubilization Index (PSI)**. **Step 1 — Apply the PSI definition:** $$PSI = \frac{D_{colony} + D_{halo}}{D_{colony}} = \frac{8 + 20}{8} = \frac{28}{8} = 3.5$$ > [!NOTE] > A PSI > 2.0 indicates an **industrially promising PSB strain**. A value of 3.5 places this isolate in the **high-efficiency category**, suitable for scale-up fermentation. ## 3.4 Derivation: Carbon-Economics of Azolla–Anabaena Symbiosis **Given:** Azolla fronds contain 4% N (dry weight), biomass yield = 30 tonnes fresh weight/ha/season (moisture 90%). **Step 1 — Compute dry biomass:** $$M_{dry} = 30\,000 \times (1 - 0.90) = 3\,000\,\text{kg/ha}$$ **Step 2 — Compute N contribution:** $$M_N = 3\,000 \times 0.04 = 120\,\text{kg N/ha}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to urea-equivalent (46% N):** $$M_{urea} = \frac{120}{0.46} = 260.9\,\text{kg urea/ha}$$ **Step 4 — Translate to economic value (assuming urea = ₹ 12/kg N):** $$\text{Value} = 120 \times 12 = ₹\,1\,440/\text{ha per season}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This 120 kg N/ha figure is **comparable to a full chemical urea application** for lowland rice — the ecological and economic case for Azolla biofertilizer in KTU case-study questions is overwhelming. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance | Parameter | Rhizobium (Symbiotic) | Azotobacter (Asymbiotic) | Azospirillum (Associative) | BGA + Azolla | Mycorrhiza (VAM) | PSB | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **N₂ fixed (kg N/ha/yr)** | 50–200 | 5–20 | 10–40 | 20–70 | 0 (P-mobilizer) | 0 (P-solubilizer) | | **Crop specificity** | Legumes only | Non-legumes | Cereals/grasses | Rice only | Most crops | All crops | | **Shelf life (carrier)** | 6 months | 6 months | 3–4 months | 4–6 months | 6–8 months | 6 months | | **Inoculum cost (₹/ha)** | 50–100 | 50–100 | 60–120 | 80–150 | 100–200 | 75–125 | | **Establishment time** | 7–14 days | 3–7 days | 5–10 days | 10–20 days | 20–40 days | 5–10 days | | **Nodulation / infection %** | 60–80% | — | — | — | 70–90% | — | | **Re-inoculation cycle** | Each season | Each season | Each season | Annual | Once every 2–3 yr | Each season | # 4.
